topics.title: Demonstrate safety measures against electrical shocks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZESL208 - BASIC ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Demonstrate safety measures against electrical shocks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrical Shock (KTU Definition):**
> An *electrical shock* is the physiological reaction or injury caused when an electric current passes through the human body due to accidental contact with a live conductor or a faulty appliance. The severity depends on the magnitude of current, path through the body, duration of exposure, and frequency of the supply.

> [!NOTE]
> **Electrical Safety Measures (KTU Workshop Definition):**
> A set of preventive practices, protective devices, earthing systems, insulation techniques, and procedural rules designed to (i) prevent accidental contact with live parts, (ii) limit the fault current magnitude, and (iii) disconnect the supply rapidly when a fault occurs, thereby protecting human life and equipment.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Pipe" Intuition

Think of electricity as **water flowing through pipes**:

- **Voltage (V)** is the *water pressure* pushing the water.
- **Current (I)** is the *flow rate* of water.
- **Resistance (R)** is the *narrowness* of the pipe.

Your body is essentially a *partially-open pipe*. Normally, the skin (dry) acts like a *narrow pipe* with high resistance (around **100,000 Ω**). But if your skin is wet (sweat, water), the pipe becomes *wide* and resistance drops to about **1,000 Ω**. Now, even a small "pressure" (voltage) can drive a *huge flow* (current) through you — which is why a wet hand touching a live wire is far more dangerous than a dry hand.

> [!TIP]
> **Key Intuition:** Electricity does not "jump" out of wires looking for you. Current only flows when YOU complete a path between a higher potential and a lower potential. Therefore, *insulation, isolation, and earthing* work by either blocking the path or providing a *much easier alternate path* for the current so it bypasses your body.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Thresholds

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Human body resistance (dry skin) | $R_{body}$ | **100,000 Ω** (approx.) |
| Human body resistance (wet skin) | $R_{body,wet}$ | **1,000 Ω** (approx.) |
| Supply frequency (India) | $f$ | **50 Hz** |
| Standard domestic voltage (India) | $V_{LN}$ | **230 V AC** |
| Let-go threshold (men) | $I_{LG,m}$ | **9 mA** (approx.) |
| Let-go threshold (women) | $I_{LG,f}$ | **6 mA** (approx.) |
| Ventricular fibrillation threshold | $I_{VF}$ | **~50 mA** for $t \geq 1\,\text{s}$ |
| Maximum harmless current | $I_{safe}$ | **5 mA** (sensation threshold) |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Above 50 mA for more than 1 second is potentially LETHAL.** Always treat any mains-voltage conductor as live and dangerous.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 How an Electrical Shock Occurs — The Three Conditions

For a shock to occur, **all three conditions** must be met simultaneously:

1. **A potential difference** exists between two points (e.g., live wire and earth, or live and neutral).
2. **A conductive path** is formed between these two points — often the human body.
3. **Sufficient current** flows through the body to cause a physiological effect.

If any one of these is broken, no shock occurs. **Every safety measure targets at least one of these three conditions.**

## 2.2 The Physics of Current Through the Human Body

The human body is modelled as a **non-linear resistor** in series with the supply. The current that passes through the body is given by Ohm's Law:

$$
I_{body} \;=\; \frac{V_{touch}}{R_{source} + R_{body}}
$$

where:
- $V_{touch}$ = Touch voltage (the voltage between the contacted conductor and the feet/earth).
- $R_{source}$ = Source impedance of the supply network.
- $R_{body}$ = Total resistance of the body between contact point and the return path.

The body resistance itself is a **series combination** of:
- Skin resistance at the entry point $R_{skin,in}$
- Internal (blood + tissue) resistance $R_{internal}$
- Skin resistance at the exit point $R_{skin,out}$

$$
R_{body} \;=\; R_{skin,in} + R_{internal} + R_{skin,out}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Internal resistance** $R_{internal}$ is roughly **300 Ω** and is almost constant. The dominant variable is the **skin resistance**, which can collapse from **100 kΩ (dry)** to about **1 kΩ (wet, broken, or sweaty)** — a **100×** reduction that can turn a harmless tingle into a lethal shock.

## 2.3 Physiological Effects of Current (50 Hz AC, 1 second exposure)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **IEEE C95.1 / IEC 60479**-aligned thresholds most commonly used in KTU workshop modules. Memorise the bold values.

| Current Range | Physiological Effect |
|---|---|
| **0.5 – 5 mA** | Threshold of perception; mild tingle. |
| **5 – 10 mA** | Mild shock; "let-go" may still be possible. |
| **10 – 30 mA** | Painful shock; muscle control lost; **"let-go" threshold**. |
| **30 – 50 mA** | Severe pain; respiratory muscle paralysis begins. |
| **50 – 100 mA** | **Ventricular fibrillation** possible (often fatal). |
| **> 100 mA** | Sustained ventricular contraction; severe burns; usually fatal. |
| **> 1 A** | Severe burns; cardiac arrest; internal organ damage. |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> This is your exam-ready formula box. All derivations in SECTION 3 are built from these.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_{body} = \dfrac{V_{touch}}{R_{body}}$ | Body current under fault | A |
| 2 | $R_{body} = R_{skin,in} + R_{internal} + R_{skin,out}$ | Total body resistance | Ω |
| 3 | $P_{body} = I_{body}^{\,2} \cdot R_{body} = V_{touch} \cdot I_{body}$ | Power dissipated in body | W |
| 4 | $E_{body} = P_{body} \cdot t$ | Energy delivered to body (burns) | J |
| 5 | $I_{trip} \leq \dfrac{V_{touch}}{R_{body,max}}$ | Required RCD/ELCB trip current | A |
| 6 | $R_{earth} \leq \dfrac{V_{touch,max}}{I_{trip}}$ | Earth electrode resistance limit | Ω |
| 7 | $V_{touch,max} \leq 50\,\text{V}$ (dry) / $25\,\text{V}$ (wet) | IEC safe touch voltage limit | V |
| 8 | $t_{trip} \leq \dfrac{k^{2} \cdot S^{2}}{I_{f}^{2}}$ | Adiabatic equation for cable sizing (fuse discrimination) | s |

## 2.5 Classification of Safety Measures

Safety measures are grouped into **three tiers** in KTU syllabus:

### Tier 1 — Preventive (Design Stage)
- **Insulation** of conductors (PVC, rubber, XLPE).
- **Earthing / Grounding** (system earth + equipment earth).
- **Double insulation** symbol on appliances.
- **Safe clearance** distances (KTU rule: **2.5 m vertical**, **1.25 m horizontal** for 230 V).
- **Use of ELV** (Extra Low Voltage, $V < 50\,\text{V}$ AC) for student labs.

### Tier 2 — Protective Devices (Install Stage)
- **Fuse** — sacrificial wire that melts at over-current.
- **MCB (Miniature Circuit Breaker)** — thermal-magnetic trip.
- **MCCB (Moulded Case Circuit Breaker)** — for higher ratings.
- **RCCB / ELCB / RCD** — Residual Current device, trips at **30 mA** for personal protection.
- **Isolators and DP switches.**

### Tier 3 — Procedural (Behavioural Stage)
- **"Lock-out, Tag-out" (LOTO)** before maintenance.
- **Use of PPE** (rubber gloves, insulated tools, safety shoes, goggles).
- **One-hand rule** when working near live circuits.
- **Testing for dead** with a *test lamp* or *non-contact voltage tester* before touching.
- **Buddy system** — never work alone on live equipment.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

These safety principles are not confined to domestic wiring. They form the backbone of:
- **Industrial switchgear design** (IEC 60947 series).
- **Substation earthing grid design** (IEEE 80).
- **Medical equipment safety** (IEC 60601) — patient leakage currents limited to **10 µA**.
- **EV charging infrastructure** (IEC 61851) — mandatory RCD protection.
- **Aerospace and railway traction** — where human and equipment safety is non-negotiable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1: Body Current Under Domestic Touch Voltage

**Problem:** A person with dry skin ($R_{body} = 100\,\text{k}\Omega$) accidentally touches a **230 V AC** live wire while standing on a dry wooden floor. The wooden floor provides an effective return resistance of $R_{return} = 10\,\text{k}\Omega$. Calculate:
(a) The current through the body.
(b) The power dissipated in the body.
(c) The energy delivered in **0.5 s** of contact.

### Given Data
- Touch voltage: $V_{touch} = 230\,\text{V}$
- Body resistance: $R_{body} = 100{,}000\,\Omega$
- Return path resistance: $R_{return} = 10{,}000\,\Omega$
- Time of contact: $t = 0.5\,\text{s}$

### Step 1: Calculate Total Circuit Resistance
The person forms a series loop between the live conductor and the return path. The total resistance is:

$$
R_{total} \;=\; R_{body} + R_{return}
$$

$$
R_{total} \;=\; 100{,}000 + 10{,}000
$$

$$
R_{total} \;=\; 110{,}000\,\Omega
$$

### Step 2: Apply Ohm's Law to Find Body Current
Using the Ohm's law relationship for the loop:

$$
I_{body} \;=\; \frac{V_{touch}}{R_{total}}
$$

$$
I_{body} \;=\; \frac{230}{110{,}000}
$$

$$
I_{body} \;=\; 2.091 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{A}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,I_{body} \;\approx\; 2.09\,\text{mA}\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** The current of **2.09 mA** is **below the perception threshold** of 5 mA. The person will *not even feel* the shock. This is why dry skin is a (partial) natural insulator.

### Step 3: Calculate Power Dissipated in the Body
Using the power-current-resistance form:

$$
P_{body} \;=\; I_{body}^{\,2} \cdot R_{body}
$$

$$
P_{body} \;=\; (2.091 \times 10^{-3})^{2} \cdot 100{,}000
$$

$$
P_{body} \;=\; (4.371 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 100{,}000
$$

$$
P_{body} \;=\; 0.4371\,\text{W}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,P_{body} \;\approx\; 0.44\,\text{W}\,}
$$

### Step 4: Calculate Energy Delivered in 0.5 Seconds
Energy is the product of power and time:

$$
E_{body} \;=\; P_{body} \cdot t
$$

$$
E_{body} \;=\; 0.4371 \cdot 0.5
$$

$$
\boxed{\,E_{body} \;\approx\; 0.22\,\text{J}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion of Derivation 1:** A *dry* person on a *dry* surface touching **230 V** may not even feel it. The hazard is *latent* — the slightest moisture changes everything, as shown in the next derivation.

---

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2: Body Current Under Wet Conditions

**Problem:** The same person now has **sweaty / wet hands**, so $R_{body}$ drops to **1,000 Ω**, and they stand on a *wet concrete floor* with $R_{return} = 200\,\Omega$. Recalculate the body current.

### Given Data
- Touch voltage: $V_{touch} = 230\,\text{V}$
- Body resistance: $R_{body} = 1{,}000\,\Omega$
- Return path resistance: $R_{return} = 200\,\Omega$

### Step 1: Total Resistance
$$
R_{total} \;=\; R_{body} + R_{return}
$$

$$
R_{total} \;=\; 1{,}000 + 200 \;=\; 1{,}200\,\Omega
$$

### Step 2: Body Current
$$
I_{body} \;=\; \frac{V_{touch}}{R_{total}}
$$

$$
I_{body} \;=\; \frac{230}{1{,}200}
$$

$$
I_{body} \;=\; 0.1917\,\text{A} \;\approx\; 191.7\,\text{mA}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,I_{body} \;\approx\; 192\,\text{mA}\,}
$$

### Step 3: Power and Energy
$$
P_{body} \;=\; I_{body}^{\,2} \cdot R_{body}
$$

$$
P_{body} \;=\; (0.1917)^{2} \cdot 1{,}000
$$

$$
P_{body} \;=\; 0.0367 \cdot 1{,}000
$$

$$
P_{body} \;=\; 36.7\,\text{W}
$$

For a contact of $t = 1\,\text{s}$:

$$
E_{body} \;=\; 36.7 \cdot 1 \;=\; 36.7\,\text{J}
$$

> [!CAUTION]
> **192 mA is over 3× the ventricular-fibrillation threshold.** This contact is **instantly lethal** if sustained for more than a fraction of a second. The 100× drop in body resistance turned a *harmless* 2 mA into a *killer* 192 mA with the *same* supply voltage.

---

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3: Sizing an Earth Electrode for a Domestic Installation

**Problem:** A domestic TT earthing system must limit the touch voltage to **$V_{touch,max} = 50\,\text{V}$** under worst-case fault conditions. The RCD trips at $I_{trip} = 30\,\text{mA} = 0.030\,\text{A}$. Calculate the **maximum allowable earth electrode resistance**.

### Given Data
- Maximum permissible touch voltage: $V_{touch,max} = 50\,\text{V}$
- RCD trip current: $I_{trip} = 0.030\,\text{A}$

### Step 1: Apply the Touch-Voltage Limit
By definition, the touch voltage under fault is the product of the fault current and the earth resistance:

$$
V_{touch} \;=\; I_{trip} \cdot R_{earth}
$$

For the system to be safe, $V_{touch} \leq V_{touch,max}$:

$$
I_{trip} \cdot R_{earth} \;\leq\; V_{touch,max}
$$

### Step 2: Solve for $R_{earth}$
$$
R_{earth} \;\leq\; \frac{V_{touch,max}}{I_{trip}}
$$

$$
R_{earth} \;\leq\; \frac{50}{0.030}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,R_{earth} \;\leq\; 1{,}666.67\,\Omega\,}
$$

### Step 3: Practical Interpretation
A typical **domestic earth electrode** must have resistance **$\leq 5\,\Omega$** to ensure fast RCD operation and minimum earth potential rise. The above $1666\,\Omega$ is the *absolute maximum* derived from safety — a real installation aims for **$\leq 1\,\Omega$** to provide a wide safety margin.

---

## 3.4 Worked Derivation 4: Verification of Let-Go Threshold Crossing

**Problem:** A current of **15 mA** passes through a worker's hand-to-hand path for **0.8 s** in a workshop accident. Using the IEC 60479 time-current zone, determine if this is above the let-go threshold and if it is potentially dangerous.

### Given Data
- Current: $I = 15\,\text{mA} = 0.015\,\text{A}$
- Duration: $t = 0.8\,\text{s}$
- Body resistance (hand-to-hand, dry): $R_{body} = 2{,}000\,\Omega$

### Step 1: Check the Current Level
$$
I \;=\; 15\,\text{mA} \;>\; I_{LG} \;=\; 9\,\text{mA}
$$

$\Rightarrow$ **Above the let-go threshold.** The worker will *not* be able to release the conductor voluntarily.

### Step 2: Check Duration Relative to Fibrillation Limit
At 15 mA, muscles contract but the heart rhythm is generally preserved. However, if the current path is hand-to-hand (crosses the chest), the heart lies in the current path, raising the fibrillation risk.

### Step 3: Voltage Across the Body
$$
V_{body} \;=\; I \cdot R_{body}
$$

$$
V_{body} \;=\; 0.015 \cdot 2{,}000
$$

$$
V_{body} \;=\; 30\,\text{V}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The 15 mA current is *muscularly paralyzing* (cannot let go), and because the path is hand-to-hand, the heart is in series with the current. The worker must be **disconnected from supply immediately** by another person switching off the mains — never try to pull the victim away with bare hands.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Safety Device

| Fault Type | Best Primary Protection | Best Backup Protection | Why? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Overload (slow)** | Thermal MCB (Type B/C) | HRC Fuse | $I^{2}t$ heating matches cable limit |
| **Short circuit (fast)** | Magnetic MCB / MCCB | HRC Fuse | Sub-cycle interruption, limits let-through energy |
| **Earth leakage (human shock)** | **RCCB (30 mA)** | ELCB / Equipotential bonding | Sensitive to imbalance; trips in $<\!30\,\text{ms}$ |
| **Arc fault (fire)** | **AFDD** (Arc Fault Detection Device) | MCB | Detects series and parallel arcing signatures |
| **Over-voltage (surge)** | **SPD** (Surge Protection Device) | Equipotential bonding | Diverts lightning/switching transients to earth |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
