```mermaid
    PLUNG --> TRIP1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on Mermaid Limitations:** True pictorial wiring diagrams (with conductor lines crossing/joining as per Indian Standard symbols) cannot be rendered natively in Mermaid. The diagrams above depict the **functional architecture and power-flow logic**, which is what examiners and lab instructors expect in a workshop record. For a physical wiring diagram, students are expected to draw the line diagram by hand on graph paper using standard IS symbols in the practical record.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: Define ELCB. State two advantages of using it in domestic wiring.** `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, GZESL208]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** An **Earth Leakage Circuit Breaker (ELCB)**, also known as a **Residual Current Circuit Breaker (RCCB)**, is a protective switching device that automatically disconnects the circuit whenever the current flowing in the phase conductor differs from the current returning through the neutral conductor. This difference, called the *residual current*, indicates that some current is leaking to earth (possibly through a human body), and the device trips typically at **30 mA** within **30 milliseconds**.

**Two advantages (1 mark — 0.5 each):**
1. Provides personal protection against electric shock by detecting small leakage currents that ordinary fuses/MCBs cannot sense.
2. Prevents fire hazards caused by persistent low-level earth leakage that overheats insulation over time.

---

#### **Question 2: What is the function of the energy meter in a domestic wiring system? Write the unit in which the energy is measured.** `[KTU University Exam – Model Question, GZESL208]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Function (2 marks):** The energy meter (kWh meter) is an **induction-type integrating instrument** installed at the consumer's premises. Its function is to **measure the total electrical energy consumed by the household over a billing period**. It contains an aluminium disc whose rotational speed is proportional to the instantaneous real power $P = V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$, and the cumulative revolutions are integrated by a cyclometer register to display total energy in kWh.

**Unit (1 mark):** Energy is measured in **kilo-Watt-hour (kWh)**, commercially called a **"unit"** on the electricity bill.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A: Wiring of Power Distribution Arrangement (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam – Model Question, GZESL208, Module Experiment 2]`

**(a) Draw the single-line diagram of a domestic single-phase power distribution arrangement showing the energy meter, DP main switch, ELCB, MCB distribution board and connected loads. List the function of each component. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Single-Line Diagram (4 marks):**

The single-line diagram is to be drawn as a horizontal flow from left (supply) to right (loads), in the order:

```
[Service Pole] → [Energy Meter (kWh)] → [DP Main Switch] → [ELCB/RCCB 30 mA] → [MCB DB] → [Sub-circuits]
                                                                                       ├── 6 A MCB → Lighting
                                                                                       ├── 16 A MCB → 5A/15A Sockets
                                                                                       └── 25 A MCB → AC / Geyser
```

**Functions (3 marks — 1 mark each for first three components):**

1. **Energy Meter:** Records total energy consumed in kWh for billing purposes. Works on the Ferraris induction principle with driving torque $T_d \propto V I \cos\phi$ and braking torque from a permanent magnet.
2. **DP Main Switch:** Provides manual isolation of the entire installation from the supply during maintenance. It is a Double Pole switch that simultaneously opens Phase and Neutral conductors.
3. **ELCB / RCCB:** Detects residual (leakage) current $\Delta I = I_{ph} - I_n \neq 0$ and trips within 30 ms at 30 mA sensitivity, protecting humans from electric shock and preventing fire from sustained leakage.
4. **MCB DB:** Houses multiple Miniature Circuit Breakers that protect individual sub-circuits against overload (thermal trip) and short circuit (magnetic trip), enabling selective isolation.

---

**(b) Explain with a neat sketch the working of a single-phase induction type energy meter. A meter has a constant of 1200 rev/kWh. If the disc makes 2400 revolutions in 10 minutes, calculate the load power and the total energy consumed in 2 hours of continuous operation at the same load. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Working of Energy Meter (3 marks):**

The single-phase induction type energy meter consists of:
- A **voltage coil (potential coil)** of many turns connected across Phase and Neutral — produces a flux $\phi_v \propto V$.
- A **current coil** of few turns of thick wire connected in series with the load — produces a flux $\phi_i \propto I$.
- An **aluminium disc** mounted on a spindle, free to rotate.
- A **permanent magnet** providing braking torque.
- A **cyclometer register** that records disc revolutions.

The two alternating fluxes induce eddy currents in the disc. The interaction of these eddy currents with the fluxes produces a driving torque $T_d = K \cdot V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi \propto P$. The permanent magnet produces a braking torque $T_b \propto N$. At steady state, $T_d = T_b$, giving $P = N / K_h$. Total energy in time $t$ is $E = P \cdot t = N_{total} / K_h$.

**Calculation (4 marks — 1 mark per step):**

**Given:** $K_h = 1200$ rev/kWh, $N = 2400$ revolutions, $t = 10$ minutes $= 1/6$ hour.

**Step 1 — Energy consumed in 10 minutes:**

$$E_1 = \frac{N}{K_h} = \frac{2400}{1200} = 2.0 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 2 — Load power:**

$$P = \frac{E_1}{t_1} = \frac{2.0 \text{ kWh}}{1/6 \text{ h}} = 12.0 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Total revolutions in 2 hours at the same load:**

Number of revolutions per hour = $P \times K_h = 12.0 \times 1200 = 14400$ rev/h.

For 2 hours: $N_{2h} = 14400 \times 2 = 28800$ revolutions.

**Step 4 — Total energy in 2 hours:**

$$E_{2h} = \frac{N_{2h}}{K_h} = \frac{28800}{1200} = \boxed{24.0 \text{ kWh}}$$

*Verification using $E = P \cdot t$:* $E = 12.0 \text{ kW} \times 2 \text{ h} = 24.0$ kWh ✓

---

#### **Question B: Protection Devices in Domestic Wiring (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

`[KTU University Exam – Model Question, GZESL208, Module Experiment 2]`

**(a) Explain the working principle of an ELCB / RCCB. Why is it preferred over a fuse for personal safety? Differentiate between ELCB and MCB in terms of operating principle and protection offered. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle of ELCB / RCCB (3 marks):**

The RCCB uses a **toroidal (ring-shaped) core-balance current transformer (CBCT)** through which both the Phase and Neutral conductors of the consumer's installation pass in opposite directions. In a healthy circuit, the magnetic fluxes produced by $I_{ph}$ and $I_n$ are equal and opposite, cancelling out in the core. The secondary sensing winding therefore sees **zero flux and produces no signal**.

When a leakage occurs (e.g., a person touches a live wire), current $I_{leak}$ flows to earth. The currents no longer balance: $I_{ph} \neq I_n$. The differential current $\Delta I = I_{ph} - I_n$ creates a net alternating flux in the toroid, inducing a voltage in the secondary winding. This voltage is amplified and energises a trip coil that opens the main contacts within **30 ms** for a 30 mA rated device, disconnecting the supply.

**Why preferred over fuse (2 marks):**
- A fuse operates only at high currents (typically 5–10 A) and cannot detect low-level leakage currents (30 mA) which are lethal to humans.
- An ELCB trips on **residual current imbalance**, not on absolute current magnitude, making it sensitive to the small currents that flow through the human body during an electric shock.
- A fuse is a **one-time** device (must be replaced after blowing), whereas an ELCB can be **reset** by a simple switch.

**Differentiate ELCB vs MCB (2 marks):**

| Parameter | ELCB / RCCB | MCB |
|---|---|---|
| Detects | Residual / leakage current ($\Delta I$) | Overcurrent (overload + short circuit) |
| Operating current | Very low — 30 mA to 500 mA | 6 A to 63 A (or higher) |
| Trip mechanism | Core-balance CT + trip relay | Bimetal (thermal) + Solenoid (magnetic) |
| Protects against | Electric shock, earth leakage fire | Overload, short circuit |
| Trip time | ≤ 30 ms for 30 mA | Thermal: seconds; Magnetic: < 100 ms |
| Application | Human safety (always after MCB) | Sub-circuit protection |

---

**(b) A household has the following loads connected to a 230 V, 50 Hz single-phase supply: 5 tube lights of 40 W each, 2 fans of 75 W each, 1 refrigerator of 200 W and 1 air-conditioner of 1500 W. Calculate (i) the total connected load, (ii) the total energy consumed per day if the usage pattern is 6 h for lights, 10 h for fans, 24 h for refrigerator and 8 h for AC, (iii) the monthly electricity bill at ₹7.50 per unit, and (iv) the suitable rating of the main MCB and a 30 mA RCCB for this installation. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Total Connected Load (1 mark):**

$$P_{lights} = 5 \times 40 = 200 \text{ W}$$
$$P_{fans} = 2 \times 75 = 150 \text{ W}$$
$$P_{fridge} = 200 \text{ W}$$
$$P_{AC} = 1500 \text{ W}$$
$$P_{total} = 200 + 150 + 200 + 1500 = 2050 \text{ W} = 2.05 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 — Energy per day (2 marks):**

$$E_{lights} = 0.200 \times 6 = 1.20 \text{ kWh}$$
$$E_{fans} = 0.150 \times 10 = 1.50 \text{ kWh}$$
$$E_{fridge} = 0.200 \times 24 = 4.80 \text{ kWh}$$
$$E_{AC} = 1.500 \times 8 = 12.00 \text{ kWh}$$
$$E_{day} = 1.20 + 1.50 + 4.80 + 12.00 = \boxed{19.50 \text{ kWh/day}}$$

**Step 3 — Monthly energy and bill (2 marks):**

$$E_{month} = 19.50 \times 30 = 585 \text{ kWh}$$

$$\text{Bill} = 585 \times 7.50 = \boxed{₹4387.50}$$

**Step 4 — MCB and RCCB rating selection (2 marks):**

Maximum current drawn at full load:

$$I_{max} = \frac{P_{total}}{V \cdot \cos\phi} = \frac{2050}{230 \times 1.0} \approx 8.91 \text{ A} \quad (\text{assuming unity pf for resistive loads})$$

Considering starting inrush of AC (typically $5\times$), and future load growth, select:

- **Main MCB: 32 A, C-curve, DP** (gives 3× headroom for AC inrush and future loads)
- **RCCB: 30 mA, 40 A, DP** (30 mA for human protection; 40 A rating to match MCB)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse kW with kWh** — Power is in kW, Energy in kWh. Many students write the unit of energy as kW, which is wrong.
> 2. **Do not place the RCCB before the main switch** in your answer — the ELCB must come *after* the main switch for periodic manual testing.
> 3. **Do not say "ELCB detects high current"** — it detects *imbalance* between phase and neutral currents, not absolute magnitude.
> 4. **Do not use 30 A or 50 A for a 2050 W load** — students often over-specify. Always justify your MCB rating by showing the $I_{max}$ calculation.
> 5. **Do not forget the Earth wire in the diagram** — drawing the Earth pit connection is a free 0.5–1 mark booster.
> 6. **For the meter problem, always show two methods of calculation** (using $E = P \cdot t$ and $E = N / K_h$) — examiners give partial marks for correct steps even if the final answer has an arithmetic slip.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ The **standard sequence** is: **Service Line → Energy Meter → DP Main Switch → ELCB/RCCB → MCB DB → Loads**. Memorise this order.
- ✅ **Energy meter measures kWh, not kW.** Meter constant $K_h$ is in **revolutions per kWh** (rev/kWh).
- ✅ Driving torque $T_d \propto V \cdot I \cdot \cos\phi$ and braking torque $T_b \propto N$ — at equilibrium, $P = N / K_h$.
- ✅ **RCCB/ELCB detects residual current** $\Delta I = I_{ph} - I_n$, not absolute current. Standard rating for human safety is **30 mA**, tripping in **≤ 30 ms**.
- ✅ **MCB has two trip mechanisms**: thermal (bimetal strip — for slow overload) and magnetic (solenoid — for instant short circuit).
- ✅ MCB and RCCB are **complementary**, not substitutes. A modern distribution board uses both.
- ✅ Standard domestic MCB ratings: **6 A (lighting), 16 A (sockets), 25 A (AC/geyser), 32 A (main)**.
- ✅ The **Earth wire** is critical — it carries leakage current to the earth pit, allowing the RCCB to detect imbalance.
- ✅ Indian domestic supply is **230 V, 50 Hz, single-phase, 2-wire + Earth (3-wire system)**.
- ✅ For exam problems: convert all power to **kW** and all time to **hours** before multiplying to get kWh.
- ✅ A **DP switch opens both Phase and Neutral simultaneously** — used as a manual isolator, not an automatic protection device.
- ✅ Always use **standard IS symbols** in the wiring diagram (Energy Meter = circle with kWh, MCB = rectangle with a cross-line, Earth = three horizontal lines of decreasing length, Switch = angled line on pivot).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Familiarization of step up and step-down transformers, use of low voltage transformers; measurement and representation of voltage and waveform to scale in graph sheet with the help of CRO
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** GZESL208 - BASIC ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING WORKSHOP > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Familiarization of step up and step-down transformers, use of low voltage transformers; measurement and representation of voltage and waveform to scale in graph sheet with the help of CRO # Module Experiment 2 — Transformers & CRO Waveform Analysis ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is a Transformer? A **transformer** is a static (stationary, no moving parts) electromagnetic device that transfers electrical energy from one alternating-current (AC) circuit to another through **mutual electromagnetic induction**, without any change in frequency. It can step the voltage up, step it down, or simply isolate two circuits while keeping the power (ideally) constant. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition (verbatim from syllabus spirit):** A transformer is a static piece of apparatus which, by electromagnetic induction, transforms a system of alternating voltage and current into another system of alternating voltage and current, usually of different values and at the same frequency. ### 1.2 Step-Up vs Step-Down Transformer — At a Glance | Parameter | Step-Down Transformer | Step-Up Transformer | |---|---|---| | **Primary Voltage (V₁)** | Higher | Lower | | **Secondary Voltage (V₂)** | Lower | Higher | | **Primary Turns (N₁)** | More | Fewer | | **Secondary Turns (N₂)** | Fewer | More | | **Function** | Reduces voltage | Increases voltage | | **Typical Use** | Phone charger, doorbell, SMPS | X-ray, microwave oven, distribution at substation | ### 1.3 Low-Voltage Transformer (Workshop Perspective) A **low-voltage transformer** is a small step-down transformer (typically **230 V AC → 6 V / 9 V / 12 V / 15 V / 24 V AC**) used in laboratories and electronic workshops to safely power experimental circuits, relays, solenoids, lamps, and educational trainer kits. In the KTU workshop, the most common rating is **230 V / (6-0-6) V** or **230 V / 12 V** with a current rating of **500 mA to 2 A**. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Indian Mains Supply:** **230 V, 50 Hz, single-phase AC** is the standard input. All workshop low-voltage transformers are designed to step this down to safer, electronics-friendly levels. ### 1.4 Cathode Ray Oscilloscope (CRO) — The Visual Voltmeter A **CRO** is a versatile electronic test instrument that displays voltage signals as a **two-dimensional graph of voltage (Y-axis) versus time (X-axis)** on a phosphor-coated screen. It is the workshop "eye" used to **measure amplitude, frequency, period, phase, and waveform shape** of any time-varying electrical signal. ### 1.5 Conceptual Analogy — The Big Picture > **Think of a transformer as a set of two water tanks connected at the bottom by a pipe (the magnetic core).** If you pour water in at a high level (high voltage, low current) on one side, it naturally comes out lower on the other side — but in *greater quantity* (lower voltage, higher current). The "level difference" is voltage, the "flow" is current, and the "pipe coupling" is the magnetic core. > > **Think of a CRO as a high-speed electronic "drawing tablet" that traces the rise and fall of voltage** — like a seismograph that records earthquakes. Instead of a needle scribbling on paper, an electron beam lights up a phosphor screen, and we can read the height (voltage) and the spacing (time) directly from calibrated graticule lines. ### 1.6 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **Standard Mains Frequency in India: 50 Hz** (period = **20 ms**) - **Standard Low-Voltage Secondary Ratings: 6 V, 9 V, 12 V, 15 V, 24 V AC** - **Phosphor Persistence of CRO Screen: P31 (green) or P1 (white)**, persistence ≈ 1 ms to 1 s - **Standard Graticule: 8 × 10 divisions**, each 1 cm - **Electron Beam Speed inside CRT: ≈ 10⁷ m/s** --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Working Principle of a Transformer — Step-by-Step Logic 1. **Primary coil is energized** with an alternating voltage V₁ from the AC mains. 2. This produces an **alternating flux (Φ)** in the laminated iron core, alternating at the same frequency *f*. 3. The changing flux **links the secondary coil** through the core (mutual induction). 4. By **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, an alternating EMF is induced in the secondary coil. 5. If the secondary is connected to a load, current I₂ flows, and energy is delivered to the load. 6. Simultaneously, to balance the induced EMF, a **counter-EMF (back-EMF)** is induced in the primary that limits the primary current to a small magnetizing value. ### 2.2 Constructional Details (Workshop Familiarization) - **Core:** Made of laminated silicon steel (CRGO — Cold Rolled Grain Oriented) sheets, thickness **0.35 mm**, insulated from each other to reduce **eddy current losses**. - **Primary Winding:** Connected to the AC mains (**230 V, 50 Hz**). Has more turns of thinner wire (in step-down) or fewer turns of thicker wire. - **Secondary Winding:** Delivers the stepped-down/stepped-up voltage. Has fewer turns (step-down) or more turns (step-up) of appropriate gauge wire. - **Bobbin/Former:** Insulating material (typically bakelite or plastic) on which the windings are placed. - **Lamination Stacking:** E-I, U-I, or C-core type — common in small low-voltage transformers. ### 2.3 Identification Procedure in the Workshop > [!NOTE] > **How to physically identify a step-up vs step-down transformer in the lab:** 1. **Look at the nameplate rating** — V₁/V₂ printed on the body. If V₁ > V₂, it is step-down; if V₁ < V₂, it is step-up. 2. **Count the turns** (if accessible): more turns on the primary → step-down; more turns on the secondary → step-up. 3. **Measure the wire thickness:** The low-voltage (higher current) winding uses thicker wire. In a step-down, the secondary wire is thicker; in a step-up, the primary wire is thicker. 4. **Test with a multimeter** on AC volts to confirm output. ### 2.4 The Transformation Ratio (k) The most important single parameter of a transformer is the **transformation ratio k**: $$k \;=\; \dfrac{N_{2}}{N_{1}} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{2}}{V_{1}} \;=\; \dfrac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}$$ - If **k > 1** → **Step-up** transformer - If **k < 1** → **Step-down** transformer - If **k = 1** → **Isolation transformer** (1:1) ### 2.5 EMF Equation of a Transformer The RMS value of the EMF induced in a winding of N turns is: $$E \;=\; 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_{m}$$ Where: - **f** = supply frequency in Hz - **N** = number of turns of that winding - **Φₘ** = maximum (peak) flux in Weber (Wb) - **4.44** = form-factor constant for a sinusoidal flux waveform (= π/√2 ≈ 4.443) For an ideal transformer: V₁ = E₁ and V₂ = E₂, so: $$\dfrac{V_{1}}{V_{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{N_{1}}{N_{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{k}$$ ### 2.6 Power Conservation in an Ideal Transformer $$V_{1} \, I_{1} \;=\; V_{2} \, I_{2} \quad \text{(ideal, no losses)}$$ In a real transformer, efficiency η is typically **60% to 95%** for small workshop transformers, and **> 98%** for large power transformers. ### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Formulas for KTU Board Exams — Memorize These:** | # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $k = \dfrac{V_{2}}{V_{1}} = \dfrac{N_{2}}{N_{1}} = \dfrac{I_{1}}{I_{2}}$ | Transformation ratio | Dimensionless | | 2 | $E = 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_{m}$ | Induced EMF (RMS) | Volts | | 3 | $V_{1} \, I_{1} \, \cos\phi_{1} = V_{2} \, I_{2} \, \cos\phi_{2}$ | Power balance (real) | Watts | | 4 | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{2} \, I_{2} \, \cos\phi_{2}}{V_{1} \, I_{1} \, \cos\phi_{1}} \times 100$ | Efficiency | % | | 5 | $V_{p-p} = (\text{vertical divisions}) \times (\text{V/div})$ | CRO peak-to-peak voltage | Volts | | 6 | $V_{rms} = \dfrac{V_{p-p}}{2\sqrt{2}} \quad \text{(sine wave)}$ | RMS from CRO reading | Volts | | 7 | $T = (\text{horizontal divisions per cycle}) \times (\text{time/div})$ | Time period | Seconds | | 8 | $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Frequency | Hertz | | 9 | $\Phi_{m} = \dfrac{E}{4.44 \, f \, N}$ | Max flux (for design) | Weber | | 10 | $\text{Waveform scale on graph: } 1 \text{ cm} = \dfrac{\text{Volts}}{\text{Vertical scale chosen (V/cm)}}$ | Graph plotting | V/cm | ### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Step-up transformers** are used at generating stations (11 kV → 400 kV) for long-distance power transmission to reduce I²R losses. - **Step-down transformers** bring 400 kV → 230 V for household use. - **Low-voltage transformers** in workshops power soldering stations, relay boards, and educational trainer kits (analog/digital electronics labs). - **CROs** are used in repair shops, R&D labs, medical equipment (ECG, EEG), and communication engineering to visualize and measure signals from Hz to GHz range (with modern DSOs). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — EMF Equation of a Transformer (E = 4.44 f N Φₘ) Let the primary winding have N₁ turns and be supplied with a sinusoidal alternating voltage V₁ at frequency *f*. The flux produced in the core varies sinusoidally as: $$\Phi(t) \;=\; \Phi_{m} \, \sin(\omega t)$$ The instantaneous flux linking the secondary (or primary) is NΦ(t). By **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**, the magnitude of the induced EMF in a single turn is: $$e_{\text{turn}} \;=\; \left\vert \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} \right\vert \;=\; \left\vert \dfrac{d}{dt}\bigl[\Phi_{m}\sin(\omega t)\bigr] \right\vert$$ Taking the derivative with respect to time: $$e_{\text{turn}} \;=\; \Phi_{m} \, \omega \, \cos(\omega t) \;=\; 2\pi f \, \Phi_{m} \, \cos(\omega t)$$ The peak (maximum) value of the EMF induced per turn is reached when cos(ωt) = 1: $$E_{0,\text{turn}} \;=\; 2\pi f \, \Phi_{m}$$ For a winding with N turns, the peak EMF is N times this: $$E_{0} \;=\; 2\pi f \, N \, \Phi_{m}$$ The RMS value is obtained by dividing the peak value by √2 (form factor of a sinusoid = peak/RMS): $$E \;=\; \dfrac{E_{0}}{\sqrt{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi f \, N \, \Phi_{m}}{\sqrt{2}}$$ Since **2π/√2 = √2 × π = 4.44** (numerically), we obtain the standard EMF equation: $$\boxed{\,E \;=\; 4.44 \, f \, N \, \Phi_{m}\,} \quad \text{(Volts, RMS)}$$ This single equation is the **backbone of all transformer design calculations** and is a guaranteed KTU exam question. ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Transformation Ratio (k = N₂/N₁) Applying the EMF equation to both primary and secondary windings of an ideal transformer (no voltage drop, no losses): $$E_{1} \;=\; 4.44 \, f \, N_{1} \, \Phi_{m} \quad \text{(primary EMF)}$$ $$E_{2} \;=\; 4.44 \, f \, N_{2} \, \Phi_{m} \quad \text{(secondary EMF)}$$ Dividing E₂ by E₁ (the frequency *f* and the flux Φₘ are common, hence they cancel): $$\dfrac{E_{2}}{E_{1}} \;=\; \dfrac{4.44 \, f \, N_{2} \, \Phi_{m}}{4.44 \, f \, N_{1} \, \Phi_{m}}$$ The 4.44, f, and Φₘ cancel out: $$\dfrac{E_{2}}{E_{1}} \;=\; \dfrac{N_{2}}{N_{1}}$$ For an ideal transformer, V₁ = E₁ and V₂ = E₂. Therefore: $$\boxed{\,\dfrac{V_{2}}{V_{1}} \;=\; \dfrac{N_{2}}{N_{1}} \;=\; k\,}$$ ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Voltage, Current, and Turns Relationship (Conservation of Power) For an ideal transformer (100% efficiency), the input power equals the output power (assuming purely resistive loads where cos φ = 1): $$V_{1} \, I_{1} \;=\; V_{2} \, I_{2}$$ Rearranging for the current ratio: $$\dfrac{I_{1}}{I_{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{2}}{V_{1}}$$ But from the EMF equation ratio above, V₂/V₁ = N₂/N₁, therefore: $$\dfrac{I_{1}}{I_{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{N_{2}}{N_{1}} \;=\; k$$ This proves the **"inverse relationship"** of a transformer: **a step-down in voltage (k < 1) is a step-up in current (I₂ > I₁), and vice versa**. ### 3.4 Derivation 4 — CRO Voltage Measurement (V_pp Calculation) Let the CRO vertical sensitivity be set to **S_v volts/div** (e.g., 2 V/div). Let the visible peak-to-peak waveform span a vertical height of **y divisions** (or y cm) on the graticule. Then the peak-to-peak voltage is: $$V_{p-p} \;=\; y \times S_{v}$$ For a **symmetric sinusoidal** waveform (no DC offset), the peak voltage is half the peak-to-peak voltage: $$V_{p} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{p-p}}{2} \;=\; \dfrac{y \, S_{v}}{2}$$ And the RMS voltage (for a pure sine) is: $$\boxed{\,V_{rms} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{p}}{\sqrt{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{p-p}}{2\sqrt{2}} \;\approx\; 0.3536 \, V_{p-p}\,}$$ ### 3.5 Derivation 5 — CRO Frequency Measurement (f = 1/T) Let the CRO time-base (sweep) be set to **S_t seconds/div** (e.g., 5 ms/div). Let **one complete cycle** of the waveform occupy a horizontal distance of **x divisions**. The time period of the waveform is: $$T \;=\; x \times S_{t}$$ The frequency is the reciprocal: $$\boxed{\,f \;=\; \dfrac{1}{T} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{x \, S_{t}}\,}$$ ### 3.6 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem) **Problem:** A step-down transformer used in the workshop is rated **230 V / 12 V, 50 Hz**. The primary has 1150 turns. Calculate: (a) The transformation ratio *k*. (b) The number of secondary turns N₂. (c) The maximum core flux Φₘ. (d) The secondary RMS current if a 12 V / 6 W lamp is connected. (e) The primary RMS current (assume 100% efficiency). **Solution:** **(a) Transformation ratio:** $$k \;=\; \dfrac{V_{2}}{V_{1}} \;=\; \dfrac{12}{230} \;\approx\; 0.0522$$ **(b) Secondary turns:** $$\dfrac{N_{2}}{N_{1}} \;=\; k \quad\Longrightarrow\quad N_{2} \;=\; k \times N_{1} \;=\; 0.0522 \times 1150$$ $$N_{2} \;=\; 60 \; \text{turns}$$ **(c) Maximum core flux:** Use the EMF equation on the primary: $$E_{1} \;\approx\; V_{1} \;=\; 4.44 \, f \, N_{1} \, \Phi_{m}$$ $$\Phi_{m} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{1}}{4.44 \, f \, N_{1}} \;=\; \dfrac{230}{4.44 \times 50 \times 1150}$$ $$\Phi_{m} \;=\; \dfrac{230}{255{,}300} \;\approx\; 9.0 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{Wb} \;=\; 0.90 \; \text{mWb}$$ **(d) Secondary current** (lamp load): $$I_{2} \;=\; \dfrac{P}{V_{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{6 \; \text{W}}{12 \; \text{V}} \;=\; 0.5 \; \text{A}$$ **(e) Primary current** (ideal transformer, 100% efficiency): $$V_{1} I_{1} \;=\; V_{2} I_{2} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad I_{1} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{2} I_{2}}{V_{1}} \;=\; \dfrac{12 \times 0.5}{230}$$ $$I_{1} \;=\; \dfrac{6}{230} \;\approx\; 0.0261 \; \text{A} \;=\; 26.1 \; \text{mA}$$ ### 3.7 Worked Example — Plotting a CRO Waveform to Scale A CRO displays a sine wave with: - Vertical sensitivity: **S_v = 1 V/div** - Time-base: **S_t = 2 ms/div** - Peak-to-peak height: **y = 6 divisions** (3 divisions above and 3 below the center line) - One complete cycle spans: **x = 5 divisions** horizontally **Calculations:** $$V_{p-p} \;=\; 6 \times 1 \;=\; 6 \; \text{V}$$ $$V_{p} \;=\; \dfrac{6}{2} \;=\; 3 \; \text{V}, \quad V_{rms} \;=\; \dfrac{3}{\sqrt{2}} \;\approx\; 2.12 \; \text{V}$$ $$T \;=\; 5 \times 2 \; \text{ms} \;=\; 10 \; \text{ms} \;=\; 0.01 \; \text{s}$$ $$f \;=\; \dfrac{1}{T} \;=\; 100 \; \text{Hz}$$ **Graph Sheet Plotting Scale (as per KTU workshop record):** > [!NOTE] > Choose a convenient scale such that the entire waveform fits on the graph sheet. Example: > - **X-axis (Time) scale: 1 cm = 1 ms** (1 division = 2 ms = 2 cm) > - **Y-axis (Voltage) scale: 1 cm = 1 V** (1 division = 1 V = 1 cm) > > Draw one full cycle (T = 10 ms → 10 cm wide) with peak amplitude (3 V → 3 cm tall), centered about the X-axis. Mark the time period and peak-to-peak voltage clearly on the drawn graph. --- ## 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` > > **Q1.** Define a transformer and distinguish clearly between a step-up and a step-down transformer. Mention any two applications of each type. **(3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** A transformer is a static electromagnetic device that transfers AC electrical energy from one circuit to another at the same frequency via mutual electromagnetic induction. | Feature | Step-Up | Step-Down | |---|---|---| | Secondary voltage V₂ | > V₁ | < V₁ | | Secondary turns N₂ | > N₁ | < N₁ | | Secondary current I₂ | < I₁ | > I₁ | - **Step-up applications:** EHT power transmission, X-ray machines, microwave ovens, CRT TV flyback. - **Step-down applications:** Mobile phone chargers, doorbells, soldering iron stations, electronic trainer kits, low-voltage workshop power supplies. --- > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` > > **Q2.** What is a Cathode Ray Oscilloscope (CRO)? List the quantities that can be measured using a CRO. **(3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** A CRO is a versatile electronic test instrument that displays the instantaneous value of a time-varying voltage signal as a 2D graph (Voltage on Y-axis vs. Time on X-axis) on a phosphor screen using a deflected electron beam. **Quantities measurable using a CRO:** 1. AC and DC voltage (amplitude, peak, peak-to-peak, RMS) 2. Frequency and Time period 3. Phase difference between two signals (using Lissajous patterns) 4. Pulse width, rise time, and fall time 5. Waveform shape and distortion --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) with Module Internal Choice > **[KTU University Exam – Module Internal Choice Set]** — `[KTU University Exam - Sample 2024 Scheme]` --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Transformer Analysis > **Q(A).** A 1-phase transformer used in a workshop has 800 primary turns and 200 secondary turns. The primary is connected to a 230 V, 50 Hz AC supply. The secondary supplies a load of 8 Ω impedance. > > **(a)** Identify the type of transformer (step-up or step-down). Calculate the secondary (no-load) voltage, the transformation ratio, and the maximum core flux. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** When the 8 Ω load is connected, calculate the secondary current, primary current (assuming 100% efficiency), and the power delivered to the load. Also explain the meaning of the transformation ratio with the help of a labeled block diagram. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Identification and basic parameters (7 marks):** - N₁ = 800 turns, N₂ = 200 turns, V₁ = 230 V, f = 50 Hz. - Since **N₂ < N₁**, the transformer is a **step-down transformer**. **Transformation ratio:** $$k \;=\; \dfrac{N_{2}}{N_{1}} \;=\; \dfrac{200}{800} \;=\; 0.25$$ **Secondary no-load voltage:** $$V_{2} \;=\; k \times V_{1} \;=\; 0.25 \times 230 \;=\; 57.5 \; \text{V}$$ **Maximum core flux (from EMF equation):** $$\Phi_{m} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{1}}{4.44 \, f \, N_{1}} \;=\; \dfrac{230}{4.44 \times 50 \times 800}$$ $$\Phi_{m} \;=\; \dfrac{230}{177{,}600} \;\approx\; 1.295 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{Wb} \;\approx\; 1.30 \; \text{mWb}$$ **(b) Loaded operation and explanation (7 marks):** **Secondary current (load current):** $$I_{2} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{2}}{R_{\text{load}}} \;=\; \dfrac{57.5}{8} \;\approx\; 7.1875 \; \text{A}$$ **Primary current (assuming 100% efficiency):** $$I_{1} \;=\; k \times I_{2} \;=\; 0.25 \times 7.1875 \;\approx\; 1.797 \; \text{A}$$ *Alternate method:* $I_1 = \dfrac{V_2 I_2}{V_1} = \dfrac{57.5 \times 7.1875}{230} = 1.797 \; A$ (same result ✓) **Power delivered to the load:** $$P_{\text{load}} \;=\; V_{2} \times I_{2} \;=\; 57.5 \times 7.1875 \;\approx\; 413.28 \; \text{W}$$ **Meaning of transformation ratio + block diagram:** The transformation ratio **k = N₂/N₁ = V₂/V₁ = I₁/I₂** is the key design parameter. It tells us **how the voltage, current, and number of turns are related** between primary and secondary. For this transformer, k = 0.25 means the secondary has ¼ the voltage, ¼ the turns, but 4 times the current of the primary. The block diagram consists of an **AC source → primary winding → shared magnetic core → secondary winding → load**, with mutual induction linking primary and secondary through the core. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** — CRO Waveform Measurement (Internal Choice) > **Q(B).** A low-voltage workshop transformer supplies a 12 V, 50 Hz sinusoidal AC output. This output is connected to the Y-input of a CRO. The CRO controls are set as follows: **Vertical sensitivity = 2 V/div, Time-base = 5 ms/div**. The displayed waveform is observed to be **6 divisions peak-to-peak** vertically, and **one complete cycle** spans **4 divisions** horizontally. > > **(a)** Calculate the peak-to-peak voltage, peak voltage, RMS voltage, time period, and frequency of the observed waveform. Compare your results with the expected transformer output rating. **(7 Marks)** > > **(b)** Explain how this waveform can be plotted to scale on a standard graph sheet. Show the complete tabulation of measured values, chosen scales, and the final drawn sine wave with all critical points labeled. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) CRO Measurement Calculations (7 marks):** **Peak-to-peak voltage:** $$V_{p-p} \;=\; (\text{vertical divisions}) \times (\text{V/div}) \;=\; 6 \times 2 \;=\; 12 \; \text{V}$$ **Peak voltage:** $$V_{p} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{p-p}}{2} \;=\; \dfrac{12}{2} \;=\; 6 \; \text{V}$$ **RMS voltage:** $$V_{rms} \;=\; \dfrac{V_{p}}{\sqrt{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{6}{1.414} \;\approx\; 4.24 \; \text{V}$$ **Time period:** $$T \;=\; (\text{horizontal divisions per cycle}) \times (\text{time/div}) \;=\; 4 \times 5 \; \text{ms} \;=\; 20 \; \text{ms} \;=\; 0.02 \; \text{s}$$ **Frequency:** $$f \;=\; \dfrac{1}{T} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{0.02} \;=\; 50 \; \text{Hz}$$ **Comparison with expected rating:** | Parameter | Expected | Measured | Match? | |---|---|---|---| | V_rms | 12 V (rated secondary) | 4.24 V | No — but this is normal! | | Frequency | 50 Hz | 50 Hz | ✓ Yes | **Explanation of the mismatch:** The "12 V" rating on the transformer is its **RMS voltage** (12 V RMS → peak = 16.97 V → peak-to-peak ≈ 33.9 V). If the CRO shows 12 V peak-to-peak, the actual RMS is only ~4.24 V. This indicates either (a) the transformer is being operated under no-load / lightly-loaded condition causing voltage rise, (b) the actual transformer is a **different rating** like 6 V or 9 V RMS, or (c) the Variac/auto-transformer upstream is set to a lower voltage. **Students must carefully distinguish between "rated RMS" and "measured peak/peak-to-peak"** — this is a common KTU exam pitfall. **(b) Plotting the Waveform to Scale on a Graph Sheet (7 marks):** **Tabulation of measurements:** | Measured Quantity | Value | |---|---| | Peak voltage (Vₘ) | 6 V | | Time period (T) | 20 ms | | Frequency (f) | 50 Hz | | Number of cycles to plot | 1 (or 1.5 for clarity) | | Vertical scale chosen | **1 cm = 2 V** (so Vₘ = 6 V → 3 cm peak) | | Horizontal scale chosen | **1 cm = 5 ms** (so T = 20 ms → 4 cm wide) | **Critical points of one sine cycle (for plotting):** | t (ms) | 0 | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | ωt | 0 | π/2 | π | 3π/2 | 2π | | v(t) = 6 sin(ωt) | 0 | +6 V | 0 | −6 V | 0 | **Steps to draw the graph:** 1. Draw the X-axis (time) and Y-axis (voltage) on the graph sheet. 2. Mark the time scale: 0, 5, 10, 15, 20 ms on the X-axis. 3. Mark the voltage scale: +6 V, 0, −6 V on the Y-axis. 4. Plot the five critical points (0, 0), (5 ms, +6 V), (10 ms, 0), (15 ms, −6 V), (20 ms, 0). 5. Join them with a smooth sinusoidal curve. 6. Label the axes, units, peak amplitude (Vₘ = 6 V), peak-to-peak (V_pp = 12 V), and time period (T = 20 ms). 7. Mention the CRO settings used (CH1, 2 V/div, 5 ms/div) and the transformer rating. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams — Read Carefully:** > > 1. **Confusing RMS and Peak values:** When a transformer is rated "12 V", it means **12 V RMS**, NOT peak. Students writing V_p = 12 V lose marks. Correctly: V_p = 12√2 ≈ 16.97 V. > 2. **Forgetting the 4.44 constant in EMF equation:** E = 4.44 f N Φₘ. Writing E = 2π f N Φₘ (which gives the *peak* value) without proper conversion to RMS will be marked wrong. > 3. **Mixing up N₁ and N₂:** Always check whether the question asks for primary or secondary turns before plugging in. > 4. **Forgetting to halve the peak-to-peak:** CRO displays peak-to-peak; peak is half of that; RMS is peak/√2. This three-step conversion is the most-skipped chain. > 5. **Not drawing a properly scaled graph:** In the workshop record, simply sketching a sine wave without **scale, axes labels, units, and critical points marked** will fetch **partial marks only**. The graph must be measurable with a ruler. > 6. **Ignoring the frequency:** Many students only measure amplitude and forget that the CRO also gives frequency from the time-base setting. > 7. **Plotting negative voltages on the wrong side:** The Y-axis must have **+Vₘ above and −Vₘ below the time axis**, not all above. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exam (Module Experiment 2):** - ☐ A **transformer** is a static AC-AC electromagnetic energy-transfer device based on **mutual induction**, working only with AC. - ☐ A **step-down transformer** has **N₂ < N₁** and **V₂ < V₁**; a **step-up** has **N₂ > N₁** and **V₂ > V₁**. - ☐ The **transformation ratio** is $k = N_2/N_1 = V_2/V_1 = I_1/I_2$. - ☐ The **EMF equation** is $E = 4.44 f N \Phi_m$ — the most important formula in this module. - ☐ **Standard Indian mains:** 230 V, 50 Hz, single-phase AC. - ☐ A **low-voltage transformer** (230 V → 6/9/12/15/24 V) is used to power workshop trainer kits, relays, lamps, and small AC loads safely. - ☐ A **CRO** displays voltage vs. time; it has a **vertical (Y) amplifier** and a **horizontal (X) time-base** that together trace the waveform. - ☐ **Voltage measurement on CRO:** $V_{p-p} = (\text{vert. div.}) \times (\text{V/div})$; then $V_p = V_{p-p}/2$; then $V_{rms} = V_p/\sqrt{2}$. - ☐ **Time/Frequency measurement on CRO:** $T = (\text{horiz. div./cycle}) \times (\text{time/div})$; $f = 1/T$. - ☐ **Graph sheet plotting:** Choose a convenient scale (e.g., 1 cm = 2 V, 1 cm = 5 ms); plot critical points at t = 0, T/4, T/2, 3T/4, T; join with a smooth sine curve; label axes, units, Vₘ, V_pp, and T. - ☐ **Identification in lab:** Read the **nameplate rating** of the transformer to identify step-up/step-down; the **wire gauge** of the windings (thicker wire = high-current/low-voltage side) is a quick physical indicator. - ☐ Always **convert CRO peak-to-peak** readings through the proper chain before comparing with transformer rated RMS values. ---

topics.title: Familiarization of rheostats, measurement of potential across resistance elements and introducing the concept of relative potential using a DC circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZESL208 - BASIC ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarization of rheostats, measurement of potential across resistance elements and introducing the concept of relative potential using a DC circuit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Rheostat — Definition

A **rheostat** is a two-terminal or three-terminal **variable resistor** whose ohmic resistance can be adjusted continuously (steplessly) over a defined range without opening the circuit. In the KTU 2024 Scheme workshop context, it is used to vary the current flowing through a DC load by inserting a controllable amount of resistance in series.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim style):** A rheostat is a continuously adjustable resistive element, used as a series current-control device in DC circuits, whose effective resistance is varied by moving a sliding contact (wiper) along a uniformly wound resistance track.

## 1.2 Construction (Standard Wire-Wound Type)

- A **cylindrical ceramic or vitreous enamel former** acts as the mechanical support.
- A **resistance wire** of constantan, manganin, or nichrome is uniformly wound over the former.
- A **sliding wiper** (carbon or phosphor-bronze brush) traverses the track.
- Three terminals are brought out:
  - **Terminal A** — one end of the resistance wire.
  - **Terminal B** — the other end of the resistance wire.
  - **Terminal C (slider)** — moves along the winding.
- Only **A–C** or **B–C** are used (rheostat connection); the unused end is shorted to C to make it a true two-terminal variable resistor.

## 1.3 Potential Difference — Definition

**Potential difference (V)** between two points A and B in a DC circuit is the **work done per unit charge** in moving a positive test charge from A to B.

$$V_{AB} \;=\; \frac{W_{AB}}{Q}$$

Its SI unit is the **volt (V)**, where **1 V = 1 J/C**. In a resistor carrying current I, the potential drops in the direction of current flow (from higher to lower potential).

## 1.4 Absolute vs Relative Potential — The Core Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> An **absolute potential** is measured with respect to a universal reference (theoretically, infinity). In all practical DC workshop measurements, we measure a **relative potential** — the potential difference between a test node and a chosen **reference node (datum)**. By convention, this datum is assigned **0 V** and is called the **ground** or **common** point.

| Concept | Reference Point | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Absolute potential | Infinity | $\phi$ (phi) |
| Relative potential | Chosen circuit node (0 V) | $V_x$ (with respect to ground) |
| Potential difference | Any two nodes | $V_{AB} = V_A - V_B$ |

## 1.5 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

- **Rheostat ≈ Water Tap (Faucet):** A fully open tap lets maximum water through (low resistance, high current). A closed tap blocks water (high/infinite resistance, zero current). Twisting the tap partially is exactly what a rheostat does electrically.
- **Voltage ≈ Height of Water:** Just as water flows from a higher tank to a lower one, current flows from a higher potential to a lower one. A voltmeter reads the *height difference* (potential difference) — not the absolute height.
- **Ground Reference ≈ Sea Level:** When we say a hill is "8848 m high," we mean 8848 m above **sea level**, not above the Earth's centre. Similarly, "12 V at a node" means 12 V above our chosen 0 V reference.

## 1.6 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard DC supply voltages in lab:** **1.5 V, 3 V, 6 V, 9 V, 12 V, 24 V**
- **Standard resistor power ratings (workshop):** **0.25 W, 0.5 W, 1 W, 2 W, 5 W**
- **Standard voltmeter internal resistance:** **≥ 20 kΩ/V** for analogue meters; **10 MΩ** for typical digital multimeters (DMM)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Rheostats Used in the KTU Workshop

| Type | Construction | Typical Rating | Workshop Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Linear (Slide) Rheostat** | Straight tubular former, slider moves linearly | 100 Ω – 5 kΩ, 1–2 A | Demonstrating smooth resistance change |
| **Rotary (Circular) Rheostat** | Resistance wire wound on a toroidal former; wiper rotates | 10 Ω – 50 kΩ, 0.5–4 A | Compact bench use, potentiometric use |
| **Wire-Wound Tubular** | Heavy-gauge constantan wire on ceramic tube | 1 Ω – 500 Ω, up to 10 A | High-current series control |
| **Carbon-Film Potentiometer (rheostat-connected)** | Carbon track on phenolic strip | 100 Ω – 1 MΩ, ≤ 0.5 A | Low-current signal-level control |

## 2.2 How the Rheostat Controls Current — The Logic

- The rheostat is always connected **in series** with the load and the source.
- Increasing the active length of resistance wire **increases R**, which (by Ohm's Law) **decreases I** for a fixed source voltage V.

$$I \;=\; \frac{V_S}{R_{\text{rheostat}} + R_{\text{load}}}$$

- Decreasing the active length **decreases R**, which **increases I**.
- The **wiper position is mechanically calibrated**; the resistance between the slider and one end is **directly proportional to the length of the track engaged**.

$$R \;=\; \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$

where $\rho$ = resistivity of the wire material, $L$ = active length, $A$ = cross-sectional area.

## 2.3 Why "Relative" Potential — Engineering Reasoning

In a multi-resistor DC network, every node has a different voltage. We cannot speak of an "absolute" voltage on the bench. We must:
1. Pick a node and call it **0 V (ground)**.
2. Measure every other node's voltage **with respect to that ground**.
3. The **voltage drop across a resistor** is the *difference* of the two relative potentials at its terminals.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Board examiners often phrase the concept as: *"The potential of a point is not an intrinsic property; it has meaning only when measured against a chosen reference."*

## 2.4 Rules for Connecting Meters in a DC Circuit

| Meter | Connection | Ideal Behaviour | Polarity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Voltmeter** | **Parallel** across the element | Infinite internal resistance (no current drawn) | **+** terminal → higher potential, **−** → lower potential |
| **Ammeter** | **Series** with the element | Zero internal resistance (no voltage drop) | Current enters **+** terminal |
| **Wattmeter** | Current coil in series, voltage coil in parallel | — | — |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **high-yield formula bank** for this experiment. Memorise the units, boundary conditions, and the algebraic form for V/3-style short derivations.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Boundary / Validity Condition | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V \;=\; I \cdot R$ | Ohm's Law | Linear, ohmic conductors, isothermal | V |
| 2 | $V_{AB} \;=\; V_A - V_B$ | Relative potential | Reference (ground) is the datum | V |
| 3 | $P \;=\; V \cdot I \;=\; I^{2} R \;=\; V^{2}/R$ | Power dissipated in R | All three forms equivalent for DC | W |
| 4 | $R_{\text{series}} \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{n} R_k$ | Equivalent series resistance | Current is common | Ω |
| 5 | $\dfrac{1}{R_{\text{parallel}}} \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{R_k}$ | Equivalent parallel resistance | Voltage is common | Ω |
| 6 | $V_k \;=\; V_S \cdot \dfrac{R_k}{\sum R_k}$ | **Voltage Divider Rule (VDR)** | Series network, no load current drawn | V |
| 7 | $I_k \;=\; I_S \cdot \dfrac{G_k}{\sum G_k}$ | **Current Divider Rule (CDR)** | Parallel network, $G = 1/R$ | A |
| 8 | $I \;=\; \dfrac{V_S}{R_{\text{rheostat}} + R_{\text{load}}}$ | Rheostat current control | Series loop | A |
| 9 | $R_{\text{rheostat}} \;=\; R_{\text{total}} \cdot \dfrac{L_{\text{active}}}{L_{\text{full}}}$ | Rheostat linear scaling | Uniformly wound track | Ω |
| 10 | $V_{\text{drop, }R_k} \;=\; I \cdot R_k$ | Voltage drop across $R_k$ | Direction of current drop | V |
| 11 | $\sum V_{\text{rise}} \;=\; \sum V_{\text{drop}}$ | **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** | Any closed loop | V |
| 12 | $\sum I_{\text{in}} \;=\; \sum I_{\text{out}}$ | **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL)** | Any node | A |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The Voltage Divider Rule (row 6) is valid **only when the output terminals are unloaded** (i.e., no current is drawn by a subsequent stage). If a load $R_L$ is connected across $R_k$, the rule fails; you must use the parallel combination $(R_k \,\Vert\, R_L)$ in the divider.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **Rheostats** are the precursors of modern power electronics — they survive in motor starters, vintage dimmer switches, electric furnace controls, train dynamic braking resistors, and laboratory DC current-limiting supplies.
- **Relative potential measurement** is the foundational idea behind every electronic circuit, from a flashlight (1 cell = 1.5 V above its negative terminal) to a smartphone motherboard (every IC pin is a relative potential w.r.t. board ground).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Equivalent Resistance of Resistors in Series

**Statement:** The equivalent resistance of $n$ resistors connected in series equals the algebraic sum of the individual resistances.

**Given:** A series chain $R_1, R_2, R_3, \ldots, R_n$ carrying the same current $I$ from a source $V_S$.

**Step 1 — Write KVL around the loop:**

The applied voltage equals the sum of the voltage drops across each element (each drop is in the direction of current flow).

$$V_S \;=\; V_1 + V_2 + V_3 + \ldots + V_n$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Ohm's Law for each drop:**

$$V_S \;=\; I \cdot R_1 + I \cdot R_2 + I \cdot R_3 + \ldots + I \cdot R_n$$

**Step 3 — Factor out the common current I (since the same I flows through all):**

$$V_S \;=\; I \cdot (R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + \ldots + R_n)$$

**Step 4 — Define the equivalent resistance $R_{\text{eq}}$ such that $V_S = I \cdot R_{\text{eq}}$:**

$$I \cdot R_{\text{eq}} \;=\; I \cdot \sum_{k=1}^{n} R_k$$

**Step 5 — Cancel I from both sides:**

$$\boxed{R_{\text{eq}} \;=\; R_1 + R_2 + R_3 + \ldots + R_n \;=\; \sum_{k=1}^{n} R_k}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** The same derivation directly yields the **Voltage Divider Rule** if we divide Step 3 by Step 5:
> $\dfrac{V_k}{V_S} = \dfrac{R_k}{R_{\text{eq}}}$, hence $V_k = V_S \cdot \dfrac{R_k}{R_{\text{eq}}}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Voltage Divider Rule (VDR)

**Statement:** In a series DC circuit fed by a source $V_S$, the voltage across any resistor $R_k$ is the source voltage multiplied by the ratio of $R_k$ to the total series resistance.

**Given:** Source $V_S$ feeding a series chain $R_1, R_2$ (two-resistor case is generalisable to $n$).

**Step 1 — Apply Ohm's law to the full series loop to obtain the loop current:**

$$I \;=\; \frac{V_S}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 2 — The voltage across $R_1$ is, by Ohm's Law:**

$$V_{R_1} \;=\; I \cdot R_1$$

**Step 3 — Substitute I from Step 1:**

$$V_{R_1} \;=\; \left(\frac{V_S}{R_1 + R_2}\right) \cdot R_1$$

**Step 4 — Simplify:**

$$\boxed{V_{R_1} \;=\; V_S \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}}$$

Similarly:

$$\boxed{V_{R_2} \;=\; V_S \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}}$$

> **Sanity Check:** Adding the two outputs must recover the source.
> $V_{R_1} + V_{R_2} = V_S \cdot \dfrac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + R_2} = V_S$. ✔ Verified.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Relative Potential of an Intermediate Node

**Setup:** A 24 V DC source feeds a series chain $R_1 = 100\ \Omega$ and $R_2 = 200\ \Omega$ from node A (positive terminal) → $R_1$ → node B → $R_2$ → node C (negative terminal, taken as ground, 0 V).

**Step 1 — Compute the loop current:**

$$I \;=\; \frac{V_S}{R_1 + R_2} \;=\; \frac{24}{100 + 200} \;=\; \frac{24}{300} \;=\; 0.08\ \text{A} \;=\; 80\ \text{mA}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the potential at node A (taken relative to C as ground):**

$$V_A \;=\; V_C + I \cdot (R_1 + R_2) \;=\; 0 + 0.08 \cdot 300 \;=\; 24\ \text{V}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the potential at node B (between $R_1$ and $R_2$):**

$$V_B \;=\; V_C + I \cdot R_2 \;=\; 0 + 0.08 \cdot 200 \;=\; 16\ \text{V}$$

**Step 4 — Verification via the drop across $R_1$ alone:**

$$V_{R_1} \;=\; V_A - V_B \;=\; 24 - 16 \;=\; 8\ \text{V} \;=\; I \cdot R_1 \;=\; 0.08 \cdot 100 \;=\; 8\ \text{V} \;\;\checkmark$$

$$\boxed{V_A = +24\ \text{V},\quad V_B = +16\ \text{V},\quad V_C = 0\ \text{V (ground)}}$$

> **Interpretation:** If we *redefined* the ground as node A, the relative potentials would all shift by −24 V: $V_A = 0\ \text{V},\ V_B = -8\ \text{V},\ V_C = -24\ \text{V}$. The **differences** (voltage drops) remain invariant. This is the entire physics of "relative" potential.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Rheostat Position

A 12 V battery, a rheostat $R_{\text{rh}}$ (max 100 Ω) and a fixed load $R_L = 100\ \Omega$ are in series. The following table captures the circuit behaviour for representative wiper positions.

| Wiper position | $R_{\text{rh}}$ (Ω) | $R_{\text{total}}$ (Ω) | $I = V_S / R_{\text{total}}$ (A) | $V_{R_L}$ (V) | $V_{R_{\text{rh}}}$ (V) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Fully inserted | 100 | 200 | 0.060 | 6.0 | 6.0 |
| 75 % | 75 | 175 | 0.0686 | 6.86 | 5.14 |
| 50 % | 50 | 150 | 0.080 | 8.0 | 4.0 |
| 25 % | 25 | 125 | 0.096 | 9.6 | 2.4 |
| Zero (slider at one end) | 0 | 100 | 0.120 | 12.0 | 0.0 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-relevant observation:** As the rheostat resistance *decreases*, the load current *increases* and the load voltage rises toward the full source voltage. Conversely, the rheostat absorbs the *complement* of the load voltage. **The two voltages always sum to 12 V** (KVL).

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A 15 V DC source feeds a series network of $R_1 = 30\ \Omega$, a rheostat $R_{\text{rh}}$, and $R_2 = 60\ \Omega$. A voltmeter connected across $R_2$ reads **6 V**. Find:
(a) the loop current,
(b) the rheostat resistance at this setting,
(c) the relative potential of the node between $R_1$ and $R_{\text{rh}}$ if the negative terminal of the source is grounded.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Loop current from voltmeter reading on $R_2$:**

$$I \;=\; \frac{V_{R_2}}{R_2} \;=\; \frac{6\ \text{V}}{60\ \Omega} \;=\; 0.10\ \text{A} \;=\; 100\ \text{mA}$$

**Part (b) — Apply KVL around the loop to find $R_{\text{rh}}$:**

$$V_S \;=\; I \cdot R_1 + I \cdot R_{\text{rh}} + I \cdot R_2$$

$$15 \;=\; 0.10 \cdot 30 + 0.10 \cdot R_{\text{rh}} + 0.10 \cdot 60$$

$$15 \;=\; 3 + 0.10 \cdot R_{\text{rh}} + 6$$

$$0.10 \cdot R_{\text{rh}} \;=\; 15 - 9 \;=\; 6$$

$$\boxed{R_{\text{rh}} \;=\; 60\ \Omega}$$

**Part (c) — Relative potential of the intermediate node (call it node X) w.r.t. the grounded negative terminal:**

Ground the negative terminal: $V_{\text{neg}} = 0\ \text{V}$. Traversing from ground towards the positive terminal through $R_2$, then through $R_{\text{rh}}$:

$$V_{\text{between }R_2 \text{ and } R_{\text{rh}}} \;=\; 0 + I \cdot R_2 \;=\; 0.10 \cdot 60 \;=\; 6\ \text{V}$$

$$V_X \;=\; 6 + 0.10 \cdot 60 \;=\; 6 + 6 \;=\; 12\ \text{V}$$

$$\boxed{V_X \;=\; +12\ \text{V (relative to the negative terminal)}}$$

**Verification (KVL):** From positive terminal (+15 V) to negative terminal (0 V):
$V_{\text{drop across }R_1} = 0.10 \cdot 30 = 3\ \text{V}$ → potential after $R_1$ = 15 − 3 = **12 V** ✔ matches $V_X$.

---

## 3.6 Step-by-Step Experimental Procedure (Worksheet-Ready)

1. Identify the **type, rated resistance, and rated current** printed on the rheostat body.
2. Connect the **A–C terminals** (slider + one end) of the rheostat in **series** with a fixed resistor $R_L$, a DC ammeter, a toggle switch, and a DC source.
3. Set the rheostat slider to its **maximum resistance** (fully inserted) before closing the switch — this protects the ammeter from inrush.
4. Connect a **DC voltmeter in parallel** across the fixed resistor $R_L$. Note the polarity (+ to the higher-potential side).
5. Connect a **second voltmeter across the rheostat terminals** to read $V_{R_{\text{rh}}}$.
6. Close the switch, record $(I,\ V_{R_L},\ V_{R_{\text{rh}}})$ at **5 slider positions**.
7. Verify KVL at each position: $V_S = V_{R_L} + V_{R_{\text{rh}}}$.
8. Repeat the measurement across the rheostat terminals, with the *negative* terminal of the source designated as 0 V ground, and tabulate the relative potential of each node.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Lab Error:** Inserting the ammeter in parallel with the load (instead of in series). This **short-circuits the source through a near-zero resistance path** and will blow the fuse or burn the ammeter coil. Always remember: **Ammeter = Series**, **Voltmeter = Parallel**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Internal Construction of a Linear Wire-Wound Rheostat

```mermaid
graph LR
    ceramicFormer[ceramic former]
    winding[resistance wire winding]
    wiperSlider[sliding wiper brush]
    termA[terminal A left end]
    termB[terminal B right end]
    termC[terminal C slider shaft]

    ceramicFormer --- winding
    wiperSlider --- winding
    termA --- ceramicFormer
    termB --- ceramicFormer
    termC --- wiperSlider

    classDef mechanical fill:#E8F0FF,stroke:#1F3A8A,color:#000000
    classDef electrical fill:#FFF4E5,stroke:#B45309,color:#000000
    classDef terminal fill:#E6F4EA,stroke:#1E7B3A,color:#000000

    class ceramicFormer,wiperSlider mechanical
    class winding electrical
    class termA,termB,termC terminal
```

## 4.2 Schematic of the Workshop Test Circuit

```mermaid
graph LR
    dcSource[DC Source V sub S]
    switchS[SPST Switch S]
    ammeterA[Ammeter A]
    rheostatR[Rheostat R sub rh slider between A and C]
    resistorR1[Fixed Resistor R sub L]
    voltmeterV1[Voltmeter V1 across R sub L]
    voltmeterV2[Voltmeter V2 across R sub rh]
    groundNode[Ground reference 0 V at negative terminal]

    dcSource --- switchS
    switchS --- ammeterA
    ammeterA --- rheostatR
    rheostatR --- resistorR1
    resistorR1 --- groundNode
    groundNode --- dcSource

    voltmeterV1 -. parallel .- resistorR1
    voltmeterV2 -. parallel .- rheostatR

    classDef source fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#B91C1C,color:#000000
    classDef meter fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,color:#000000
    classDef passElement fill:#FEF9C3,stroke:#A16207,color:#000000
    classDef refNode fill:#E7E5E4,stroke:#44403C,color:#000000

    class dcSource source
    class ammeterA,voltmeterV1,voltmeterV2 meter
    class rheostatR,resistorR1,switchS passElement
    class groundNode refNode
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Flow of the Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA[Step 1 Identify rheostat rating and select two end terminals]
    stepB[Step 2 Wire the series loop source switch ammeter rheostat load]
    stepC[Step 3 Connect voltmeters in parallel across load and rheostat]
    stepD[Step 4 Designate the negative terminal as 0 V ground reference]
    stepE[Step 5 Move slider in 5 steps and record I V sub load V sub rh]
    stepF[Step 6 Verify Kirchhoff Voltage Law at each slider position]
    stepG[Step 7 Tabulate relative potential of each node against ground]
    stepH[Step 8 Plot V vs R and discuss the linear VDR relationship]

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH

    classDef procStep fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1D4ED8,color:#000000
    classDef verifyStep fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#166534,color:#000000
    classDef outputStep fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#9D174D,color:#000000

    class stepA,stepB,stepC,stepD,stepE procStep
    class stepF,stepG verifyStep
    class stepH outputStep
```

## 4.4 Node-Potential Map of the Two-Resistor Series Chain

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Node A plus terminal of source]
    nodeB[Node B between R1 and R2]
    nodeC[Node C minus terminal of source set to 0 V]
    dropR1[Voltage drop 24 minus 16 equals 8 V across R1]
    dropR2[Voltage drop 16 minus 0 equals 16 V across R2]

    nodeA ---|24 V above ground| nodeC
    nodeA ---|8 V drop R1| nodeB
    nodeB ---|16 V drop R2| nodeC
    dropR1 --- nodeA
    dropR2 --- nodeB

    classDef highNode fill:#FEE2E2,stroke:#B91C1C,color:#000000
    classDef midNode fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,color:#000000
    classDef groundNode fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#166534,color:#000000

    class nodeA highNode
    class nodeB midNode
    class nodeC groundNode
    class dropR1,dropR2 highNode
```

> **How to read the map:** The left-most branch (Node A to Node C, 24 V) is the source. The two right-most branches (8 V and 16 V) are the **drops**. KVL guarantees $8\ \text{V} + 16\ \text{V} = 24\ \text{V}$. The relative potentials are $V_A = +24\ \text{V}$, $V_B = +16\ \text{V}$, $V_C = 0\ \text{V}$.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define a rheostat. With a neat sketch, explain its working principle and state one practical application.**

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**
A rheostat is a two-terminal variable resistor used in DC circuits to vary the magnitude of current flowing through a series-connected load by changing its effective resistance continuously.
**Construction:** A resistance wire (constantan/manganin) is uniformly wound on a ceramic or vitreous-enamel former. A sliding wiper makes contact with the wire and is brought out as the slider terminal. The two end terminals of the winding and the slider terminal are accessible.
**Working principle:** As the slider is moved, the *effective length* of resistance wire between the active terminals changes. Since $R = \rho L / A$, a greater engaged length means a larger resistance and hence a smaller load current (Ohm's law). A shorter length means a smaller resistance and a larger current.
**Application:** Used as a series current-limiter in DC motor starters, in dimmerstat circuits for lamp brightness control, and in laboratory DC power supplies as the coarse current adjustment.

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner's tip: a labelled diagram (worth 1 mark in 3-mark answers) must show the winding, slider, and the *two terminals used* (e.g., A and C). Mentioning the unused end is shorted to the slider earns the full 3 marks.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is meant by the relative potential of a point in a DC circuit? Why is it preferred over absolute potential in practical measurements?**

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks):**
The relative potential of a point P in a DC circuit is the potential difference measured between P and a chosen **reference node** (called the *ground* or *datum*), to which a value of **0 V** is arbitrarily assigned. Symbolically, $V_P = V_P - V_{\text{ground}}$.
**Why preferred:** Absolute potential is defined with respect to a point at infinity, which is physically unreachable and cannot host a voltmeter lead. A chosen ground node, by contrast, is a real circuit terminal (typically the negative terminal of the source, the chassis, or the earth pin) to which a voltmeter reference lead can be physically clipped. All node voltages in the circuit can then be expressed in a single, consistent frame of reference. Importantly, **differences** between node potentials (which are the physically meaningful quantities driving current) are invariant under any shift of the ground.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a circuit diagram, describe an experiment to measure the potential difference across two resistors connected in series across a DC source using a rheostat and a voltmeter. Explain the role of the rheostat in the circuit.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 24 V DC source is connected in series with $R_1 = 40\ \Omega$, a rheostat, and $R_2 = 80\ \Omega$. When the rheostat is set to 30 Ω, calculate:
(i) the loop current,
(ii) the potential difference across $R_2$,
(iii) the relative potential of the node between $R_1$ and the rheostat, taking the negative terminal of the source as the 0 V reference.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Description (7 marks):**

**Apparatus:** 24 V DC regulated supply, SPST switch, DC ammeter (0–1 A), linear wire-wound rheostat (0–200 Ω, 1 A), fixed carbon resistor $R_1 = 470\ \Omega$, fixed carbon resistor $R_2 = 1\ \text{k}\Omega$, two DC voltmeters (0–30 V) or a DMM, connecting wires.

**Circuit description (mark split: diagram 2 marks, procedure 3 marks, role of rheostat 2 marks):**

1. The rheostat is connected in **series** with the source, the ammeter, the switch, and the two fixed resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$ in the order **Source + → Switch → Ammeter → $R_1$ → $R_2$ → Source −**.
2. **Voltmeter V1** is connected in parallel across $R_1$, and **Voltmeter V2** is connected in parallel across $R_2$. Both voltmeters are connected with their **positive (red) lead on the higher-potential side** of the element.
3. Before closing the switch, the rheostat slider is set to its **maximum-resistance** position to limit the inrush current.
4. The switch is closed. The loop current $I$ and the two voltmeter readings $V_1$ and $V_2$ are noted. The rheostat is adjusted in steps, and readings are repeated.
5. **Verification of KVL:** At every step, the source voltage must equal the algebraic sum of all voltmeter readings: $V_S = V_1 + V_2 + V_{R_{\text{rh}}}$.

**Role of the rheostat:** It acts as a **series variable current-limiting resistor**. By increasing its resistance, it drops more of the source voltage, leaving less across the fixed resistors, and hence reduces the current through them. It also serves as a **voltage-divider element** — the fraction of the source voltage dropped across $R_1$ and $R_2$ can be continuously varied by moving the slider.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks, 1+2+2+2 split):**

Given: $V_S = 24\ \text{V}$, $R_1 = 40\ \Omega$, $R_{\text{rh}} = 30\ \Omega$, $R_2 = 80\ \Omega$.

**(i) Loop current (3 marks):**

$$R_{\text{total}} \;=\; R_1 + R_{\text{rh}} + R_2 \;=\; 40 + 30 + 80 \;=\; 150\ \Omega$$

$$\boxed{I \;=\; \frac{V_S}{R_{\text{total}}} \;=\; \frac{24}{150} \;=\; 0.16\ \text{A} \;=\; 160\ \text{mA}}$$

**(ii) P.D. across $R_2$ (2 marks):**

$$\boxed{V_{R_2} \;=\; I \cdot R_2 \;=\; 0.16 \cdot 80 \;=\; 12.8\ \text{V}}$$

**(iii) Relative potential of the node X between $R_1$ and $R_{\text{rh}}$ (2 marks):**

Set the negative terminal of the source to 0 V (ground). Traversing from ground to node X through $R_2$ and then $R_{\text{rh}}$:

$$V_X \;=\; 0 + I \cdot R_2 + I \cdot R_{\text{rh}} \;=\; 0.16 \cdot 80 + 0.16 \cdot 30$$

$$V_X \;=\; 12.8 + 4.8 \;=\; 17.6\ \text{V}$$

$$\boxed{V_X \;=\; +17.6\ \text{V (relative to the negative terminal)}}$$

**Verification (KVL):** Potential at the positive terminal = $V_X + I \cdot R_1 = 17.6 + 0.16 \cdot 40 = 17.6 + 6.4 = 24\ \text{V}$ ✔.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the voltage divider rule (VDR) for two resistors $R_1$ and $R_2$ connected in series across a DC source $V_S$. State the two essential conditions under which the rule is valid.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A DC voltmeter of internal resistance $R_V = 20\ \text{k}\Omega$ is used to measure the voltage across $R_2 = 5\ \text{k}\Omega$ in a series circuit where $R_1 = 5\ \text{k}\Omega$ and the source is $V_S = 20\ \text{V}$.
(i) Calculate the *true* voltage across $R_2$ (without the meter).
(ii) Calculate the *measured* voltage when the voltmeter is connected.
(iii) Comment on the loading effect and the practical remedy.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks; steps 2+2+1+1+1):**

Consider a series circuit of $R_1$ and $R_2$ connected across a source $V_S$ as shown in the standard two-resistor series schematic (drawn by the student).

**Step 1 — Ohm's law for the full series loop (2 marks):**

The same current $I$ flows through both resistors. By Ohm's law applied to the equivalent resistance $R_{\text{eq}} = R_1 + R_2$:

$$I \;=\; \frac{V_S}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 2 — Voltage across $R_1$ and $R_2$ by Ohm's law on each element (2 marks):**

$$V_{R_1} \;=\; I \cdot R_1 \;=\; \frac{V_S \cdot R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$

$$V_{R_2} \;=\; I \cdot R_2 \;=\; \frac{V_S \cdot R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 3 — Box the result (1 mark):**

$$\boxed{V_{R_2} \;=\; V_S \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}}$$

**Step 4 — Two essential validity conditions (2 marks):**
1. The network must be a **purely series** connection of resistors (no parallel branch stealing current).
2. The **output terminals across $R_2$ must be unloaded** — i.e., the device used to measure $V_{R_2}$ must draw *negligible* current. In practice, the voltmeter's internal resistance $R_V$ must satisfy $R_V \gg R_2$.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks, 2+2+1.5+1.5 split):**

**(i) True voltage across $R_2$ (2 marks):**

Using VDR with no meter connected:

$$V_{R_2, \text{true}} \;=\; V_S \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \;=\; 20 \cdot \frac{5}{5 + 5} \;=\; 20 \cdot 0.5 \;=\; \boxed{10\ \text{V}}$$

**(ii) Measured voltage when voltmeter is connected (2 marks):**

The voltmeter is in parallel with $R_2$, so the effective resistance at the $R_2$ terminals becomes:

$$R_2' \;=\; R_2 \;\Vert\; R_V \;=\; \frac{R_2 \cdot R_V}{R_2 + R_V} \;=\; \frac{5 \cdot 20}{5 + 20} \;=\; \frac{100}{25} \;=\; 4\ \text{k}\Omega$$

The total series resistance is now $R_1 + R_2' = 5 + 4 = 9\ \text{k}\Omega$. The new loop current is $I' = 20\ \text{V} / 9\ \text{k}\Omega = 2.222\ \text{mA}$. The voltage across the parallel combination (which is what the voltmeter reads) is:

$$V_{\text{measured}} \;=\; I' \cdot R_2' \;=\; 2.222\ \text{mA} \cdot 4\ \text{k}\Omega \;=\; \boxed{8.89\ \text{V}}$$

**(iii) Loading effect and remedy (3 marks):**

The voltmeter reads **8.89 V** instead of the true **10 V**, an error of **11.1 %**. This is the **loading effect** — a non-ideal voltmeter with finite internal resistance draws a small but non-negligible current, alters the circuit's equivalent resistance, and hence changes the very quantity it is trying to measure.

**Practical remedy:** Use a voltmeter with as large an internal resistance as possible — preferably a **digital multimeter (input impedance 10 MΩ)** or an **electrometer (input impedance > 10¹² Ω)**. The rule of thumb is $R_V \ge 100 \cdot R_2$ to keep the loading error below 1 %.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> 1. **Reverse polarity of voltmeter:** Many students draw the voltmeter "+" lead on the lower-potential side, obtaining a *negative* reading. The examiner deducts **1 mark** for polarity convention. Always align the **red (+)** lead of the voltmeter with the **higher-potential** terminal of the element.
> 2. **Ammeter in parallel:** If the ammeter is mistakenly connected across the load, it acts as a near-short and the ammeter fuse blows. Examiners check the **circuit diagram for meter placement** before reading the numerical answer — wrong placement costs 2 marks even if the final number is correct.
> 3. **Forgetting the rheostat is in series, not parallel:** A frequent conceptual slip. The rheostat must carry the **full load current**; placing it in parallel does nothing useful and is unsafe. Examiners explicitly test this with the wording "*explain the role of the rheostat*".
> 4. **Mixing up VDR and CDR:** In a *series* circuit use the **Voltage Divider Rule** (ratio of resistances); in a *parallel* circuit use the **Current Divider Rule** (ratio of conductances). The two are routinely interchanged by careless students — losing 1–2 marks per occurrence.
> 5. **Not stating the reference for "relative potential":** Every relative potential must be quoted **with respect to a stated node**. "The potential is 17.6 V" is incomplete; "17.6 V with respect to the negative terminal" earns the full mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Rheostat** = continuously adjustable 2-terminal variable resistor; used **in series** to control current; resistance $R = \rho L / A$.
- A rheostat has three terminals (A, B, C). Only **two are used**; the third is shorted to the slider.
- **Voltmeter** is always connected in **parallel** (ideal $R_V = \infty$). **Ammeter** is always connected in **series** (ideal $R_A = 0$).
- **Absolute potential** is relative to infinity (unmeasurable). **Relative potential** is relative to a chosen **0 V ground node** in the circuit.
- The **voltage drop** across a resistor is always in the **direction of current flow** (from the + side to the − side).
- **Ohm's Law:** $V = IR$, valid for linear, isothermal, ohmic conductors only.
- **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL):** $\sum V_{\text{rise}} = \sum V_{\text{drop}}$ around any closed loop.
- **Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL):** $\sum I_{\text{in}} = \sum I_{\text{out}}$ at any node.
- **Voltage Divider Rule:** $V_k = V_S \cdot \dfrac{R_k}{\sum R_k}$ — valid **only in a series, unloaded** network.
- **Series equivalent:** $R_{\text{eq}} = \sum R_k$. **Parallel equivalent:** $\dfrac{1}{R_{\text{eq}}} = \sum \dfrac{1}{R_k}$.
- **Loading effect:** A non-ideal voltmeter with finite $R_V$ draws current and disturbs the circuit; use a DMM (10 MΩ) or higher-impedance meter to minimise it.
- **Power dissipation:** $P = VI = I^2 R = V^2/R$. Choose a resistor with a power rating at least **2× the computed dissipation** for reliability.
- **Rheostat linear scaling:** $R_{\text{rh,active}} = R_{\text{max}} \cdot \dfrac{L_{\text{active}}}{L_{\text{full}}}$.
- **Always start the experiment with the rheostat at its maximum resistance** to limit inrush current and protect the ammeter.
- **Always quote a relative potential with respect to a stated reference** — "with respect to the negative terminal" or "w.r.t. ground".
- **KTU-typical mistakes to avoid:** wrong meter polarity, wrong meter placement, mixing VDR/CDR, dropping the reference in a relative-potential answer, and forgetting to verify KVL experimentally.

> **Final KTU Board Mantra for this experiment:**
> *Rheostat in series, voltmeter in parallel, ground at the negative, KVL sums to zero, relative potential always named.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Identify battery specifications using different types of batteries (Lead acid, Li Ion, NiCd etc.)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZESL208 - BASIC ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Identify battery specifications using different types of batteries (Lead acid, Li Ion, NiCd etc.)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Battery? (Formal KTU Definition)

A **battery** is a collection of one or more electrochemical cells whose stored chemical energy is converted into electrical energy through redox (reduction-oxidation) reactions. Each cell consists of a **positive electrode (cathode)**, a **negative electrode (anode)**, and an **electrolyte** that enables ionic conduction between them.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module Experiment 2**
> The objective of this experiment is to *physically identify*, *read*, and *interpret* the manufacturer-printed specifications on the nameplate/casing of commonly used batteries: **Lead-Acid (Pb-Acid)**, **Lithium-Ion (Li-Ion)**, **Nickel-Cadmium (NiCd)**, and **Nickel-Metal Hydride (NiMH)**. The student must correlate the printed data with the electrochemical family of the cell.

## 1.2 Battery Specification — Definition

A **battery specification** is a set of standardized technical parameters printed or engraved on the battery by the manufacturer. These parameters completely describe the electrical, mechanical, thermal, and safety characteristics of the cell, enabling the user to select the correct battery for a given load.

The five *primary* specification parameters that every student must be able to identify are:

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Units |
|---|-----------|--------|----------------|
| 1 | Nominal Voltage | $V_{nom}$ | Volts (V) |
| 2 | Capacity | $C$ | Ampere-hours (Ah) |
| 3 | Energy | $E$ | Watt-hours (Wh) or kilojoule (kJ) |
| 4 | C-Rate | $C_{rate}$ | Dimensionless (e.g., 1C, 2C, 0.2C) |
| 5 | Cycle Life | $N_{cyc}$ | Number of charge-discharge cycles |

Secondary parameters include: internal resistance, operating temperature range, state of charge (SOC), depth of discharge (DOD), self-discharge rate, and form factor.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tank" Model

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Battery as a Water Reservoir**
> Imagine a water tank fitted with a tap at the bottom.
> - **Voltage ($V$)** is analogous to the **water pressure (head)** at the tap — it is the "push" that drives current through a load.
> - **Capacity ($Ah$)** is analogous to the **total volume of the tank** — the total charge the battery can deliver.
> - **Energy ($Wh$)** is analogous to the **total work the water can do** (pressure × volume) — the true usable energy.
> - **C-Rate** is analogous to the **diameter of the tap opening** — a wider tap drains the tank faster.
> - **Internal Resistance** is analogous to **friction inside the pipe** — it dissipates energy as heat and limits peak current.
>
> Just as a plumber must read the tank's volume and pressure rating before connecting it to a pipeline, an engineer must read a battery's $V_{nom}$, $Ah$, and $C$-rate before connecting it to a circuit.

## 1.4 Standard Identification Markings

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **What You Will See on a Real Battery**
> 1. **Chemistry code** — printed in plain text, e.g., *Sealed Lead Acid*, *Li-ion*, *NiCd*.
> 2. **Nominal voltage** — e.g., *12 V*, *3.7 V*, *1.2 V*.
> 3. **Rated capacity** — e.g., *7 Ah*, *2600 mAh*, *1500 mAh*.
> 4. **C-rate or max discharge current** — e.g., *20 HR* (means rated at a 20-hour discharge), or *1C*, *2C*, *5C*.
> 5. **Manufacturing date / batch code** — for warranty tracking.
> 6. **Polarity markings** — `+` and `−` symbols, often with red/black color coding.
> 7. **Safety certifications** — *IS*, *IEC*, *BIS*, *UL*, *CE* marks.

A **standard test condition** universally accepted by manufacturers is discharge at **$25\,^\circ\text{C}$** until the cell reaches its **end-of-discharge voltage** (e.g., 1.75 V/cell for lead-acid, 2.75 V/cell for Li-ion).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How to Read Battery Specifications

The identification procedure, as a structured KTU lab step, follows this logic chain:

- **Step 1 — Visual Inspection of Form Factor**
  Identify the physical construction: prismatic, cylindrical (e.g., 18650, 21700), coin-cell, or vented/flooded container. Form factor is the first hint to chemistry.

- **Step 2 — Read the Chemistry Label**
  Look for explicit text such as *"VRLA"*, *"Li-ion"*, *"NiCd"*, or *"NiMH"*. If absent, infer from nominal voltage per cell: ~2 V → lead-acid; ~3.6–3.7 V → Li-ion; ~1.2 V → NiCd/NiMH.

- **Step 3 — Read Nominal Voltage ($V_{nom}$)**
  This is the *average* operating voltage under load, not the open-circuit voltage. The actual voltage during discharge varies between the *end-of-charge* and *end-of-discharge* values.

- **Step 4 — Read Rated Capacity ($C_{rated}$)**
  The capacity is *always* quoted at a *specific discharge rate*. For example, a battery labelled *"12 V, 7 Ah @ 20HR"* delivers $7\,\text{Ah}$ only if discharged over **20 hours** (i.e., at $0.35\,\text{A}$). If discharged faster, the deliverable capacity drops — this is the **Peukert Effect**.

- **Step 5 — Identify C-Rating and Maximum Discharge**
  The C-rate tells you the safe continuous discharge current:
  $$I_{max} = C_{rate} \times C_{Ah}$$

- **Step 6 — Cross-Check Energy Rating**
