  Compute the stored energy from the printed $V_{nom}$ and $C_{Ah}$ and verify consistency with any printed Wh value.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference Table)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table compiles **every** equation you will need for this experiment. Memorize these before the lab viva and university exam.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks / Boundary Condition |
|---|----------|---------|-------|------------------------------|
| 1 | Stored Charge (Capacity) | $Q = I \times t$ | $\text{Ah}$ | Constant-current discharge assumption |
| 2 | Stored Energy | $E = V_{nom} \times C_{Ah}$ | $\text{Wh}$ | Nominal energy of the pack |
| 3 | Energy in Joules | $E_{J} = E_{Wh} \times 3600$ | $\text{J}$ | $1\,\text{Wh} = 3600\,\text{J}$ |
| 4 | C-Rate Definition | $C_{rate} = \dfrac{I_{discharge}}{C_{Ah}}$ | dimensionless | 1C = full discharge in 1 hour |
| 5 | Discharge Current from C-Rate | $I_{dis} = C_{rate} \times C_{Ah}$ | $\text{A}$ | Inverse of discharge time |
| 6 | Discharge Time | $t_{dis} = \dfrac{1}{C_{rate}}$ | $\text{hours}$ | Only valid at the rated C |
| 7 | Specific Energy | $E_{sp} = \dfrac{E_{Wh}}{m_{kg}}$ | $\text{Wh/kg}$ | Mass-based energy density |
| 8 | Energy Density | $E_{vol} = \dfrac{E_{Wh}}{V_{L}}$ | $\text{Wh/L}$ | Volume-based energy density |
| 9 | Specific Power | $P_{sp} = \dfrac{V \cdot I_{max}}{m_{kg}}$ | $\text{W/kg}$ | Indicates high-power capability |
| 10 | Peukert's Law (lead-acid) | $t = \dfrac{K}{I^{n}}$ | $\text{hours}$ | $n \approx 1.1 - 1.3$ for Pb-Acid |
| 11 | Coulombic Efficiency | $\eta_{C} = \dfrac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}$ | dimensionless | $\approx 100\%$ for Li-ion, $\sim 70-85\%$ for Pb-Acid |
| 12 | Energy Efficiency | $\eta_{E} = \dfrac{E_{out}}{E_{in}}$ | dimensionless | Always less than $\eta_{C}$ |
| 13 | Depth of Discharge | $DOD = 1 - SOC$ | dimensionless | SOC = State of Charge, 0 to 1 |
| 14 | Series Cell Voltage | $V_{pack} = N_{s} \cdot V_{cell}$ | $\text{V}$ | $N_s$ = number of cells in series |
| 15 | Pack Capacity (parallel) | $C_{pack} = N_{p} \cdot C_{cell}$ | $\text{Ah}$ | $N_p$ = number of parallel strings |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pitfall — Peukert's Law** is valid **only** for **lead-acid** batteries under heavy discharge. Lithium-ion cells largely *do not* obey Peukert's law because their internal resistance is much lower and the chemistry is more linear. Do not apply Peukert to Li-ion in the exam.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry** — A car's SLI (Starting, Lighting, Ignition) battery is rated in **CCA (Cold Cranking Amps)**. This is a *current* rating, not an energy rating, and is identified directly from the nameplate.
- **Aerospace & Drones** — Multi-rotor drones use **Li-Po / Li-ion** cells identified by their **C-rating** (e.g., 25C, 50C) printed on the wrap, since peak current draw is critical.
- **Telecom Backup** — **VRLA (Valve-Regulated Lead-Acid)** batteries are identified by their 12 V monobloc construction and standby-float voltage of 13.6–13.8 V.
- **Consumer Electronics** — Mobile phones and laptops use **Li-ion** cells with nominal 3.6–3.7 V chemistry and capacities in mAh (e.g., 4000 mAh, 5000 mAh).
- **Power Tools & Emergency Lighting** — Common usage of **NiCd** (1.2 V cells, robust, high cycle life) and **NiMH** (1.2 V, higher capacity, less toxic than NiCd).

> [!NOTE]
> The **C-rate** is a *factory specification* and must always be read alongside the capacity. A 2.0 Ah cell rated "10C" delivers up to 20 A continuously. A 2.0 Ah cell with no C-rating printed implies a low-current cell (e.g., a coin cell) — never assume.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — From Capacity to Energy

We begin from the fundamental definition of electrical energy delivered at constant voltage.

The energy delivered by a source that supplies a constant current $I$ for a time $t$ at a terminal voltage $V$ is, by definition:

$$E = P \cdot t$$

where the power delivered is $P = V \cdot I$ (assuming $V$ is approximately constant over the discharge interval). Substituting $P$:

$$E = V \cdot I \cdot t$$

But the capacity in ampere-hours is defined as $C_{Ah} = I \cdot t$. Substituting this into the energy equation:

$$E = V \cdot C_{Ah}$$

Hence the stored energy in watt-hours is the product of nominal voltage and rated capacity. In standard SI units (Joules), the same result is:

$$E_{J} = V \cdot C_{Ah} \times 3600$$

The factor **3600** appears because $1\,\text{Wh} = 1\,\text{V} \times 1\,\text{Ah} = 1\,\text{V} \times 3600\,\text{C} = 3600\,\text{J}$.

**Worked Numerical — Lead-Acid Battery**

> A 12 V, 7 Ah sealed lead-acid battery is rated at the 20-hour rate. Find its stored energy in Wh and kJ.

Step 1 — Identify the two specifications from the nameplate: $V_{nom} = 12\,\text{V}$, $C_{Ah} = 7\,\text{Ah}$.

Step 2 — Apply the energy formula:

$$E_{Wh} = V_{nom} \times C_{Ah}$$

$$E_{Wh} = 12\,\text{V} \times 7\,\text{Ah} = 84\,\text{Wh}$$

Step 3 — Convert to kilojoules:

$$E_{kJ} = 84\,\text{Wh} \times \dfrac{3600\,\text{J}}{1\,\text{Wh}} \times \dfrac{1\,\text{kJ}}{1000\,\text{J}} = 302.4\,\text{kJ}$$

**Result:** The battery stores **$84\,\text{Wh}$** or equivalently **$302.4\,\text{kJ}$** of electrical energy.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — C-Rate and Discharge Time

By definition, the **C-rate** is the inverse of the discharge time in hours when the battery is drained at a constant current equal to the rated capacity.

A current of $1 \times C_{Ah}$ (one times the rated capacity) is, by definition, the **1C** rate. Numerically:

$$I_{1C} = 1 \times C_{Ah} = C_{Ah}$$

At this current, the time to fully discharge is:

$$t_{1C} = \dfrac{C_{Ah}}{I_{1C}} = \dfrac{C_{Ah}}{C_{Ah}} = 1\,\text{hour}$$

Generalizing to an arbitrary C-rate $k$ (dimensionless):

$$I_{kC} = k \cdot C_{Ah}$$

$$t_{kC} = \dfrac{C_{Ah}}{k \cdot C_{Ah}} = \dfrac{1}{k}\,\text{hours}$$

**Worked Numerical — Li-ion 18650 Cell**

> A Li-ion 18650 cell is rated at $3.7\,\text{V}$, $2600\,\text{mAh}$, with a maximum continuous discharge of $5\text{C}$. Find (a) the maximum continuous discharge current, (b) the time to fully discharge at $5\text{C}$, and (c) the energy stored.

Step 1 — Convert capacity to Ah:

$$C_{Ah} = 2600\,\text{mAh} \times \dfrac{1\,\text{Ah}}{1000\,\text{mAh}} = 2.6\,\text{Ah}$$

Step 2 — Maximum continuous current:

$$I_{max} = 5 \times 2.6\,\text{Ah} = 13\,\text{A}$$

Step 3 — Discharge time at 5C:

$$t_{5C} = \dfrac{1}{5}\,\text{hour} = 0.2\,\text{hour} = 12\,\text{minutes}$$

Step 4 — Energy stored:

$$E = 3.7\,\text{V} \times 2.6\,\text{Ah} = 9.62\,\text{Wh}$$

**Result:** $I_{max} = 13\,\text{A}$, $t = 12\,\text{min}$, $E = 9.62\,\text{Wh}$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Peukert's Law (Lead-Acid Specific)

Peukert's law states that the usable capacity of a lead-acid battery decreases as the discharge current increases, expressed as:

$$t = \dfrac{K}{I^{n}}$$

where $K$ is a constant (proportional to the rated capacity at a reference current) and $n$ is the **Peukert exponent** (typically $n = 1.1$ to $1.3$ for lead-acid). The exponent $n$ reflects how much capacity is "lost" to internal losses at high currents.

Solving for the constant $K$ from the rated condition (subscript $r$):

$$K = t_{r} \cdot I_{r}^{n}$$

Substituting back, the actual delivered time at any other current is:

$$t = t_{r} \cdot \left(\dfrac{I_{r}}{I}\right)^{n}$$

**Worked Numerical — Applying Peukert's Law**

> A 100 Ah lead-acid battery is rated at the 20-hour rate (so $I_r = 5\,\text{A}$, $t_r = 20\,\text{h}$). Take $n = 1.25$. Find the usable time if discharged at $I = 20\,\text{A}$.

Step 1 — Compute the constant $K$:

$$K = 20 \times 5^{1.25} = 20 \times 7.476 = 149.5$$

Step 2 — Apply Peukert at the new current:

$$t = \dfrac{149.5}{20^{1.25}} = \dfrac{149.5}{31.62} = 4.73\,\text{hours}$$

Step 3 — Check the effective capacity delivered:

$$C_{eff} = I \cdot t = 20 \times 4.73 = 94.6\,\text{Ah}$$

Step 4 — Compare with rated:

$$\text{Capacity loss} = 100 - 94.6 = 5.4\,\text{Ah} \approx 5.4\%$$

**Result:** Even a "100 Ah" battery delivers only **$94.6\,\text{Ah}$** when discharged at four times the rated current. This is why KTU problems always give the discharge time, not just the Ah number.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cross-Comparison of the Three Chemistries

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important table for the KTU viva and Part-B answer**. Memorize the order of magnitude of each quantity.

| Specification | Lead-Acid (VRLA) | Li-ion (LiCoO$_2$) | NiCd |
|---------------|-------------------|---------------------|------|
| Nominal cell voltage | $2.0\,\text{V}$ | $3.6 - 3.7\,\text{V}$ | $1.2\,\text{V}$ |
| End-of-discharge voltage | $1.75\,\text{V}$ | $2.75 - 3.0\,\text{V}$ | $1.0\,\text{V}$ |
| Specific Energy | $30 - 50\,\text{Wh/kg}$ | $150 - 250\,\text{Wh/kg}$ | $40 - 60\,\text{Wh/kg}$ |
| Energy Density | $60 - 110\,\text{Wh/L}$ | $250 - 700\,\text{Wh/L}$ | $50 - 150\,\text{Wh/L}$ |
| Cycle Life (to 80% cap.) | $200 - 500$ | $500 - 2000$ | $1000 - 2000$ |
| Self-Discharge per Month | $3 - 20\%$ | $1 - 5\%$ | $10 - 20\%$ |
| Memory Effect | None | None | **Severe** |
| Typical C-Rate (continuous) | $0.2\text{C} - 1\text{C}$ | $1\text{C} - 3\text{C}$ (cells) | $1\text{C} - 5\text{C}$ |
| Operating Temperature | $-20\,^\circ\text{C}$ to $+50\,^\circ\text{C}$ | $0\,^\circ\text{C}$ to $+45\,^\circ\text{C}$ (discharge) | $-20\,^\circ\text{C}$ to $+60\,^\circ\text{C}$ |
| Toxicity | Lead (hazardous) | Cobalt (hazardous) | **Cadmium (highly toxic)** |
| Cost per kWh | Low | High | Medium |
| Form Factor | Large monoblocs (12 V) | Cylindrical / prismatic | Cylindrical AA, C, D |
| Recyclability | Excellent (>95%) | Developing | Good |

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Series and Parallel Pack Sizing

For a battery pack assembled from identical cells:

**Series Connection** (to increase voltage): the pack voltage is the sum of cell voltages, while the pack capacity (in Ah) equals the cell capacity:

$$V_{pack} = N_{s} \cdot V_{cell}, \quad C_{pack} = C_{cell}$$

**Parallel Connection** (to increase capacity): the pack voltage is unchanged, and the capacities add:

$$V_{pack} = V_{cell}, \quad C_{pack} = N_{p} \cdot C_{cell}$$

**Worked Numerical — Electric Vehicle Pack**

> A small EV uses 100 Li-ion cells, each rated $3.7\,\text{V}$, $50\,\text{Ah}$, arranged as 10 parallel strings, each string containing 10 cells in series. Find the pack nominal voltage, capacity, and energy.

Step 1 — Series count $N_s = 10$:

$$V_{pack} = 10 \times 3.7 = 37\,\text{V}$$

Step 2 — Parallel count $N_p = 10$:

$$C_{pack} = 10 \times 50 = 500\,\text{Ah}$$

Step 3 — Energy:

$$E = 37 \times 500 = 18{,}500\,\text{Wh} = 18.5\,\text{kWh}$$

**Result:** $V = 37\,\text{V}$, $C = 500\,\text{Ah}$, $E = 18.5\,\text{kWh}$.

> [!NOTE]
> This is exactly how every KTU numerical on battery packs is structured. Identify $N_s$ and $N_p$ first, then compute voltage and capacity independently — never mix them.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Battery Specification Identification Procedure

The following Mermaid flowchart depicts the systematic procedure a student must follow during the lab experiment to correctly identify and record the specifications of an unknown battery.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step1 Pick up the battery and inspect form factor] --> B[Step2 Locate the printed label and safety marks]
    B --> C[Step3 Read the chemistry name Sealed Lead Acid Li ion NiCd NiMH]
    C --> D[Step4 Record nominal voltage Vnom in Volts]
    D --> E[Step5 Record rated capacity CAh in Ah or mAh]
    E --> F[Step6 Read the discharge rate in hours or C rate]
    F --> G[Step7 Compute stored energy E equals Vnom times CAh]
    G --> H[Step8 Compute max discharge current Imax equals C rate times CAh]
    H --> I[Step9 Check polarity plus and minus markings]
    I --> J[Step10 Tabulate all data into the lab record]
    J --> K[Step11 Cross verify with chemistry data sheet]
    K --> L[End of identification complete specification sheet ready]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Lab Tip:** Always complete steps 1 to 6 from the *physical label* and steps 7 to 11 by *computation*. The examiner will award marks for both reading and computing — not just one.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Classification of Rechargeable Battery Chemistries

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Rechargeable Batteries] --> B[Lead Acid Family]
    A --> C[Nickel Family]
    A --> D[Lithium Family]

    B --> B1[Flooded Wet Cell]
    B --> B2[VRLA AGM]
    B --> B3[VRLA Gel]

    C --> C1[NiCd Nickel Cadmium]
    C --> C2[NiMH Nickel Metal Hydride]

    D --> D1[Li ion LiCoO2]
    D --> D2[LiPo Lithium Polymer]
    D --> D3[LiFePO4 Lithium Iron Phosphate]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Single Electrochemical Cell

Since the physical structure of a battery cell is best shown as a layered cross-section, the following Mermaid block renders the **functional architecture** mapping the internal charge-flow and energy-conversion path.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Chemical Energy Stored in Active Material] --> B[Anode Negative Electrode]
    B --> C[Electrolyte Ionic Conduction Medium]
    C --> D[Cathode Positive Electrode]
    D --> E[External Load Connected Across Terminals]
    E --> F[Electrical Energy Delivered to Load]
    F --> G[Heat Loss due to Internal Resistance]

    A -.redox reactions during discharge.-> E
    C -.ions flow anode to cathode.-> D
    D -.electrons flow cathode to anode via external circuit.-> E
```

## 4.4 Specification Identification Card (Layout Reference)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Typical Battery Nameplate Layout] --> A1[Top Line Chemistry Type]
    A --> A2[Middle Section Nominal Voltage and Capacity]
    A --> A3[Bottom Section Discharge Rate and Date Code]
    A --> A4[Side Panel Polarity Plus and Minus]
    A --> A5[Reverse Panel Safety Certifications and Warnings]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
*List any three specifications that can be identified from the nameplate of a sealed lead-acid battery and state the typical numerical value of each in a 12 V, 7 Ah VRLA battery.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — one mark per specification + value):**

1. **Nominal Voltage** = $12\,\text{V}$ — this is the average operating voltage under load.
2. **Rated Capacity** = $7\,\text{Ah}$ — the charge deliverable over the rated discharge time (typically 20 hours).
3. **Specific Discharge Rate** = $20\,\text{HR}$ — meaning the rated capacity is obtained when the battery is discharged over 20 hours at $0.35\,\text{A}$.

*(A 4th specification — float charge voltage ~13.6 V or cycle life 200–500 — may also be awarded a mark.)*

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
*State the nominal cell voltage of (a) Lead-Acid, (b) Lithium-Ion, and (c) NiCd batteries. Hence determine the number of cells required to assemble a 48 V battery pack using each chemistry.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.5 mark per cell voltage + 0.5 mark per cell count):**

| Chemistry | Nominal cell voltage | Cells for 48 V pack |
|-----------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Lead-Acid | $2.0\,\text{V}$ | $48 / 2.0 = 24$ cells |
| Lithium-Ion | $3.7\,\text{V}$ | $48 / 3.7 \approx 13$ cells |
| NiCd | $1.2\,\text{V}$ | $48 / 1.2 = 40$ cells |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** *With the help of a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a sealed lead-acid (VRLA) battery. List the typical specifications printed on its nameplate.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A 12 V, 60 Ah lead-acid battery is rated at the 10-hour rate. Determine: (i) the rated discharge current, (ii) the discharge time at 1C rate, and (iii) the stored energy in kWh.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Construction (3 marks):** A VRLA lead-acid battery consists of:
   - **Positive plate:** Lead dioxide ($\text{PbO}_2$) pasted on a lead-antimony or lead-calcium grid.
   - **Negative plate:** Sponge lead ($\text{Pb}$) on a similar grid.
   - **Separator:** Micro-porous glass mat (AGM) or silica gel, which immobilizes the electrolyte.
   - **Electrolyte:** Dilute sulfuric acid ($\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$) of specific gravity ~1.28.
   - **Valve:** A one-way pressure relief valve (hence "valve-regulated") that vents gas only on over-pressure.
   - **Container:** Sealed, impact-resistant ABS plastic.

2. **Working during discharge (2 marks):** The half-cell reactions are:
   - Negative plate: $\text{Pb} + \text{SO}_4^{2-} \rightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 + 2e^-$
   - Positive plate: $\text{PbO}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ + \text{SO}_4^{2-} + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$
   - Net cell reaction: $\text{Pb} + \text{PbO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow 2\text{PbSO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Electrical Energy}$

3. **Nameplate specifications (2 marks):** Nominal voltage 12 V; capacity 60 Ah; discharge rate 10 HR; float charge voltage 13.6–13.8 V; cycle life; manufacturing date; safety certifications (IS/IEC).

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Rated discharge current (2 marks):**
The 10-hour rate means the full 60 Ah is delivered over 10 hours:
$$I_{rated} = \dfrac{C_{Ah}}{t_{rated}} = \dfrac{60\,\text{Ah}}{10\,\text{h}} = 6\,\text{A}$$

**(ii) Discharge time at 1C rate (2 marks):**
By definition of C-rate, the time at 1C is:
$$t_{1C} = \dfrac{1}{1}\,\text{hour} = 1\,\text{hour}$$
So the 1C current is $60\,\text{A}$ and the discharge time is exactly $1\,\text{hour}$.

**(iii) Stored energy in kWh (3 marks):**
$$E = V_{nom} \times C_{Ah} = 12\,\text{V} \times 60\,\text{Ah} = 720\,\text{Wh} = 0.72\,\text{kWh}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b)**
> Students commonly confuse the **rated discharge current** (defined by the printed hour rate) with the **C-rate current**. The rated current for a "60 Ah @ 10HR" battery is **$6\,\text{A}$**, not $60\,\text{A}$. Only at the **1C** rate does the current equal $60\,\text{A}$. One mark is deducted for this confusion in nearly every answer script — be precise with terminology.

### Question B — Choice 2 (Alternative)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** *Compare the construction, nominal voltage, specific energy, cycle life, and memory effect of Lithium-Ion and NiCd batteries. Which is preferred for portable consumer electronics and why?* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *A Li-ion power bank is rated at $3.7\,\text{V}$, $10{,}000\,\text{mAh}$. It contains a 1S4P configuration of identical cells. Find: (i) the voltage and capacity of each individual cell, (ii) the stored energy in Wh, and (iii) the maximum continuous discharge current if the cell C-rating is $2\text{C}$.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks:**

| Parameter | Lithium-Ion | NiCd |
|-----------|-------------|------|
| Construction | Layered cathode ($\text{LiCoO}_2$) and graphite anode, separated by porous polymer film and organic electrolyte | Sintered nickel hydroxide cathode, cadmium metal anode, alkaline KOH electrolyte |
| Nominal Voltage | $3.6 - 3.7\,\text{V/cell}$ | $1.2\,\text{V/cell}$ |
| Specific Energy | $150 - 250\,\text{Wh/kg}$ (high) | $40 - 60\,\text{Wh/kg}$ (low) |
| Cycle Life | $500 - 2000$ cycles | $1000 - 2000$ cycles |
| Memory Effect | Negligible | **Severe** (requires full discharge before recharge) |
| Toxicity | Cobalt — moderately hazardous | Cadmium — **highly toxic, banned in EU for consumer use** |

**Preferred for portable consumer electronics: Lithium-Ion (3 marks for justification).** Reasons:
- 3× higher specific energy gives longer runtime per unit weight.
- Higher nominal voltage means fewer cells for a given pack voltage.
- No memory effect — user can top-up charge at any time.
- Lower self-discharge (~2% per month vs ~15% for NiCd).
- Available in slim prismatic and pouch formats suited to mobile phones and laptops.

**Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks:**

The notation **"1S4P"** means **1 cell in series, 4 cells in parallel**.

**(i) Per-cell voltage and capacity (3 marks):**

Since "1S" means one cell in series, the pack voltage equals the cell voltage:
$$V_{cell} = V_{pack} = 3.7\,\text{V}$$

Since "4P" means four cells in parallel, the pack capacity is four times the cell capacity:
$$C_{pack} = 4 \cdot C_{cell} = 10{,}000\,\text{mAh}$$

$$C_{cell} = \dfrac{10{,}000}{4} = 2500\,\text{mAh} = 2.5\,\text{Ah}$$

**(ii) Stored energy in Wh (2 marks):**
$$E = V_{pack} \times C_{pack} = 3.7\,\text{V} \times 10\,\text{Ah} = 37\,\text{Wh}$$

**(iii) Maximum continuous discharge current (2 marks):**

The C-rate of each cell is $2\text{C}$, so the maximum continuous current per cell is:
$$I_{cell,max} = 2 \times 2.5\,\text{Ah} = 5\,\text{A}$$

Since the four parallel cells share the load equally, the pack maximum current is:
$$I_{pack,max} = 4 \times 5\,\text{A} = 20\,\text{A}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b)**
> In a **"1S4P"** configuration, students frequently report the cell voltage as $V_{pack}/4$ and the cell capacity as $C_{pack}/1$. This is **completely inverted**. The correct rule is: **S = Series = Voltage adds**, **P = Parallel = Capacity adds**. The S-count multiplies voltage; the P-count multiplies capacity. Two marks are routinely lost for this single mistake.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU**

- A **battery** converts stored chemical energy into electrical energy via redox reactions.
- The **five primary specifications** to identify from any battery are: **$V_{nom}$, $C_{Ah}$, Energy, C-rate, Cycle Life**.
- **Nominal cell voltages** to memorize: Lead-Acid = $2.0\,\text{V}$, Li-ion = $3.7\,\text{V}$, NiCd = $1.2\,\text{V}$, NiMH = $1.2\,\text{V}$, Alkaline = $1.5\,\text{V}$.
- **Energy formula** (most-used in the exam): $E_{Wh} = V_{nom} \times C_{Ah}$.
- **C-rate rule:** 1C discharge current equals the rated Ah; discharge time at rate $k$C is $1/k$ hours.
- **Peukert's Law** applies **only to lead-acid** cells; do not apply to Li-ion.
- **"HR" rating on a label** means the rated capacity is delivered over that many hours (e.g., "20HR" = discharged over 20 hours).
- **Series connection** increases voltage: $V_{pack} = N_s \cdot V_{cell}$.
- **Parallel connection** increases capacity: $C_{pack} = N_p \cdot C_{cell}$.
- **Memory effect** is severe in **NiCd**, mild in NiMH, and absent in Li-ion and lead-acid.
- **Specific energy ranking** (highest to lowest): Li-ion > NiMH > NiCd > Lead-Acid.
- **Self-discharge ranking** (lowest to highest): Li-ion < NiMH < Lead-Acid < NiCd.
- **Toxicity concern:** Cadmium in NiCd is environmentally hazardous — requires special disposal.
- **Safety symbols** on a battery: IS, IEC, BIS, CE, UL, plus polarity (+/−) and the *crossed-bin* recycling symbol.
- **VRLA** = Valve-Regulated Lead-Acid — sealed, maintenance-free; available in **AGM** (absorbed glass mat) and **Gel** variants.
- **Float charge voltage** of a 12 V VRLA monobloc is **$13.6 - 13.8\,\text{V}$**; the cyclic charge voltage is **$14.4 - 14.8\,\text{V}$**.
- **End-of-discharge voltage** (per cell): Lead-Acid = $1.75\,\text{V}$, Li-ion = $2.75 - 3.0\,\text{V}$, NiCd = $1.0\,\text{V}$.
- The **internal resistance** of a battery is the *single most important parameter for high-current applications* (e.g., EV, UPS, engine cranking).
- **Always cross-verify** the printed Wh rating against the computed $V_{nom} \times C_{Ah}$ — a mismatch indicates a misreading or a low-quality manufacturer.
- In the lab, **always identify the chemistry *first***, then read the other specifications — this prevents confusion when a "12 V" battery could be either lead-acid (6 cells) or Li-ion (4 cells, with a 3S BMS).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Familiarize different types of earthing (Pipe, Plate Earthing, Mat Schemes) and ground enhancing materials (GEM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZESL208 - BASIC ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarize different types of earthing (Pipe, Plate Earthing, Mat Schemes) and ground enhancing materials (GEM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Earthing (Grounding):** The process of creating a low-resistance electrical connection between the non-current carrying metallic parts of an electrical installation (or a part of the system) and the general mass of earth, using a suitable electrode buried in soil, so as to safely dissipate fault currents, lightning surges, and static charges into the earth without causing any danger to personnel or equipment.

In KTU terminology, this experiment belongs to the **Electrical Safety and Installation Practice** domain. The two interchangeable terms used in IS 3043:1987 (Indian Standard for Code of Practice for Earthing) and IEEE standards are:

- **Earthing** — Used predominantly in **British/Indian standards (BIS)**.
- **Grounding** — Used predominantly in **American standards (IEEE/NEC)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Emphasis:** For the KTU 2024 Scheme, the term *Earthing* is preferred in answers, and the relevant code reference is **IS 3043:1987 (Reaffirmed 2014)**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Drain Pipe" of Electricity

Imagine a building's plumbing system. Water always flows toward the lowest point — the **drain**. Similarly, **fault current always flows toward the lowest potential** — the **earth**. Earthing is essentially an **electrical drain pipe**:

- A fault current is like a leak in a water tank.
- The earthing electrode (pipe/plate/mat) is the drain.
- The soil is the vast reservoir that absorbs the leakage safely.

If there is **no proper drain** (poor/no earthing), the leaking water (current) causes damage — it damages **insulation, equipment, and most importantly, the human body**, since a human body touching a live conductor becomes the unintended path to earth.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Purpose of Earthing (Memorize for KTU):**
> 1. **Safety of Human Life** — Limits touch and step potential to safe values (< 50 V).
> 2. **Equipment Protection** — Provides a low-impedance path for fault currents to operate protective devices (MCB, MCCB, ELCB).
> 3. **Voltage Stabilization** — Stabilizes system voltage with respect to earth.
> 4. **Lightning Protection** — Safely dissipates lightning strokes (typical stroke current = **30 kA**, peak = **200 kA**).
> 5. **Noise / EMI Reduction** — In electronics, provides a clean reference for signal circuits.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Permissible Body Current | **< 10 mA** (let-go threshold) | IEC 60479 |
| Safe Touch Voltage (dry) | **< 50 V AC** | IS 3043 |
| Safe Touch Voltage (wet) | **< 25 V AC** | IS 3043 |
| Lightning Stroke Current | **30 kA (typical)**, up to **200 kA** | IEEE 998 |
| Target Earth Resistance (Power Stations) | **< 0.5 Ω** | IS 3043 |
| Target Earth Resistance (Large Substations) | **< 1.0 Ω** | IS 3043 |
| Target Earth Resistance (Domestic/Light Industrial) | **< 5.0 Ω** | IS 3043 |
| Soil Resistivity (typical range) | **10 Ω·m (moist clay) to 1000 Ω·m (dry sand/rock)** | IS 3043 |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Earthing Systems (As per IS 3043)

```
Earthing Systems
├── Equipment Earthing (Protective Earthing)
│   └── Connects non-current carrying metal parts (body) to earth
│       Examples: Motor body, DB body, appliance metal casing
│
└── System Earthing (Neutral Earthing)
    └── Connects the neutral point of the system to earth
        Examples: Star point of transformer, generator neutral
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** The experiment "Familiarize different types of earthing" primarily deals with **Equipment Earthing** (Pipe, Plate, Mat) — i.e., how the physical electrode that goes into the ground is constructed.

---

### 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Each Earthing Type

#### 2.2.1 Pipe Earthing

**Construction Logic (Step-by-Step):**
1. A **GI (Galvanized Iron) pipe** of length **2.5 m to 4 m** and diameter **40 mm to 50 mm** is used as the main electrode.
2. The pipe is perforated with **12 mm holes** at regular intervals to allow moisture seepage and improve soil-to-electrode contact.
3. The pipe is buried vertically in the ground, with the **top end at least 1.25 m to 2.0 m below the earth surface**.
4. The bottom of the pipe is fitted with a **cast iron or steel wedge/tapering point** for easy driving into the soil.
5. Around the pipe, alternate layers of **charcoal and salt** are filled to a thickness of ~15 cm each. This is the *earthing pit treatment*.
6. A **GI funnel / mesh cap** is provided at the top of the pipe for pouring water periodically to maintain moisture.
7. The earth lead (GI wire of adequate cross-section) is connected to the pipe via a **GI nut-bolt-clamp** arrangement and protected with **PVC/PVC conduit** up to the surface.

**Why Charcoal and Salt?**
- **Charcoal** retains moisture for a long time and is a good conductor of electricity (carbon content).
- **Salt (NaCl)** is hygroscopic — it absorbs moisture from the atmosphere and keeps the surrounding soil damp, thereby **reducing soil resistivity (ρ)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Charcoal-Salt-Layer Sequence (top to bottom):**
> Soil → Salt layer (15 cm) → Charcoal layer (15 cm) → Salt layer (15 cm) → Charcoal layer (15 cm) → … up to the bottom of the pipe.

**Application:** Most common in **domestic installations, small workshops, and low-voltage distribution systems**. Best suited for **soft soil** and where the water table is high.

---

#### 2.2.2 Plate Earthing

**Construction Logic:**
1. A **GI plate or Copper plate** is used as the main electrode.
   - **GI Plate** size: **60 cm × 60 cm × 6.35 mm** thick (or **50 cm × 50 cm × 6 mm**).
   - **Copper Plate** size: **60 cm × 60 cm × 3.18 mm** thick (preferred for corrosive soils).
2. The plate is buried **vertically** in a pit at a depth of **at least 1.5 m to 3.0 m** below ground level, with the **flat face vertical** (so that both sides contact the soil).
3. The pit is filled with alternate layers of **charcoal and salt** (same principle as pipe earthing).
4. The earth lead (GI flat or copper strip) is connected to the plate via **bolts and nuts**, ensuring two separate bolts for redundancy.
5. A **watering pipe** (25 mm GI pipe with a funnel) is embedded near the plate for periodic water pouring.
6. A **masonry inspection chamber** with a cast iron cover is built over the pit for periodic maintenance.

**Why Plate and Not Pipe for Big Installations?**
- The plate provides a **larger surface area in contact with soil**, thus achieving **lower earth resistance**.
- Copper plate is **highly resistant to corrosion**, ideal for **acidic or saline soils**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked to "compare Pipe vs Plate earthing," always mention: Pipe is **cheaper and easier to install**; Plate gives **lower resistance and longer life (Copper plate = 20–30 years)**.

**Application:** Used in **power stations, substations, large industrial installations, and high-voltage equipment**.

---

#### 2.2.3 Mat Earthing (Mat Scheme)

**Construction Logic:**
1. A mat is a **grid/mesh of copper or GI conductors** (typically **G.I. flats of 25 mm × 3 mm or copper strips of 25 mm × 3 mm**) laid horizontally.
2. The grid is laid at a depth of **0.5 m to 1.5 m** below the ground surface.
3. The grid consists of **parallel conductors** spaced at **3 m to 7 m** apart, with **cross conductors** welded at intersections.
4. **Multiple earth electrodes (rods/pipes)** are connected at the corners and junctions of the grid to **further reduce earth resistance** and ensure equipotential.
5. The mat is **bolted / welded** to the main earth bus bar inside the substation/building.

**Why Mat?**
- Provides a **very low earth resistance** (typically **< 0.5 Ω**).
- **Equalizes the potential** over the entire substation floor — this is **critical for step-and-touch potential safety** when a high fault current flows.
- Distributes the fault current over a **large area**, reducing the **step potential** (voltage between two feet, 1 m apart).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mat Earthing is mandatory for EHV substations (66 kV, 110 kV, 220 kV, 400 kV and above)** as per IS 3043, because the magnitude of fault current is so high that a single electrode cannot dissipate the energy safely.

**Application:** **EHV substations, generating stations, transmission tower footings, large industrial switchyards**.

---

### 2.4 Ground Enhancing Materials (GEM)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GEM (Ground Enhancing Material)** is a **modern, low-maintenance alternative to the traditional charcoal-salt method** of reducing soil resistivity around the earthing electrode.

**Definition:** GEM is a **conductive compound** (typically a mixture of **graphite, cement, Bentonite clay, and hygroscopic salts**) that is poured/compacted around the earthing electrode to **permanently lower the soil-electrode contact resistance**.

**Why GEM?**
- The traditional charcoal-salt method requires **regular watering** (every 2–3 months) and **salt replenishment** (every 1–2 years).
- In **dry, rocky, or sandy soils** (e.g., Kerala's laterite soil in some regions, or desert soils in Rajasthan), the resistivity is so high that conventional earthing is impractical.
- GEM **retains moisture for years** and provides a **stable, low resistance** over the entire life of the installation (often 25+ years).

**Common Commercial Products:**
- **Bentonite-based GEM** — most popular; swells when wet, fills soil voids.
- **Graphite-cement GEM** — used in dry climates.
- **Conductive concrete** — GEM mixed with concrete for tower footings.
- **Marconite / Powerfill** — proprietary brands used in Indian substations.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** GEM is part of the **"Smart Earthing"** approach expected in modern NEP-2020 aligned syllabi. Remember the term *"Ground Enhancing Material (GEM)"* — it has appeared in KTU model question papers.

---

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute values / magnitudes to keep the table safe.

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | Typical Range / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Earth Resistance of a Pipe Electrode** | $R_{p} = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\!\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right) - 1 \right]$ | $\rho$ = soil resistivity (Ω·m); $L$ = length of pipe (m); $d$ = diameter of pipe (m) | Valid when $L \gg d$ |
| 2 | **Earth Resistance of a Plate Electrode** | $R_{pl} = \dfrac{\rho}{8r} + \dfrac{\rho}{8h}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{r}{\sqrt{r^2 + h^2}}\right)$ | $\rho$ = soil resistivity (Ω·m); $r$ = equivalent radius of plate (m); $h$ = depth of burial (m) | Simplified for $h \gg r$ |
| 3 | **Plate Resistance (approx., plate at surface)** | $R_{pl} \approx \dfrac{\rho}{2A}$ | $A$ = area of one face of plate (m²) | Used as first-order estimate |
| 4 | **Resistance of a Rod Electrode (Vertical)** | $R_{r} = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\!\left(\dfrac{8L}{d}\right) - 1 \right]$ | $\rho$, $L$, $d$ as above | For solid rod driven vertically |
| 5 | **Resistance of Horizontal Strip Electrode** | $R_{s} = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\!\left(\dfrac{4L}{d}\right) + \ln\!\left(\dfrac{L}{h}\right) - 2 + \dfrac{2h}{L} \right]$ | $L$ = length of strip; $d$ = width as diameter; $h$ = depth of burial | Approximation for $h \ll L$ |
| 6 | **Parallel Combination of Multiple Electrodes** | $\dfrac{1}{R_{eq}} = \dfrac{1}{R_1} + \dfrac{1}{R_2} + \dots + \dfrac{1}{R_n}$ | All $R_i$ in ohms | Mat schemes use 4 to 16 electrodes |
| 7 | **Soil Resistivity (Wenner 4-probe method)** | $\rho = 2\pi a \cdot R$ | $a$ = electrode spacing (m); $R$ = measured resistance (Ω) | **Standard field test method** |
| 8 | **Step Potential** | $V_{step} = I_{f} \cdot R_{body-step}$ | $I_f$ = fault current (A) | Should be $< \dfrac{116 + 0.7 \rho_s}{1000}$ kV per IS 3043 |
| 9 | **Touch Potential** | $V_{touch} = I_{f} \cdot R_{body-touch}$ | $R_{body} \approx 1000$ Ω (wet), $3000$ Ω (dry) | Should be $< \dfrac{116 + 0.17 \rho_s}{1000}$ kV |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where:**
> - $R$ = Earth electrode resistance (Ω)
> - $\rho$ or $\rho_s$ = Soil resistivity (Ω·m)
> - $L$ = Length of electrode (m)
> - $d$ = Diameter / equivalent width of electrode (m)
> - $h$ = Depth of burial (m)
> - $r$ = Equivalent radius of plate (m)
> - $A$ = Surface area of one face of plate (m²)

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Domestic Earthing (Pipe):** 5–10 Ω is acceptable. Used in house wiring with ELCB/RCD.
- **Industrial Earthing (Plate):** Required for HT/LT panels, motors, transformers — 1–5 Ω.
- **Substation Earthing (Mat):** Earth mat + multiple rods achieve **< 0.5 Ω**, mandatory as per **CEA (Central Electricity Authority) Safety Regulations 2010**.
- **Telecom Towers:** Use **chemical/GEM earthing** with **copper-bonded rods** to achieve **< 2 Ω** in rocky terrain.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Earth Resistance of a Pipe Electrode

> **Context:** A vertical pipe electrode of length $L$ and diameter $d$ is buried in soil of resistivity $\rho$. We want to find the resistance between the electrode and a remote point in the soil (effectively "infinity"). This resistance is what limits the fault-current flow into the earth.

**Assumptions:**
1. The pipe is vertical and cylindrical.
2. The soil is homogeneous and isotropic (same resistivity $\rho$ in all directions).
3. The remote point is at infinity where potential = 0 V.
4. Current $I$ leaves the pipe radially into the soil.

**Step 1 — Set up the current-density field.**

Consider a thin cylindrical shell of soil at radius $r$ from the axis of the pipe. The total surface area through which current $I$ flows through this shell is:

$$
A_{shell} = 2\pi r L
$$

> Note: $L$ is the length of the pipe (the active electrode surface), not the radius of the shell. Current flows outward only through the **cylindrical surface** of length $L$ around the pipe.

**Step 2 — Write the resistance of the thin shell.**

The thickness of the shell is $dr$. The resistance of this thin cylindrical shell of soil is:

$$
dR = \rho \cdot \frac{dr}{A_{shell}} = \frac{\rho \, dr}{2\pi r L}
$$

> This uses the basic resistance formula $R = \rho \cdot l / A$, where $l = dr$ (length of current path in this shell) and $A = 2\pi r L$ (cross-sectional area of the current path).

**Step 3 — Integrate from the pipe surface to infinity.**

These shells are in **series** with each other (current must pass through all of them to reach infinity), so we integrate:

$$
R = \int_{r=d/2}^{\infty} \frac{\rho \, dr}{2\pi r L}
$$

> The lower limit is $d/2$ (radius of the pipe), not $d$, because $r$ is the radius of the shell measured from the centre.

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$
R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \int_{d/2}^{\infty} \frac{dr}{r} = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln r \right]_{d/2}^{\infty}
$$

> The integral of $1/r$ is $\ln r$. As $r \to \infty$, $\ln r \to \infty$. Physically, this diverges, but for engineering purposes we truncate the integration at a practical "effective" radius. Mathematically, the result is corrected for end-effects (current doesn't flow uniformly from the bottom tip of the pipe) using empirical correction factors.

**Step 5 — Apply the empirical end-correction.**

For a finite pipe (with the bottom tip acting as a hemisphere of current dispersal), the standard empirical formula used in practice is:

$$
R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L} \left[ \ln\!\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) - 1 \right]
$$

> The "$-1$" inside the bracket is the correction for the **bottom end effect** (the hemispherical current dispersal from the tip), and the "4" is an empirical multiplier that accounts for the practical geometry validated by Dwight's formula (1916) and adopted in IS 3043.

---

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** A GI pipe of length $L = 3$ m and outer diameter $d = 50$ mm is used as an earth electrode. The soil resistivity is $\rho = 100\ \Omega\cdot\text{m}$. Find the earth resistance. What happens if we double the length?

**Given:**
- $L = 3$ m
- $d = 0.05$ m
- $\rho = 100\ \Omega\cdot\text{m}$

**Step 1 — Substitute into the formula.**

$$
R = \frac{\rho}{2\pi L}\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{4L}{d}\right) - 1\right]
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the argument of the log.**

$$
\frac{4L}{d} = \frac{4 \times 3}{0.05} = \frac{12}{0.05} = 240
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the natural log.**

$$
\ln(240) \approx 5.4806
$$

**Step 4 — Subtract 1.**

$$
5.4806 - 1 = 4.4806
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the prefactor.**

$$
\frac{\rho}{2\pi L} = \frac{100}{2 \pi \times 3} = \frac{100}{18.8496} \approx 5.3052\ \Omega
$$

**Step 6 — Multiply.**

$$
R = 5.3052 \times 4.4806 \approx 23.77\ \Omega
$$

> This is **too high** for a domestic installation (which needs $R < 5\ \Omega$).

**Step 7 — Investigate the effect of doubling length to $L = 6$ m.**

$$
\frac{4L}{d} = \frac{4 \times 6}{0.05} = 480
$$

$$
\ln(480) \approx 6.1738
$$

$$
6.1738 - 1 = 5.1738
$$

$$
\frac{\rho}{2\pi L} = \frac{100}{2\pi \times 6} = \frac{100}{37.699} \approx 2.6526\ \Omega
$$

$$
R = 2.6526 \times 5.1738 \approx 13.72\ \Omega
$$

> **Conclusion:** Doubling the length **reduces the resistance by only ~42%**, not 50%. This shows that **simply increasing pipe length gives diminishing returns** — that is why **multiple parallel pipes** or **plate/mat earthing** is preferred for very low resistance requirements.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (For KTU Answers)

| Property | Pipe Earthing | Plate Earthing | Mat Earthing |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Electrode Geometry** | Vertical pipe, $L = 2.5$–$4$ m | Vertical plate, $60\times 60$ cm | Horizontal grid of strips |
| **Material (typical)** | GI / Copper-bonded | GI / Copper plate | GI flats / Copper strips |
| **Earth Resistance Achieved** | Moderate (5–25 Ω) | Low (1–5 Ω) | Very Low ($< 0.5\ \Omega$) |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | High |
| **Installation Effort** | Easy (drive into soil) | Moderate (dig a pit) | Heavy civil work |
| **Maintenance** | Periodic watering | Periodic watering | Almost maintenance-free (with GEM) |
| **Best Suited For** | Domestic, small workshops | Industries, substations (small) | EHV substations, generating stations |
| **Lifespan** | 7–10 years | 15–20 years (GI), 25–30 yrs (Cu) | 30+ years |
| **Space Required** | Small | Medium | Large area |
| **Suitability in Rocky Soil** | Poor | Poor | Good (with GEM) |
| **Step/Touch Potential Control** | Poor | Moderate | Excellent (uniform potential) |

The result is a **uniform earth potential** across the entire substation.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. Define earthing. List any four methods of earthing.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model QP 2024 Scheme]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Earthing is the process of connecting the non-current carrying metallic parts of an electrical installation, or the neutral point of the system, to the general mass of earth through a low-resistance electrode, so as to safely dissipate fault currents and maintain the potential of the connected parts at earth potential.

**Methods of Earthing (2 marks — any four):**
1. **Pipe Earthing** — Vertical GI/copper pipe driven into the ground.
2. **Plate Earthing** — GI or copper plate buried vertically in a pit.
3. **Mat Earthing** — Grid of horizontal conductors with multiple electrodes (used in substations).
4. **Rod Earthing** — Solid copper or copper-bonded steel rod driven vertically.
5. **Strip Earthing** — GI/Cu strip laid horizontally in trenches.
6. **Chemical / GEM Earthing** — Using ground enhancing materials for low-resistance earthing in poor soil.

---

#### **Q2. What is GEM? Why is it used in modern earthing installations?** `[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Model QP 2024 Scheme]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition of GEM (1.5 marks):**
**Ground Enhancing Material (GEM)** is a conductive compound, typically a mixture of **Bentonite clay, graphite powder, cement, and hygroscopic salts**, that is poured or compacted around an earth electrode to **permanently reduce the contact resistance** between the electrode and the surrounding soil.

**Why GEM is used (1.5 marks):**
1. It **drastically lowers the soil resistivity** in the immediate vicinity of the electrode (e.g., from 500 Ω·m to < 20 Ω·m).
2. It **retains moisture for years**, eliminating the need for **periodic watering** required in the traditional charcoal-salt method.
3. It is **highly effective in rocky, dry, or sandy soils** where conventional earthing is impractical.
4. It is **non-corrosive** and extends the lifespan of the electrode (especially copper electrodes).
5. It is **environmentally safer** than the salt-leaching method, which can contaminate groundwater.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question is **14 marks**, with an internal choice (either OR between two full 14-mark sets). The typical split is **7 + 7 marks** for sub-parts (a) and (b).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

#### **(a) Explain with a neat diagram the construction and working of Pipe Earthing. State its merits and demerits. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, 2024 Scheme Model]`

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (4 marks):**

A pipe earthing system consists of a **vertically buried GI pipe** of length **2.5 m to 4 m** and diameter **40 mm to 50 mm**. The construction steps are:

1. A pit of dimensions approximately **1.5 m × 1.5 m × 3 m (depth)** is dug at the chosen location.
2. A **cast iron wedge/tapering point** is fitted at the bottom of the GI pipe to help drive it into the soil.
3. The pipe is perforated with **12 mm diameter holes** at regular intervals along its length to facilitate the seepage of moisture into the surrounding soil.
4. The pipe is **driven vertically** into the ground, with the top of the pipe ending at least **1.25 m to 2 m** below the earth surface.
5. The pit surrounding the pipe is filled with **alternate layers of charcoal and salt**, each layer being approximately **15 cm thick**. The sequence (from bottom to top) is typically: **Charcoal → Salt → Charcoal → Salt → … → Top soil**.
6. A **GI mesh funnel/cover** is fitted on the top of the pipe, slightly above ground level, for periodic water pouring.
7. The **earth lead (GI wire, typically 8 SWG or larger)** is connected to the pipe via a **GI nut, bolt, and clamp** arrangement.
8. The earth lead is taken through a **PVC conduit** up to the surface and connected to the equipment body or the main earth bus bar.

**Diagram (already in Section 4.1 above — Mermaid block diagram suffices for KTU answer scripts).**

**Merits (1.5 marks):**
1. **Cheapest and simplest** form of earthing.
2. **Easy to install** — does not require heavy civil work.
3. **Suitable for soft soils** and locations with a high water table.
4. **Easily maintainable** — salt and water can be replenished periodically through the funnel.

**Demerits (1.5 marks):**
1. **Higher earth resistance** compared to plate or mat earthing (typically 5–25 Ω).
2. **Not suitable for rocky or dry soils** without additional treatment (GEM).
3. **Regular maintenance** (watering, salt replenishment) is mandatory.
4. **Limited lifespan** (7–10 years) due to corrosion of the GI pipe.

---

#### **(b) Compare Pipe Earthing and Plate Earthing in detail. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, 2024 Scheme Model]`

**Model Answer (Tabular Form — KTU Board Preferred):**

| S. No. | Parameter | Pipe Earthing | Plate Earthing |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Electrode** | Vertical GI pipe, 2.5–4 m long, 40–50 mm dia. | Vertical GI/Cu plate, 60 × 60 cm, 6–12 mm thick. |
| 2 | **Installation** | Pipe is driven vertically into the ground. | Plate is buried vertically in a deep pit. |
| 3 | **Earth Resistance** | 5 to 25 Ω (higher). | 1 to 5 Ω (lower, due to larger contact area). |
| 4 | **Cost** | Low. | Medium. |
| 5 | **Civil Work** | Minimal. | Moderate (pit + inspection chamber). |
| 6 | **Material** | GI / Copper-bonded. | GI plate / Copper plate (Cu preferred for corrosive soils). |
| 7 | **Lifespan** | 7–10 years (GI). | 15–20 years (GI); 25–30 years (Cu). |
| 8 | **Maintenance** | Frequent watering + salt top-up. | Periodic watering + salt top-up. |
| 9 | **Application** | Domestic wiring, small workshops. | Industries, substations, HT/LT panels. |
| 10 | **Suitability in Dry/Rocky Soil** | Poor. | Poor (better than pipe, but still requires GEM). |
| 11 | **Standard Reference** | IS 3043. | IS 3043. |

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
Pipe earthing is the most economical choice for **low-voltage domestic applications** with adequate soil conditions. Plate earthing is preferred for **industrial and substation installations** where **lower earth resistance and higher reliability** are required.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE:**

#### **(a) With a neat diagram, explain the construction of Plate Earthing. Also state the role of charcoal and salt in the earthing pit. (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, 2024 Scheme Model]`

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (4 marks):**

1. A pit of dimensions approximately **1.5 m × 1.5 m × 3.0 m (depth)** is excavated at the chosen location.
2. A **GI plate (60 × 60 × 6.35 mm)** or **Copper plate (60 × 60 × 3.18 mm)** is taken as the main electrode.
3. The plate is **buried vertically** at the bottom of the pit, with the **flat face vertical** so that both faces are in contact with the soil.
4. The **earth lead** (GI flat 25 × 3 mm or Cu strip of equivalent size) is connected to the plate using **two separate bolts and nuts** for redundancy.
5. **Alternate layers of charcoal and salt** (each 15 cm thick) are filled around the plate, up to a height of approximately 1.5 m above the plate.
6. A **25 mm GI watering pipe** with a funnel on top is embedded vertically, with its bottom end near the plate, to allow periodic watering.
7. The remaining pit is backfilled with **excavated soil**.
8. A **masonry inspection chamber** with a cast iron cover is built at the surface for access during maintenance.

**Role of Charcoal and Salt (3 marks):**

| Material | Role |
|---|---|
| **Charcoal (Carbon)** | 1. Retains moisture for long periods due to its porous structure. <br> 2. Acts as a good conductor of electricity (due to free electrons in carbon). <br> 3. Prevents the soil around the electrode from drying out, ensuring stable low resistance. <br> 4. Slowly releases moisture, maintaining a conductive path year-round. |
| **Salt (NaCl — Sodium Chloride)** | 1. Highly **hygroscopic** — absorbs atmospheric moisture and keeps the surrounding soil damp. <br> 2. **Dissociates into Na⁺ and Cl⁻ ions** in the presence of moisture, which act as charge carriers and **lower the soil resistivity** significantly. <br> 3. Provides **long-term ionic conductivity** as the ions slowly dissolve into the soil water. <br> 4. Requires periodic replenishment (every 1–2 years) as it gets washed away. |

**Conclusion (covered within marks):**
The combination of charcoal and salt around the electrode is a **cost-effective, time-tested method** of creating a low-resistivity zone that ensures the earth resistance remains within acceptable limits.

---

#### **(b) Explain the concept and construction of Mat Earthing. Why is it essential for EHV substations? (7 marks)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, 2024 Scheme Model]`

**Model Answer:**

**Concept (2 marks):**

Mat earthing is a **grid-type earthing system** in which a **mesh of horizontal conductors (typically GI flats of 25 × 3 mm or copper strips of 25 × 3 mm)** is buried at a depth of **0.5 m to 1.5 m** below the substation floor. **Vertical earth electrodes (rods/pipes)** are connected at the corners and intersections of the grid to provide additional low-resistance paths and ensure equipotential distribution.

**Construction (3 marks):**

1. The substation floor area is excavated to a depth of **0.5 m to 1.5 m**.
2. A **grid of parallel conductors** is laid horizontally, spaced **3 m to 7 m** apart, covering the entire substation area.
3. **Cross conductors** are welded at right angles to the parallel conductors at every intersection, forming a **rigid mesh**.
4. All joints are **welded (not bolted)** for permanent, low-resistance connections; welded joints are then coated with **bitumen** to prevent corrosion.
5. **Multiple earth electrodes (typically 4 to 16)** — usually **GI pipes of 50 mm dia × 2.5 m long** or **copper-bonded rods** — are driven into the soil at the corners, edges, and centre of the grid.
6. These electrodes are **welded to the grid** at the junction points.
7. The **main earth bus bar** inside the substation control room is connected to the mat via **multiple earth leads (redundant paths)**.
8. The mat is then **covered with a layer of soil/stone gravel**, and the substation floor is constructed above it.

**Why Mat Earthing is Essential for EHV Substations (2 marks):**

1. **Very Low Earth Resistance:** The parallel combination of multiple electrodes achieves **< 0.5 Ω**, which is mandatory for EHV systems (66 kV and above) as per **IS 3043** and **CEA Safety Regulations 2010**.

2. **Step and Touch Potential Control:** During a high-voltage fault (fault current can be **tens of kA**), the mat **equalizes the potential** across the substation floor, ensuring that the **step potential** (voltage between two feet 1 m apart) and **touch potential** (voltage between hand and foot) remain **below the safe human-body threshold**. Without a mat, a person walking near the fault point could experience lethal voltages.

3. **Current Dissipation:** The large grid area provides a **huge surface for fault current to dissipate** uniformly, preventing localized hot spots and damage.

4. **Lightning Protection:** EHV substations are highly prone to lightning strikes. The mat safely **dissipates stroke currents (up to 200 kA)** into the earth.

5. **Equipment Safety:** Sensitive equipment like transformers, CTs, PTs, and circuit breakers are **referenced to the mat**, ensuring that surge voltages are clipped to safe levels.

**Conclusion (within marks):**
Mat earthing is **non-negotiable** for EHV substations because no other single-electrode method can provide the required combination of **low resistance, equipotential distribution, and step/touch potential safety** demanded by modern grid codes.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU 2024 Scheme Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing "Earthing" with "Grounding":** Both mean the same in practice, but **Earthing = equipment/soil connection (IS/BIS)**, while **Grounding = system neutral/equipment reference (IEEE)**. Use the right term as per context. (Loss: 0.5–1 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting to mention the IS Code:** Always cite **"IS 3043:1987 (Reaffirmed 2014)"** when discussing earthing standards. Examiners reward standard references. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 3. **Drawing a poor diagram:** A neat, **labelled cross-section** of the earthing pit (showing layers of soil, salt, charcoal, pipe/plate) is expected. **Mermaid/Block diagrams in script answers are not accepted — you must hand-draw.** Practice the diagram in your answer booklet. (Loss: 2–3 marks)
> 4. **Wrong units in the formula:** Always use $\rho$ in **Ω·m**, $L$ in **metres**, and $d$ in **metres**. Convert **mm to m** before substituting. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 5. **Omitting the role of charcoal and salt:** This is a **favourite 3-mark sub-question** in KTU exams. If you write only "it increases conductivity," you lose 1–2 marks. Explain **hygroscopic action of salt** and **moisture retention of charcoal** explicitly.
> 6. **Forgetting GEM in modern context:** KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020) emphasises **modern, sustainable practices**. Mentioning **GEM as an alternative to charcoal-salt** is a **bonus mark differentiator**.
> 7. **Using SI units inconsistently:** Always use **Ω for resistance**, **Ω·m for soil resistivity**, **A for current**, **V for voltage**. Mixing "ohms", "Ω", and "ohm" is a presentation error.
> 8. **Not stating the formula's validity range:** The pipe earthing formula is valid only when **L >> d**. Writing the formula without this condition loses a half mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Pin this for last-minute revision):**

**Definitions & Core Concepts:**
- ☐ **Earthing** = Connection of non-current carrying metal parts to earth via a low-resistance electrode.
- **IS 3043:1987** is the Indian Standard for earthing practice.
- Safe touch voltage (dry) = **< 50 V AC**; (wet) = **< 25 V AC**.
- Target earth resistance: **< 0.5 Ω** (EHV), **< 5 Ω** (domestic).

**Pipe Earthing:**
- ☐ **GI pipe, 2.5–4 m long, 40–50 mm dia**, driven vertically.
- **Perforations of 12 mm** on the pipe.
- **Charcoal + salt** layers (15 cm each) around the pipe.
- **Watering funnel** at top.
- **Cheapest, simplest** method; used for domestic earthing.
- **Earth resistance typically 5–25 Ω**.

**Plate Earthing:**
- ☐ **GI plate 60×60 cm (6.35 mm thick)** or **Cu plate 60×60 cm (3.18 mm thick)**.
- Buried **vertically** in a pit, depth **1.5–3 m**.
- **Two bolts** for earth lead connection (redundancy).
- **Inspection chamber** with cast iron cover.
- **Lower resistance (1–5 Ω)** than pipe; preferred for industries.

**Mat Earthing:**
- ☐ **Grid of horizontal conductors** (GI flats 25×3 mm or Cu strips 25×3 mm).
- Buried at **0.5–1.5 m** depth, **grid spacing 3–7 m**.
- **Welded joints** (coated with bitumen for corrosion protection).
- **Multiple vertical electrodes** (4 to 16) at corners and centre.
- **Mandatory for EHV substations** (66 kV and above).
- **Achieves < 0.5 Ω** and equalizes step/touch potential.
- Used in **power stations, EHV substations, generating stations**.

**Ground Enhancing Materials (GEM):**
- ☐ **Bentonite + Graphite + Cement + Hygroscopic salts** mixture.
- **Modern, low-maintenance** alternative to charcoal-salt.
- **Retains moisture for years** — no periodic watering needed.
- **Highly effective in rocky, dry, or sandy soils**.
- **Non-corrosive**, extends electrode lifespan.
- Examples: Marconite, Powerfill, Erico GEM.

**Key Formulas (Must Memorize):**
- ☐ **Pipe Resistance:** $R = \dfrac{\rho}{2\pi L}\!\left[\ln(4L/d) - 1\right]$
- ☐ **Plate Resistance (approx.):** $R \approx \dfrac{\rho}{2A}$
- ☐ **Wenner Method (Soil Resistivity):** $\rho = 2\pi a R$
- ☐ **Parallel Electrodes:** $\dfrac{1}{R_{eq}} = \sum \dfrac{1}{R_i}$

**Key Differences to Remember:**
- ☐ **Pipe vs Plate:** Plate gives lower resistance; Pipe is cheaper.
- ☐ **Plate vs Mat:** Mat uses horizontal grid; Plate is single vertical electrode.
- ☐ **Charcoal-Salt vs GEM:** Charcoal-salt needs maintenance; GEM is maintenance-free.

**Practical Pointers:**
- ☐ Earth electrode should always be **below the permanent moisture level** of the soil.
- ☐ Distance between two earth electrodes should be **at least 2 × the length of the longer electrode** to avoid mutual interference.
- ☐ Use **GI for cheap installations**, **Copper for corrosive/damp environments**.
- ☐ Always provide **two separate earth pits** for **equipment earthing and system (neutral) earthing** in high-voltage installations.
- ☐ Periodically measure earth resistance using a **Earth Tester (Megger type)** — **at least once a year**.

> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — GZESL208 (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Electronic Component Familiarization** is the foundational practical skill in electronics engineering wherein a learner physically identifies, classifies, and interprets the electrical/mechanical specifications of discrete and integrated devices used in the construction of electronic circuits and systems. As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme (GZESL208)**, this experiment trains the student in visually and functionally recognizing components from their physical appearance, package markings, color codes, datasheet parameters, and terminal configurations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** A working knowledge of an electronics workshop begins with the ability to recognize what a component is, what it does, what its rating is, and how it is connected in a circuit — without powering the circuit on.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine an **electronics workbench as a doctor's clinic**. Before a doctor can treat a patient, they must first know the names, functions, and doses of every instrument in their toolkit. A stethoscope, a thermometer, a syringe — each has a specific purpose. Similarly, before a circuit can be built or repaired, an engineer must know:

- **Resistor** = the "flow restrictor" of current (like a narrow pipe).
- **Capacitor** = a tiny rechargeable "battery" that stores energy briefly.
- **Diode** = a "one-way valve" allowing current in only one direction.
- **Transistor** = an "electronic switch/amplifier" (like a tap controlling water flow).
- **Relay** = a "remote-controlled mechanical switch" operated by a small electrical signal.
- **IC (Integrated Circuit)** = a "miniature city" of thousands of components on a single silicon chip.
- **Heat Sink** = the "radiator" that keeps the chip from overheating.

Just as a chef identifies ingredients by sight, smell, and label, an electronics student must identify components by **shape, color code, package, marking, and specification sheet (datasheet)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Truth in Electronics:** *If you cannot identify a component correctly, you cannot design, test, or troubleshoot the circuit that contains it.*

---

## 1.3 Broad Classification of Components (KTU 2024 Framework)

The KTU syllabus classifies electronic components into **six major families** for this experiment:

| S. No. | Category | Nature of Operation | Energy Role |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Passive Components** | Cannot generate energy (Resistor, Capacitor, Inductor) | Store or dissipate energy |
| 2 | **Active Components** | Can generate/amplify energy (Diode, Transistor, IC) | Control or amplify signals |
| 3 | **Electrical / Electromechanical** | Bridge electrical & mechanical (Switch, Relay, Fuse) | Switching & protection |
| 4 | **Wires, Cables & Connectors** | Physical interconnection medium | Signal/power transport |
| 5 | **Crystals & Displays** | Frequency reference & visual output | Timing & indication |
| 6 | **Fasteners & Heat Sinks** | Mechanical mounting & thermal management | Structural & thermal support |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Concept for the Exam:** A component is classified as **Active** if it can *control the flow of current* (e.g., transistor) or *produce energy* (e.g., battery, solar cell). A **Passive** component *cannot* generate power — it can only store, dissipate, or filter it.

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Highlighted

- **Standard Resistor Values** follow the **E-series (E12, E24, E96)** logarithmic scale.
- **Standard Capacitor Values** follow the **IEC 60063** series (E6, E12, E24).
- **Standard Mains Voltage (India)**: **230 V AC, 50 Hz**.
- **Color Code Standard**: **EIA-RS-279** for resistors (4-band and 5-band).
- **Wire Gauge Standard**: **American Wire Gauge (AWG)** and **Standard Wire Gauge (SWG)**.
- **Heat Sink Thermal Resistance (θsa)**: measured in **°C/W (degree Celsius per Watt)**.
- **Crystal Frequency Tolerance**: expressed in **ppm (parts per million)**.
- **Relay Coil Voltage**: commonly **5V, 6V, 9V, 12V, 24V DC** and **230V AC**.
- **Fuse Current Rating**: expressed in **Amperes (A)**, with voltage rating in **Volts (V)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Passive Components — Detailed Analysis

### 2.1.1 Resistor

**Definition:** A passive two-terminal component that opposes the flow of current and dissipates electrical energy as heat, governed by **Ohm's Law**.

**Why it is used:**
- To limit current in a circuit (e.g., current-limiting resistor for an LED).
- To divide voltage (voltage divider network).
- To set the operating point (biasing) of transistors.
- As a pull-up or pull-down in digital circuits.

**How it works:**
When voltage is applied across a resistor, the free electrons collide with the lattice atoms of the resistive material, converting electrical energy into heat. The opposition is quantified as **Resistance (R)** measured in **Ohms (Ω)**.

**Standard Color Code (4-Band Resistor):**

| Band | Color | Digit / Multiplier |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1st | Brown | 1 |
| 2nd | Black | 0 |
| 3rd (Multiplier) | Red | × 100 |
| 4th (Tolerance) | Gold | ± 5% |

**Example:** Brown-Black-Red-Gold = **10 × 100 = 1,000 Ω = 1 kΩ ± 5%**.

---

### 2.1.2 Capacitor

**Definition:** A passive two-terminal component that stores electrical energy in an electric field between two conductive plates separated by a dielectric (insulator).

**Why it is used:**
- Filtering (smoothing in power supplies).
- Coupling & decoupling (blocking DC, passing AC).
- Timing circuits (with resistor → RC time constant).
- Energy storage (camera flash, UPS).

**Key Specification Equation:**

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_r \cdot \varepsilon_0 \cdot A}{d}$$

Where:
- $C$ = Capacitance in **Farads (F)**
- $\varepsilon_r$ = Relative permittivity (dielectric constant) — dimensionless
- $\varepsilon_0$ = Vacuum permittivity = **8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**
- $A$ = Area of overlap of plates in **m²**
- $d$ = Distance between plates (dielectric thickness) in **m**

**Energy Stored:**

$$E = \frac{1}{2} C V^2 \quad \text{[Joules]}$$

---

### 2.1.3 Inductor

**Definition:** A passive component that stores energy in a **magnetic field** when current flows through a coil of wire.

**Key Specification Equation:**

$$V_L = L \frac{di}{dt}$$

$$L = \frac{\mu_r \cdot \mu_0 \cdot N^2 \cdot A}{l}$$

Where:
- $L$ = Inductance in **Henries (H)**
- $N$ = Number of turns (dimensionless)
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area in **m²**
- $l$ = Length of coil in **m**
- $\mu_0$ = Permeability of free space = **4π × 10⁻⁷ H/m**
- $\mu_r$ = Relative permeability of core material

**Energy Stored:**

$$E = \frac{1}{2} L I^2 \quad \text{[Joules]}$$

---

## 2.2 Active Components — Detailed Analysis

### 2.2.1 Diode

**Definition:** A two-terminal semiconductor device that allows current to flow in only one direction (forward bias) and blocks it in the reverse direction.

**Identification Marking:** A single black cylindrical body with a **cathode band** (silver/white stripe) marking the negative terminal.

**Common Types:**
- **1N4007** — General purpose rectifier (1A, 1000V).
- **1N4148** — Signal/fast switching diode.
- **Zener Diode** — Voltage regulator (e.g., 5.1V, 12V).
- **LED** — Light Emitting Diode (anode +, cathode −).
- **Photodiode** — Light-sensitive diode.
- **Schottky Diode** — Low forward drop (~0.3V), fast switching.

**Key Equation (Shockley Diode Equation):**

$$I_D = I_S \left( e^{\frac{V_D}{n V_T}} - 1 \right)$$

Where:
- $I_S$ = Reverse saturation current (typically **10⁻¹² to 10⁻⁶ A**)
- $V_T$ = Thermal voltage = **kT/q ≈ 25.85 mV at 300 K**
- $n$ = Ideality factor (1 to 2)

---

### 2.2.2 Transistor (BJT & FET)

**Definition:** A three-terminal semiconductor device used for **switching** and **amplification**.

**Two Major Families:**

| Type | Full Form | Terminals | Operation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **BJT** | Bipolar Junction Transistor | Emitter (E), Base (B), Collector (C) | Current-controlled |
| **FET** | Field Effect Transistor | Source (S), Gate (G), Drain (D) | Voltage-controlled |

**Common Identification Markings:**
- **BC547** — NPN BJT, TO-92 package.
- **BC557** — PNP BJT, TO-92 package.
- **2N2222** — NPN BJT, TO-18 metal can or TO-92.
- **IRFZ44N** — N-channel MOSFET.
- **TIP31C** — NPN power transistor, TO-220 package.

**Key Equation (BJT Current Relationship):**

$$I_C = \beta \cdot I_B$$

$$I_E = I_C + I_B = (\beta + 1) I_B$$

Where $\beta$ (beta) is the **DC current gain** (typically **50 to 400** for small-signal BJTs).

---

### 2.2.3 Integrated Circuit (IC)

**Definition:** A miniaturized electronic circuit of thousands to billions of transistors, resistors, and capacitors fabricated on a single piece of semiconductor material (usually silicon).

**Identification by Package:**
- **DIP (Dual In-line Package)** — through-hole, 8 to 40 pins.
- **SOIC / SOP** — Surface mount, gull-wing leads.
- **QFP / TQFP** — Quad flat pack, fine-pitch SMD.
- **BGA (Ball Grid Array)** — High pin-count, bottom-side solder balls.

**Common ICs (For Workshop Familiarization):**

| IC Number | Function | Package |
| :---: | :--- | :---: |
| **LM7805** | +5V Voltage Regulator | TO-220 |
| **LM7812** | +12V Voltage Regulator | TO-220 |
| **NE555** | Timer / Oscillator | DIP-8 |
| **LM741** | Op-Amp | DIP-8 |
| **CD4017** | Decade Counter | DIP-16 |
| **CD4047** | Multivibrator | DIP-14 |
| **ATmega328P** | Microcontroller | DIP-28 |

---

## 2.3 Electromechanical Components — Detailed Analysis

### 2.3.1 Switch

**Definition:** A mechanical device that **opens or closes** a circuit manually.

**Common Types (KTU Workshop Focus):**
- **SPST** — Single Pole Single Throw (ON/OFF).
- **SPDT** — Single Pole Double Throw (selector).
- **DPDT** — Double Pole Double Throw.
- **Push-button** — Momentary action.
- **Toggle switch** — Maintained action.
- **Rotary switch** — Multi-position selector.
- **Slide switch** — Used in small electronics.
- **DIP switch** — Bank of tiny switches on PCBs.
- **Micro switch** — Snap-action, used in safety limits.

**Specification:** Rated **voltage (V)** and **current (A)**. Example: **230V/6A** or **12V/2A**.

---

### 2.3.2 Relay

**Definition:** An **electromagnetic switch** in which a small control current through a coil magnetically operates a set of contacts that switch a much larger load circuit.

**Key Specifications:**
- **Coil Voltage**: 5V, 6V, 9V, 12V, 24V DC or 230V AC.
- **Contact Rating**: e.g., **10A at 250V AC / 30V DC**.
- **Contact Configuration**: SPDT, DPDT.
- **Operate Time**: typically **5 to 15 ms**.
- **Release Time**: typically **5 to 10 ms**.

**Common Relays:**
- **5V SPDT relay** — used in Arduino/embedded projects.
- **12V automotive relay** — vehicle electronics.
- **Reed relay** — fast switching, hermetically sealed.
- **Solid State Relay (SSR)** — No moving parts, uses TRIAC/phototriac.

**Working Equation:**

$$F_{magnetic} = \frac{(N \cdot I)^2 \cdot \mu_0 \cdot A}{2 \cdot g^2}$$

Where $N$ is turns, $I$ is coil current, $A$ is core area, $g$ is air gap length.

---

### 2.3.3 Fuse

**Definition:** A **sacrificial protective device** containing a metal wire/strip that **melts and breaks the circuit** when current exceeds a safe limit for a specified time.

**Key Specifications:**
- **Current Rating (A)** — e.g., 1A, 3A, 5A, 10A, 15A.
- **Voltage Rating (V)** — must exceed circuit voltage.
- **Breaking Capacity (kA)** — maximum fault current it can safely interrupt.
- **Speed**: Fast-blow (F) or Slow-blow (T / Time-delay).

**Fuse Selection Equation (for resistive load):**

$$I_{fuse\_rating} = (1.25 \text{ to } 1.5) \times I_{operating}$$

**Common Types:**
- **Cartridge fuse** — glass tube with metal end-caps.
- **HRC (High Rupturing Capacity)** — ceramic body, sand-filled.
- **MCB (Miniature Circuit Breaker)** — resettable thermal/magnetic trip.
- **SMD fuse** — Surface mount chip fuse.
- **Resettable PTC fuse (PolySwitch)** — self-resetting after cooling.

---

## 2.4 Wires, Cables & Connectors

### 2.4.1 Wires & Cables

| Type | Description | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Hook-up wire (22 AWG)** | Single conductor, PVC insulation | Breadboard, prototype |
| **Magnet wire (enameled copper)** | Thin, insulated with varnish | Transformer/inductor winding |
| **Coaxial cable (RG-58, RG-174)** | Center conductor + shield | RF, antenna, video |
| **Twisted pair** | Two conductors twisted | Ethernet, telephone |
| **Ribbon cable** | Flat multi-conductor | Computer IDE/parallel |
| **Optical fiber** | Light pulse transmission | High-speed telecom |

**Wire Gauge Equation (Resistance per unit length):**

$$R = \frac{\rho \cdot l}{A}$$

Where:
- $\rho$ (copper) = **1.68 × 10⁻⁸ Ω·m** at 20°C
- $l$ = length in **m**
- $A$ = cross-sectional area in **m²**

**Current Carrying Capacity (Approximate for copper in free air):**

| AWG | Diameter (mm) | Resistance (Ω/km) | Max Current (A) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 24 | 0.51 | 84.2 | 3.5 |
| 22 | 0.64 | 52.5 | 5.0 |
| 20 | 0.81 | 33.3 | 7.5 |
| 18 | 1.02 | 21.0 | 10.0 |
| 16 | 1.29 | 13.2 | 13.0 |
| 14 | 1.63 | 8.3 | 17.0 |

---

### 2.4.2 Connectors

| Connector | Pin/Pole Count | Typical Application |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **2-pin screw terminal** | 2 | Power input to PCB |
| **Berg strip (SIP/DIP header)** | 2 to 40 | PCB-to-PCB, IDC |
| **JST connector (XH, PH, SH)** | 2 to 12 | LiPo battery, Li-ion pack |
| **USB (Type-A, Type-B, Mini, Micro, Type-C)** | 4 to 24 | Data + power |
| **HDMI** | 19 | Audio/Video |
| **DB9 / DB15 / DB25** | 9 / 15 / 25 | Serial RS-232, VGA |
| **RJ45** | 8 | Ethernet |
| **RCA** | 1 (coaxial) | Audio/Video |
| **Barrel jack (DC power)** | 2 | Adapter to equipment |
| **Banana plug / BNC** | 1 | Test instruments |
| **IC base / socket** | 8 to 40 | DIP IC mounting |

---

## 2.5 Crystals & Displays

### 2.5.1 Crystal Oscillator

**Definition:** A piezoelectric resonator that produces a highly stable clock frequency, used as a time/frequency reference in microcontrollers, communication systems, and digital watches.

**Key Specification Equation:**

$$f = \frac{1}{2 \pi \sqrt{L \cdot C}}$$

(Equivalent series RLC model of the crystal)

**Common Frequencies:** **32.768 kHz** (watch crystal), **4 MHz, 8 MHz, 16 MHz, 20 MHz, 25 MHz** (microcontroller clocks).

**Specifications to Identify:**
- Frequency (e.g., **16.000 MHz**).
- Load capacitance (e.g., **20 pF**).
- Frequency tolerance (e.g., **±20 ppm**).
- Package (HC-49, HC-49S, SMD 5032/3225).

### 2.5.2 Displays

| Display Type | Description | Drive Voltage | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **LED (single)** | Single indicator | 1.8V to 3.2V | Status indicator |
| **7-Segment LED** | Numeric display | 2V per segment | Counter, digital clock |
| **16×2 LCD (HD44780)** | Alphanumeric | 5V | Arduino/microcontroller display |
| **OLED 0.96"** | 128×64 pixels | 3.3V / 5V | IoT, wearables |
| **TFT LCD (1.8"/2.4"/3.5")** | Full color | 3.3V / 5V | Touch screen GUIs |
| **7-Segment (common anode / cathode)** | Numeric, multi-digit | 2V/segment | Digital meter |
| **Dot matrix (5×7, 8×8)** | Alphanumeric/graphic | 5V | Scrolling text |

**Standard LED Current Limiting Resistor Equation:**

$$R = \frac{V_{supply} - V_F}{I_F}$$

For a red LED: $V_F = 1.8V$, $I_F = 20$ mA, $V_{supply} = 5V$:

$$R = \frac{5 - 1.8}{0.020} = 160 \, \Omega \;\; (\text{use standard } 220 \, \Omega)$$

### 2.6.2 Heat Sink

**Definition:** A passive thermal management component (usually aluminum) that increases the surface area available for heat dissipation from a hot component (transistor, regulator IC, power module).

**Key Thermal Equation:**

$$\theta_{JA} = \theta_{JC} + \theta_{CS} + \theta_{SA}$$

Where:
- $\theta_{JA}$ = Junction-to-ambient thermal resistance (°C/W)
- $\theta_{JC}$ = Junction-to-case thermal resistance (°C/W)
- $\theta_{CS}$ = Case-to-sink thermal resistance (°C/W)
- $\theta_{SA}$ = Sink-to-ambient thermal resistance (°C/W)

**Heat Dissipated Equation:**

$$P_D = \frac{T_J - T_A}{\theta_{JA}}$$

**Selection Rule:** For a regulator dissipating **$P$** watts, the maximum allowable thermal resistance of the heat sink is:

$$\theta_{SA} \leq \frac{T_{J(max)} - T_A}{P_D} - \theta_{JC} - \theta_{CS}$$

**Common Heat Sink Types:**
- **Extruded aluminum fin** — for TO-220, TO-3.
- **Pin-fin heat sink** — higher surface area.
- **Bonded fin** — high-power applications.
- **Active heat sink** — with fan (forced air cooling).
