# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Worked Example 1: Resistor Color Code Decoding

**Problem:** Decode the value of a resistor with the following color bands:
**Yellow – Violet – Orange – Gold**

**Step 1 — Identify the color table reference:**

| Color | Digit | Multiplier (×10ⁿ) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Black | 0 | 10⁰ = 1 |
| Brown | 1 | 10¹ = 10 |
| Red | 2 | 10² = 100 |
| Orange | 3 | 10³ = 1,000 |
| Yellow | 4 | 10⁴ = 10,000 |
| Green | 5 | 10⁵ |
| Blue | 6 | 10⁶ |
| Violet | 7 | 10⁷ |
| Grey | 8 | 10⁸ |
| White | 9 | 10⁹ |
| Gold | — | 10⁻¹ = 0.1 |
| Silver | — | 10⁻² = 0.01 |

**Step 2 — Apply the 4-band logic:**

The 1st band gives the first significant digit, the 2nd band gives the second significant digit, the 3rd band gives the multiplier, and the 4th band gives the tolerance.

**Step 3 — Substitute the colors:**

$$\text{1st band: Yellow} = 4$$
$$\text{2nd band: Violet} = 7$$
$$\text{3rd band: Orange} = \times 10^3$$
$$\text{4th band: Gold} = \pm 5\%$$

**Step 4 — Compute the value:**

$$R = (4 \times 10 + 7) \times 10^3 = 47 \times 10^3 \, \Omega$$

$$R = 47{,}000 \, \Omega = 47 \, k\Omega \pm 5\%$$

**Step 5 — Compute tolerance bounds:**

$$R_{min} = 47{,}000 \times 0.95 = 44{,}650 \, \Omega$$

$$R_{max} = 47{,}000 \times 1.05 = 49{,}350 \, \Omega$$

**Final Answer:** The resistor is **47 kΩ ± 5%** (44.65 kΩ to 49.35 kΩ).

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Worked Example 2: LED Current-Limiting Resistor Design

**Problem:** A red LED ($V_F = 1.8$ V, $I_F = 20$ mA) is to be connected to a 5V DC supply. Calculate the series resistor value.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation:**

$$R = \frac{V_{supply} - V_F}{I_F}$$

**Step 2 — List the known values:**

$$V_{supply} = 5 \, \text{V}, \quad V_F = 1.8 \, \text{V}, \quad I_F = 20 \, \text{mA} = 0.020 \, \text{A}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and compute:**

$$R = \frac{5 - 1.8}{0.020} = \frac{3.2}{0.020}$$

$$R = 160 \, \Omega$$

**Step 4 — Choose the nearest standard E24 value:**

Standard E24 values near 160 Ω: **150 Ω** and **180 Ω**.

For safety (slightly lower current → longer LED life), choose **180 Ω**.

**Step 5 — Verify actual current with 180 Ω:**

$$I_{actual} = \frac{5 - 1.8}{180} = 17.8 \, \text{mA}$$

This is below 20 mA — **safe and within spec**.

**Step 6 — Compute power dissipation in the resistor:**

$$P_R = I^2 R = (0.0178)^2 \times 180 = 0.057 \, \text{W}$$

A standard **¼ W (0.25 W)** resistor is more than sufficient.

**Final Answer:** Use **R = 180 Ω, ¼ W**. The LED will draw **17.8 mA** safely.

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Worked Example 3: Heat Sink Selection for LM7805

**Problem:** A **LM7805** voltage regulator (TO-220 package) drops **7 V** (input 12V, output 5V) and supplies a load current of **1 A**. The ambient temperature is **45°C**. Determine if a heat sink is needed, and if so, what is the maximum allowable $\theta_{SA}$.

**Step 1 — Compute the power dissipated in the regulator:**

$$P_D = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \times I_{load}$$

$$P_D = (12 - 5) \times 1 = 7 \, \text{W}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the regulator's thermal limits (from datasheet):**

- Maximum junction temperature: $T_{J(max)} = 125^\circ \text{C}$
- Junction-to-case thermal resistance: $\theta_{JC} = 5^\circ \text{C/W}$
- Case-to-sink thermal resistance (with mica insulator + thermal paste): $\theta_{CS} = 1^\circ \text{C/W}$

**Step 3 — Compute junction-to-ambient thermal resistance without a heat sink:**

The TO-220 alone has $\theta_{JA} \approx 50^\circ \text{C/W}$.

$$T_J = T_A + P_D \cdot \theta_{JA} = 45 + 7 \times 50 = 395^\circ \text{C}$$

This is **far above** 125°C — **the regulator will burn out without a heat sink**.

**Step 4 — Compute the maximum allowable sink-to-ambient thermal resistance:**

$$\theta_{SA} \leq \frac{T_{J(max)} - T_A}{P_D} - \theta_{JC} - \theta_{CS}$$

$$\theta_{SA} \leq \frac{125 - 45}{7} - 5 - 1$$

$$\theta_{SA} \leq \frac{80}{7} - 6 = 11.43 - 6$$

$$\theta_{SA} \leq 5.43^\circ \text{C/W}$$

**Step 5 — Select a standard heat sink:**

Choose an extruded aluminum heat sink rated at **θSA ≤ 5°C/W** (e.g., a 50 mm finned heat sink).

**Step 6 — Verify the new junction temperature:**

$$T_J = T_A + P_D (\theta_{JC} + \theta_{CS} + \theta_{SA})$$

$$T_J = 45 + 7 \times (5 + 1 + 5) = 45 + 77 = 122^\circ \text{C}$$

This is below the 125°C limit — **safe operation**.

**Final Answer:** A heat sink with **θSA ≤ 5.43°C/W** is required. An extruded aluminum heat sink of 50 mm length is suitable.

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Worked Example 4: Crystal Oscillator Frequency Check

**Problem:** A microcontroller board uses a **16.000 MHz** crystal with a tolerance of **±20 ppm**. What is the actual frequency range?

**Step 1 — Apply the ppm definition:**

$$\text{Tolerance in Hz} = \text{Frequency} \times \frac{\text{ppm}}{10^6}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the deviation:**

$$\Delta f = 16{,}000{,}000 \times \frac{20}{1{,}000{,}000} = 320 \, \text{Hz}$$

**Step 3 — Express the frequency range:**

$$f_{min} = 16{,}000{,}000 - 320 = 15{,}999{,}680 \, \text{Hz}$$

$$f_{max} = 16{,}000{,}000 + 320 = 16{,}000{,}320 \, \text{Hz}$$

**Final Answer:** The crystal frequency can range from **15,999,680 Hz to 16,000,320 Hz**.

## 5.1 PART A — 3 Mark Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**List any six passive electronic components and state one function of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| S. No. | Component | Function |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Resistor** | Limits current and divides voltage |
| 2 | **Capacitor** | Stores charge and filters AC ripple |
| 3 | **Inductor** | Stores energy in magnetic field, filters |
| 4 | **Potentiometer** | Variable voltage divider |
| 5 | **Transformer** | Steps up/step down AC voltage |
| 6 | **Crystal** | Provides stable clock reference |

**Conclusion:** Passive components cannot generate energy — they can only store, dissipate, or filter it.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Active and Passive components with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Active Components | Passive Components |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Definition** | Can generate, control, or amplify energy | Can only store, dissipate, or filter energy |
| **Power source** | Requires external supply to function | Does not need external power (responds to signal) |
| **Gain** | Provides gain (>1) | No gain (≤1) |
| **Examples** | Diode, Transistor, IC, Op-amp, SCR | Resistor, Capacitor, Inductor, Transformer |
| **Directionality** | Often directional (anode/cathode, E/B/C) | Generally non-directional |
| **Typical package** | TO-220, DIP, SOIC, BGA | Axial, radial, SMD chip |

**Conclusion:** Active components are the "thinking" elements; passive components are the "supporting" elements of any circuit.

## 5.2 PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working of a relay**. List its important specifications.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A red LED ($V_F = 2V$, $I_F = 15$ mA) is connected to a **9V supply** through a series resistor. **(i)** Calculate the resistor value. **(ii)** Choose the nearest standard E24 value. **(iii)** Verify the actual current and power dissipated.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — Relay Construction and Specifications (7 Marks)**

**Definition (1 Mark):** A **relay** is an electromechanical switch in which a small control current through an electromagnet (coil) physically moves a set of contacts to switch a higher-power circuit.

**Construction Diagram (2 Marks):**

```
        +------------------+
        |   ENCLOSURE      |
        |                  |
   Coil |---CCCC-----------|    Core
        |                  |    (iron)
        |                  |
   +5V  +                  +  Contact1 (Common)
        |                  |---\
        |   ARMATURE       |    \  Contact2 (NO)
        |   (movable)      |     o
        |                  |    /
        |                  |---/  Contact3 (NC)
        |                  |
   GND  +                  +
        |                  |
        +------------------+
```

**Working Explanation (2 Marks):**

1. When **DC voltage is applied** to the coil, current flows through the turns of wire wound around an iron core.
2. The current **magnetizes the iron core**, creating a magnetic field.
3. The magnetic field **pulls the armature** (a movable iron plate) toward the core against a spring.
4. This movement **closes the Normally Open (NO) contact** and **opens the Normally Closed (NC) contact**.
5. The high-power load circuit is now **switched ON** through the common terminal.
6. When the coil is **de-energized**, the spring pulls the armature back, and the contacts return to their default position.

**Important Specifications (2 Marks):**

| Specification | Typical Value | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Coil Voltage** | 5V, 6V, 9V, 12V, 24V DC | Voltage needed to energize coil |
| **Coil Resistance** | 50–700 Ω | DC resistance of coil |
| **Contact Rating** | 10A, 250V AC / 30V DC | Maximum load current/voltage |
| **Contact Configuration** | SPDT, DPDT | Number of poles and throws |
| **Operate Time** | 5–15 ms | Delay from coil energize to contact close |
| **Release Time** | 5–10 ms | Delay from coil de-energize to contact open |
| **Mechanical Life** | 10⁷ operations | Number of switching cycles |
| **Electrical Life** | 10⁵ operations | With rated load |

---

#### **Part (b) — LED Resistor Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Given:** $V_F = 2V$, $I_F = 15$ mA, $V_{supply} = 9V$.

**(i) Calculate the resistor value (3 Marks):**

$$R = \frac{V_{supply} - V_F}{I_F} = \frac{9 - 2}{0.015} = \frac{7}{0.015}$$

$$R = 466.67 \, \Omega$$

**(ii) Choose the nearest standard E24 value (2 Marks):**

The standard E24 values near 467 Ω are **470 Ω** and **430 Ω**.

Choosing the **higher** value (470 Ω) for a slightly lower LED current → **longer LED life** → **R = 470 Ω**.

**(iii) Verify the actual current and power (2 Marks):**

$$I_{actual} = \frac{9 - 2}{470} = 14.89 \, \text{mA}$$

This is **less than 15 mA** — within safe operating range.

$$P_R = I^2 R = (0.01489)^2 \times 470 = 0.104 \, \text{W}$$

A standard **¼ W (0.25 W)** resistor is adequate.

**Final Answer:** Use **R = 470 Ω, ¼ W**. The LED will draw **14.89 mA**.

---

### **Question B: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]**
**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **4-band resistor color code** with a suitable example. A resistor has color bands **Brown, Black, Red, Silver**. Determine its value and tolerance.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, describe the **function of a heat sink in electronic circuits**. An LM7805 dissipates 6W in a 35°C ambient. Junction-to-case θJC = 5°C/W. Determine the maximum allowable heat-sink thermal resistance θSA if the maximum junction temperature is 125°C. Assume θCS = 1°C/W.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — Resistor Color Code (7 Marks)**

**Color Code Table (3 Marks):**

| Band | Color | Digit | Multiplier | Tolerance |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1st | Brown | 1 | — | — |
| 2nd | Black | 0 | — | — |
| 3rd | Red | — | × 10² (= 100) | — |
| 4th | Silver | — | — | ± 10% |

**Logic (2 Marks):**
- 1st band = 1st significant digit.
- 2nd band = 2nd significant digit.
- 3rd band = multiplier (power of 10).
- 4th band = tolerance (acceptable deviation from nominal).

**Calculation (2 Marks):**

$$R = (1 \times 10 + 0) \times 100 = 10 \times 100 = 1000 \, \Omega = 1 \, k\Omega \pm 10\%$$

**Tolerance range:**

$$R_{min} = 1000 \times 0.90 = 900 \, \Omega$$

$$R_{max} = 1000 \times 1.10 = 1100 \, \Omega$$

**Final Answer:** **1 kΩ ± 10%** (actual range: 900 Ω to 1100 Ω).

---

#### **Part (b) — Heat Sink Calculation (7 Marks)**

**Function of Heat Sink (3 Marks):**

A heat sink is a passive aluminum (or copper) component with extended fins that:
1. **Increases surface area** available for heat radiation and convection.
2. **Lowers thermal resistance** from junction to ambient.
3. **Prevents thermal runaway** of power semiconductors.
4. **Extends component life** by keeping junction temperature below rated maximum.

**Thermal Path Diagram (1 Mark):**

```
Junction (Tj) → θJC → Case (Tc) → θCS → Heat Sink (Ts) → θSA → Ambient (Ta)
```

**Given Data (1 Mark):**
- $P_D = 6$ W
- $T_A = 35°C$
- $T_{J(max)} = 125°C$
- $\theta_{JC} = 5°C/W$
- $\theta_{CS} = 1°C/W$

**Calculation (2 Marks):**

$$\theta_{SA} \leq \frac{T_{J(max)} - T_A}{P_D} - \theta_{JC} - \theta_{CS}$$

$$\theta_{SA} \leq \frac{125 - 35}{6} - 5 - 1 = \frac{90}{6} - 6$$

$$\theta_{SA} \leq 15 - 6 = 9°C/W$$

**Final Answer:** A heat sink with **θSA ≤ 9°C/W** is required. A standard 30 mm extruded aluminum heat sink will meet this requirement.

**Verification:**

$$T_J = 35 + 6 \times (5 + 1 + 9) = 35 + 6 \times 15 = 35 + 90 = 125°C$$

This matches the maximum allowed — **safe operation confirmed**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Mixing up color-code digits and multiplier** (e.g., treating Red as a digit 2 instead of ×100).
> 2. **Forgetting to convert mA to A** in LED resistor formula — leads to 1000× error.
> 3. **Wrong tolerance assignment** — Silver is ±10%, Gold is ±5%, NOT the other way around.
> 4. **In heat sink problems, students often forget θCS** (case-to-sink resistance) and get incorrect θSA values.
> 5. **Confusing Active and Passive** — Crystal is **active** (it has gain, drives oscillators), NOT passive.
> 6. **Wrong relay pin identification** — Students often swap Common and NO terminals in viva.
> 7. **Confusing common-anode vs common-cathode** 7-segment displays — a recurring error in digital experiments.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must Know Before Exam):**

### **A. Passive Components**
- Resistor: Unit **Ω**, identified by **color code (4-band / 5-band)**.
- Capacitor: Unit **F**, types include **ceramic, electrolytic, tantalum, film**.
- Inductor: Unit **H**, identified by **wire wound coil** (with or without core).
- Color code mnemonic: "**B**etter **B**e **R**ight **O**r **Y**our **G**reat **B**ig **V**entures **G**o **W**rong" (0–9).
- Gold = ×0.1, Silver = ×0.01 multiplier.
- Gold = ±5% tolerance, Silver = ±10% tolerance.

### **B. Active Components**
- Diode has **cathode band** marking.
- Forward voltage: Si diode ≈ **0.7V**, Germanium ≈ **0.3V**, Schottky ≈ **0.3V**, Red LED ≈ **1.8V**, Blue/White LED ≈ **3.0–3.2V**.
- BJT types: **NPN (e.g., BC547)** and **PNP (e.g., BC557)**.
- IC identification by **notch, dot, or laser mark** for pin 1.
- **LM7805** is a +5V regulator, **LM7905** is a -5V regulator.
- **NE555** is a timer IC (used in astable, monostable, bistable modes).

### **C. Electromechanical**
- Relay: **Coil + Contacts**. SPDT = 1 pole, 2 throws (NO and NC).
- Switch: SPST (simple ON/OFF), SPDT (selector), DPDT (reverse polarity).
- Fuse rating rule: $I_{fuse} = 1.25 \times I_{load}$ (resistive), $1.5 \times I_{load}$ (inductive).

### **D. Wires & Connectors**
- Standard hook-up wire: **22 AWG** for breadboard.
- AWG table: **lower AWG = thicker wire = more current**.
- USB-C is **24-pin** reversible. USB-A is **4-pin**.
- RJ45 has **8 pins** (Ethernet). DB9 has **9 pins** (RS-232).

### **E. Crystals & Displays**
- **32.768 kHz** crystal for RTC circuits.
- **16 MHz** crystal for Arduino/AVR.
- 7-segment displays: **common anode** (sink current) or **common cathode** (source current).
- **16×2 LCD** uses the **HD44780** controller, 16 characters × 2 lines.

### **F. Fasteners & Heat Sinks**
- Standard PCB mounting: **M3 screws** with **M3 nuts** and **M3 washers**.
- Use **Nylon standoffs** to avoid short circuits on PCB.
- **Cable ties (zip ties)** are **Nylon 66**, rated for **temperature up to 85°C** (standard) or **120°C** (heat-stabilized).
- Heat sink material: **Aluminum 6063** (most common) or **copper** (better conductivity, heavier).
- Thermal paste (thermal compound) reduces **θCS** from ~1°C/W to ~0.1°C/W.
- Always check the **datasheet** of LM7805: $T_{J(max)} = 125°C$, $\theta_{JC} = 5°C/W$ (TO-220).

### **G. Universal Workshop Formulas to Memorize**
- $V = IR$ (Ohm's Law)
- $E = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$ (Capacitor energy)
- $E = \tfrac{1}{2}LI^2$ (Inductor energy)
- $R = (V_S - V_F)/I_F$ (LED resistor)
- $I_C = \beta I_B$ (BJT)
- $\theta_{SA} \le (T_J - T_A)/P_D - \theta_{JC} - \theta_{CS}$ (Heat sink)

### **H. Common Viva Questions**
1. *"How do you identify a resistor value?"* → **Color code**.
2. *"What is the difference between 1N4007 and 1N4148?"* → **1N4007 is a power rectifier (1A), 1N4148 is a fast signal diode**.
3. *"Why are heat sinks made of aluminum and not steel?"* → **Aluminum has higher thermal conductivity (205 W/m·K) and is lightweight**.
4. *"Why is a fuse wire made of tin or zinc, not copper?"* → **Tin/zinc have lower melting points (~232°C/420°C) than copper (1085°C), so they melt first and break the circuit**.
5. *"What is the function of a decoupling capacitor?"* → **It filters out high-frequency noise from the power supply line at each IC**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** KTU 2024 Scheme values **concept clarity and practical identification** over rote learning. Always remember to mention **both** the *identification method* (visual/marking) AND the *key specification* (electrical rating) for full marks. For example, "A 1N4007 is identified by a black cylindrical body with a silver cathode band; it is a silicon rectifier diode rated at **1 A forward current and 1000 V reverse voltage**."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Drawing of electronic circuit diagrams using BIS/IEEE symbols and interpret data sheets of discrete components and ICs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZESL208 - BASIC ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Drawing of electronic circuit diagrams using BIS/IEEE symbols and interpret data sheets of discrete components and ICs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Electronic Circuit Symbols** are standardized graphical notations used to represent electrical and electronic components in schematic diagrams. Two principal standards govern their use globally:

- **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards) Symbols** — Defined under the **IS 2032** series (Indian Standard for Graphical Symbols used in Electro-technology), this is the official Indian standard adopted for circuit representation in Indian engineering curricula and IS-compliant documentation.
- **IEEE (Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers) Symbols** — Defined under **IEEE Std 315 / ANSI Y32.2** (combined) and **IEEE Std 91** for binary logic elements, this is the internationally adopted convention for schematic representation in academic, research, and industrial design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Schematic Diagram** is *not* a physical layout drawing. It is a logical representation showing how components are interconnected electrically, irrespective of their physical size or location on a PCB.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of circuit symbols as a **universal language for engineers**, much like **musical notation** is for musicians.

- A musician anywhere in the world can read a treble clef and know to play a particular note.
- Similarly, an engineer in Kerala, Tokyo, or California can read a zig-zag line (resistor symbol) and know it represents a 1 kΩ carbon-film resistor.

Just as standardized road signs let drivers navigate any country without knowing the local language, **standardized circuit symbols** let any electronics engineer interpret, debug, and design circuits without ambiguity.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Two Standards?** BIS and IEEE symbols often look similar but have minor differences (e.g., the IEEE resistor is a zig-zag, while IEC/BIS often prefers a hollow rectangle). Both are accepted; the rule is *consistency within one schematic*.

### 1.3 What is a Datasheet?

A **Datasheet (Data Sheet)** is a technical document published by a component manufacturer (e.g., Texas Instruments, ON Semiconductor, STMicroelectronics) that provides complete electrical, mechanical, thermal, and application information about a discrete component or Integrated Circuit (IC).

> [!NOTE]
> A typical datasheet contains: **Absolute Maximum Ratings**, **Electrical Characteristics**, **Pin Configuration**, **Functional Block Diagram**, **Typical Application Circuit**, and **Package Dimensions**.

---

## 2. Standards Bodies and Governing Documents

| Standard | Governing Body | Scope | KTU Relevance |
|----------|---------------|-------|---------------|
| **IS 2032 (Part I–XIV)** | **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS)** | Graphical symbols for electrical/electronic components | Mandatory reference for Indian engineering practice |
| **IEEE Std 315-1975** | **Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers** | American National Standard for graphic symbols | Widely used globally; matches KTU textbooks |
| **IEEE Std 91-1984** | IEEE | Binary logic element symbols (AND, OR, NOT gates) | Used in digital electronics schematics |
| **IEC 60617** | **International Electrotechnical Commission** | International standard for graphical symbols | Foundational; BIS and IEEE derive from it |

---

## 3. Categorization of Components

For systematic study, components are classified into:

1. **Passive Components** — Resistors, Capacitors, Inductors
2. **Active Discrete Components** — Diodes, Transistors (BJT, FET, MOSFET)
3. **Sources** — Independent and Dependent voltage/current sources
4. **Integrated Circuits (ICs)** — Op-Amps, 555 Timer, Logic Gates, Regulators
5. **Miscellaneous** — Switches, Relays, Lamps, Fuses, Meters
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 4. Deep Theoretical Analysis — Standard Symbols Reference

### 4.1 Passive Component Symbols

#### 4.1.1 Resistor (IEEE Symbol: Zig-Zag / IEC Symbol: Rectangle)

The **Resistor** is a two-terminal passive element that opposes current flow. Its symbol in IEEE is a **zig-zag line**, while in BIS/IEC it is a **hollow rectangle**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Variable Resistor (Rheostat / Potentiometer)** is shown with an arrow through it. **LDR** (Light Dependent Resistor) has two arrows pointing toward it. **Thermistor** has a slash through the rectangle with a 't' or temperature indicator.

#### 4.1.2 Capacitor

A **Capacitor** stores energy in an electric field. Two parallel lines represent the plates. A **polarized (electrolytic) capacitor** has one curved plate and a '+' sign; a **non-polarized** has two straight lines.

- **Variable Capacitor:** Arrow through the plates
- **Trimmer Capacitor:** Arrow with strikethrough

#### 4.1.3 Inductor

An **Inductor** stores energy in a magnetic field. Represented as a series of loops or "humps." An **iron-core inductor** has parallel lines next to the loops; an **air-core** does not.

- **Variable Inductor:** Arrow through the coils

### 4.2 Semiconductor Diode Symbols

| Component | Symbol Description | Polarity Indicator |
|-----------|-------------------|--------------------|
| **PN Junction Diode** | Triangle pointing to a bar | Anode (A), Cathode (K) |
| **Zener Diode** | Diode with bent cathode line | Used for voltage regulation |
| **Light Emitting Diode (LED)** | Diode with two outward arrows | Anode (long lead), Cathode (short lead) |
| **Photodiode** | Diode with two inward arrows | Reverse biased typically |
| **Schottky Diode** | Diode with S-shaped cathode | Fast switching applications |

### 4.3 Transistor Symbols

**BJT (Bipolar Junction Transistor):**
- **NPN:** Arrow on emitter pointing **outward** (Not Pointing iN)
- **PNP:** Arrow on emitter pointing **inward** (Pointing iN Permanently)

**FET Family:**
- **JFET (N-channel):** Arrow on source pointing inward
- **MOSFET (N-channel, Enhancement):** Broken line for channel, arrow inward on source
- **MOSFET (P-channel):** Arrow outward on source

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Mnemonic: **"Arrow follows current"** for diodes. For BJTs, the **emitter arrow follows conventional current** in the direction of NPN current flow.

### 4.4 Integrated Circuit (IC) Symbols

ICs are represented as a **rectangular block** with numbered pins along the sides. The most common representation in KTU workshop experiments:

- **Op-Amp 741:** Triangle with two inputs (+, −), one output, two power supply pins (V+, V−)
- **555 Timer:** Rectangle with 8 pins (GND, Trigger, Output, Reset, Control Voltage, Threshold, Discharge, VCC)
- **Voltage Regulator 78xx:** Rectangle with 3 pins (Input, Ground, Output)

### 4.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Key Identification Parameters

The following table summarizes the **essential numerical parameters** a student must extract from a datasheet, with their units and physical meaning.

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Meaning |
|-----------|--------|------|---------|
| **Resistance** | $R$ | $\Omega$ (Ohm) | Opposition to DC current |
| **Capacitance** | $C$ | F (Farad) | Charge storage per volt |
| **Inductance** | $L$ | H (Henry) | Magnetic flux per ampere |
| **Forward Voltage** | $V_F$ | V (Volt) | Voltage drop across diode when forward biased |
| **Reverse Breakdown Voltage** | $V_{BR}$ or $V_Z$ | V | Voltage at which Zener conducts in reverse |
| **Collector-Emitter Voltage** | $V_{CEO}$ | V | Maximum allowable C-E voltage (BJT) |
| **Continuous Collector Current** | $I_C$ | A | Max DC current through collector |
| **DC Current Gain** | $h_{FE}$ or $\beta$ | dimensionless | $I_C \div I_B$ |
| **Power Dissipation** | $P_D$ | W (Watt) | Max heat the device can safely dissipate |
| **Operating Temperature** | $T_{op}$ | $^\circ$C | Safe ambient temperature range |
| **Supply Voltage (Op-Amp/IC)** | $V_{CC}$, $V_{EE}$ | V | Power supply rail voltages |
| **Input Offset Voltage** | $V_{IO}$ | mV or $\mu$V | Differential DC error at op-amp input |
| **Slew Rate** | $SR$ | $\text{V} \div \mu\text{s}$ | Max rate of output voltage change |
| **Gain-Bandwidth Product** | $GBW$ | MHz | Op-amp bandwidth at unity gain |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** In workshop practice, students rarely use the absolute maximum ratings. Instead, designers operate devices at **70% derated values** to ensure reliability and thermal headroom.

### 4.6 Reading a Datasheet — The "Five-Box" Method

Every datasheet, whether for a 1N4007 diode or a TL081 op-amp, follows a similar structure. Locating these five boxes covers 90% of practical needs:

1. **Absolute Maximum Ratings** — What destroys the device.
2. **Electrical Characteristics** — How it behaves in normal operation.
3. **Pin Configuration / Functional Diagram** — How to wire it.
4. **Typical Performance Curves** — How it behaves across temperature/frequency.
5. **Typical Application Circuit** — How the manufacturer recommends using it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "interpret a datasheet," always begin by stating **(a) part number, (b) manufacturer, (c) function, (d) key absolute maximum rating, (e) typical application.**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 5. Step-by-Step Analytical Procedures

### 5.1 Procedure to Draw a Circuit Schematic (BIS/IEEE Compliant)

**Step 1 — Identify the Circuit Topology**
Determine whether the circuit is series, parallel, or a combination. List all required components in a Component Specification Table.

**Step 2 — Choose the Symbol Standard**
For KTU submissions, **IEEE symbols are universally accepted**. For IS-compliant industry work, use **IS 2032** (rectangular IEC-style symbols). Consistency is the rule.

**Step 3 — Lay Out the Schematic Logically**
- Place **inputs on the left**, **outputs on the right**
- Place **positive supply at top**, **ground/negative at bottom**
- Avoid crossing wires; use the **"jumper" dot convention** (a filled dot at every electrical connection; no dot means no connection — the "hop-over" arc indicates wires crossing without contact)

**Step 4 — Label Every Component**
Use the standard convention: **R1, R2, C1, C2, D1, Q1, U1, etc.** Place the reference designator *next to* the symbol and the value *below* it (e.g., R1 = 1 kΩ, C1 = 10 µF).

**Step 5 — Add Power, Ground, and Signal Labels**
Mark **VCC = +5V, GND, IN, OUT** clearly. Every IC must show its supply pin connections even if not drawn.

**Step 6 — Add a Title Block and Revision Stamp**
Bottom-right corner should contain: **Title, Author, Date, Sheet Number, Revision**.

### 5.2 Procedure to Interpret a Datasheet

**Step 1 — Read the Header Block**
Identify: Part Number (e.g., **1N4007**), Manufacturer (**ON Semiconductor**), Package type (**DO-41**), Function (**General Purpose Rectifier Diode**).

**Step 2 — Extract Absolute Maximum Ratings**
For 1N4007, this typically includes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{RRM} &= 1000 \text{ V} \quad \text{(Peak Repetitive Reverse Voltage)} \\
I_F(AV) &= 1.0 \text{ A} \quad \text{(Average Rectified Forward Current)} \\
I_{FSM} &= 30 \text{ A} \quad \text{(Non-Repetitive Peak Forward Surge Current)} \\
P_D &= 3.0 \text{ W} \quad \text{(Power Dissipation at } T_A = 25^\circ\text{C)} \\
T_J &= -55 \text{ to } +175 \text{ } ^\circ\text{C}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Logic:** $V_{RRM} = 1000$ V means the diode can block up to 1000 V in reverse without avalanche breakdown. Exceeding this destroys the junction.

**Step 3 — Read Electrical Characteristics**
For 1N4007 at $I_F = 1$ A and $T_A = 25^\circ$C:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_F &= 1.1 \text{ V (typical), max 1.6 V} \\
I_R &= 5.0 \text{ }\mu\text{A (max) at } V_R = 1000 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation Logic:** When forward biased with 1 A flowing, the diode drops 1.1 V (typical). The remaining voltage in the circuit equation $V_S = V_F + V_R$ is dropped across other series elements.

**Step 4 — Examine the Pin Configuration**
For a TO-220 voltage regulator **LM7805**:

- **Pin 1:** Input (typically 7–35 V DC)
- **Pin 2:** Ground
- **Pin 3:** Output (regulated 5 V DC)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often swap input and output on three-terminal regulators. This is a destructive fault. The **LM7805 input must always be higher than 5 V** (typically ≥ 7 V) to maintain regulation.

**Step 5 — Cross-Reference with Typical Application Circuit**
The datasheet's "Typical Application" diagram shows the manufacturer-recommended external components (input/output bypass capacitors, feedback resistors). These should be followed unless the application specifically requires otherwise.

### 5.3 Worked Analytical Example — Designing a 5V Power Supply Section

Given a 230 V AC mains input, design a rectifier and regulator section, and identify each component's datasheet parameters.

**Step 1 — Transformer**
Standard step-down transformer: **230 V AC primary → 9 V AC secondary, 500 mA rating**.

**Step 2 — Bridge Rectifier**
Use four **1N4007** diodes in bridge configuration.

From the datasheet:
- $V_F = 1.1$ V per diode → 2 diodes conduct in series each half-cycle
- Total diode drop per half-cycle = $2 \times 1.1 = 2.2$ V
- Peak DC output ≈ $9 \times \sqrt{2} - 2.2 \approx 10.53$ V

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{DC(peak)} &= V_{sec} \times \sqrt{2} - 2V_F \\
&= 9 \times 1.414 - 2.2 \\
&= 12.726 - 2.2 \\
&= 10.526 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Filter Capacitor**
Choose $C = 1000 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$, 25 V rating. From capacitor datasheet:
- **Working Voltage** = 25 V (must exceed $V_{DC(peak)} = 10.53$ V ✓)
- **Ripple Current Rating** must be checked against the rectifier output

**Step 4 — Voltage Regulator**
Use **LM7805** (5 V, 1 A regulator).
- Input must be ≥ **7 V** for regulation (from datasheet)
- $V_{DC(filtered)} = 10.53$ V ≥ 7 V ✓ **Regulation guaranteed**
- **Power dissipation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_D &= (V_{IN} - V_{OUT}) \times I_{LOAD} \\
&= (10.53 - 5.0) \times 0.5 \\
&= 5.53 \times 0.5 \\
&= 2.765 \text{ W}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Thermal Pitfall:** The TO-220 package of LM7805 is rated for ~2 W without a heatsink at $T_A = 25^\circ$C. Our calculation gives 2.765 W → **heatsink mandatory**, otherwise the thermal shutdown will activate.

### 5.4 Analytical Matrix — Component Selection Logic

| Design Requirement | Datasheet Parameter to Check | Failure Mode if Ignored |
|--------------------|------------------------------|--------------------------|
| DC motor control up to 12 V, 2 A | $V_{CEO}$, $I_C$ of BJT/MOSFET | Transistor burnout |
| Op-amp audio preamplifier | $SR$ (Slew Rate), $GBW$ | Distortion at high frequency |
| 5V logic supply | $V_{OUT}$ tolerance, line/load regulation | Logic errors, IC malfunction |
| LED indicator at 20 mA | $V_F$, $I_F(max)$, $P_D$ | LED dim or burned out |
| Zener reference | $V_Z$, $I_{ZT}$, $Z_Z$ (Zener impedance) | Unstable reference voltage |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 6. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 6.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Component Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0[Electronic Components] --> B0[Passive]
    A0 --> C0[Active Discrete]
    A0 --> D0[Integrated Circuits]
    A0 --> E0[Miscellaneous]

    B0 --> B1[Resistor]
    B0 --> B2[Capacitor]
    B0 --> B3[Inductor]

    B1 --> B1A[Fixed Resistor]
    B1 --> B1B[Variable Resistor]
    B1 --> B1C[Special Resistor]

    B1B --> B1B1[Rheostat]
    B1B --> B1B2[Potentiometer]
    B1C --> B1C1[LDR]
    B1C --> B1C2[Thermistor]

    B2 --> B2A[Fixed Capacitor]
    B2 --> B2B[Variable Capacitor]
    B2A --> B2A1[Polarized]
    B2A --> B2A2[Non Polarized]
    B2A1 --> B2A1A[Electrolytic]
    B2A1 --> B2A1B[Tantalum]

    C0 --> C1[Diode Family]
    C0 --> C2[Transistor Family]

    C1 --> C1A[PN Junction]
    C1 --> C1B[Zener]
    C1 --> C1C[LED]
    C1 --> C1D[Schottky]
    C1 --> C1E[Photodiode]

    C2 --> C2A[BJT]
    C2 --> C2B[FET]
    C2 --> C2C[MOSFET]

    C2A --> C2A1[NPN]
    C2A --> C2A2[PNP]
    C2C --> C2C1[N Channel]
    C2C --> C2C2[P Channel]

    D0 --> D1[Linear ICs]
    D0 --> D2[Digital ICs]
    D0 --> D3[Mixed Signal ICs]

    D1 --> D1A[Op Amps]
    D1 --> D1B[Voltage Regulators]
    D1 --> D1C[Comparators]
    D2 --> D2A[Logic Gates]
    D2 --> D2B[Timers like 555]
    D2 --> D2C[Counters and Flip Flops]
    D3 --> D3A[ADC and DAC]
    D3 --> D3B[Microcontrollers]

    E0 --> E1[Switches]
    E0 --> E2[Relays]
    E0 --> E3[Fuses]
    E0 --> E4[Meters]
    E0 --> E5[Lamps and LEDs]
```

### 6.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Systematic Datasheet Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Start: Receive Datasheet] --> S1[Read Header: Part Number and Function]
    S1 --> S2[Identify Package Type and Pinout]
    S2 --> S3[Extract Absolute Maximum Ratings]
    S3 --> S4[Extract Electrical Characteristics at Test Conditions]
    S4 --> S5[Study Typical Performance Curves]
    S5 --> S6[Examine Typical Application Circuit]
    S6 --> S7[Calculate Operating Point: V and I]
    S7 --> S8{Check Thermal Dissipation}
    S8 --> S9[P_D within package rating]
    S8 --> S10[Need external heatsink]
    S9 --> S11[Design is SAFE]
    S10 --> S12[Add heatsink or reduce current]
    S12 --> S11
    S11 --> SEnd[Document final design parameters]
```

### 6.3 Mermaid Block — Schematic Drawing Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W1[Identify Topology] --> W2[Choose Symbol Standard IEEE or BIS]
    W2 --> W3[Layout Inputs Left, Outputs Right]
    W3 --> W4[Draw Components with Reference Designators]
    W4 --> W5[Connect Wires with Dots at Junctions]
    W5 --> W6[Add Power and Ground Labels]
    W6 --> W7[Add Title Block and Notes]
    W7 --> W8[Peer Review for Crossings and Errors]
    W8 --> W9[Final Schematic Complete]
```

### 6.4 Reference Standard Symbol Gallery (Text Representation)

| Component | IEEE Symbol (Text) | BIS/IEC Symbol (Text) |
|-----------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| **Resistor** | `—/\/\/\—` (zig-zag) | `—[ ]—` (rectangle) |
| **Capacitor (NP)** | `—||—` | `—||—` (same) |
| **Inductor** | `—UUUU—` | `—mm—` |
| **Diode** | `—▷|—` | `—▷|—` (same) |
| **NPN BJT** | Arrow outward on emitter | Same |
| **Op-Amp** | Triangle with 2 inputs | Rectangle with 2 inputs |
| **Ground** | Three horizontal lines decreasing | Same |
| **VCC** | Arrow up with label | Same |
| **Switch (SPST)** | `—/—` | Same |

> [!NOTE]
> The above text representations are simplified for documentation. In actual schematic capture tools like **KiCad, LTSpice, or Proteus**, these symbols are built into the component libraries and follow IEEE/IEC conventions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 7. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*List any six standard electronic symbols with their corresponding BIS/IEEE notation. State one specific application for each.*

**Model Answer (6 points):**

1. **Resistor** — Symbol: zig-zag (IEEE) or rectangle (IEC). Application: Current limiting in LED circuits.
2. **Capacitor** — Symbol: two parallel lines. Application: Smoothing in power supply filter.
3. **Inductor** — Symbol: series of loops. Application: Energy storage in SMPS.
4. **Diode (PN)** — Symbol: triangle with cathode bar. Application: Half-wave rectifier.
5. **LED** — Symbol: diode with outward arrows. Application: Indicator lamp.
6. **NPN BJT** — Symbol: BJT with outward emitter arrow. Application: Switching transistor in relay driver.
7. **Op-Amp** — Symbol: triangle with inverting and non-inverting inputs. Application: Inverting/non-inverting amplifiers.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
*What is a datasheet? Mention any four important parameters typically listed in the datasheet of a 1N4007 diode.*

**Model Answer (6 points):**

A **datasheet** is a technical document published by the manufacturer providing complete electrical, mechanical, thermal, and application specifications of a component.

**Four key parameters of 1N4007:**
1. **$V_{RRM}$ = 1000 V** — Peak repetitive reverse voltage
2. **$I_F(AV)$ = 1.0 A** — Average rectified forward current
3. **$V_F$ = 1.1 V** — Forward voltage drop at 1 A
4. **$P_D$ = 3.0 W** — Power dissipation at 25°C

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** When drawing schematics, students often forget to **(a) label component values**, **(b) mark polarity on electrolytic capacitors and diodes**, and **(c) include power supply connections for ICs**. Each omission typically costs **1–2 marks**. Always include a **title block** stating the circuit name and date.

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**

### Part A (14 Marks Option A)

**(a)** Draw the IEEE standard symbols for the following components: Resistor, Capacitor, Inductor, Zener Diode, NPN Transistor, Op-Amp, AND gate, and SPDT switch. State one application of each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a 1N4733 Zener diode, the datasheet lists: $V_Z = 5.1$ V, $I_{ZT} = 49$ mA, $Z_Z = 7$ Ω, $P_D = 1$ W. Calculate the maximum safe Zener current and the voltage variation when current changes by ±10 mA from the test point. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Symbols and applications (7 × 1 = 7 marks):**

- **Resistor** — Zig-zag; *application:* voltage divider.
- **Capacitor** — Two parallel lines; *application:* filter in rectifier.
- **Inductor** — Loop series; *application:* tuned LC circuit.
- **Zener Diode** — Diode with bent cathode bar; *application:* voltage reference.
- **NPN Transistor** — BJT symbol with arrow on emitter pointing outward; *application:* common-emitter amplifier.
- **Op-Amp** — Triangle with + and − inputs and one output; *application:* inverting amplifier.
- **AND Gate** — D-shaped symbol with flat input side and curved output; *application:* digital logic decision.
- **SPDT Switch** — Pole with two throw positions; *application:* selector between two signal sources.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Maximum safe Zener current limited by power dissipation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{Z(\text{max})} &= \frac{P_D}{V_Z} \\
&= \frac{1.0 \text{ W}}{5.1 \text{ V}} \\
&\approx 0.196 \text{ A} \\
&\approx 196 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

Voltage variation for $\Delta I_Z = \pm 10$ mA:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta V_Z &= \Delta I_Z \times Z_Z \\
&= 10 \text{ mA} \times 7 \text{ }\Omega \\
&= 70 \text{ mV}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{Z(\text{range})} = 5.1 \pm 0.07 \text{ V} = 5.03 \text{ V to } 5.17 \text{ V}}
$$

**Physical interpretation:** The Zener acts as a near-ideal voltage source with an internal "resistance" $Z_Z = 7$ Ω. For every 10 mA change in load current, the output voltage changes by only 70 mV — a regulation of about **1.4%**, which is acceptable for most reference applications.

---

### Part B (14 Marks Option B)

**(a)** With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of a **half-wave rectifier with capacitor filter**. Label all components using standard IEEE symbols and mark the polarity of capacitor and diode. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A silicon diode (1N4148) has the following datasheet values: $V_F$ = 0.7 V at $I_F$ = 10 mA, $P_D(\text{max})$ = 500 mW, $V_R(\text{max})$ = 75 V. Determine: (i) the maximum continuous forward current, (ii) the value of the series resistor to be used with a 12 V DC source, and (iii) the power dissipated by the diode. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Half-Wave Rectifier with Capacitor Filter (7 marks):**

**Components:** 230V/12V step-down transformer, single diode 1N4007, filter capacitor 1000 µF / 25 V electrolytic, load resistor $R_L$.

**Operation during positive half-cycle:** The diode is forward biased, conducts, and charges the capacitor to the peak value of the AC input: $V_{DC} \approx V_{sec} \times \sqrt{2} - V_F \approx 12 \times 1.414 - 0.7 \approx 16.27$ V.

**Operation during negative half-cycle:** The diode is reverse biased and acts as an open switch. The capacitor discharges slowly through $R_L$, maintaining nearly constant output voltage.

**Ripple voltage:**

$$
V_{\text{ripple}} = \frac{I_L}{f \times C}
$$

where $f$ is the ripple frequency (50 Hz for half-wave). For smaller ripple, increase $C$ or $R_L$.

**Labeling:** Diode anode connected to transformer secondary; cathode to load positive terminal. Capacitor positive terminal at the load positive side, negative terminal at ground.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Maximum continuous forward current:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{F(\text{max})} &= \frac{P_{D(\text{max})}}{V_F} \\
&= \frac{500 \text{ mW}}{0.7 \text{ V}} \\
&\approx 714 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Series resistor calculation:**
Assume the diode operates safely at $I_F = 10$ mA (the datasheet test point). Then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_S &= \frac{V_S - V_F}{I_F} \\
&= \frac{12 \text{ V} - 0.7 \text{ V}}{10 \text{ mA}} \\
&= \frac{11.3}{0.010} \\
&= 1130 \text{ }\Omega
\end{aligned}
$$

Use standard value **1.2 kΩ, 0.25 W resistor**.

**(iii) Power dissipated by the diode:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_D &= V_F \times I_F \\
&= 0.7 \times 0.010 \\
&= 0.007 \text{ W} \\
&= 7 \text{ mW}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is well within the 500 mW rating, providing a **derating factor of ~71×** — the design is highly conservative and safe.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall — Reverse Voltage:** Always verify that the **peak reverse voltage across the diode** does not exceed $V_R(\text{max})$. In a half-wave rectifier, the diode sees $V_{RRM} = V_{sec}(\text{peak}) = 16.97$ V. For 1N4148, $V_R(\text{max}) = 75$ V ✓ — design safe. If you had used a low-voltage diode like 1N4148 in a 230 V circuit, **instant destruction** would occur.

---

## 8. Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **BIS = IS 2032**; **IEEE = IEEE Std 315 / IEEE Std 91**; **IEC = 60617** — three key standards.
- **Resistor:** IEEE uses zig-zag; IEC/BIS uses rectangle. A **variable resistor has an arrow**; **LDR has inward arrows**; **thermistor has a 't' label**.
- **Capacitor:** Polarized (electrolytic) has a **'+' sign and curved plate**; non-polarized has **two straight plates**.
- **Diode arrow points in the direction of conventional current** (anode to cathode).
- **BJT mnemonic:** "**N**PN: arrow pointing i**N**? **N**o — pointing **O**ut"; "**P**NP: arrow pointing i**N**? **Y**es."
- **Datasheet reading order:** Part Number → Function → Pinout → Absolute Max Ratings → Electrical Characteristics → Typical Application.
- **Power dissipation** $P_D = V \times I$ is the single most important derating check.
- **Thermal rule of thumb:** Operate at **≤ 70% of absolute maximum** for reliability.
- **Slew Rate** $SR$ (V/µs) limits high-frequency op-amp performance: $f_{\text{max}} = SR \div (2\pi V_{peak})$.
- **For any IC, never forget** the **supply pin connections** — an IC with no power is a "dead block" in the schematic.
- **Workshop drawing rule:** Use **reference designators** (R1, C1, D1, Q1, U1) consistently, label every value, and include a **title block**.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Strategy for KTU Exam:** Memorize the symbols of the **eight most-asked components**: Resistor, Capacitor, Inductor, Diode, Zener, LED, BJT (NPN), and Op-Amp. Then memorize the **"Five-Box Datasheet Method"** (Max Ratings, Electrical, Pinout, Curves, Application). Together they cover ~80% of all expected questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Familiarization and application of testing instruments and commonly used tools (Multimeter, Function generator, Power supply, DSO, Soldering iron, Desoldering pump, Pliers, Cutters, Wire strippers, Screw drivers, Tweezers, Crimping tool, Hot air soldering and de-soldering station)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZESL208 - BASIC ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Familiarization and application of testing instruments and commonly used tools (Multimeter, Function generator, Power supply, DSO, Soldering iron, Desoldering pump, Pliers, Cutters, Wire strippers, Screw drivers, Tweezers, Crimping tool, Hot air soldering and de-soldering station)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Testing Instruments — Formal Definitions

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Testing Instrument (KTU 2024 Glossary)**
> A *testing instrument* is an electronic/electrical device used to measure, generate, observe, or supply electrical quantities (voltage, current, resistance, frequency, waveform) with calibrated accuracy for the purpose of verification, troubleshooting, and characterisation of electrical/electronic circuits.

The four primary testing instruments covered in this experiment are:

**1. Digital Multimeter (DMM)**
A multi-range, multi-function electronic measuring instrument capable of measuring **DC Voltage, AC Voltage, DC Current, AC Current, Resistance, Capacitance, Frequency, Continuity, Diode forward voltage, and Temperature** (in advanced models). Modern DMMs use a **dual-slope integrating ADC** and present readings on a **3½ to 4½ digit LCD/LED display**.

**2. Function Generator**
A signal source that produces **repetitive waveforms** — sine, square, triangular, ramp, and pulse — over a wide frequency range (typically **0.1 Hz to 20 MHz**) with adjustable amplitude, DC offset, and duty cycle. It is the "voice" that excites a circuit-under-test (CUT).

**3. DC Regulated Power Supply (RPS)**
A linear/switch-mode power unit that converts **230 V AC mains** into a stable, regulated, low-ripple **DC voltage** (typically **0–30 V**, **0–2 A or 0–5 A**) with constant-voltage (CV) and constant-current (CC) modes.

**4. Digital Storage Oscilloscope (DSO)**
A real-time, digitising oscilloscope that samples analogue signals via a high-speed **ADC** (8-bit typical, up to **4 GS/s** sampling rate) and reconstructs them on an **LCD colour display** for time-domain and trigger-based analysis of waveforms.

### 1.2 Hand Tools — Formal Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition — Hand Tool (KTU 2024 Glossary)**
> A *hand tool* is a non-powered, manually-operated mechanical device used for gripping, cutting, stripping, holding, fastening, or applying thermal energy during the assembly, repair, and prototyping of electronic/electrical circuits.

The hand tools and station-equipment covered are: **Soldering iron, Desoldering pump (Solder sucker), Pliers, Cutters, Wire strippers, Screw drivers, Tweezers, Crimping tool, Hot-air soldering and de-soldering station.**

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Electronics Workshop as a Doctor's Clinic"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy for Students**
> Think of the electronics workbench as a **doctor's clinic**:
> - The **Multimeter** is the *thermometer & stethoscope* — quick vital-sign checks (V, I, R).
> - The **Function Generator** is the *diet plan* — a controlled "stimulus" given to the patient (circuit).
> - The **Power Supply** is the *IV drip* — clean, regulated life support to the patient.
> - The **DSO** is the *ECG / MRI scan* — a real-time moving picture of the circuit's "heartbeat" (waveform).
> - The **Soldering iron & Hot-air station** is the *scalpel* — precise joining/repair with controlled heat.
> - **Pliers, Cutters, Strippers, Tweezers, Screwdrivers, Crimping tool** are the *surgeon's hands* — mechanical finesse in handling the "tissue" (wires and components).

### 1.4 Why This Topic is Critical in the KTU 2024 Scheme

This experiment is the **gateway skill** of the entire B.Tech electronics/electrical curriculum. Every subsequent lab — Analog Electronics, Digital Electronics, Microcontrollers, VLSI, Power Electronics, Communication — depends on the student's ability to **safely and correctly** use these instruments. The **Course Outcome (CO)** mapping typically evaluates: *identification, range selection, connection method, safety precaution, and result interpretation*.

### 1.5 Safety Constants & Standard Bench Metrics

- **Mains AC (India):** **230 V, 50 Hz** (as per IS 1293 / IS 616)
- **Logic-Level DC:** **3.3 V / 5 V** (TTL standard)
- **Standard Soldering Temperature:** **300 °C – 380 °C** (lead-free: ~370 °C; leaded: ~330 °C)
- **Acceptable Soldering Iron Wattage (general PCB work):** **15 W – 60 W**
- **ESD-Safe Tweezers Resistance:** **10⁶ Ω – 10⁹ Ω**
- **Acceptable DSO Probe Input Impedance:** **1 MΩ ∥ 10–20 pF** (standard) / **10 MΩ** (low-capacitance active probe)
- **Acceptable RPS Ripple:** **< 1 mV RMS** (for linear), **< 50 mV RMS** (for SMPS)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Digital Multimeter (DMM) — Operational Theory

**Why it exists:** A single portable instrument that replaces the separate voltmeter, ammeter, ohmmeter, and continuity-tester of a traditional workbench.

**How it works — Logic Steps:**

1. **Mode Selection (Rotary Switch):** Routes the input signal to the appropriate internal signal-conditioning block.
2. **Signal Conditioning:**
   - *Voltage mode* → **voltage divider** (high-value precision resistors) attenuates the input to within the ADC range.
   - *Current mode* → internal **shunt resistor** ($R_{shunt}$) develops a small voltage proportional to current (Ohm's law).
   - *Resistance mode* → the unknown resistor $R_x$ is placed in series with a known internal reference voltage; the resulting current/voltage is measured and converted via $R = V/I$.
   - *Continuity mode* → buzzer sounds if $R < \sim 30\,\Omega$.
3. **ADC Conversion:** A **dual-slope integrating ADC** (immune to mains-frequency noise) or **sigma-delta ADC** (in higher-end DMMs) converts the conditioned analogue signal to a digital count.
4. **Display:** A **3½ digit LCD** (max display **1999**) or **4½ digit** (max display **19999**) shows the reading with auto-ranging or manual range.
5. **Auto/Manual Polarity:** The ADC inherently handles negative readings by sign-bit logic.

**Working Formulae for DMM:**

$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \quad \text{(voltage divider)}$$

$$I = \frac{V_{shunt}}{R_{shunt}} \quad \text{(current via shunt)}$$

$$R_x = R_{ref} \cdot \frac{V_{ref} - V_{measured}}{V_{measured}} \quad \text{(resistance via ratiometric method)}$$

### 2.2 Function Generator — Operational Theory

**Why it exists:** To provide a *known, adjustable, repeatable stimulus* so that the behaviour of any linear or non-linear circuit can be observed and measured.

**How it works — Logic Steps:**

1. **Master Oscillator (DDS — Direct Digital Synthesis):** A high-frequency crystal reference (typically **25 MHz – 100 MHz**) is divided/synthesised digitally to generate the output frequency with extreme precision.
2. **Waveform Shaper:** A **lookup table** containing 1024–4096 samples of one period of the desired waveform (sine, square, triangle, ramp) is read out sequentially and passed to a DAC.
3. **DAC + Reconstruction Filter:** Converts digital samples to a stepped analogue waveform; a **low-pass filter** smooths the steps into a clean sinusoid/ramp.
4. **Output Attenuator (Variable Amplitude):** A precision attenuator network provides **fine and coarse amplitude control** (typically **0–20 Vpp** into **50 Ω**).
5. **DC Offset Circuit:** A summing amplifier adds a user-set DC level to the AC waveform (bipolar or unipolar output).
6. **TTL/CMOS Sync Output:** A separate digital-level synchronising pulse is provided for triggering the DSO.

**Working Formulae for Function Generator:**

$$f_{out} = \frac{M \cdot f_{clock}}{2^N} \quad \text{(DDS output frequency, M = tuning word, N = accumulator bits)}$$

$$V_{pp} \text{ (into 50 Ω)} = 0.5 \times V_{pp} \text{ (into high-Z)} \quad \text{(due to source-impedance loading)}$$

$$V_{rms} = \frac{V_{pp}}{2\sqrt{2}} \quad \text{(for sine wave only)}$$

### 2.3 DC Regulated Power Supply (RPS) — Operational Theory

**Why it exists:** Mains **230 V AC** is unsuitable for DC electronics; a clean, stable, user-adjustable DC source is required.

**How it works — Logic Steps:**

1. **Step-Down Transformer:** Reduces **230 V AC** to e.g. **24 V AC** (centre-tapped, full-wave rectified).
2. **Rectifier:** A **bridge rectifier** (4 × 1N4007) converts AC to pulsating DC.
3. **LC/π Filter (Smoothing):** A large electrolytic capacitor (e.g. **2200 µF, 50 V**) followed by an **LC π-filter** reduces ripple.
4. **Series-Pass Regulator (IC 723 / LM317 / LM7805/12/15 family):** Maintains constant output voltage despite variations in load current or input mains.
5. **Feedback & Error Amplifier:** A sample of $V_{out}$ is compared with an internal band-gap reference (**1.25 V** for LM317); the error signal drives the pass transistor.
6. **Current-Limit / CC Mode:** A separate sense resistor monitors load current; when it exceeds the set value, the regulator switches to constant-current mode — protecting both supply and load.

**Working Formulae for RPS:**

$$V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{R_2}{R_1}\right) \quad \text{(LM317 adjustable regulator)}$$

$$V_{ref} = 1.25 \text{ V} \quad \text{(LM317 internal band-gap reference)}$$

$$V_{ripple} \approx \frac{I_{load}}{f \cdot C} \quad \text{(full-wave rectifier, f = 100 Hz)}$$

$$P_{dissipation} = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot I_{load} \quad \text{(heat-sink design check)}$$

### 2.4 Digital Storage Oscilloscope (DSO) — Operational Theory

**Why it exists:** A voltmeter only tells *one number*; an oscilloscope tells *the whole story* — amplitude, frequency, rise time, distortion, phase, noise, transients.

**How it works — Logic Steps:**

1. **Attenuator / Input Amplifier:** A front-end attenuator + variable-gain amplifier scales the input to the ADC range.
2. **ADC (Real-time Sampler):** Samples the waveform at rates up to **4 GS/s**; resolution typically **8 bits (256 levels)**.
3. **Acquisition Memory:** Stores the digitised samples (typically **1 Mpts – 100 Mpts** per channel).
4. **Trigger System:** A user-set level + edge (rising/falling) synchronises the sweep; equivalent-time sampling extends bandwidth for repetitive signals.
5. **Display Processing:** Samples are reconstructed and rasterised onto a colour **TFT LCD** (typically **7" – 10"**, **800 × 480** or higher).
6. **Measurement Engine:** Automated on-screen readouts — **Vpp, Vrms, Vavg, Frequency, Period, Rise time, Fall time, Duty cycle, Phase**.

**Working Formulae for DSO:**

$$f_{signal} = \frac{1}{T_{measured}} \quad \text{(frequency from period)}$$

$$V_{pp} = V_{max} - V_{min} \quad \text{(peak-to-peak voltage)}$$

$$V_{rms} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T v^2(t)\, dt} \approx 0.707 \cdot V_{peak} \quad \text{(sine wave only)}$$

$$\text{Duty Cycle} = \frac{T_{high}}{T_{period}} \times 100\%$$

$$\text{Nyquist Criterion: } f_{sample} \geq 2 \cdot f_{max} \quad \text{(to avoid aliasing)}$$

$$t_{rise} = \text{time for signal to go from 10\% to 90\% of final value}$$

### 2.5 Soldering Iron — Operational Theory

**Working Principle:** Resistive heating of a metallic **bit (tip)** using a **nichrome/Kanthal heating element**; temperature regulated by either a *simple bimetal thermostat* (fixed-temp pencil irons) or a *PID-controlled ceramic heater* (soldering station, e.g. **Hakko FX-888D**).

**Why temperature matters:** Below **~250 °C**, solder will not wet the pad (cold joint). Above **~400 °C**, the pad lifts, flux burns, and the component is damaged.

> [!NOTE]
> **Eutectic Solder Note:** The classic **Sn-Pb (63/37)** alloy is *eutectic* — it melts and freezes at a single temperature (**183 °C**), making it the easiest to work with. Lead-free alloys (e.g. **SAC305 — Sn96.5/Ag3.0/Cu0.5**) melt at **~217 °C** and require higher tip temperatures (**~370 °C**).

### 2.6 Desoldering Pump (Solder Sucker) & Desoldering Braid

**Desoldering Pump:** A spring-loaded vacuum plunger; when released, a sharp suction pulse removes molten solder from a through-hole pad.
**Desoldering Braid (Wick):** A copper braid impregnated with flux; molten solder is drawn into the braid via capillary action.

### 2.7 Hot-Air Soldering & De-soldering Station

**Working Principle:** A **brushless DC blower** (or diaphragm pump) forces air through a **ceramic heating element**; the hot air (typically **100 °C – 500 °C**, flow **1–10 L/min**) reflows solder paste on **SMD pads** for soldering/desoldering surface-mount components (QFP, QFN, SOIC, 0402, 0201 resistors, BGAs).

### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Quick-Reference Table (use `\vert` for absolute value to avoid table-breaking):**

| # | Quantity / Law | Formula | Standard Unit / Constant |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ohm's Law | $V = I \cdot R$ | V, A, Ω |
| 2 | Power Dissipated | $P = V \cdot I = I^2 R = \dfrac{V^2}{R}$ | W |
| 3 | Sine-wave RMS | $V_{rms} = \dfrac{V_{pp}}{2\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.3536 \cdot V_{pp}$ | V |
| 4 | Sine-wave Average | $V_{avg} = \dfrac{2V_p}{\pi} \approx 0.637 \cdot V_p$ | V |
| 5 | Frequency–Period | $f = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Hz, s |
| 6 | Reactance (Capacitive) | $X_C = \dfrac{1}{2\pi f C}$ | Ω |
| 7 | Reactance (Inductive) | $X_L = 2\pi f L$ | Ω |
| 8 | DSO Probe Loading | $f_{-3dB} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi R_{probe} C_{probe}}$ | Hz |
| 9 | LM317 Output | $V_{out} = 1.25 \left(1 + \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right) + I_{adj} R_2$ | V (typ. $I_{adj} \le 100\,\mu A$) |
| 10 | Power-Supply Ripple | $V_{ripple} \approx \dfrac{I_{load}}{f \cdot C}$ | V (f = 100 Hz for FW rectifier) |
| 11 | Nyquist Sampling | $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ | Hz |
| 12 | Solder Iron Heat Transfer | $P = m c \Delta T / t$ | W (m=pad mass, c=specific heat) |
| 13 | Wire-Strip Length Rule | Strip length $\approx 5 \times$ conductor diameter | mm |
| 14 | Continuity Threshold | $R < \sim 30\,\Omega \Rightarrow$ buzzer ON | Ω (DMM-specific) |
| 15 | DMM Input Impedance | $R_{in} \ge 10\,\text{M}\Omega$ (typical) | Ω |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Usage Procedures & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Step-by-Step Procedure — Using a Digital Multimeter (DMM)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the *exact sequence* a KTU examiner expects when you demonstrate a DMM in lab.

**Step 1 — Visual Inspection**
Check the DMM case for cracks, the test leads for frayed insulation, and the probe tips for bent or oxidised ends. A damaged probe can give a **false reading** or pose a **shock hazard**.

**Step 2 — Insert the Probes**
- **Black probe → COM (common) socket** (always).
- **Red probe → V/Ω/Hz socket** (for voltage, resistance, frequency).
- **Red probe → A or mA socket** (only for current measurement — high-current socket is fused).

**Step 3 — Select the Quantity and Range**
Turn the rotary switch to the desired symbol (V⎓, V~ , A⎓, Ω, etc.). If the DMM is *manual-ranging*, choose the *lowest range* that is greater than the expected value to maximise resolution.

**Step 4 — Connect in Parallel (for Voltage) or Series (for Current)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Connection Rule (Memorise This):**
> - **Voltmeter** → connect in **PARALLEL** across the component.
> - **Ammeter** → connect in **SERIES** with the component.
> - **Ohmmeter** → connect across the component with the circuit **DE-ENERGISED**.

**Step 5 — Read and Record**
Note the value, the unit symbol, and the range. If a **"1." or "OL"** is displayed, the range is too low — increase it.

**Step 6 — Power Down and Reset**
Turn the rotary switch to **OFF** when done. Remove probes from the circuit.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Procedure — Using a Function Generator

**Step 1 — Power On** the instrument; allow a **3-minute warm-up** for frequency stability.

**Step 2 — Select Waveform** via the **WAVE key** (SINE / SQUARE / TRIANGLE / RAMP / PULSE / ARB).

**Step 3 — Set Frequency** using the **FREQ key** + numeric keypad or rotary encoder. Confirm on the **7-segment LED/LCD**.

**Step 4 — Set Amplitude** using the **AMPL key**. If the load is **50 Ω**, remember the actual delivered voltage is **half** the indicated open-circuit value.

**Step 5 — Set DC Offset** (if needed) via the **OFFSET key**; useful for biasing transistor circuits.

**Step 6 — Output Enable** — press the **OUTPUT button**; an LED confirms the BNC is live.

**Step 7 — Verify on DSO** — always verify the actual output on a DSO before connecting to the CUT.

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Procedure — Using a DC Regulated Power Supply

**Step 1 — Set Voltage and Current Limits BEFORE connecting the load.**
Use the **V-SET** and **I-SET** knobs with the output **OFF** (or in preview mode).

**Step 2 — Connect Load** — red (positive) and black (negative) to the binding posts; observe polarity.

**Step 3 — Enable Output** — press the **OUTPUT button**; the **CV LED** glows green for constant-voltage mode.

**Step 4 — Monitor** — verify the actual voltage and current on the **digital display**; the unit may auto-switch to **CC mode** (red LED) if the load draws more than the set current.

**Step 5 — Disconnect Safely** — turn off output, then discharge any capacitive load, then remove the leads.

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Procedure — Using a Digital Storage Oscilloscope (DSO)

**Step 1 — Power On** and connect the **probe compensation terminal** to **CH1**; adjust the probe's **trimmer capacitor** for a *perfect square wave* (no overshoot/undershoot).

**Step 2 — Connect the Probe** to the test point; set the **probe attenuation switch** to **×1** or **×10**; **match it in the DSO menu** (otherwise readings will be 10× wrong).

**Step 3 — Auto-Set** — press the **AUTO button** for an instant optimised display.

**Step 4 — Manual Adjustment** — use the **VOLTS/DIV** knob (vertical) and **TIME/DIV** knob (horizontal) to centre and size the waveform.

**Step 5 — Set Trigger** — set **TRIGGER LEVEL** to half the peak-to-peak amplitude; select **EDGE** (rising or falling); set the **trigger source** to the active channel.

**Step 6 — Use Cursors / Measure** — for high-precision readings, use **manual cursors** rather than the graticule.

**Step 7 — Save / Export** (if available) — save the waveform as a CSV/PNG for the lab record.

### 3.5 Step-by-Step Procedure — Soldering (Through-Hole Component)

**Step 1 — Clean the Tip** with a **damp brass-wool sponge** (never use a regular sponge aggressively — it drops tip temperature).

**Step 2 — Tin the Tip** — apply a small bead of fresh solder to the tip ("tinning") to improve thermal conduction.

**Step 3 — Heat the Joint** — touch the *pad AND the lead* simultaneously with the tip; hold for **~2 seconds**.

**Step 4 — Apply Solder** — feed solder onto the *opposite side* of the joint (not onto the iron); the heat will wick it in.

**Step 5 — Remove Solder First, Then Iron** — a clean, shiny **volcano-shaped** fillet should form.

**Step 6 — Inspect** — a good joint is **shiny, concave, and wetting both the pad and the lead**. A *dull, blistered, or ball-shaped* joint is a **cold joint** — reheat it.

### 3.6 Step-by-Step Procedure — Desoldering (Through-Hole)

**Step 1 — Heat the Joint** with the soldering iron.

**Step 2 — Use Solder Sucker** — press the plunger, place the Teflon nozzle over the molten joint, release the plunger (vacuum pulse removes solder).

**Step 3 — OR Use Solder Wick** — place the braid on the joint, press the hot iron on top, the solder wicks into the braid by capillary action.

**Step 4 — Remove the Component** — once the joint is solder-free, gently pull the lead out.

**Step 5 — Clean the Pad** — use **isopropyl alcohol (IPA)** and a brush to remove flux residue.

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Tool/Instrument Specification Summary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Specification Table (Examiners love this kind of comparative matrix):**

| # | Tool / Instrument | Key Specifications | Primary Use | Safety / Caution |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Digital Multimeter** | 3½ digit, 10 MΩ input Z, ±0.5% DCV accuracy, CAT-III 600 V | Measure V, I, R, C, f, continuity, diode | Never measure current with leads in V/Ω socket — *blows internal fuse* |
| 2 | **Function Generator** | 0.1 Hz – 20 MHz, 0–20 Vpp, 50 Ω output Z, sine/sq/tri/ramp/TTL | Stimulus for amplifier, filter, logic tests | Do **not** connect output to a powered circuit — the generator's 50 Ω fights the supply |
| 3 | **DC RPS** | 0–30 V, 0–2 A, CV/CC auto, ripple < 1 mV | Power breadboard/PCB circuits | Observe **polarity**; never reverse-connect to electrolytic capacitors |
| 4 | **DSO** | 4 channels, 100 MHz – 1 GHz BW, 4 GS/s, 8-bit ADC, 1 Mpts | Time-domain waveform analysis | Match probe **×1/×10** switch to DSO menu; never exceed max input voltage (typically **300 Vrms** for passive probe) |
| 5 | **Soldering Iron** | 15–60 W, 200–450 °C, 230 V | Solder through-hole, wires | Use **stand**; never lay hot iron on bench; **vent fumes** |
| 6 | **Desoldering Pump** | Spring plunger, Teflon nozzle | Remove molten solder | Preheat the joint *first*; pump on molten solder only |
| 7 | **Pliers (Long-nose / Flat-nose)** | 6" insulated handles | Bend leads, hold components | Insulated handles rated to **1000 V** are mandatory for electrical work |
| 8 | **Side Cutters / Diagonal Cutters** | Flush-cut, 5" – 6" | Trim component leads | Wear **safety glasses** — leads fly off at high velocity |
| 9 | **Wire Strippers** | Adjustable gauge (10–24 AWG) | Strip insulation from wires | Match the **AWG slot** to the wire — wrong gauge cuts strands |
| 10 | **Screw Drivers (Phillips / Slotted)** | Insulated, VDE-rated to 1000 V | Open enclosures, terminal blocks | Use the **exact size** — wrong size damages screw head |
| 11 | **Tweezers (ESD-safe)** | Stainless steel / anti-magnetic, 10⁶–10⁹ Ω | Place SMDs, hold fine wires | **Anti-magnetic** is critical when handling reed switches / sensitive magnetic parts |
| 12 | **Crimping Tool** | Ratcheted, for 6P/8P RJ45, AWG 22–28 | Crimp connectors, lugs, RJ45 | Use the **correct die** for the connector; a *cold crimp* is a fire hazard |
| 13 | **Hot-Air SMD Station** | 100–500 °C, 1–10 L/min airflow, brushless blower | Solder/desolder SMD, BGAs, heat-shrink | **Mask adjacent components** with Kapton tape; keep nozzle moving to avoid PCB scorching |

### 3.8 Decision Flow — Which Instrument When?

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagnostic Decision Tree (frequently asked as a "short note" in KTU exams):**
>
> - **Need a single number (V, I, R)?** → **Multimeter**
> - **Need to *generate* a known waveform?** → **Function Generator**
> - **Need clean DC power for a circuit?** → **RPS**
> - **Need to *see* the waveform shape / measure timing?** → **DSO**
> - **Need to *join* two metal parts electrically?** → **Soldering Iron + Solder**
> - **Need to *remove* a soldered joint?** → **Desoldering Pump / Braid**
> - **Need to *remove/rework* an SMD?** → **Hot-Air Station**
> - **Need to *cut* a wire / lead?** → **Side Cutters**
> - **Need to *remove insulation* from a wire?** → **Wire Strippers**
> - **Need to *attach a connector* (RJ45 / lug)?** → **Crimping Tool**
> - **Need to *hold / bend / position* a component?** → **Pliers / Tweezers**
