## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]**
*List any six commonly used hand tools in an electronics workshop and state the specific purpose of each.*

**Model Answer (6 × ½ = 3 marks):**

| # | Tool | Specific Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Soldering iron** | To join electronic components to a PCB by melting solder |
| 2 | **Desoldering pump** | To remove molten solder from a joint for component removal |
| 3 | **Side cutters (diagonal cutters)** | To trim component leads and cut wires flush to the board |
| 4 | **Wire strippers** | To remove insulation from wires without nicking the conductor |
| 5 | **Long-nose pliers** | To bend leads, hold small components, reach into tight spaces |
| 6 | **Tweezers (ESD-safe)** | To pick and place SMD components safely without ESD damage |

*(Any other correct tool with a valid purpose — e.g., crimping tool, screw driver, hot-air station — also earns full marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set A]**
*With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a DC regulated power supply.*

**Model Answer:**

A DC regulated power supply converts **230 V, 50 Hz AC mains** into a stable, low-ripple DC voltage suitable for electronic circuits. Its block diagram is:

1. **Step-down transformer** — reduces 230 V AC to a low AC voltage (e.g., 24 V AC).
2. **Bridge rectifier** — four diodes (e.g., 1N4007) convert AC to pulsating DC (full-wave).
3. **Smoothing filter** — a large electrolytic capacitor (≈2200 µF) reduces the pulsations; ripple voltage is given by $V_{ripple} \approx \dfrac{I_{load}}{f \cdot C}$.
4. **Voltage regulator (IC 723 / LM317 / 78xx series)** — maintains constant output despite line and load variations; output for LM317 is $V_{out} = 1.25\left(1 + \dfrac{R_2}{R_1}\right)$.
5. **Feedback & error amplifier** — samples the output and compares with an internal band-gap reference (1.25 V) to drive the series pass element.
6. **Current-limit / CC mode** — a sense resistor protects the supply and load in the event of a short circuit.

*(Diagram sketch worth 1 mark; description of any 4 blocks worth 2 marks. Total = 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a labelled block diagram, explain the working of a **Digital Storage Oscilloscope (DSO)**. List its front-panel controls. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **working of a Digital Multimeter (DMM)** with its block diagram. How is AC and DC voltage measured using it? Compare DMM with an analog multimeter. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — DSO (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram:**

1. **Input BNC + Attenuator + Buffer** (1 mark) — The signal enters through a BNC connector; a switched attenuator (×1, ×10, ×100) and a high-impedance buffer prevent loading of the circuit under test. Input impedance is typically **1 MΩ ∥ 10–20 pF** for a passive probe.

2. **Variable-gain vertical amplifier** (½ mark) — Amplifies the attenuated signal to span the full ADC input range; controlled by the **VOLTS/DIV knob**.

3. **8-bit ADC** (1 mark) — Samples the analogue waveform at rates up to **4 GS/s**; the bit-resolution determines the vertical detail (256 discrete levels).

4. **Acquisition memory** (½ mark) — Stores the digital samples (typically 1 M to 100 M points per channel).

5. **Trigger system** (1 mark) — A user-set level + edge (rising/falling) + source (CH1/CH2/EXT/LINE) synchronises the sweep so the waveform appears *stationary* on the display. Modes: edge, pulse, video, logic.

6. **Time-base / horizontal amplifier** (½ mark) — Controls the **TIME/DIV** knob and generates the saw-tooth ramp that sweeps the beam.

7. **Display processor + colour TFT LCD** (1 mark) — Reconstructs and rasterises the samples for display (7"–10" LCD, 800 × 480 typical).

8. **Front-panel controls** (1 mark) — VOLTS/DIV, TIME/DIV, TRIGGER LEVEL, AUTO SET, RUN/STOP, CURSORS, MEASURE, MATH, ACQUIRE, SAVE/RECALL, PROBE COMPENSATION terminal.

9. **Communication ports** (½ mark) — USB, Ethernet, RS-232 for data export.

**Working Principle (1 mark):** The DSO continuously samples the input waveform using its ADC, stores the digital samples in acquisition memory, and reconstructs the waveform on the LCD. The trigger system ensures phase-coherent, jitter-free display of repetitive signals and *single-shot capture* of transients — a key advantage over an analogue CRO.

---

**(b) — DMM (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

1. **Input jacks (COM and V/Ω/Hz/A)** (½ mark) — The black probe always goes to COM; the red probe to the appropriate socket.
2. **Mode selector (rotary switch)** (½ mark) — Routes the input to the right signal-conditioning path.
3. **Voltage divider** (½ mark) — for V mode; uses high-stability precision resistors to attenuate the input to the ADC range.
4. **Shunt resistor ($R_{shunt}$)** (½ mark) — for current measurement; voltage across it follows $V = I \cdot R_{shunt}$.
5. **Reference current source + ratio circuit** (½ mark) — for resistance measurement; ratiometric conversion gives $R_x = R_{ref} \cdot \dfrac{V_{ref} - V_{measured}}{V_{measured}}$.
6. **Dual-slope (or sigma-delta) ADC** (½ mark) — Converts the conditioned analogue to a digital count; dual-slope is chosen for **excellent 50 Hz mains-noise rejection**.

**AC vs DC voltage measurement (1 mark):**
- *DC mode* — the signal-conditioning block is a DC-coupled attenuator/amplifier; reading is the average level of the input.
- *AC mode* — the input passes through a blocking capacitor and a precision **true-RMS converter** (for true-RMS DMMs) or a precision rectifier + averaging stage (for average-responding DMMs calibrated to the sine-wave form factor 1.11). AC DMMs display the **RMS** value of the input waveform.

**DMM vs Analog Multimeter (1 mark):**

| Feature | DMM | Analog (VOM) |
|---|---|---|
| Display | 3½–4½ digit numeric LCD | Pointer + scale |
| Input impedance | ≥ 10 MΩ | 20 kΩ/V (low — loads circuit) |
| Accuracy | ±0.5% typical | ±2–3% |
| Power source | 9 V battery + mains | No battery for V measurement |
| Continuity / Diode | Built-in buzzer | None |
| Mechanical damage | None (solid state) | Movement can be damaged by overload |

**DMM Advantages (1 mark):** high accuracy, high input impedance (no loading error), auto-polarity, multi-function in one instrument, robust against overload, easy to read.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **soldering process** in detail. List the different types of solder and the **safety precautions** to be observed during soldering. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Function Generator**. List its applications. Compare function generators with **signal generators**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Soldering Process (7 marks):**

**Definition (½ mark):** Soldering is a *metallurgical joining process* in which a filler metal (solder) is melted at a temperature *below* the melting point of the base metals, forming a **mechanical and electrical bond** upon solidification.

**Steps of Soldering (3 marks):**
1. *Preparation* — clean the pad and component lead with IPA; ensure no oxidation.
2. *Iron pre-heat* — turn the iron on and let it stabilise at the set temperature (300–380 °C).
3. *Tinning the tip* — melt a small bead of fresh solder onto the clean tip to improve heat transfer.
4. *Joint heating* — simultaneously touch the *pad* and *lead* with the tip; hold for ~2 s.
5. *Solder application* — feed the solder wire onto the *opposite side* of the joint; the heat wicks it in.
6. *Cooling* — remove solder first, then iron; do not move the joint during solidification.
7. *Inspection* — a good joint is **shiny, concave, and wets both the pad and the lead** (volcano shape); a *cold joint* (dull, blistered) must be reworked.

**Types of solder (1 mark):**
- **Sn-Pb (63/37)** — eutectic, melts at **183 °C**, easiest to use, contains lead (hazardous).
- **SAC305 (Sn96.5/Ag3.0/Cu0.5)** — lead-free, melts at **~217 °C**, requires higher tip temperature.
- **Sn-Cu, Sn-Ag, Sn-Bi** — specialty lead-free alloys for specific applications.
- **Rosin-core solder** — contains a flux core for oxide removal (most common in electronics).

**Safety precautions (1 mark):**
- Always use the **iron stand**; never lay a hot iron on the bench.
- **Vent the fumes** with a fume-extractor or exhaust fan; lead-free fumes are still irritant.
- **Wash hands** after handling lead-based solder.
- Wear **safety glasses** when cutting leads (projectile hazard).
- Use **ESD-safe practices** (wrist strap) when handling static-sensitive components.
- Keep flammable materials away from the iron.

**Tools used (½ mark):** Soldering iron, soldering stand, brass-wool tip cleaner, flux, desoldering braid/pump, fume extractor, magnifying lamp, side cutters.

---

**(b) — Function Generator (7 marks):**

**Definition (½ mark):** A function generator is an electronic signal source that generates **repetitive waveforms** (sine, square, triangle, ramp, pulse) over a wide frequency range (typically **0.1 Hz to 20 MHz**) with adjustable amplitude, DC offset, and duty cycle.

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

1. **Crystal reference oscillator** (½ mark) — A highly-stable quartz crystal provides the master clock (e.g., 25 MHz). It defines the frequency accuracy of the generator.

2. **Frequency-setting / DDS block (Phase Accumulator + Lookup Table)** (1 mark) — A digital phase accumulator increments by a user-set *tuning word* $M$ on every clock cycle; the accumulator output indexes into a **sine-wave lookup table** (typically 1024–4096 samples). The output frequency is $f_{out} = \dfrac{M \cdot f_{clock}}{2^N}$, where $N$ is the accumulator bit-width (e.g., 32 bits).

3. **DAC + Reconstruction Low-Pass Filter** (½ mark) — The digital samples from the lookup table are converted to a stepped analogue waveform; an anti-aliasing low-pass filter smooths the steps into a clean sinusoid.

4. **Output amplifier + attenuator** (½ mark) — Provides the user-set amplitude (typically 0–20 Vpp open-circuit, 0–10 Vpp into 50 Ω). The output impedance is maintained at **50 Ω** for proper matching with coax and the CUT.

5. **DC-offset circuit** (½ mark) — A summing amplifier adds a user-set DC level to the AC waveform (bipolar or unipolar operation).

6. **Comparator / TTL-CMOS sync output** (½ mark) — A separate, digital-level (TTL 0–5 V) synchronising pulse for triggering the DSO.

**Applications (2 marks):**
- Testing the **frequency response** of amplifiers, filters, and op-amp circuits.
- **Modulation studies** (AM, FM, PWM) when used with a modulator.
- **Clock generation** for digital logic circuits and microcontrollers.
- **Time-domain characterisation** of passive and active networks.
- **Troubleshooting audio equipment, communication receivers, and servos**.
- **Vibration testing, sensor excitation, and biomedical instrumentation**.

**Function Generator vs Signal Generator (1½ mark):**

| Feature | Function Generator | Signal Generator (RF / Analog) |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency range | 0.1 Hz to 20 MHz | 30 kHz to several GHz |
| Waveforms | Sine, square, triangle, ramp, pulse, arb | Mostly sine (with optional modulation) |
| Modulation | Limited (sweep, AM) | Full AM, FM, PM, pulse, I/Q |
| Phase-noise / spectral purity | Moderate | Excellent (low phase noise) |
| Typical use | Lab experiments, audio, low-RF | RF / communication testing |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Voltmeter–Ammeter connection rule** — *Voltmeter in PARALLEL, Ammeter in SERIES.* Mixing this up is the single most common reason students lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **DMM current-measurement mistake** — Always move the **red probe to the "A" or "mA" socket** before measuring current. Forgetting this blows the *internal fuse* (a guaranteed 1-mark deduction).
> 3. **DSO probe attenuation mismatch** — If the probe is set to **×10** but the DSO menu is on **×1**, the displayed amplitude will be **10× too small**. Always *match the switch and the menu*.
> 4. **RPS set current limit** — In KTU labs, you MUST set the **current limit BEFORE connecting the load**. If a short occurs and current is unlimited, the supply, the load, or both will be destroyed.
> 5. **Soldering iron temperature** — "Hot enough to melt solder" is *not* an answer. State the **range 300–380 °C** and the *reason* (cold joint vs pad lift).
> 6. **Function generator output loading** — If the load is **50 Ω**, the *actual* delivered $V_{pp}$ is **half** the displayed value (voltage divider between the 50 Ω source and the 50 Ω load).
> 7. **Desoldering pump timing** — You must apply the pump when the solder is **molten**, not after it solidifies. This is worth 1 mark.
> 8. **Block diagrams** — Draw a *proper labelled block diagram*, not just a textual list. Examiners allocate a full 1–2 marks specifically for the diagram.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Cheat-Sheet — Memorise Before the Exam:**

- [ ] **DMM:** Voltmeter in *parallel*, Ammeter in *series*, Ohmmeter on *de-energised* circuit. 3½-digit display, 10 MΩ input impedance, dual-slope ADC, true-RMS for AC.
- [ ] **Function Generator:** Sine, square, triangle, ramp, pulse. **DDS-based** architecture. Frequency range **0.1 Hz – 20 MHz**. Output impedance **50 Ω**. $V_{pp}$ into 50 Ω is *half* the open-circuit $V_{pp}$.
- [ ] **RPS:** Steps = Transformer → Rectifier → Filter → Regulator → Feedback. LM317 output $V_{out} = 1.25(1 + R_2/R_1)$. CV mode (green) and CC mode (red). Always *set current limit first*.
- [ ] **DSO:** 8-bit ADC, up to 4 GS/s. Bandwidth vs sample rate (Nyquist ≥ 2× signal frequency). Match probe **×1/×10** to DSO menu. Trigger level = ½ $V_{pp}$.
- [ ] **Soldering iron:** **300–380 °C** working range. Eutectic Sn-Pb melts at **183 °C**; lead-free SAC305 at **217 °C**. Tinned tip improves heat transfer.
- [ ] **Desoldering pump:** Spring-loaded vacuum pump; apply on **molten** solder.
- [ ] **Desoldering braid (wick):** Copper braid with flux; **capillary action** removes solder.
- [ ] **Hot-air station:** **100–500 °C**; 1–10 L/min airflow; for **SMD** rework; use **Kapton tape** to mask adjacent components.
- [ ] **Pliers:** Long-nose for bending, flat-nose for gripping. Insulated to **1000 V** for electrical safety.
- [ ] **Side cutters:** Diagonal cutters; **flush-cut** types for PCB work; always wear **safety glasses**.
- [ ] **Wire strippers:** Match the **AWG slot** to the wire; strip length ≈ 5× conductor diameter.
- [ ] **Screw drivers:** VDE-rated insulated to **1000 V**; use *exact* size to avoid cam-out.
- [ ] **Tweezers:** **ESD-safe (10⁶–10⁹ Ω)** and **anti-magnetic** for SMD work.
- [ ] **Crimping tool:** Ratcheted mechanism; use *correct die* for the connector; cold-crimp → fire hazard.
- [ ] **Bench safety:** Always **power off** before changing connections; **discharge capacitors**; use the **iron stand**; observe **ESD precautions**; keep a **fire extinguisher (Class C)** within reach.
- [ ] **Universal formula to recall:** $V = IR$, $P = VI$, $V_{rms} = V_{pp}/(2\sqrt{2})$, $f = 1/T$, $X_C = 1/(2\pi f C)$, $X_L = 2\pi f L$, $V_{out(LM317)} = 1.25(1 + R_2/R_1)$.
- [ ] **Most-asked KTU question pattern:** "Explain with block diagram the working of [RPS/DSO/Function Generator]" — *memorise at least one block diagram per instrument*.
- [ ] **Common 2-mark "short note":** *DMM vs Analog Multimeter, Hot-air vs Soldering iron, Function Generator vs Signal Generator, Cold joint vs Good joint, CV vs CC mode of RPS.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing of electronic components using multimeter (Resistor, Capacitor, Diode, Transistor and JFET)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZESL208 - BASIC ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Testing of electronic components using multimeter (Resistor, Capacitor, Diode, Transistor and JFET)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multimeter-Based Component Testing** is the systematic application of a **Digital Multimeter (DMM)** or **Analog Multimeter (VOM)** in its various functional modes — namely **Ohms (Ω)**, **Continuity (Beeper)**, **Diode-Test (V_f)**, **Capacitance (F)**, and **DC Current (A)** — to verify the electrical health, terminal polarity, and quantitative value of discrete and active electronic components outside a powered circuit (de-energized board).

In the **GZESL208 — Basic Electrical and Electronics Engineering Workshop** syllabus (KTU 2024 Scheme), this experiment is classified under **CO2: Demonstrate the use of basic electronic instruments for testing and measurement**, and the experiment is rated at **Bloom's Cognitive Level: L3 (Apply)** because students must *physically operate* the instrument, *interpret* the readings, and *conclude* on the component's condition.

### Standard Multimeter Modes Used in This Experiment

| S.No | Multimeter Mode | Symbol on DMM | Primary Component Tested |
|------|------------------|---------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Resistance | `Ω` | Resistor, winding, fuse, trace |
| 2 | Continuity | `•)))` | Wires, fuses, PCB tracks |
| 3 | Diode Test | `▶⊢` | Diode, transistor junctions |
| 4 | Capacitance | `⊣⊢` | Capacitor |
| 5 | DC Voltage | `V⎓` | Battery, supply rail |
| 6 | hFE / Transistor | `hFE` | BJT identification |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — A "Doctor's Stethoscope" for Electronics

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a multimeter as a doctor's stethoscope for electronic components.**

A doctor checks your **pulse, temperature, reflexes, and blood pressure** to determine if your body is healthy. In the same way, an engineer uses a multimeter to "probe" a component's electrical "vital signs":

- A **resistor** is like checking a **person's blood pressure** → measure the *resistance* in ohms and compare to the colour-code value.
- A **capacitor** is like checking a **water tank's capacity** → measure *capacitance* in farads; a healthy tank holds charge, a leaky tank does not.
- A **diode** is like checking a **one-way swinging door** → current must pass in *one direction only*; a broken door swings both ways (shorted) or doesn't swing at all (open).
- A **transistor** is like checking **two one-way doors stacked back-to-back** (BJT has two PN junctions) → each door must swing the right way.
- A **JFET** is like checking a **garden hose with a pinch valve** → the gate pinches off the channel between Drain and Source.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Reference Values (Bolded)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following **standard reference values** must be memorised for KTU viva and lab record entry:
> - **Silicon PN junction forward voltage drop: V_F ≈ 0.6 V to 0.7 V**
> - **Germanium PN junction forward voltage drop: V_F ≈ 0.2 V to 0.3 V**
> - **Schottky diode forward voltage drop: V_F ≈ 0.15 V to 0.4 V**
> - **LED red forward voltage drop: V_F ≈ 1.8 V to 2.2 V**
> - **LED white/blue forward voltage drop: V_F ≈ 3.0 V to 3.6 V**
> - **Healthy resistor tolerance: ±5 % (gold band) or ±10 % (silver band)**
> - **Healthy electrolytic capacitor leakage resistance: R_leak > 1 MΩ**
> - **Multimeter internal test current in Diode mode: I_test ≈ 1 mA to 5 mA**

> [!NOTE]
> Always **discharge all capacitors** before testing, **remove the component from the circuit** (whenever practical) or isolate one terminal to avoid parallel-path errors, and **remove the battery / unplug the mains** from any device under test. A typical workshop rule is: *"If it has a plug, unplug it; if it has a battery, take it out."*

---

## 1.4 Pre-Lab Safety and Setup (KTU Mandatory Checklist)

| Step | Action | Reason |
|------|--------|--------|
| 1 | Verify DMM battery is healthy | Low battery shifts readings by tens of ohms |
| 2 | Inspect test leads for cracked insulation | Prevents shock and false continuity |
| 3 | Insert **black lead in COM** socket and **red lead in V/Ω** socket | Standard convention |
| 4 | Select appropriate range; start from highest range if unknown | Prevents DMM damage |
| 5 | Short the probes together to verify 0 Ω (continuity check) | Confirms lead integrity |
| 6 | For active components, set DMM to **Diode mode** | Diode mode uses a low current source |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle of Each Multimeter Mode

### 2.1.1 Resistance Measurement (Ω Mode)

The DMM injects a small **constant DC test current (typically 0.5 mA to 5 mA)** from its internal battery through the test leads into the unknown resistance. The DMM then measures the **voltage drop** across the unknown resistance using Ohm's Law and digitally computes the resistance value.

$$R_{unknown} = \frac{V_{measured}}{I_{test}}$$

**Reasoning:** If $I_{test}$ is constant, then $V_{measured}$ is directly proportional to $R_{unknown}$. The internal ADC of the DMM digitises this voltage and displays the equivalent ohms.

**Why auto-ranging vs manual-ranging matters:** A manual DMM has discrete ranges (200 Ω, 2 kΩ, 20 kΩ, 200 kΩ, 2 MΩ, 20 MΩ, 200 MΩ). For maximum accuracy, the unknown resistance should fall in the **upper third of the selected range**. For example, a 470 Ω resistor should be measured on the **2 kΩ range**, not the 200 Ω range (out of range → "1" overflow) nor the 20 kΩ range (poor resolution).

### 2.1.2 Diode-Test Mode (▶⊢ Mode)

The DMM applies a small DC current (typically 1 mA to 5 mA) between the red (+) and black (−) probes and measures the **forward voltage drop V_F** at that test current. This is essentially a **constant-current source + voltmeter** combination.

**Forward bias** (red probe on Anode, black probe on Cathode): the meter reads **V_F in volts** (≈ 0.6 V for silicon).
**Reverse bias** (red probe on Cathode, black probe on Anode): the meter reads **OL (over-range / open loop)**, displayed as "1" or "OL" on the LCD.

> [!NOTE]
> The same principle is extended to test **BJTs and JFETs**, because these devices are made of PN junctions. By testing the junctions pairwise, we can identify the **Base**, **Emitter**, **Collector** (for BJT) or the **Gate, Drain, Source** (for JFET) terminals.

### 2.1.3 Capacitance Measurement (⊣⊢ Mode)

Modern DMMs apply a small AC signal (or a known DC charging current) to the capacitor and compute the capacitance from the **charge-time relationship**:

$$C = \frac{Q}{V} = \frac{I \cdot t}{V}$$

Some DMMs use an **AC impedance method**, where a known frequency $f$ is applied and the reactance $X_C$ is measured:

$$C = \frac{1}{2 \pi f X_C}$$

A **healthy capacitor** shows its nominal value ± tolerance. A **shorted capacitor** reads near 0 Ω. An **open capacitor** reads near 0 nF (or OL). A **leaky capacitor** shows reduced capacitance and increased Equivalent Series Resistance (ESR).

### 2.1.4 Continuity Test (Beeper Mode)

A simple sub-set of the Ω mode. The DMM sounds a **buzzer** (typically 2 kHz to 4 kHz tone) when the resistance between the probes is **below a threshold** (usually 10 Ω to 50 Ω, depending on the DMM model). Used to check **fuses, wires, solder joints, and PCB tracks**.

### 2.1.5 Transistor hFE Mode

The DMM applies small base currents $I_B$ to the BJT and measures the resulting collector current $I_C$, then computes the **DC current gain**:

$$h_{FE} = \frac{I_C}{I_B}\bigg|_{V_{CE} = const}$$

Typical hFE for a small-signal BJT (BC547, 2N3904) is between **100 and 800**. The value depends strongly on $I_C$ and temperature, so it should only be used as a *qualitative* pass/fail indicator.

---

## 2.2 Testing Procedure — Component by Component

### 2.2.1 Testing a Resistor

**Step 1:** Set DMM to **Ω mode** at an appropriate range.
**Step 2:** Touch probes to the two leads of the resistor.
**Step 3:** Read the displayed value and compare with the **colour-code value**.

**Colour Code (4-band) Formula:**

$$R = (A \times 10 + B) \times 10^{C} \pm D \,\%$$

where A = first digit band, B = second digit band, C = multiplier band, D = tolerance band.

**Example:** Yellow-Violet-Orange-Gold = 47 × 10³ = **47 kΩ ± 5 %**

**Decision Table:**

| Measured Value | Conclusion |
|----------------|------------|
| Within ± tolerance band | **GOOD** |
| Reads **0 Ω** or very low | **SHORTED** (failed) |
| Reads **OL** or very high | **OPEN** (burnt out) |
| Drifting value | **Intermittent / damaged** |

### 2.2.2 Testing a Capacitor

**Step 1:** **DISCHARGE** the capacitor by shorting its leads with a 1 kΩ resistor (NEVER short with bare hands for large electrolytics — risk of burn).
**Step 2:** Set DMM to **Capacitance (⊣⊢) mode**.
**Step 3:** Connect probes to the two leads (observe polarity for electrolytic).
**Step 4:** Read the displayed capacitance.

> [!TIP]
> If your DMM has no capacitance mode, switch to **Ω mode** and observe the charging transient: a healthy electrolytic initially shows a **low resistance that ramps up to OL** within 1–10 seconds. A shorted capacitor immediately reads 0 Ω. An open capacitor jumps to OL instantly.

**Decision Table for Capacitor Test:**

| Test Mode | Healthy Capacitor | Shorted | Open / Leaky |
|-----------|------------------|---------|--------------|
| Capacitance mode | Reads nominal ± 20 % | Reads ≈ 0 F | Reads 0 F or OL |
| Resistance mode (Ω) | Slowly rises to OL | Stays at 0 Ω | Jumps to OL immediately |
| Diode mode | Reads OL both ways | Reads 0.000 V both ways | Reads OL both ways |

### 2.2.3 Testing a Diode

**Step 1:** Set DMM to **Diode (▶⊢) mode**.
**Step 2:** Connect the **red (+) probe to the Anode** and the **black (−) probe to the Cathode** (stripe-marked end).
**Step 3:** Read the forward voltage drop.
**Step 4:** **Reverse** the probes; the meter should read **OL**.

**Decision Table for Diode Test:**

| Forward Reading (Red on A) | Reverse Reading | Conclusion |
|----------------------------|-----------------|------------|
| 0.5 V to 0.8 V (Si) | OL | **GOOD** |
| 0.2 V to 0.3 V (Ge) | OL | **GOOD** |
| 0.000 V (or beep) | 0.000 V | **SHORTED** |
| OL | OL | **OPEN** |
| 1.5 V to 3.0 V | OL | **LED (light-emitting)** |

> [!WARNING]
> When using a **standard DMM in Diode mode**, the test current (~1–5 mA) is often **insufficient to light a normal LED brightly**, but it WILL show the correct forward voltage. Some DMMs have a higher "LED test" current (e.g., 20 mA) for this purpose.

### 2.2.4 Testing a BJT (Bipolar Junction Transistor)

A BJT is essentially **two PN junctions** sharing a common region (the Base):

- **NPN BJT**: Base is P-type, sandwiched between two N-regions (Emitter and Collector). Equivalent to **two diodes with their cathodes tied to the Base**.
- **PNP BJT**: Base is N-type, sandwiched between two P-regions. Equivalent to **two diodes with their anodes tied to the Base**.

**Step-by-step identification for an NPN transistor (e.g., BC547):**

1. Set DMM to **Diode mode**.
2. **Find the Base**: Probe a pin with the red lead. Touch the black lead to the other two pins in turn.
   - If BOTH readings show 0.5–0.8 V → that pin is the **BASE**, and the transistor is **NPN**.
3. **Identify Emitter vs Collector**: Use the **hFE mode** socket on the DMM (if available). Insert the transistor into the NPN slot; try both orientations. The orientation that gives the **higher hFE reading** is the **correct** pinout.

**For PNP**: Step 2 is reversed — both readings of 0.5–0.8 V appear when the **black lead** is on the Base.

**Decision Table for BJT Junction Test (NPN):**

| Red Probe on | Black Probe on | Expected Reading | If Abnormal |
|--------------|----------------|------------------|-------------|
| Base | Emitter | 0.5 V – 0.8 V | 0 V (shorted BE) / OL (open BE) |
| Base | Collector | 0.5 V – 0.8 V | 0 V (shorted BC) / OL (open BC) |
| Emitter | Base | OL | Voltage reading = reversed BE (PNP or shorted) |
| Emitter | Collector | OL | Short = transistor is shorted E-C |
| Collector | Base | OL | Voltage reading = reversed BC |
| Collector | Emitter | OL | Short = transistor is shorted C-E |

### 2.2.5 Testing a JFET (Junction Field-Effect Transistor)

A JFET is a **voltage-controlled resistor**. The channel between **Drain (D) and Source (S)** is pinched by the reverse-biased **Gate (G) – Channel junction**.

**Internally equivalent model:**
- The **Gate-to-Source** is a **diode** (anode = Source/Channel, cathode = Gate for N-channel JFET).
- The **Gate-to-Drain** is also a **diode** (anode = Drain/Channel, cathode = Gate for N-channel JFET).
- The **Drain-to-Source** is a **resistor** (channel resistance R_DS) of typically 10 Ω to 1 kΩ for a small-signal JFET.

**Step-by-step identification for an N-channel JFET (e.g., 2N3819, BF245):**

1. Set DMM to **Diode mode**.
2. Identify the **GATE**: Probe a pin with the **red (+) lead**. If BOTH other pins show OL, that pin is the **Gate** (because both G-S and G-D junctions are reverse-biased).
3. Confirm Gate: Reverse the leads — both should now show **0.5 V to 0.8 V**.
4. **Drain and Source** are usually interchangeable for DC operation, but the **Source** is typically tied to the lower-potential side of the circuit. The D-S resistance on a healthy JFET should be a **finite, stable value** (typically 50 Ω to 500 Ω for a small-signal JFET).

**Decision Table for N-channel JFET (e.g., 2N3819):**

| Red Probe on | Black Probe on | Expected Reading | Interpretation |
|--------------|----------------|------------------|----------------|
| Gate | Source | 0.5 V – 0.8 V | G-S forward biased (G-S diode) |
| Gate | Drain | 0.5 V – 0.8 V | G-D forward biased (G-D diode) |
| Source | Gate | OL | G-S reverse biased |
| Drain | Gate | OL | G-D reverse biased |
| Drain | Source | Finite R (50–500 Ω) | Channel conduction |
| Source | Drain | Finite R (50–500 Ω) | Channel conduction (symmetric) |

> [!NOTE]
> A **MOSFET** is **NOT** tested this way because its gate is insulated by a thin oxide layer. Attempting diode-mode test on a MOSFET can **damage** the gate oxide if the DMM's open-circuit voltage exceeds the gate-oxide breakdown voltage (typically ±20 V). Always **discharge MOSFET gates** by shorting G to S before handling.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Application |
|---|---------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | $V = I \cdot R$ | Ohm's Law | DMM Ω mode principle |
| 2 | $R = \dfrac{V_{measured}}{I_{test}}$ | Resistance from test current | DMM internal calculation |
| 3 | $R = (A \cdot 10 + B) \cdot 10^{C} \pm D\,\%$ | Resistor colour code (4-band) | Identify nominal value |
| 4 | $C = \dfrac{Q}{V} = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{V}$ | Capacitance definition | DMM capacitance mode |
| 5 | $C = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi f X_C}$ | Capacitive reactance inversion | AC impedance capacitance test |
| 6 | $V_F \approx 0.6 \text{ V to } 0.7 \text{ V}$ | Silicon forward drop | Diode test reference |
| 7 | $V_F \approx 0.2 \text{ V to } 0.3 \text{ V}$ | Germanium forward drop | Diode test reference |
| 8 | $h_{FE} = \dfrac{I_C}{I_B}$ | DC current gain (BJT) | hFE mode identification |
| 9 | $I_D = I_{DSS} \left(1 - \dfrac{V_{GS}}{V_P}\right)^{2}$ | JFET transfer characteristic | (Conceptual — JFET behaviour) |
| 10 | $R_{DS(on)} = \dfrac{V_{DS}}{I_D}\big\vert_{V_{GS}=0}$ | JFET channel ON resistance | D-S resistance measurement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Memorise **V_F = 0.6 V to 0.7 V for silicon**. This single value is the answer to ~30 % of the viva questions on diodes. Also remember that **the cathode end of a diode is marked with a coloured stripe**.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Service technicians** in TV/mobile repair shops use DMMs daily to identify burnt components on PCBs.
- **Quality control (QC)** in electronics manufacturing uses automated multimeter-based testers (bed-of-nails fixtures) to verify every component before final assembly.
- **R&D prototyping** — engineers test breadboard components before soldering to avoid waste.
- **Field service** — automotive electricians use DMMs to test alternator diodes, regulator rectifiers, and ECU-driver transistors in situ.
- **Failure analysis** — reverse-engineering engineers use DMMs to map out unknown circuits, identifying every component and its pinout.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Resistance from DMM Test Current

**Starting Point:** A DMM in Ω mode contains an internal precision current source $I_{test}$ and a voltmeter. The unknown resistor $R_x$ is connected in series with $I_{test}$ and the voltmeter measures the voltage across $R_x$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given:} \quad & V_x = I_{test} \cdot R_x \quad \text{(Ohm's Law)} \\
\text{Therefore:} \quad & R_x = \frac{V_x}{I_{test}} \\
\text{Since } I_{test} \text{ is constant in the DMM, } R_x \propto V_x \\
\text{The DMM's ADC digitises } V_x \text{ and the display driver converts it to ohms.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example:** Suppose the DMM uses $I_{test} = 1 \text{ mA}$ and measures $V_x = 4.7 \text{ V}$ across the unknown resistor.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_x &= \frac{V_x}{I_{test}} \\
&= \frac{4.7 \text{ V}}{1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}} \\
&= 4700 \text{ Ω} \\
&= 4.7 \text{ kΩ}
\end{aligned}
$$

The DMM therefore displays **"4.70 kΩ"**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Resistor Colour-Code Value (4-Band)

**Given:** A resistor with bands **Yellow – Violet – Orange – Gold**.

**Step 1 — Identify the digit values** from the standard colour table:
- Yellow = **4**
- Violet = **7**
- Orange = multiplier $10^{3}$ = **1000**
- Gold = tolerance **±5 %**

**Step 2 — Form the mantissa:**

$$
M = (A \times 10) + B = (4 \times 10) + 7 = 47
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the multiplier:**

$$
R = M \times 10^{C} = 47 \times 10^{3} = 47{,}000 \text{ Ω} = 47 \text{ kΩ}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the tolerance band:**

$$
R_{range} = 47 \text{ kΩ} \pm 5 \%
$$

The 5 % tolerance window is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{min} &= 47{,}000 - (0.05 \times 47{,}000) = 47{,}000 - 2{,}350 = 44{,}650 \text{ Ω} \\
R_{max} &= 47{,}000 + (0.05 \times 47{,}000) = 47{,}000 + 2{,}350 = 49{,}350 \text{ Ω}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore, **any DMM reading between 44.65 kΩ and 49.35 kΩ** is a **PASS** for this resistor.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Capacitance from Charge-Time (DMM Method 1)

**Given:** A DMM charges the unknown capacitor $C_x$ with a known constant current $I_{test}$ and measures the time $t$ taken to charge from 0 V to a reference voltage $V_{ref}$ (typically around 1.0 V).

**Step 1 — Recall the definition of capacitance:**

$$
C = \frac{Q}{V}
$$

**Step 2 — Express charge $Q$ in terms of current and time:**

$$
Q = I_{test} \cdot t
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $Q$ into the capacitance equation:**

$$
C_x = \frac{I_{test} \cdot t}{V_{ref}}
$$

**Numerical Example:** Suppose $I_{test} = 10 \text{ μA}$, $V_{ref} = 1.0 \text{ V}$, and the DMM measures $t = 0.1 \text{ s}$ for a small capacitor.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_x &= \frac{10 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A} \times 0.1 \text{ s}}{1.0 \text{ V}} \\
&= \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ C}}{1.0 \text{ V}} \\
&= 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F} \\
&= 1 \text{ μF}
\end{aligned}
$$

The DMM therefore displays **"1.000 μF"**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Capacitance from AC Reactance (DMM Method 2)

**Given:** Some DMMs apply a small AC signal of known frequency $f$ (typically 1 kHz) across the unknown capacitor and measure the reactance $X_C$.

**Step 1 — Recall the capacitive reactance formula:**

$$
X_C = \frac{1}{2 \pi f C}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $C$:**

$$
C = \frac{1}{2 \pi f X_C}
$$

**Numerical Example:** Suppose $f = 1 \text{ kHz}$ and the DMM measures $X_C = 159.15 \text{ Ω}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{1}{2 \pi \times 1000 \times 159.15} \\
&= \frac{1}{1{,}000{,}000.5} \\
&\approx 1.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ F} \\
&= 1 \text{ μF}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation: BJT hFE and B-E Junction Check

**Given:** A DMM in **Diode mode** injects $I_{test} \approx 1 \text{ mA}$ between the Base and Emitter of an NPN BJT.

**Step 1 — For a healthy NPN B-E junction:**

$$
V_{BE} = V_F \approx 0.6 \text{ V to } 0.7 \text{ V (at } I_B = 1 \text{ mA)}
$$

**Step 2 — The corresponding collector current (in normal operation) is:**

$$
I_C = h_{FE} \cdot I_B
$$

**Numerical Example:** If $I_B = 1 \text{ mA}$ and the DMM measures $h_{FE} = 300$ in the hFE mode, then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_C &= h_{FE} \times I_B \\
&= 300 \times 1 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 0.3 \text{ A} = 300 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

This shows the **amplification action** of the BJT.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Master Reference Table

| Component | Test Mode | Step | Healthy Reading | Faulty Reading 1 | Faulty Reading 2 |
|-----------|-----------|------|------------------|------------------|------------------|
| **Resistor** | Ω | Measure across leads | Within ± tolerance | 0 Ω (shorted) | OL (open) |
| **Capacitor** | ⊣⊢ | Measure across leads | Nominal ± 20 % | 0 F (shorted) | 0 F / OL (open) |
| **Capacitor** | Ω | Observe transient | Rises to OL | Stays at 0 Ω | Jumps to OL |
| **Diode (Si)** | ▶⊢ | Forward bias | 0.6 V – 0.7 V | 0.000 V (shorted) | OL (open) |
| **Diode (Si)** | ▶⊢ | Reverse bias | OL | 0.000 V (shorted) | OL (open) |
| **LED** | ▶⊢ | Forward bias | 1.8 V – 3.6 V (no light) | 0.000 V | OL |
| **BJT (NPN)** | ▶⊢ | Red on Base, Black on E or C | 0.5 V – 0.8 V (both) | 0.000 V (shorted) | OL (open) |
| **BJT (NPN)** | ▶⊢ | Red on E or C, Black on Base | OL (both) | < 0.5 V (shorted) | — |
| **BJT (PNP)** | ▶⊢ | Black on Base, Red on E or C | 0.5 V – 0.8 V (both) | 0.000 V (shorted) | OL (open) |
| **JFET (N-ch)** | ▶⊢ | Red on Gate, Black on S or D | 0.5 V – 0.8 V (both) | 0.000 V (shorted) | OL (gate open) |
| **JFET (N-ch)** | ▶⊢ | Black on Gate, Red on S or D | OL (both) | < 0.5 V (shorted) | — |
| **JFET (N-ch)** | Ω | Measure D-S | 50 Ω – 500 Ω (finite) | 0 Ω (shorted) | OL (channel open) |

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Question:** A 4-band resistor has colour bands **Brown – Black – Red – Silver**. A technician measures it with a DMM and the reading is **970 Ω**. Is the resistor good, and if yes, justify.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Decode the colour code:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Brown} &= 1 \\
\text{Black} &= 0 \\
\text{Red} &= 10^{2} = 100 \\
\text{Silver} &= \pm 10\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the nominal resistance:**

$$
R_{nom} = (1 \times 10 + 0) \times 10^{2} = 10 \times 100 = 1000 \text{ Ω} = 1 \text{ kΩ}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the tolerance window (±10 %):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{min} &= 1000 - 100 = 900 \text{ Ω} \\
R_{max} &= 1000 + 100 = 1100 \text{ Ω}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compare with the measured value 970 Ω:**

$$
900 \text{ Ω} \leq 970 \text{ Ω} \leq 1100 \text{ Ω} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{TRUE}
$$

**Conclusion:** The resistor is **GOOD** because the measured value 970 Ω lies within the allowed tolerance window of 900 Ω to 1100 Ω.

---

## 3.8 Worked Example: Identifying BJT Terminals

**Question:** A technician has an unknown 3-pin device (suspected BJT) marked X1, X2, X3. Diode-mode measurements give the following:

| Red on | Black on | Reading |
|--------|----------|---------|
| X1 | X2 | 0.65 V |
| X1 | X3 | 0.65 V |
| X2 | X1 | OL |
| X3 | X1 | OL |
| X2 | X3 | OL |
| X3 | X2 | OL |

Identify the type, Base, Emitter, and Collector.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the Base:**
Both X1→X2 and X1→X3 show 0.65 V (forward-biased silicon), while reverse readings show OL. Therefore, **X1 is the BASE**, and the device is **NPN** (because the red lead is on the Base and produces the forward voltage).

**Step 2 — Distinguish Emitter from Collector:**
- X1 (Base) → X2 = 0.65 V → **X2 is either Emitter or Collector**
- X1 (Base) → X3 = 0.65 V → **X3 is either Emitter or Collector**

Since the E-C resistance on a typical BJT is **OL** (no conduction), the table confirms this: X2 → X3 = OL and X3 → X2 = OL. To distinguish them, the technician must use the **hFE mode** of the DMM and try both orientations; the one with the higher hFE is the correct pinout.

**Conclusion:** The device is an **NPN BJT** with **X1 = Base**, and **{X2, X3} = {Emitter, Collector}** (determined by hFE test).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Digital Multimeter (DMM)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Function Selector Knob] --> B[Mode Selection Logic]
    B --> C1[Voltage Mode]
    B --> C2[Current Mode]
    B --> C3[Resistance Mode]
    B --> C4[Diode Mode]
    B --> C5[Capacitance Mode]
    B --> C6[Continuity Mode]
    C1 --> D1[High Z Attenuator + ADC]
    C2 --> D2[Shunt Resistor + ADC]
    C3 --> D3[Constant Current Source + ADC]
    C4 --> D4[1 mA Source + Voltmeter]
    C5 --> D5[AC Excitation or Charge Timer]
    C6 --> D6[Buzzer Driver - threshold 30 Ω]
    D1 --> E[Microcontroller - Range + Display]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    D5 --> E
    D6 --> E
    E --> F[3.5 digit or 4.5 digit LCD Display]
    G[Internal 9V Battery] --> D3
    G --> D4
    G --> D5
    H[COM and V Ohm Sockets] --> A
```

**Reading the diagram:** The selector knob routes the test leads to one of six internal measurement channels. Each channel uses a different front-end circuit tailored to the physical quantity being measured. All channels share a common ADC and display subsystem driven by a microcontroller.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: BJT Diode-Mode Test Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1 - Set DMM to Diode mode] --> B[Step 2 - Probe pin 1 with red lead]
    B --> C{Both other pins show 0.6 V?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Pin 1 is BASE - NPN transistor]
    C -- No --> E[Step 3 - Probe pin 2 with red lead]
    E --> F{Both other pins show 0.6 V?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Pin 2 is BASE - NPN transistor]
    F -- No --> H[Step 4 - Probe pin 3 with red lead]
    H --> I{Both other pins show 0.6 V?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Pin 3 is BASE - NPN transistor]
    I -- No --> K[Red lead found no base - try BLACK lead on each pin]
    K --> L{Black lead on a pin shows 0.6 V on both others?}
    L -- Yes --> M[That pin is BASE - PNP transistor]
    L -- No --> N[Device is faulty or not a BJT]
    D --> O[Step 5 - Use hFE mode to find E and C]
    G --> O
    J --> O
    M --> O
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is the standard KTU lab procedure for terminal identification. The flowchart covers all four cases: (i) NPN, (ii) PNP, (iii) faulty device, (iv) not a BJT.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: JFET Test Strategy (N-Channel)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1 - Set DMM to Diode mode] --> B[Step 2 - Touch red lead to pin 1]
    B --> C{Pin 1 to pin 2 and pin 1 to pin 3 both show OL?}
    C -- Yes --> D[Pin 1 is GATE - red lead reverse biases both junctions]
    C -- No --> E[Step 3 - Touch red lead to pin 2]
    E --> F{Both readings show OL?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Pin 2 is GATE]
    F -- No --> H[Step 4 - Touch red lead to pin 3]
    H --> I{Both readings show OL?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Pin 3 is GATE]
    I -- No --> K[Device is faulty]
    D --> L[Step 5 - Reverse leads: black on gate shows 0.6 V on both others]
    G --> L
    J --> L
    L --> M[Step 6 - Remaining two pins are DRAIN and SOURCE]
    M --> N[Step 7 - Measure D to S resistance - should be 50 to 500 Ω]
    N --> O{Finite R both directions?}
    O -- Yes --> P[HEALTHY JFET]
    O -- No --> Q[FAULTY JFET - shorted or open channel]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The trick to identify a JFET gate is the **unidirectional behaviour**: red lead on gate shows OL both ways, while black lead on gate shows 0.6 V both ways. The D-S channel is symmetric for most small-signal JFETs.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram: Master Test Sequence for an Unknown Component

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Unknown 2 or 3 terminal component] --> B{Number of pins?}
    B -- 2 pins --> C[Use DMM in DIODE mode]
    B -- 3 pins --> D[Use DMM in DIODE mode - terminal identification]
    C --> C1{Forward 0.6 V and reverse OL?}
    C1 -- Yes --> E[Diode - identify anode cathode by stripe]
    C1 -- No --> F{Forward and reverse both OL?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Possibly open - try CAPACITANCE mode]
    G --> H{Capacitance reads near marked value?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Capacitor - GOOD]
    H -- No --> J[Capacitor - OPEN or LEAKY]
    F -- No --> K{Forward and reverse both 0 V?}
    K -- Yes --> L[SHORTED component - REPLACE]
    D --> D1{One pin shows 0.6 V on both others with red on it?}
    D1 -- Yes --> M[NPN BJT or N-channel JFET]
    D1 -- No --> D2{One pin shows 0.6 V on both others with BLACK on it?}
    D2 -- Yes --> N[PNP BJT or P-channel JFET]
    D2 -- No --> O[Use hFE mode to confirm BJT or finite R_DS for JFET]
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is the master decision tree that a KTU student is expected to follow in the lab exam. It is the synthesis of all individual test procedures into one unified diagnostic flow.

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Resistor Colour-Code Decoding Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Resistor with N colour bands] --> B{N equals 4 or 5?}
    B -- 4 bands --> C[Band 1 is A, Band 2 is B, Band 3 is C multiplier, Band 4 is D tolerance]
    B -- 5 bands --> E[Band 1 is A, Band 2 is B, Band 3 is B2, Band 4 is C multiplier, Band 5 is D tolerance]
    C --> F[Compute R = A times 10 + B times 10 to power C plus minus D percent]
    E --> G[Compute R = A times 100 plus B times 10 plus B2 times 10 to power C plus minus D percent]
    F --> H[Compare with DMM reading - if within tolerance - GOOD]
    G --> H
```

**Reading the diagram:** A 4-band resistor has 2 significant digits, while a 5-band resistor has 3 significant digits (higher precision). Both decoding formulae are shown for completeness, though most workshop resistors are 4-band.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Q: With a neat sketch, explain how a digital multimeter (DMM) is used to test the condition of a silicon diode.**

**Model Answer:**

A DMM is switched to the **diode-test mode**, indicated by the symbol `▶⊢`. In this mode, the DMM injects a small constant current (typically 1 mA to 5 mA) and measures the forward voltage drop.

**Procedure:**
1. Identify the **cathode** of the diode by the coloured stripe on its body.
2. Connect the **red (+) probe to the anode** and the **black (−) probe to the cathode** (forward bias).
3. Read the display. A healthy silicon diode shows **V_F = 0.6 V to 0.7 V**.
4. Reverse the leads (reverse bias). The display should read **OL** (over-range / open circuit).

**Conclusion:**
| Forward Reading | Reverse Reading | Diode Condition |
|-----------------|-----------------|------------------|
| 0.6 V – 0.7 V | OL | GOOD |
| 0.000 V | 0.000 V | SHORTED |
| OL | OL | OPEN |

A simple sketch would show the DMM probes across the diode with the polarity indicated.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Supple Paper]
**Q: List the steps to test a bipolar junction transistor (BJT) using a multimeter. How will you identify the base terminal?**

**Model Answer:**

**Steps to test a BJT:**
1. Set the DMM to **diode-test mode**.
2. Probe each pin with the **red (+) lead**. Check the reading on the other two pins.
3. The pin that shows **0.5 V to 0.8 V on BOTH other pins** is the **BASE**, and the transistor is **NPN**.
4. If the same condition is found with the **black (−) lead**, the transistor is **PNP**.
5. Use the **hFE mode** to identify the Emitter and Collector (correct orientation gives the higher hFE value).

**Identification of Base:** In a healthy BJT, the base is the common terminal of two back-to-back PN junctions. Therefore, one probe polarity will forward-bias BOTH junctions (showing 0.6 V on the other two pins), identifying that pin as the Base.

**Fault conditions:**
- If any junction reads 0.000 V in both directions → **shorted junction** (transistor faulty).
- If any junction reads OL in both directions → **open junction** (transistor faulty).
- If E to C reads less than 1 MΩ → **leaky transistor** (faulty).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the procedure to test a **resistor** using a multimeter, with reference to the **4-band colour code**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe how a multimeter is used to test the **health of a capacitor** and differentiate between a **shorted**, **open**, and **leaky** capacitor. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Resistor Testing Procedure:**

1. **Identify the colour-code value:** Hold the resistor with the **tolerance band (gold/silver)** on the right. Read the bands from left to right as: **First digit, Second digit, Multiplier, Tolerance**.

2. **Apply the colour-code formula:**

$$R = (A \times 10 + B) \times 10^{C} \pm D\,\%$$

3. **Measure with the DMM:**
   - Set the DMM to the **Ω (resistance) mode**.
   - Select the appropriate range (e.g., 2 kΩ range for a 1 kΩ resistor).
   - Connect the probes to the two leads of the resistor.
   - Read the displayed value.

4. **Compare the measured value with the colour-code value** ± tolerance.

**Example:** A resistor with bands **Brown – Black – Red – Gold** has:
- A = 1, B = 0, C = 2, D = ±5 %
- $R_{nom} = (1 \times 10 + 0) \times 10^{2} = 1000 \text{ Ω} = 1 \text{ kΩ}$
- Tolerance window: **950 Ω to 1050 Ω**

5. **Decision:**
   - If measured value is within 950–1050 Ω → **GOOD**
   - If measured value is 0 Ω → **SHORTED**
   - If measured value is OL → **OPEN**
   - If measured value is outside the tolerance window → **OUT OF SPEC**

**(b) Capacitor Testing Procedure:**

A capacitor stores electrical energy. To test it, we must first **safely discharge it** by shorting its leads through a **1 kΩ bleed resistor** (to limit inrush current and prevent sparks).

1. **Discharge the capacitor** before any test.
2. **Set the DMM to capacitance mode (⊣⊢)** if available, or to **Ω mode** as a fallback.
3. **Connect the probes** to the capacitor leads (observe polarity for polarised capacitors: red probe to + terminal, black to − terminal).

4. **Interpretation:**

| Condition | Capacitance Mode | Ω Mode (Transitional) | Conclusion |
|-----------|------------------|------------------------|------------|
| Healthy | Reads nominal value ± 20 % | Rises slowly to OL | GOOD |
| Shorted | Reads 0 F (or near 0) | Stays at 0 Ω | FAULTY (replace) |
| Open | Reads 0 F or OL | Jumps to OL immediately | FAULTY (replace) |
| Leaky | Reads lower than nominal | Rises to a low finite R | FAULTY (replace) |

For electrolytic capacitors, also check the **leakage current** by measuring the **steady-state Ω reading** after the charging transient. A healthy electrolytic shows **R_leak > 1 MΩ**, while a leaky one shows **R_leak < 100 kΩ**.

For **non-polarised capacitors** (ceramic, film), the same procedure applies; the charging transient in Ω mode is very brief because of the small capacitance values.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Regular]

**(a)** With a suitable procedure, explain how a **multimeter in diode mode** is used to identify the **terminals (Base, Emitter, Collector) of an NPN BJT**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the multimeter-based test procedure for an **N-channel JFET**, including the differentiation between a **healthy** and a **faulty** device. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) NPN BJT Terminal Identification:**

An NPN BJT can be modelled as **two diodes with their cathodes tied to the Base** (Common-Cathode model). The DMM's diode-test mode allows us to forward-bias each junction individually and identify the Base.

**Step 1 — Find the Base:**
1. Set DMM to **diode mode (▶⊢)**.
2. Place the **red (+) probe** on a pin and the **black (−) probe** on each of the other two pins in turn.
3. If **both** readings show **0.5 V to 0.8 V**, that pin is the **BASE** and the device is **NPN**.

**Step 2 — Find Emitter and Collector:**
4. Insert the transistor into the DMM's **hFE socket** in the NPN slot.
5. Try both orientations of the E and C pins.
6. The orientation that gives the **higher hFE value** is the **correct pinout**; the corresponding pin is the **Emitter** (B) and the other is the **Collector** (C).

**Step 3 — Verify all junctions:**

| Test (Red on) | Test (Black on) | Expected Reading | If Faulty |
|---------------|------------------|------------------|-----------|
| Base | Emitter | 0.5 V – 0.8 V | 0 V (shorted) / OL (open) |
| Base | Collector | 0.5 V – 0.8 V | 0 V (shorted) / OL (open) |
| Emitter | Collector | OL | < 0.5 V (shorted E-C) |
| Collector | Emitter | OL | < 0.5 V (shorted C-E) |

**(b) N-Channel JFET Testing Procedure:**

An N-channel JFET has a **Gate-to-Channel PN junction** that is normally reverse-biased. The Gate and Drain, and the Gate and Source, form two diodes with the **Gate as the cathode** (in N-channel). The Drain and Source are connected by a **conductive channel** of resistance $R_{DS(on)}$.

**Step 1 — Identify the Gate:**
1. Set DMM to **diode mode**.
2. Touch the **red (+) lead** to a pin. If the readings to the **other two pins both show OL**, that pin is the **Gate** (because both G-S and G-D junctions are reverse-biased).
3. Confirm by reversing the leads — with the **black (−) lead on the Gate**, both other pins should show **0.5 V to 0.8 V** (forward-biased).

**Step 2 — Verify D-S Channel:**
4. With the Gate identified, the remaining two pins are **Drain and Source**.
5. Switch DMM to **Ω mode** and measure the resistance between Drain and Source.
6. A **healthy JFET** shows a **finite resistance of 50 Ω to 500 Ω** in both directions (channel is symmetric for DC testing).
7. **Swap the leads** and re-measure — the value should be similar.

**Step 3 — Decision Matrix for N-channel JFET:**

| Test | Red on | Black on | Healthy | Shorted | Open |
|------|--------|----------|---------|---------|------|
| 1 | Gate | Source | 0.5 V – 0.8 V | 0.000 V | OL |
| 2 | Gate | Drain | 0.5 V – 0.8 V | 0.000 V | OL |
| 3 | Source | Gate | OL | < 0.5 V | OL |
| 4 | Drain | Gate | OL | < 0.5 V | OL |
| 5 | Drain | Source | 50 Ω – 500 Ω | 0 Ω | OL |
| 6 | Source | Drain | 50 Ω – 500 Ω | 0 Ω | OL |

**Conclusion:** If all six tests pass as in the "Healthy" column, the JFET is **GOOD**. Any deviation indicates a **faulty** JFET that must be replaced.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks in this experiment:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to discharge the capacitor** before testing — examiners specifically look for this safety step in the record. Loss: 1 mark.
> 2. **Not mentioning the colour-code formula** $R = (A \times 10 + B) \times 10^{C} \pm D\,\%$ in the resistor test — examiners want the **formula written explicitly**, not just the decoded value. Loss: 2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing NPN and PNP** during the BJT test — students often forget to mention the *polarity* of the probe that produces the 0.6 V reading. The rule is: **Red lead on Base = NPN, Black lead on Base = PNP**. Loss: 2 marks.
> 4. **Using Ω mode on a polarised electrolytic without discharging** — the inrush current can damage the DMM's input protection or shift the reading. Always use a **1 kΩ discharge resistor**, not a screwdriver or finger. Loss: 1 mark.
> 5. **Not specifying the test current** of the DMM in Diode mode (typically 1 mA to 5 mA) — examiners appreciate the mention because it justifies why the LED does not glow brightly. Loss: 1 mark.
> 6. **Failing to identify the cathode stripe** of the diode as the reference for probe placement — examiners will deduct marks if the stripe is not mentioned. Loss: 1 mark.
> 7. **Writing "the reading is approximately 0.6 V"** without specifying that the DMM is in **Diode mode (▶⊢)**, not Ω mode. The reading in Ω mode on a forward-biased diode junction is meaningless and varies with current. Loss: 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final rapid-revision checklist for the GZESL208 Module 2 viva and lab exam:**

### Resistor Testing
- [ ] Always identify the **tolerance band (gold/silver)** first; read other bands left-to-right.
- [ ] Memorise the colour code: **Black = 0, Brown = 1, Red = 2, Orange = 3, Yellow = 4, Green = 5, Blue = 6, Violet = 7, Grey = 8, White = 9**.
- [ ] Use the formula $R = (A \times 10 + B) \times 10^{C} \pm D\,\%$.
- [ ] A shorted resistor reads **0 Ω**; an open resistor reads **OL**.

### Capacitor Testing
- [ ] **Always discharge** the capacitor first using a 1 kΩ bleed resistor.
- [ ] A healthy capacitor shows **nominal value ± 20 %** in capacitance mode.
- [ ] A healthy electrolytic shows a **charging transient** in Ω mode (rises to OL).
- [ ] Shorted → **0 Ω**; Open → **OL in Ω mode and 0 F in capacitance mode**; Leaky → **R_leak < 100 kΩ**.

### Diode Testing
- [ ] Cathode is marked with a **coloured stripe**; anode is the unmarked end.
- [ ] Forward bias (red on A, black on K): **V_F = 0.6 V to 0.7 V** for silicon.
- [ ] Reverse bias: **OL** (open).
- [ ] LED forward voltage is **1.8 V to 3.6 V** depending on colour.
- [ ] Germanium diodes have **V_F = 0.2 V to 0.3 V**.

### BJT Testing
- [ ] NPN: **red lead on Base** gives 0.6 V on both other pins.
- [ ] PNP: **black lead on Base** gives 0.6 V on both other pins.
- [ ] Use **hFE mode** to distinguish Emitter from Collector.
- [ ] E-C resistance should be **OL** in both directions; any reading < 1 MΩ is a leaky/faulty device.

### JFET Testing
- [ ] N-channel: **red lead on Gate** gives OL on both other pins (reverse biased).
- [ ] N-channel: **black lead on Gate** gives 0.6 V on both other pins.
- [ ] D-S channel resistance: **50 Ω to 500 Ω** for healthy small-signal JFETs.
- [ ] **DO NOT** test MOSFETs with a standard DMM in diode mode — risk of **gate-oxide damage**.

### General Workshop Safety
- [ ] **Unplug** the device under test; **remove the battery**.
- [ ] **Discharge** all capacitors before testing.
- [ ] **Use the correct socket**: red lead in **V/Ω**, black lead in **COM**.
- [ ] **Inspect test leads** for cracked insulation before each session.
- [ ] **Never apply mains voltage** to the Ω or Diode mode of the DMM — the input is fused but the fuse may blow, costing marks in the lab record.

### Single-Line Memory Hooks
- **"Stripe is Cathode, Current flows from Anode."**
- **"Red on Base = NPN, Black on Base = PNP."**
- **"Capacitor's first letter 'C' = Charge first, then measure."**
- **"Discharge, then Discharge again."**
- **"Silicon says 0.7, Germanium says 0.3."**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Printed circuit boards (PCB) - Types, single sided, double sided, processing methods; design and fabrication of a single sided PCB for a simple circuit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZESL208 - BASIC ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Printed circuit boards (PCB) - Types, single sided, double sided, processing methods; design and fabrication of a single sided PCB for a simple circuit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of a Printed Circuit Board (PCB)

A **Printed Circuit Board (PCB)** is a flat, rigid (or flexible) insulating substrate laminated with a thin layer of copper foil, on which conductive tracks, pads, and geometric patterns are etched or printed to mechanically support and electrically interconnect discrete electronic components such as resistors, capacitors, integrated circuits (ICs), transistors, and connectors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A PCB is the **physical foundation** of virtually all modern electronic circuits. It replaces the traditional *point-to-point wiring* (also called "rat's nest wiring") with a copper-foil conductor pattern bonded permanently to a dielectric base, providing both **mechanical rigidity** and **electrical connectivity** in a single, mass-producible platform.

### Standard PCB Stack-up (Single-Sided)

A typical single-sided PCB is composed of three primary layers:

| Layer | Function | Material |
|-------|----------|----------|
| **Substrate (Base)** | Provides mechanical strength and electrical insulation | **FR-2** (phenolic paper) or **FR-4** (fiberglass epoxy) |
| **Copper Foil** | Conductive layer that forms the circuit traces | Electro-deposited (ED) copper, typically **35 µm (1 oz/ft²)** |
| **Solder Mask / Silkscreen** | Protective green coating + component legend | Epoxy-based polymer + ink |

The standard copper thickness in KTU laboratory experiments is **1 oz/ft² ≈ 35 µm**, and the standard substrate thickness is **1.6 mm**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "City Road Network" Viewpoint

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of a PCB as the **road network of a city**, and the electronic components as the **buildings (houses, factories, power stations)** standing on the lots along those roads.
>
> - The **copper tracks** are the **roads** that allow electrical "traffic" (current) to flow between components.
> - The **substrate** is the **ground/earth** on which the city is built — it holds everything in place but does not conduct electricity itself.
> - The **solder mask** is the **paint and lane markings** on the road, protecting the copper from corrosion and accidental short-circuits (just as road paint prevents vehicles from going off-track).
> - The **vias and through-holes** are the **bridges and tunnels** that let traffic cross over or through barriers between layers.
> - The **components** (resistors, ICs, capacitors) are the **buildings** that do the actual "work" — they process the electricity flowing through the roads.

This analogy makes it easy to remember that a PCB is essentially a **"wired city" for electrons**, where every road (trace) is pre-planned, pre-drawn, and permanent.

## 1.3 Why PCBs Are Used in Modern Electronics

Before PCBs, electronic circuits were assembled using **hand-soldered wires** between components mounted on phenolic boards or terminal strips. This approach suffered from:

- **Inconsistent performance** due to wire placement variations
- **Poor reliability** under vibration and thermal stress
- **High parasitic capacitance and inductance** from long, unshielded wires
- **Bulky size** — not suitable for miniaturized products
- **Manual, error-prone manufacturing** that could not be scaled

PCBs solve all these problems by providing:

> [!NOTE]
> - **Repeatable, predictable electrical performance** (controlled impedance)
> - **Mass-producibility** (one artwork → thousands of identical boards)
> - **Mechanical stability** under shock, vibration, and thermal cycling
> - **Compactness** — components can be placed on both sides of the board
> - **Lower assembly cost** through automated component placement and wave/reflow soldering

## 1.4 Historical Context (Brief)

The PCB was invented by **Paul Eisler** in **1936** in Austria, but it was not widely adopted until after World War II. The first major application was in the U.S. military's *proximity fuse* during WWII. By the 1960s, PCBs became standard in consumer electronics (radios, televisions), and by the 1980s, **Surface Mount Technology (SMT)** further revolutionized PCB design by allowing components to be soldered directly onto copper pads without through-holes.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types of Printed Circuit Boards (Comprehensive Classification)

PCBs are classified based on **two main criteria**: (1) the number of conductive layers, and (2) the mechanical flexibility of the substrate.

### 2.1.1 Classification Based on Number of Copper Layers

#### A. Single-Sided PCB (SSPCB)

- **Copper layer count:** One (only the top side)
- **Substrate:** Phenolic paper (FR-2) or fiberglass (FR-4)
- **Component mounting:** Through-hole only (in most low-cost designs)
- **Conductor width:** Typically **0.5 mm to 2 mm**
- **Applications:** Power supplies, calculators, LED lighting, radio circuits, toys, low-frequency consumer electronics
- **Cost:** Lowest
- **KTU Focus:** This is the **primary type fabricated in the KTU workshop lab experiment**

**Structure of Single-Sided PCB (cross-section view):**
```
   ┌─────────────────────────┐  ← Solder mask (green coating)
   ├─────────────────────────┤
   │   Copper traces (1 oz)  │  ← Conductive layer
   ├─────────────────────────┤
   │   Substrate (FR-2/FR-4) │  ← Insulating base
   └─────────────────────────┘
```

#### B. Double-Sided PCB (DSPCB)

- **Copper layer count:** Two (both top and bottom sides)
- **Substrate:** Typically FR-4 (fiberglass epoxy)
- **Vias required:** Plated Through-Holes (PTH) to connect top and bottom layers
- **Component mounting:** Through-hole and/or Surface Mount Technology (SMT)
- **Applications:** Computer motherboards, amplifiers, industrial controllers, automotive ECUs
- **Cost:** Moderate

**Structure of Double-Sided PCB:**
```
   ┌─────────────────────────┐  ← Top solder mask
   ├─────────────────────────┤
   │   Top copper layer      │
   ├─────────────────────────┤
   │   Substrate (FR-4)      │
   ├─────────────────────────┤
   │   Bottom copper layer   │
   ├─────────────────────────┤
   └─────────────────────────┘  ← Bottom solder mask
```

#### C. Multi-Layer PCB

- **Copper layer count:** 4, 6, 8, 10, or even 20+ layers
- **Structure:** Alternating copper and prepreg (pre-impregnated fiberglass) layers, laminated under heat and pressure
- **Internal layers:** Power planes, ground planes, signal layers
- **Applications:** Smartphones, laptops, server motherboards, aerospace electronics, 5G equipment
- **Cost:** Highest
- **Manufacturing complexity:** Requires precise layer alignment and lamination under vacuum

### 2.1.2 Classification Based on Substrate Flexibility

| Type | Substrate | Key Property | Typical Application |
|------|-----------|--------------|---------------------|
| **Rigid PCB** | FR-4, FR-2, CEM-1, CEM-3 | Hard, does not bend | Desktop computers, TVs |
| **Flexible PCB (Flex PCB)** | Polyimide (Kapton), PET | Can bend and fold | Smartphones, cameras, wearables |
| **Rigid-Flex PCB** | Combination of FR-4 + polyimide | Rigid in some zones, flexible in others | Medical devices, aerospace |
| **High-Frequency PCB** | PTFE (Teflon), Rogers | Low dielectric loss at GHz frequencies | Radar, satellite, RF modules |
| **Metal-Core PCB (MCPCB)** | Aluminum or copper base | Excellent heat dissipation | High-power LEDs, automotive headlights |

### 2.1.3 KTU-High-Yield Comparison Table

| Property | Single-Sided | Double-Sided | Multi-Layer |
|----------|--------------|--------------|-------------|
| Copper layers | 1 | 2 | 4 to 20+ |
| Circuit density | Low | Medium | High |
| Cost | Lowest | Moderate | High |
| Manufacturing time | Hours | 1–2 days | 1–3 weeks |
| Suitable for | Simple circuits | Medium complexity | High-speed digital |
| KTU lab feasibility | **Yes (most common)** | Limited | Not feasible in lab |

## 2.2 Processing Methods for PCB Fabrication

There are **three principal processing methods** for manufacturing PCBs. KTU lab experiments focus on the **chemical etching (subtractive) method**, but students must know all three.

### 2.2.1 Subtractive Method (Etching) — Most Common

In the **subtractive method**, the unwanted copper is *removed* chemically from the board, leaving only the desired trace pattern protected by an etch resist.

**Workflow:**
1. Cut copper-clad laminate to required size
2. Clean and polish the copper surface
3. Apply etch resist (photoresist, toner, or permanent marker) in the pattern of the desired circuit
4. Etch in ferric chloride (FeCl₃) solution to remove unprotected copper
5. Wash, drill holes, and apply solder mask

> [!NOTE]
> **Chemistry of Etching:**
> The reaction of copper with ferric chloride is:
> $$\text{Cu} + 2\text{FeCl}_3 \rightarrow \text{CuCl}_2 + 2\text{FeCl}_2$$
> The etchant works by **oxidizing metallic copper to copper(II) chloride**, which is soluble in water. Etching temperature is typically **40–50 °C**, and etching time is **15–30 minutes** depending on copper thickness and etchant concentration.

### 2.2.2 Additive Method (Plating Up)

In the **additive method**, copper is *deposited* (plated) only where the circuit pattern is desired, using electroless or electroplating techniques. There is no bulk copper removal step.

**Advantages:**
- Less chemical waste
- Sharper trace edges
- Better for fine-pitch designs

**Disadvantages:**
- Slower process
- Requires controlled chemistry

### 2.2.3 Semi-Additive Process (SAP) — Modern Industry Standard

Used for **High-Density Interconnect (HDI)** boards. Combines a thin electroless copper base layer with electroplating to build up traces, followed by differential etching of unwanted base copper.

## 2.3 Materials Required for Single-Sided PCB Fabrication (KTU Lab List)

| Material / Equipment | Purpose | Specification |
|---------------------|---------|---------------|
| **Copper-clad laminate** | Base material with copper foil | FR-2, 1.6 mm thick, 1 oz/ft² copper |
| **Ferric chloride (FeCl₃)** | Etchant solution | 30–40% concentration in water |
| **Laser printer / Photocopier** | Artwork transfer | 600 dpi minimum |
| **Glossy paper / Photo paper** | Toner transfer medium | 90–120 gsm glossy |
| **Iron box / Laminator** | Heat-transfer of toner | 150–200 °C |
| **Drilling machine** | Hole making for component leads | 0.8 mm to 1.2 mm bits |
| **Sandpaper / Steel wool** | Surface cleaning | 400–600 grit |
| **Acetone / Thinner** | Toner removal after etching | Industrial grade |
| **Permanent marker** | Manual trace drawing (backup) | Black, fine tip |
| **Plastic tray** | Etching container | Non-reactive (PP/PVC) |
| **PCB cutter / hacksaw** | Board sizing | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety Note:** Ferric chloride is **corrosive and stains skin/clothing permanently**. Always wear **rubber gloves, safety goggles, and an apron** while handling it. Etching must be done in a **well-ventilated area** or under a fume hood.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Key Design Parameters

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes the **fundamental design rules and formulas** that govern PCB trace geometry, current-carrying capacity, and fabrication tolerances. These are essential for solving KTU numerical/design problems.

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Value | Unit | Notes |
|-----------|--------|-----------------|------|-------|
| **Trace width** | $w$ | $w = \frac{A}{t}$ | mm | $A$ = trace cross-section area, $t$ = copper thickness |
| **Current capacity** | $I$ | $I = k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44} \cdot A^{0.725}$ (IPC-2152) | A | $k \approx 0.048$ for internal, $0.024$ for external |
| **Trace resistance** | $R$ | $R = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{A} = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{w \cdot t}$ | Ω | $\rho_{\text{Cu}} = 1.68 \times 10^{-8}$ Ω·m |
| **Voltage drop** | $V_d$ | $V_d = I \cdot R$ | V | Used to verify power integrity |
| **Etch factor** | EF | $\text{EF} = \frac{\text{Depth}}{\text{Undercut}}$ | ratio | Typical EF = 1.5 to 3.0 |
| **Minimum trace width** | $w_{\min}$ | $\geq 0.2$ mm (lab) / 0.1 mm (industry) | mm | Limited by toner resolution |
| **Minimum spacing** | $s_{\min}$ | $\geq 0.2$ mm (lab) / 0.1 mm (industry) | mm | Prevents short circuits |
| **Hole diameter (drilled)** | $D_{\text{hole}}$ | $D_{\text{hole}} = D_{\text{pad}} - 2 \cdot r_{\text{annular}}$ | mm | $r_{\text{annular}} \geq 0.15$ mm |
| **Pad diameter** | $D_{\text{pad}}$ | $D_{\text{pad}} \geq 2 \cdot D_{\text{hole}}$ | mm | Standard for through-hole |
| **Aspect ratio (drill)** | AR | $\text{AR} = \frac{\text{Board thickness}}{D_{\text{hole}}}$ | ratio | AR $\leq 10$ for reliable plating |
| **Copper weight** | CW | $1 \text{ oz/ft}^2 = 35$ µm thickness | µm | Standard KTU lab value |
| **Dielectric constant (FR-4)** | $\varepsilon_r$ | $4.2$ to $4.6$ at 1 MHz | — | Affects trace impedance |
| **Skin depth at 100 MHz** | $\delta$ | $\delta = \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\pi \cdot f \cdot \mu_0}}$ | µm | $\delta \approx 6.6$ µm for Cu at 100 MHz |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In examination numericals, students often forget to **convert copper thickness to meters** when using the resistivity formula. Always write $t = 35 \times 10^{-6}$ m, not 35 mm or 35 µm directly in the equation.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

PCBs are the backbone of every modern electronic device — from a **$5 digital watch** to a **$100 million satellite**. Mastering PCB design and fabrication gives a B.Tech engineer the ability to:

- Prototype custom circuits for academic projects, IoT devices, and embedded systems
- Design compact, reliable products for startups and R&D labs
- Understand signal integrity, EMI/EMC, and thermal management
- Transition smoothly into industries like consumer electronics, automotive (especially **EVs**), medical devices, and **5G/6G telecommunications**
