# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix — Fabrication of a Single-Sided PCB

## 3.1 The "Hello World" Example Circuit

To make this experiment concrete, let us design a **simple LED indicator circuit** powered by a 9 V battery. This is one of the most commonly fabricated PCBs in KTU workshops.

### 3.1.1 Circuit Specification

| Component | Value | Function |
|-----------|-------|----------|
| **R₁** (Resistor) | 470 Ω, ¼ W | Limits current through LED |
| **D₁** (LED) | Red, 5 mm, V_F = 2 V, I_F = 20 mA | Visual indicator |
| **Battery** | 9 V (e.g., Duracell) | Power source |
| **Connector** | 2-pin screw terminal | Power input |

**Current calculation (theoretical verification):**
$$I = \frac{V_{CC} - V_F}{R_1} = \frac{9 - 2}{470} = \frac{7}{470} \approx 14.89 \text{ mA}$$

This is well below the LED's maximum forward current of 20 mA, so the design is safe.

**Power dissipated by the resistor:**
$$P_{R_1} = I^2 \cdot R_1 = (14.89 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times 470 \approx 0.104 \text{ W}$$

Since this is less than 0.25 W, a standard ¼ W resistor is sufficient.

## 3.2 Detailed Step-by-Step Fabrication Procedure (Analytical Matrix)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following is a **complete, exhaustive, lab-ready procedure** suitable for a 2–3 hour KTU workshop session. Every step is explained with the underlying reasoning, the equipment used, the time required, and the expected outcome.

### Step 1: Circuit Design and Schematic Capture

**Objective:** Convert the circuit idea into a precise schematic diagram.

- **Tool used:** Pen and paper (or free software like KiCad, EasyEDA, or Proteus)
- **Output:** A schematic showing R₁, D₁, and the battery connected in series
- **Time required:** 10–15 minutes
- **Outcome:** A clear drawing with component symbols, values, and net connections

**Schematic representation:**
```
        +9V
         |
         |   R₁ = 470 Ω
         ├──/\/\/\──┐
         |          |
         |          |  ▶|─  D₁ (LED, anode → cathode)
         |          |
         └──────────┴──── GND
```

### Step 2: Component Layout and PCB Track Routing

**Objective:** Physically arrange the components on the board and draw the copper traces that will connect them.

- **Tool used:** Graph paper or PCB design software
- **Considerations:**
   - Place components with **at least 2 mm edge clearance** from board edges
   - Maintain **trace-to-trace spacing ≥ 0.5 mm** to avoid shorts during etching
   - Use **45° bends** (never 90° right angles) for trace corners — this reduces acid traps during etching and avoids signal reflection at high frequencies
   - Mount the **battery connector** at the board edge for easy access
   - Place the **LED** with the anode (longer lead, A) and cathode (shorter lead, K) clearly marked

**Layout (top view, actual size for a 5 cm × 4 cm board):**
```
   ┌──────────────────────────┐
   │  ┌──┐                    │
   │  │  ● R₁    ▶|─          │
   │  └──┘   (LED)             │
   │   ○           ●           │
   │  (+)         (-)          │
   │   ●───●───●───●           │
   └──────────────────────────┘
```

### Step 3: Artwork Generation (Print the PCB Pattern)

**Objective:** Print the circuit pattern at **exact 1:1 scale** onto glossy paper using a **laser printer** (inkjet will not work — its ink dissolves in water and during etching).

- **Tool used:** Laser printer (HP/Canon), glossy photo paper
- **Critical settings:**
   - Print at **600 dpi or higher** for sharp trace edges
   - Set printer to **"Plain Paper"** mode but with **glossy paper** loaded manually
   - **Mirror the image** if using the *toner-transfer method* (Step 4)
   - **Do not mirror** if using the *photoresist method*

- **Time required:** 5 minutes
- **Outcome:** A glossy paper print with the **black toner** representing the desired copper pattern

### Step 4: Toner Transfer onto the Copper Board

**Objective:** Transfer the toner pattern from the paper onto the copper-clad board using heat.

- **Tools:** Iron box (set to **cotton/linen mode, ~180 °C**) or a **laminator**
- **Procedure:**
   1. Cut the copper-clad board to size (e.g., 5 cm × 4 cm) using a hacksaw or PCB cutter
   2. Sand the copper surface with **400-grit sandpaper** in a circular motion until it is bright and shiny — this removes oxidation and improves toner adhesion
   3. Clean the board with **acetone** to remove grease and fingerprints
   4. Place the printed glossy paper **toner-side down** onto the copper surface
   5. Iron the back of the paper firmly for **2–3 minutes**, applying even pressure. The heat melts the toner, and the pressure bonds it to the copper.
   6. Allow the board to **cool completely** (do not peel while hot)
   7. Slowly peel the paper. Most of the toner should remain on the copper. If parts are missing, touch them up with a **permanent marker**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why glossy paper?** Glossy paper has a **polyethylene or clay coating** that prevents the toner from soaking deep into the paper fibers. When heated, the toner sits on the surface and releases cleanly onto the copper, producing a sharp pattern. Regular paper absorbs the toner and leaves ragged edges.

### Step 5: Etching in Ferric Chloride Solution

**Objective:** Chemically remove all **unprotected copper**, leaving only the trace pattern.

- **Equipment:** Plastic tray (polypropylene or PVC — **never metal**), ferric chloride solution, warm water bath (optional)
- **Procedure:**
   1. Pour ferric chloride into the plastic tray to a depth of ~1 cm
   2. Optionally, place the tray in a warm water bath at **40–50 °C** to speed up etching (but never above 60 °C, as the etchant decomposes)
   3. Submerge the board **copper-side up** in the etchant
   4. **Gently agitate** the tray every 2–3 minutes (rocking or using a plastic stirrer) to remove bubbles from the trace edges
   5. Wait **15–30 minutes** until all unwanted copper has dissolved — you will see the shiny copper disappear, leaving a dull brown substrate where the toner-protected traces remain
   6. Remove the board with **tongs or plastic tweezers** (never bare hands) and rinse thoroughly under running water
   7. **Optional:** Test with a multimeter — all non-trace areas should show **infinite resistance** (no continuity)

> [!WARNING]
> **Over-etching** is a common error. If you leave the board in the etchant too long, the **edges of the traces are also attacked (undercut)**, narrowing the trace width and increasing resistance. **Under-etching** leaves copper islands that can cause short circuits. Check the board every 5 minutes.

### Step 6: Toner Removal and Surface Cleaning

**Objective:** Strip the protective toner from the traces, revealing the bright copper pattern.

- **Tools:** Acetone, cotton swabs, soft cloth
- **Procedure:** Soak a cotton swab in acetone and rub gently over the board. The toner dissolves and comes off, leaving shiny copper traces.
- **Time required:** 2–3 minutes
- **Outcome:** A clean, bright PCB with the desired circuit pattern in copper

### Step 7: Drilling Holes for Component Leads

**Objective:** Drill holes at the pad locations for through-hole components.

- **Equipment:** PCB drilling machine (preferred) or a hand-held Dremel / mini drill
- **Drill bit sizes:**
   - **Resistor and LED leads:** 0.8 mm or 1.0 mm
   - **Battery connector pins:** 1.2 mm
- **Procedure:**
   1. Place the board on a soft wooden base (to avoid breaking the drill bit)
   2. Drill **slowly** with light pressure — copper and FR-2 are abrasive and can snap thin bits
   3. After drilling, **deburr** the holes by lightly sanding the bottom side

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Lab Tip:** If a PCB drilling machine is not available, holes can be made with a **handheld iron nail and hammer** — but this is crude and may crack the board. A small hand drill (Dremel) is the second-best option.

### Step 8: Component Mounting and Soldering

**Objective:** Insert the components into the drilled holes and solder them in place.

- **Procedure:**
   1. Insert R₁ (resistor) — bend leads at 90° using long-nose pliers, push leads through holes
   2. Insert D₁ (LED) — observe **polarity**: the longer lead (anode) goes to the resistor side
   3. Mount the 2-pin screw terminal
   4. Flip the board and solder each lead using a **soldering iron (25–40 W)** and **60/40 lead-tin solder with rosin core**
   5. **Soldering time per joint:** 2–3 seconds (longer than this can lift the copper pad)
   6. After soldering, **clip the excess leads** with a side cutter

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Soldering Iron Tip Temperature:** For lead-tin solder, the tip temperature should be **300–350 °C**. For lead-free SAC305 solder, use **350–400 °C**. Always **tin the tip** (apply a small amount of solder) before and after use to prevent oxidation.

### Step 9: Testing and Verification

**Objective:** Verify that the fabricated PCB functions correctly.

- **Tools:** 9 V battery, multimeter
- **Procedure:**
   1. Connect the battery to the screw terminal — **red wire to (+), black to (−)**
   2. The LED should light up immediately
   3. If it does not, check for:
      - **Cold solder joints** (re-heat each joint with fresh solder)
      - **Reversed LED polarity** (reverse the LED)
      - **Broken trace** (use a multimeter in continuity mode to check the trace)
      - **Short circuit** between adjacent traces (visually inspect for copper bridges)

**Expected observation:** A **bright red LED glows at approximately 15 mA**, confirming that the PCB is functional.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Time, Materials, and Expected Yield

| Step | Duration | Critical Parameter | Common Failure Mode |
|------|----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1. Schematic | 10 min | Correct component values | Wrong resistor value → LED burnout |
| 2. Layout | 15 min | Trace spacing ≥ 0.5 mm | Traces too close → short during etching |
| 3. Printing | 5 min | Laser printer, glossy paper | Inkjet print → toner washes off in etchant |
| 4. Toner transfer | 5 min | Iron temperature, even pressure | Uneven heat → partial transfer |
| 5. Etching | 20 min | Agitation, temperature control | Over-etch → traces dissolve; Under-etch → shorts |
| 6. Toner removal | 3 min | Acetone purity | Incomplete removal → poor solder wetting |
| 7. Drilling | 10 min | Bit size, slow speed | Snapped bit, misaligned holes |
| 8. Soldering | 15 min | Iron temperature, flux | Cold joints, lifted pads |
| 9. Testing | 5 min | Correct polarity | Reversed LED does not light |

**Total lab time:** ~90 minutes for a single board (first attempt)

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These questions test the **Remember** and **Understand** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy, aligned with **CO1 / CO2** of the KTU 2024 syllabus for GZESL208.

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] — 3 Marks

**"Define a Printed Circuit Board. List any four advantages of using a PCB over conventional point-to-point wiring."**

**Model Answer:**

A Printed Circuit Board (PCB) is a flat, insulating substrate (typically FR-2 or FR-4) laminated with a thin layer of copper foil, on which conductive tracks, pads, and component-mounting holes are etched or printed to provide **mechanical support** and **electrical interconnection** for electronic components.

**Four advantages over point-to-point wiring:**

1. **Compactness:** Components can be placed very close together on both sides, enabling miniaturized products.
2. **Repeatability:** The same artwork produces thousands of identical boards, ensuring uniform performance.
3. **Reliability:** Permanent soldered connections resist vibration, shock, and thermal cycling better than hand-wired joints.
4. **Mass-producibility:** Automated component placement and wave/reflow soldering drastically reduce assembly time and cost.

*(Acceptable substitutes: reduced wiring errors, better high-frequency performance, easier troubleshooting, lower parasitic effects.)*

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — 3 Marks

**"Differentiate between single-sided and double-sided PCBs in terms of construction, cost, and application."**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Single-Sided PCB | Double-Sided PCB |
|-----------|------------------|------------------|
| **Copper layers** | One (on top side only) | Two (on both top and bottom) |
| **Holes / Vias** | Only component through-holes | Plated Through-Holes (PTH) connect top and bottom layers |
| **Component density** | Low (one side only) | Medium (both sides can carry components) |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Higher (requires PTH plating) |
| **Typical application** | Power supplies, toys, calculators, radios | Computer motherboards, amplifiers, automotive ECUs |
| **KTU lab feasibility** | **Yes (most common)** | Limited (requires chemical plating) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following two questions represent the **standard KTU 2024 ESE pattern** for a 14-mark question. Each has two sub-parts of **7 marks each**. Students must answer **either Option A OR Option B** (internal choice as per KTU regulations).

### Option A — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a) Explain with neat sketches the classification of PCBs based on (i) number of layers and (ii) flexibility of substrate. Mention at least two applications for each type. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

PCBs can be classified based on **two main criteria**:

#### (i) Based on Number of Conductive Layers

**1. Single-Sided PCB (SSPCB):**
- One copper layer on one side of the substrate.
- **Applications:** Calculators, LED drivers, FM radio circuits, low-power SMPS.

**2. Double-Sided PCB (DSPCB):**
- Copper on both sides, connected via **Plated Through-Holes (PTH)**.
- **Applications:** Computer motherboards, audio amplifiers, automotive dashboard electronics.

**3. Multi-Layer PCB:**
- 4 to 20+ layers of copper, separated by prepreg and core laminates.
- Internal layers act as **ground planes** and **power planes**.
- **Applications:** Smartphones, laptops, 5G base stations, server boards.

#### (ii) Based on Substrate Flexibility

**1. Rigid PCB:**
- Hard substrate (FR-4, FR-2, CEM-1).
- **Applications:** Desktop computers, TVs, industrial controllers.

**2. Flexible PCB (Flex PCB):**
- Polyimide (Kapton) substrate; can bend repeatedly.
- **Applications:** Smartphones (display-to-motherboard flex), wearable devices, foldable electronics.

**3. Rigid-Flex PCB:**
- Combination of rigid and flexible zones in one board.
- **Applications:** Medical implants, aerospace control systems, military radios.

**4. High-Frequency PCB:**
- PTFE (Teflon) or Rogers substrate with low dielectric loss.
- **Applications:** Radar, satellite communication, mmWave 5G modules.

**5. Metal-Core PCB (MCPCB):**
- Aluminum or copper base for heat dissipation.
- **Applications:** High-power LED lighting, EV battery management, motor drives.

*(Neat sketch must show cross-sections of each type, with copper layers highlighted.)*

---

**(b) Describe the step-by-step procedure for fabricating a single-sided PCB for a simple LED indicator circuit (9 V supply, 470 Ω resistor, red LED). Include the materials used, the chemistry of etching, and safety precautions. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Circuit:** 9 V battery → 470 Ω resistor → red LED (V_F = 2 V, I_F = 15 mA) → back to battery.

**Step 1 — Schematic and Layout:** Draw the schematic and place components on a 5 cm × 4 cm board with proper spacing (≥ 2 mm from edges, ≥ 0.5 mm between traces).

**Step 2 — Artwork Printing:** Print the trace pattern at 1:1 scale on **glossy paper** using a **laser printer** (600 dpi).

**Step 3 — Board Preparation:** Cut the copper-clad laminate (FR-2, 1.6 mm, 1 oz copper) to size. Sand the copper surface with 400-grit sandpaper until shiny. Clean with acetone.

**Step 4 — Toner Transfer:** Place the print toner-side down on the copper and iron at **180 °C for 3 minutes** with firm pressure. Cool and peel slowly.

**Step 5 — Etching:** Submerge the board in **ferric chloride (FeCl₃) solution at 40–50 °C for 20 minutes**, agitating gently every 2–3 minutes.

**Chemistry:**
$$\text{Cu} + 2\text{FeCl}_3 \rightarrow \text{CuCl}_2 + 2\text{FeCl}_2$$

**Step 6 — Cleaning and Drilling:** Rinse the board, remove toner with acetone, and drill **0.8 mm holes** at the component pad locations.

**Step 7 — Component Mounting and Soldering:** Insert R₁, D₁ (observe polarity: long lead = anode), and the 2-pin terminal. Solder using a **25–40 W iron at 300 °C** with **60/40 Pb-Sn solder**.

**Step 8 — Testing:** Connect a 9 V battery — the LED should glow at ~15 mA. Verify with a multimeter.

**Safety Precautions:**
- Wear **rubber gloves and safety goggles** while handling FeCl₃
- Etch in a **well-ventilated area** or fume hood
- **Never** use metal containers for etchant
- Wash hands thoroughly after the experiment
- Dispose of used etchant as **hazardous chemical waste**, not in regular drains

---

### Option B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a) With the help of a neat flowchart, explain the toner-transfer method of fabricating a single-sided PCB. List the materials required and mention two common defects that may arise at each major step. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Materials Required:**
- Copper-clad laminate (FR-2, 1.6 mm, 1 oz/ft²)
- Laser printer, glossy paper
- Iron box (180 °C)
- Ferric chloride (FeCl₃) solution, plastic tray
- Acetone, sandpaper (400-grit)
- PCB drill, drill bits (0.8 mm, 1.0 mm)
- Soldering iron (25–40 W), 60/40 solder

**Toner-Transfer Method Flowchart:**

1. **Design PCB layout** (KiCad / manual on graph paper)
2. **Print artwork** on glossy paper using laser printer (mirror image)
3. **Clean and sand** copper surface (removes oxide, improves adhesion)
4. **Iron transfer** of toner (180 °C, 3 min, firm pressure)
5. **Cool and peel** paper (do not peel while hot)
6. **Inspect pattern** — touch up missing areas with permanent marker
7. **Etch in FeCl₃** at 40–50 °C, 15–30 min, with agitation
8. **Rinse and remove toner** with acetone
9. **Drill holes** at pad locations
10. **Mount and solder** components
11. **Test** with power supply / battery

**Common Defects at Each Step:**

| Step | Defect 1 | Defect 2 |
|------|----------|----------|
| Printing | Inkjet used (toner washes off) | Wrong scale (not 1:1) |
| Toner transfer | Uneven heating (partial transfer) | Paper peeled too early (smudges pattern) |
| Etching | Over-etching (traces too thin) | Under-etching (copper islands cause shorts) |
| Drilling | Bit snaps in hole | Misaligned holes break pad |
| Soldering | Cold joint (dull, grainy) | Solder bridge between adjacent pads |

---

**(b) For the PCB designed in part (a), calculate: (i) the current through the LED, (ii) the power dissipated in the resistor, and (iii) the trace resistance of a 10 cm long, 1 mm wide copper trace of standard 1 oz/ft² thickness. Assume $\rho_{\text{Cu}} = 1.68 \times 10^{-8}$ Ω·m. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Supply voltage $V_{CC} = 9$ V
- Resistor $R_1 = 470$ Ω
- LED forward voltage $V_F = 2$ V
- LED forward current $I_F = 20$ mA (max)
- Trace length $L = 10$ cm $= 0.1$ m
- Trace width $w = 1$ mm $= 1 \times 10^{-3}$ m
- Copper thickness $t = 35$ µm $= 35 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $\rho_{Cu} = 1.68 \times 10^{-8}$ Ω·m

**(i) Current through the LED:**

Applying KVL around the loop:
$$V_{CC} = V_F + I \cdot R_1$$

$$I = \frac{V_{CC} - V_F}{R_1} = \frac{9 - 2}{470} = \frac{7}{470}$$

$$I \approx 0.01489 \text{ A} = 14.89 \text{ mA}$$

This is **below the LED's maximum rating** of 20 mA, confirming the design is safe.

**(ii) Power dissipated in the resistor:**

$$P_{R_1} = I^2 \cdot R_1 = (0.01489)^2 \times 470$$

$$P_{R_1} = 2.217 \times 10^{-4} \times 470 = 0.1042 \text{ W}$$

A **¼ W (0.25 W) resistor** is sufficient with a comfortable safety margin.

**(iii) Trace resistance of the 10 cm copper trace:**

Cross-sectional area:
$$A = w \cdot t = (1 \times 10^{-3}) \times (35 \times 10^{-6}) = 35 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^2$$

Resistance:
$$R_{\text{trace}} = \frac{\rho \cdot L}{A} = \frac{1.68 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.1}{35 \times 10^{-9}}$$

$$R_{\text{trace}} = \frac{1.68 \times 10^{-9}}{35 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{1.68}{35}$$

$$R_{\text{trace}} = 0.048 \text{ Ω}$$

**Voltage drop on the trace at 14.89 mA:**
$$V_d = I \cdot R_{\text{trace}} = 0.01489 \times 0.048 \approx 0.715 \text{ mV}$$

This drop is **negligible** compared to the 7 V drop across the resistor, confirming that trace resistance is not a concern at low currents.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Wrong unit conversions** in numerical problems — always convert copper thickness to **meters** ($35 \times 10^{-6}$ m, not 35 µm directly in the formula)
> 2. **Confusing single-sided with double-sided** — in SSPCB, only the top side has copper; in DSPCB, both sides have copper joined by PTH vias
> 3. **Forgetting safety precautions** — FeCl₃ is corrosive; examiners specifically look for "rubber gloves, fume hood, plastic tray" in your answer
> 4. **Skipping steps in fabrication** — never jump from "printing" to "etching"; you MUST mention board cutting, sanding, cleaning, transfer, and inspection
> 5. **Wrong chemistry equation** — the correct equation is $\text{Cu} + 2\text{FeCl}_3 \rightarrow \text{CuCl}_2 + 2\text{FeCl}_2$. Writing it as $\text{Cu} + \text{FeCl}_3 \rightarrow \text{CuCl}_3$ will fetch zero marks
> 6. **Missing neat sketches** — KTU examiners allocate **1–2 marks** specifically for diagrams; always draw a labeled cross-section and a flowchart
> 7. **Not mentioning the reason behind each step** — explaining *why* you sand the board (to remove oxide) or *why* you use glossy paper (to get sharp toner release) earns extra understanding marks

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary — Read This Before the Exam!**

- A **PCB** is a copper-clad insulating board with etched conductive tracks that mechanically support and electrically interconnect electronic components
- **Single-sided PCB** has **one copper layer** (on top); cheapest and most common in KTU labs
- **Double-sided PCB** has **two copper layers** joined by **plated through-holes (PTH)**
- **Multi-layer PCBs** have **4 to 20+ layers**; used in smartphones, laptops, and high-speed digital systems
- **Rigid PCBs** use FR-2/FR-4; **flex PCBs** use polyimide (Kapton); **MCPCBs** use aluminum for heat dissipation
- The **three processing methods** are: (1) **Subtractive (etching)**, (2) **Additive (plating up)**, (3) **Semi-additive (SAP)**
- **Standard copper thickness** in KTU lab = **1 oz/ft² = 35 µm**
- **Standard substrate thickness** = **1.6 mm**
- **Standard trace width** in lab = **0.5 mm to 2 mm**
- **Minimum trace spacing** = **0.5 mm** (lab) / **0.1 mm** (industry)
- **Etchant used in KTU lab = Ferric chloride (FeCl₃)**
- **Etching temperature** = **40–50 °C**, duration = **15–30 min**
- **Etching reaction:** $\text{Cu} + 2\text{FeCl}_3 \rightarrow \text{CuCl}_2 + 2\text{FeCl}_2$
- **Toner transfer** uses **laser printer + glossy paper + iron at 180 °C**
- **Hole drilling** uses **0.8–1.2 mm drill bits**
- **Soldering iron temperature** = **300–350 °C** (lead-tin) or **350–400 °C** (lead-free)
- **Solder alloy** = **60/40 Pb-Sn** (lab) or **SAC305** (industry, lead-free)
- **Trace resistance formula:** $R = \rho L / (w \cdot t)$ with $\rho_{Cu} = 1.68 \times 10^{-8}$ Ω·m
- **Current capacity formula** (IPC-2152): $I = k \cdot \Delta T^{0.44} \cdot A^{0.725}$
- **Use 45° bends**, never 90° right angles, on trace corners
- **Always wear gloves and goggles** when handling FeCl₃
- **Dispose of used etchant** as **hazardous chemical waste** — never pour down the drain
- **Common defects:** over-etching (thin traces), under-etching (shorts), cold joints (dull solder), reversed LED polarity
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inter-connection methods and soldering practice (Bread board, Wrapping, Crimping, Soldering - types, selection of materials and safety precautions; soldering practice in connectors and general-purpose PCB, Crimping)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZESL208 - BASIC ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Inter-connection methods and soldering practice (Bread board, Wrapping, Crimping, Soldering - types, selection of materials and safety precautions; soldering practice in connectors and general-purpose PCB, Crimping)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Inter-Connection Methods?

In any electronic or electrical circuit, individual components such as **resistors**, **capacitors**, **transistors**, **integrated circuits (ICs)**, and **connectors** must be electrically and mechanically joined to form a functional system. The techniques used to achieve these permanent or semi-permanent joints are collectively termed **Inter-Connection Methods**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *Inter-connection methods* refer to the standardized electrical, electronic, and mechanical techniques — **Bread boarding**, **Wire Wrapping**, **Crimping**, and **Soldering** — used to establish reliable electrical continuity between components, wires, and PCBs in laboratory and industrial environments.

The four primary methods covered in this KTU workshop module are:

1. **Bread Board** (Solderless prototyping)
2. **Wire Wrapping** (Solderless, gas-tight mechanical connection)
3. **Crimping** (Mechanical deformation-based connection)
4. **Soldering** (Metallurgical alloy bonding)

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Plumbing Analogy"** — Think of an electronic circuit like a building's water plumbing system. Wires are the pipes, components are the valves and taps, and joints are the pipe-fittings. Just as a plumber chooses between glue-joints, compression-fittings, threaded joints, or push-fit connectors depending on whether the joint is temporary, permanent, or high-pressure, an electronics engineer chooses between soldering, crimping, wire-wrapping, or bread-boarding depending on **current rating**, **mechanical stress**, **reworkability**, and **durability** requirements.

| Method | Real-World Analogy | Permanence |
|---|---|---|
| Bread Board | LEGO blocks — snap-together, easily rearranged | Temporary |
| Wire Wrapping | Twisting a wire tightly around a screw post | Semi-permanent |
| Crimping | Squeezing a metal sleeve onto two pipes (like a compression fitting) | Permanent |
| Soldering | Welding two metal pipes with molten filler metal | Permanent |

---

### 1.3 Key Terminology & Physical Standards

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Vocabulary for KTU Board Exams:**
> - **Solder**: A fusible metal alloy (typically **Tin-Lead (Sn-Pb)** or **Lead-Free (SAC — Sn-Ag-Cu)**) used to create a permanent metallic bond between two workpieces.
> - **Flux**: A chemical cleaning agent (rosin-based) that removes oxides from metal surfaces during soldering, enabling a clean metallurgical bond.
> - **Crimp**: A mechanical deformation of a metal terminal around a conductor to form a gas-tight, low-resistance electrical joint.
> - **Soldering Iron Temperature**: Typically maintained between **300°C and 400°C** for lead-based solder and **350°C to 400°C** for lead-free solder.
> - **AWG (American Wire Gauge)**: Standardized wire-sizing system (e.g., **AWG 22**, **AWG 24**, **AWG 26**) used to select proper wire diameters for each interconnection method.

---

### 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in the KTU Workshop

The KTU 2024 Scheme laboratory exam for GZESL208 directly evaluates a student's ability to:
- Identify and select the **correct interconnection method** for a given application.
- Demonstrate **safe soldering practice** on a **general-purpose PCB** and on **connectors**.
- Perform a **mechanical crimp** on a terminal lug using a proper crimping tool.
- Follow all **electrical and personal safety precautions** in a workshop environment.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners frequently award or deduct **1 to 2 marks** purely on the basis of whether the student mentions **safety precautions** and **correct tool/material selection** in their viva-voce and record. Always include these in your answers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Bread Board (Solderless Prototyping Board)

A **Bread Board** (also called a **solderless breadboard** or **plug-block**) is a rectangular plastic platform with a grid of small holes spaced at a standardized **2.54 mm (0.1 inch)** pitch. Internally, it contains phosphor-bronze or stainless-steel **spring clips** that grip component leads and wire ends inserted into the holes.

**Internal Architecture:**
- **Power Rails**: The two outer columns (typically marked **+** and **−**) are internally connected vertically in strips of ~25 holes each.
- **Terminal Strips**: The central 5-hole groups are connected horizontally. A standard full-size breadboard has **63 rows** of such 5-hole groups separated by a central notch for ICs.

> [!NOTE]
> **"Why a Notch in the Middle?"** — The central channel is designed to accommodate **Dual In-line Package (DIP) ICs**, allowing each IC pin to access a separate 5-hole connected strip, preventing short-circuits between adjacent pins.

**Advantages:**
- Zero soldering required — completely **reworkable**.
- Ideal for **circuit prototyping**, laboratory experiments, and student learning.
- Components can be inserted and removed hundreds of times.

**Limitations:**
- Not suitable for high-current (>1 A) applications due to contact resistance (~10 mΩ per hole).
- Susceptible to vibration and accidental dislodgement.
- **Not** recommended for high-frequency circuits due to parasitic capacitance.

---

### 2.2 Wire Wrapping

**Wire Wrapping** is a solderless interconnection technique in which a solid wire (typically **AWG 30**, Kynar-insulated) is tightly coiled around a square or rectangular post (**0.025 inch × 0.025 inch**) using a manual or motorized wire-wrap tool.

**How It Works:**
The wire-wrap tool rotates the wire around the post, creating either a **standard wrap (7 turns)** or a **modified wrap (7 + 1 insulated turn at the base)**. The corner of the square post **plastically deforms** the soft copper wire, producing a **gas-tight** connection that excludes oxygen and prevents oxidation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Specs for KTU:**
> - Post material: **Phosphor bronze** or **nickel-silver alloy**.
> - Wire: **Solid copper, AWG 30, Kynar-insulated**.
> - Standard wrap: **7 turns** minimum; Modified wrap: **7+1 turns**.
> - Contact resistance: **< 3 mΩ** (lower than breadboard).
> - Reliable up to **10,000+ insertions/removals**.

**Advantages:** Highly reliable, gas-tight, reworkable with a special unwrap tool.
**Disadvantages:** Requires specialized tools and posts; limited to **point-to-point** wiring.

---

### 2.3 Crimping

**Crimping** is a mechanical process in which a **metal terminal (crimp lug)** is deformed around a stripped wire conductor using a calibrated **crimping tool**. The resulting joint is permanent, gas-tight, and is the **industry standard** for cable harnesses, automotive wiring, and power distribution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine using a metal clamp to squeeze a rubber hose onto a pipe fitting. The compression creates both **mechanical grip** and a **gas-tight seal** that conducts electricity with minimal resistance.

**Types of Crimp Terminals:**
1. **Ring Terminals** — For bolted stud connections.
2. **Spade (Fork) Terminals** — For screw-clamp connections, easier to install/remove.
3. **Butt Connectors** — For joining two wire ends end-to-end.
4. **Hook Terminals** — For limited-space applications.
5. **Insulated vs. Non-Insulated** — Insulated versions have a colored PVC sleeve (Red, Blue, Yellow indicating wire gauge).

---

### 2.4 Soldering — The Core Workshop Skill

**Soldering** is the process of joining two or more metal surfaces by melting and flowing a **filler metal (solder)** into the joint. The filler metal has a **lower melting point** (typically **183°C – 230°C** for lead-based, **217°C – 227°C** for lead-free SAC) than the workpieces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Question Alert:** Examiners frequently ask — *"Why is solder used and not welding?"*
> **Answer:** Soldering operates at temperatures **below 450°C**, so it does **not melt the base metals** of the components or PCB pads. This protects heat-sensitive semiconductor devices while still producing a strong electrical and mechanical bond.

#### 2.4.1 Types of Soldering

| Type | Temperature Range | Filler Metal | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Soft Soldering** | Below **450°C** | Sn-Pb (60/40, 63/37) or SAC lead-free | Electronics, PCBs, general wiring |
| **Hard (Brazing) Soldering** | **450°C – 950°C** | Brass, silver brazing alloys | Plumbing, refrigeration, high-strength joints |
| **Sweat Soldering** | ~250°C | Soft solder | Joining copper sheets, large surfaces |

For KTU GZESL208, the focus is exclusively on **Soft Soldering** for **electronics and PCBs**.

#### 2.4.2 Selection of Soldering Materials

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Material Selection Cheat Sheet (KTU Board-Exam Ready):**

| Component | Common Choice | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| **Solder Wire** | **63/37 Sn-Pb (rosin-core)** for ease, or **SAC305 (Sn96.5/Ag3.0/Cu0.5)** for lead-free compliance | Eutectic 63/37 melts sharply at **183°C** with no plastic range |
| **Flux** | **Rosin (RMA — Rosin Mildly Activated)** | Removes oxides, non-corrosive residue |
| **Soldering Iron Tip** | **Copper core plated with Iron (Fe)** | Good thermal conductivity, durable |
| **Tip Cleaning** | **Brass wire ball (sponge alternative)** or damp cellulose sponge | Prevents oxidation buildup on tip |
| **Wick (Desoldering Braid)** | **Copper braid with flux coating** | Removes excess solder for rework |
| **PCB** | **General-Purpose PCB (FR-2 phenolic or FR-4 fiberglass, copper-clad)** | Standard 1.6 mm thickness, single-sided or double-sided |

#### 2.4.3 Soldering Iron Specifications

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Power Rating | **15 W – 60 W** (KTU lab typically uses **25 W – 40 W**) |
| Operating Tip Temperature | **300°C – 400°C** |
| Voltage Supply | **230 V AC, 50 Hz** (India standard) |
| Tip-to-Ground Leakage | Must be **< 2 mV** for ESD-sensitive work (use **ESD-safe** station) |

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet & Critical Numerical Specs

> [!NOTE]
> Although this is a workshop topic, a few key equations govern the joint quality, tool selection, and electrical continuity. These are **commonly asked** in viva-voce.

| # | Formula / Spec | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **R_joint = ρ · L / A** | Resistance of a soldered/crimped joint (lowest is best) | Ohms (Ω) |
| 2 | **V_drop = I · R_joint** | Voltage drop across a poor joint — must be < **50 mV** for power joints | Volts (V) |
| 3 | **P_loss = I² · R_joint** | Power dissipated as heat in a faulty joint — cause of "cold joints" | Watts (W) |
| 4 | **Pull-out Force (Crimp)** | Per UL 486A: minimum **~ 36 kgf** for AWG 14 | kgf (kilogram-force) |
| 5 | **Soldering Iron Tip Temp** | **T_tip = T_ambient + ΔT**; ΔT ≈ **270°C** for lead solder | °C |
| 6 | **Wire Wrap Turns** | Standard: **7 turns**; Modified: **7 + 1 insulated turn** | dimensionless |
| 7 | **Breadboard Pitch** | Standard: **2.54 mm** = **0.1 inch** | mm |
| 8 | **Solder Melting Point (Eutectic 63/37)** | **T_m = 183°C** | °C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Table Notation:** The `$\vert$` symbol used above represents the *vertical bar* (such as absolute value or "divides") in mathematical expressions, written using LaTeX `\vert` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

---

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Method | Industry / Application |
|---|---|
| Bread Board | R&D labs, student projects, Arduino/Raspberry Pi prototyping |
| Wire Wrapping | Aerospace avionics (historically), telephone exchanges, mainframes |
| Crimping | Automotive wiring harnesses, aerospace, household appliances, power distribution panels |
| Soldering (PCB) | Every consumer electronic device — smartphones, laptops, TVs, medical devices |
| Soldering (Connectors) | Power supplies, servo motors, industrial control panels |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedures & Analytical Matrix

This section provides **exhaustive, mark-winning procedural breakdowns** for each interconnection method as expected in the KTU 2024 Scheme lab record and university examinations.

---

### 3.1 Soldering Procedure (Step-by-Step)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most heavily tested** procedure in KTU GZESL208 viva and lab exams. Memorize all 9 steps.

**Step 1: Preparation of the Workbench and Tools**
- Lay out the **soldering iron (25–40 W)**, **stand**, **solder wire (60/40 or 63/37)**, **flux (rosin-core)**, **soldering wick**, **sponge/brass wool**, **tweezers**, and **safety goggles**.
- Ensure proper **ventilation** — switch on the **fume extractor** or work near an open window. Solder fumes (especially from rosin flux) are irritants.

**Step 2: Preparation of the Soldering Iron**
- Switch on the soldering iron and allow it to heat to the working temperature of approximately **300°C to 350°C** for lead-based solder.
- **Tin the tip** immediately when hot: apply a small amount of solder to the tip and wipe it on the brass wool/sponge until the tip is uniformly shiny. This is called **tinning**, and it prevents oxidation.

**Step 3: Cleaning the Components and PCB Pads**
- Use **isopropyl alcohol (IPA)** and a lint-free cloth or **emery paper (fine grade)** to gently clean the component leads and the copper pads on the general-purpose PCB.
- Oxidation and grease prevent solder from wetting the surface properly.

**Step 4: Inserting the Component**
- Bend the component leads (for axial components like resistors and diodes) to fit the hole spacing on the PCB.
- Insert the component into the PCB holes from the **non-copper (silkscreen) side**, and ensure the body sits flush on the board.
- Bend the leads outward at **~45°** on the copper side to hold the component in place temporarily.

**Step 5: Applying Flux (if external flux is used)**
- For non-rosin-core solders, apply a small amount of **rosin flux** to the joint using a brush or flux pen. Rosin-core solder wire usually does not need external flux.

**Step 6: Making the Solder Joint (The "3-Second Rule")**
- Touch the **tinned tip of the iron** to **both the component lead AND the PCB pad simultaneously** — this heats both surfaces to the same temperature.
- After **1–2 seconds**, apply solder wire to the **junction of lead, pad, and iron tip** (not directly to the iron).
- The solder should **flow freely** and form a **concave fillet** (a shiny volcano shape) around the lead. The entire joint should take no more than **3–5 seconds** to avoid damaging the component or lifting the pad.

**Step 7: Removing the Iron and Cooling**
- Remove the solder wire first, then remove the soldering iron tip. Do **not** move the joint during cooling — this prevents a **"cold joint"** (dull, grainy, high-resistance defect).

**Step 8: Inspection of the Joint**
- A good solder joint should be:
  - **Shiny and smooth** (for lead-based) or **slightly dull/satin** (for lead-free SAC).
  - **Concave fillet** shape, like a small volcano.
  - The component lead should be visible through the solder.
- A **bad joint (cold joint)** will appear **dull, grainy, blistered**, or **balled up** — it must be **re-heated and reflowed** or **removed with wick and re-soldered**.

**Step 9: Trimming the Leads and Cleaning**
- Use **diagonal cutting pliers (side cutters)** to trim the excess component leads flush with the top of the solder joint.
- Clean the board with **IPA** to remove flux residue, which is mildly corrosive over time.

---

### 3.2 Soldering Practice on Connectors

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU board exams frequently test **soldering on a multi-pin connector** (e.g., a 9-pin D-sub male/female connector or a 2-pin screw terminal block). The technique differs slightly from PCB soldering.

**Procedure:**
1. **Identify the connector pin numbering** — Pin 1 is usually marked with a triangle or "1" on the connector body.
2. **Tin the connector pins** — Apply a small amount of solder to each cup or pin of the connector first. This is called **pre-tinning** and helps the final joint flow better.
3. **Strip the wire** to approximately **3–5 mm** of exposed conductor.
4. **Twist the stranded wire strands** tightly to prevent fraying.
5. **Solder the wire to the cup terminal** — Heat the cup and wire together, then apply solder until the cup is filled and the wire is fully wetted.
6. **Inspect** — The solder should be smooth and shiny, completely filling the cup with no air gaps or voids.
7. **Use heatshrink or PVC sleeve** over the joint for **strain relief** and insulation.

---

### 3.3 Crimping Procedure (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1: Select the Correct Terminal and Tool**
- Match the **terminal color** to the **wire gauge**:
  - **Red** insulation = AWG 22 to AWG 16 (~0.5 to 1.5 mm²)
  - **Blue** insulation = AWG 16 to AWG 14 (~1.5 to 2.5 mm²)
  - **Yellow** insulation = AWG 12 to AWG 10 (~4 to 6 mm²)
- Use a **ratcheting crimping tool** (preferred) that releases only when full crimp pressure is applied.

**Step 2: Strip the Wire**
- Strip exactly **5–7 mm** of insulation from the wire end using a **wire stripper** — **do not nick the copper strands**.

**Step 3: Insert the Wire into the Terminal**
- Push the stripped wire into the **conductor barrel** of the terminal (the longer metallic sleeve) until the copper strands are fully seated.
- The **insulation barrel** (shorter sleeve) should grip the insulated portion of the wire for strain relief.

**Step 4: Position in the Crimping Tool**
- Place the terminal in the **appropriately sized notch** of the crimping tool (matched to the wire gauge).

**Step 5: Apply the Crimp**
- Squeeze the tool handles firmly until the **ratchet releases** (for ratcheting tools) — this guarantees the correct pressure.
- The terminal is plastically deformed around the wire, creating a **gas-tight** connection.

**Step 6: Inspect the Crimp**
- A good crimp will have:
  - A visible **indentation** (the crimp die mark) on the barrel.
  - A **gentle pull test** should NOT dislodge the wire.
  - The insulation grip should be firm but **not cutting into** the insulation.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

| Parameter | Bread Board | Wire Wrapping | Crimping | Soldering |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Joint Type** | Spring contact | Mechanical wrap | Mechanical deformation | Metallurgical alloy bond |
| **Joint Resistance** | ~10 mΩ | < 3 mΩ | < 1 mΩ | < 1 mΩ |
| **Current Rating** | < 1 A | < 1 A | Up to **>100 A** (with proper terminal) | Up to PCB trace limit |
| **Re-workable?** | Yes (very easy) | Yes (with unwrap tool) | No (permanent) | Difficult (needs wick) |
| **Tool Cost** | ₹200 – ₹500 | ₹1500 – ₹5000 | ₹500 – ₹3000 | ₹400 – ₹3000 |
| **Skill Required** | Low | Medium | Low–Medium | High |
| **Vibration Resistance** | Poor | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Gas-Tight?** | No | **Yes** | **Yes** | **Yes (with proper flux/wetting)** |
| **Best For** | Prototyping | Vintage aerospace, telecom | Power wiring, harnesses | Final PCB assemblies |

---

### 3.5 Safety Precautions (KTU Board-Favorite Question)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Safety precautions are often a **standalone 2–3 mark question** in the viva and the lab record's "Inference" section.

**Personal Safety:**
1. **Wear safety goggles** — molten solder can splash into eyes.
2. **Wear cotton clothing, avoid loose sleeves** — synthetic fabrics can melt and stick to skin.
3. **Tie back long hair** — keeps it away from the hot iron tip.
4. **Wash hands after handling solder** — especially **lead-based solder** (Pb is toxic).
5. **Use a fume extractor or work in a well-ventilated area** — rosin flux fumes can cause **occupational asthma** with prolonged exposure.

**Electrical Safety:**
1. **Switch off and unplug** the soldering iron when not in use.
2. **Never touch** the metal shaft or tip of the soldering iron — it reaches **400°C**.
3. Always return the iron to its **stand** when idle.
4. **Do not solder live circuits** — always disconnect power.

**Fire Safety:**
1. Keep a **fire extinguisher** (Class C / CO₂ type for electrical fires) nearby.
2. Do not leave a hot iron unattended.
3. Keep the iron stand on a **non-flammable, heat-resistant surface**.

**Material Safety:**
1. **Lead-free solder (SAC)** is recommended for student labs to minimize lead exposure.
2. Dispose of solder dross and trimming waste in a **dedicated e-waste bin** — **not in regular trash**.

---

### 3.6 Cold Joint vs. Good Joint — Diagnostic Quick Reference

| Property | Good Joint | Cold Joint |
|---|---|---|
| **Appearance** | Shiny, smooth (Pb) or satin (SAC) | Dull, grainy, blistered |
| **Shape** | Concave fillet (volcano) | Balled up, convex |
| **Mechanical Strength** | Strong | Weak (breaks easily) |
| **Electrical Resistance** | < 1 mΩ | High (>10 mΩ) |
| **Cause** | Proper temp, flux, wetting | Insufficient heat, moved while cooling |
| **Remedy** | None — leave it | Re-heat and reflow; or remove with wick and redo |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Complete Soldering Workflow (Block Architecture Flow)

This diagram maps the **end-to-end soldering process** in a KTU laboratory setting — from tool selection to final inspection.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Power Off] --> B[Gather Tools: Iron, Solder, Flux, Sponge]
    B --> C[Switch On Iron: Set 300 to 350 degrees C]
    C --> D[Tin the Iron Tip with Solder]
    D --> E[Clean PCB Pads with IPA and Emery]
    E --> F[Insert Component into PCB Holes]
    F --> G[Bend Leads at 45 degrees to Hold Component]
    G --> H[Touch Iron Tip to Lead AND Pad]
    H --> I[Wait 1 to 2 seconds for Heat Transfer]
    I --> J[Apply Solder Wire at Junction of Lead, Pad, and Tip]
    J --> K{Does Solder Flow and Form a Concave Fillet?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Remove Solder Wire First, Then Iron]
    K -- No --> M[Re-heat and Add Flux if Needed]
    M --> H
    L --> N[Hold Joint Still for 3 seconds Cooling]
    N --> O[Inspect Joint: Shiny Concave Shape]
    O --> P{Is Joint Good?}
    P -- Yes --> Q[Trim Excess Lead with Cutter]
    P -- No --> R[Desolder with Wick and Retry]
    R --> H
    Q --> S[Clean Board with IPA]
    S --> T[End: Functional PCB Assembled]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The two **diamond-shaped decision nodes** (`K` and `P`) are the quality-control gates. The KTU examiner expects you to **mentally check** the joint for the *concave fillet* and *shiny appearance* at these points.

---

### 4.2 Comparative Selection Logic Flow

This diagram shows **how a KTU workshop engineer decides which interconnection method to use** for a given application.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: New Project] --> B{Is this a temporary prototype?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Use BREAD BOARD]
    B -- No --> D{Is this a high-vibration or aerospace-grade permanent joint?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Use WIRE WRAPPING with Solid AWG 30 Wire]
    D -- No --> F{Is this a power cable or wire harness?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Use CRIMPING with Ratcheting Tool]
    F -- No --> H{Is this a PCB-mounted component or connector pin?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Use SOLDERING with 25 to 40 W Iron]
    I --> J{Lead-based or Lead-free required?}
    J -- Lead-based --> K[Use 60/40 or 63/37 Sn-Pb Rosin-Core]
    J -- Lead-free --> L[Use SAC305 with Higher Iron Temp 350 to 400 degrees C]
    C --> M[End: Method Selected]
    E --> M
    G --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

---

### 4.3 Solder Joint Anatomy (Functional Architecture)

This block diagram represents the **cross-sectional anatomy of a properly formed solder joint** on a PCB, identifying the key zones.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Component Lead - Copper] --> B[Solder Fillet - Concave Volcano Shape]
    B --> C[PCB Copper Pad - Plated Surface]
    C --> D[PCB Substrate - FR-2 or FR-4]
    A -.Heat flow direction.-> B
    B -.Heat flow direction.-> C
    E[Flux Residue Layer - To be cleaned with IPA] -.Coats.-> B
    F[Iron Tip at 320 degrees C] -.Transfers heat to.-> A
    F -.Transfers heat to.-> C
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key takeaway from the anatomy diagram:** A good solder joint involves **three surfaces being heated simultaneously** — the component lead, the PCB pad, and the solder. The iron tip is the *heat source*; the solder wire is the *filler material*. The flux cleans all three surfaces during heating.

---

### 4.4 Crimping Tool Operation Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wire Stripped to 5 to 7 mm] --> B[Insert into Terminal Conductor Barrel]
    B --> C[Place Terminal in Crimping Tool Notch]
    C --> D[Squeeze Handles Firmly]
    D --> E{Ratchet Type Tool?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Tool Releases Only at Full Pressure]
    E -- No --> G[Operator Must Judge Pressure]
    F --> H[Inspect: Indentation, Pull Test]
    G --> H
    H --> I{Crimp Passes Inspection?}
    I -- Yes --> J[End: Gas-tight Crimp Joint]
    I -- No --> K[Cut Off and Re-crimp with New Terminal]
    K --> B
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]**
> *List any **three** safety precautions that must be observed while performing soldering in the workshop.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE):** Wear safety goggles to protect the eyes from molten solder splashes, and use a fume extractor or work in a well-ventilated area to avoid inhaling rosin flux fumes, which can cause respiratory irritation and occupational asthma.
2. **Iron Handling:** Always return the hot soldering iron to its **stand** when not in use, and never touch the metal shaft or tip, as it operates at temperatures between **300°C and 400°C** and can cause severe burns.
3. **Electrical Safety:** Ensure the soldering iron is switched off and unplugged at the end of the lab session. **Never solder a live circuit** — always disconnect power before making or breaking joints. Wash hands thoroughly after handling lead-based solder.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question]**
> *Differentiate between **soft soldering** and **hard soldering (brazing)** in two points each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Soft Soldering | Hard Soldering (Brazing) |
|---|---|---|
| **Temperature** | Below **450°C** (typically 200°C – 350°C) | Above **450°C** (typically 600°C – 950°C) |
| **Filler Metal** | Tin-Lead (Sn-Pb) or SAC lead-free alloys | Brass, silver brazing alloys, copper-phosphorus |
| **Application** | Electronics, PCBs, general wiring | Plumbing, refrigeration, high-strength joints |
| **Base Metal Effect** | Does not melt the base metals | Base metal may soften but is not melted |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Q3A. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 14 Marks, Internal Choice Pair with Q3B]**
> *(a)* Explain in detail the **step-by-step procedure for soldering a resistor on a general-purpose PCB**, listing the tools and materials required. Mention the criteria for judging a good solder joint versus a cold joint. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* With a neat block diagram, describe the **soldering workflow** and explain why a **flux** is essential in the soldering process. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Soldering Procedure on a General-Purpose PCB (7 marks):**

**Tools and Materials Required:**
- Soldering iron (**25 W – 40 W**, 230 V, temperature-controlled)
- Solder wire (**60/40 or 63/37 Sn-Pb**, rosin-core) or **SAC305 lead-free**
- Soldering stand, brass wool/cleaning sponge
- General-purpose PCB (FR-2 or FR-4, copper-clad)
- Diagonal cutting pliers
- Tweezers, flux (if using non-rosin-core solder)
- Isopropyl alcohol (IPA) and lint-free cloth
- Safety goggles, fume extractor

**Step-by-Step Procedure:**
1. **Tin the iron tip:** Switch on the iron; when it reaches ~320°C, apply a small amount of solder to the tip and wipe clean on brass wool. Repeat until the tip is uniformly shiny. (1 mark)
2. **Clean and prepare:** Clean the PCB copper pads and the resistor leads using IPA and fine emery paper to remove oxidation. (0.5 mark)
3. **Insert the component:** Insert the resistor leads through the PCB holes from the non-copper side, bend the leads ~45° on the copper side to hold the resistor in place. (1 mark)
4. **Heat the joint:** Touch the **tinned iron tip** to **both the resistor lead and the PCB pad** simultaneously for **1–2 seconds**. (1 mark)
5. **Apply solder:** Feed the solder wire into the heated junction. The solder should **flow freely** and form a **concave fillet** within **3–5 seconds**. (1 mark)
6. **Remove and cool:** Remove the solder wire first, then the iron. **Do not move** the joint for ~3 seconds while it cools. (0.5 mark)
7. **Trim and clean:** Cut the excess leads with diagonal cutters. Clean the flux residue with IPA. (0.5 mark)
8. **Inspect:** Verify the joint is **shiny, concave, and has a smooth fillet**. (0.5 mark)

**Good Joint vs. Cold Joint (KTU Board-Favorite Comparison):**

| Property | Good Joint | Cold Joint |
|---|---|---|
| Appearance | **Shiny, smooth, concave fillet** | **Dull, grainy, blistered, balled up** |
| Strength | Strong mechanical and electrical bond | Weak — high resistance, prone to cracking |
| Cause | Correct temperature, flux, and undisturbed cooling | Insufficient heat, movement during cooling, or dirty surfaces |
| Remedy | None — joint is reliable | **Re-heat and reflow with flux**, or **remove with desoldering wick** and redo |

**(b) Soldering Workflow and Role of Flux (7 marks):**

The **soldering workflow** (as shown in the block diagram in Section 4.1) consists of:
1. **Pre-soldering preparation:** Tool arrangement, workbench safety check, PCB and component cleaning, and **tinning the iron tip**. (1.5 marks)
2. **Soldering stage:** Heating the joint to ~183°C (Sn-Pb eutectic), applying flux-core solder, allowing the solder to wet both surfaces, and forming a **concave fillet**. (1.5 marks)
3. **Post-soldering inspection:** Visual inspection for shininess, fillet shape, and wetting; electrical continuity test; trimming leads; cleaning flux residue. (1.5 marks)

**Why Flux is Essential (2.5 marks):**
- **Removes oxides:** Metals like copper and tin form a thin invisible oxide layer when exposed to air. This oxide layer **prevents the molten solder from wetting** the surface, leading to cold joints.
- **Prevents re-oxidation:** During heating, flux forms a protective liquid blanket that prevents oxygen from contacting the molten solder and base metal.
- **Improves wetting:** Flux **reduces the surface tension** of the molten solder, allowing it to flow and spread evenly across the joint.
- **Common flux:** **Rosin (colophony)** — a natural resin from pine trees — is the most widely used in electronics. **Rosin Mildly Activated (RMA)** is preferred in KTU labs because it is non-corrosive and does not require post-solder cleaning for low-voltage circuits.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write "flux removes dirt" — this is **partially correct** but **incomplete**. The *primary* function of flux is to **chemically reduce metal oxides** and **reduce surface tension** to enable proper wetting. Saying only "removes dirt" will cost you **1 mark**.

---

> **Q3B. [Alternative Choice, 14 Marks]**
> *(a)* Describe the **wire-wrapping technique** for interconnection. List its advantages, limitations, and the specifications of wire and post used. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* Explain the **crimping technique** with a neat step-by-step procedure. State the **color coding** of insulated crimp terminals and the matching wire gauges. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Wire Wrapping Technique (7 marks):**

**Definition:** Wire wrapping is a **solderless interconnection technique** in which a solid wire is tightly coiled around a square or rectangular metal post under controlled tension, creating a **gas-tight, low-resistance** connection without heat. (1 mark)

**Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Strip ~25 mm of insulation from one end of an **AWG 30, Kynar-insulated solid copper wire**.
2. Insert the stripped end into the **wire-wrap tool's bit**.
3. Place the tool's center hole over the **square post (0.025" × 0.025")**.
4. Activate the tool — it rotates the wire around the post, producing either:
   - **Standard wrap:** 7 turns of bare wire around the post.
   - **Modified wrap:** 7 turns of bare wire + 1 turn of insulated wire at the base for **mechanical strain relief**.

**Specifications (1.5 marks):**
- **Post material:** Phosphor bronze or nickel-silver alloy
- **Post size:** 0.025" × 0.025" (square)
- **Wire:** AWG 30 (0.25 mm diameter), solid copper, Kynar-insulated
- **Contact resistance:** < 3 mΩ
- **Minimum turns:** 7 (per IEEE Std 1111)

**Advantages (1.5 marks):**
1. **Gas-tight connection** — the post corners plastically deform the copper wire, excluding oxygen and preventing oxidation.
2. **Higher reliability** than breadboards — used in aerospace and telecom.
3. **No heat** required — safe for heat-sensitive components.
4. **Easily reworkable** with a special unwrap tool.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
1. Requires specialized **square posts** on the board, restricting this to point-to-point wiring only.
2. **Tool cost** is higher than a simple soldering iron.
3. Limited to **low-current** signal-level applications.
4. Not suitable for **high-density** modern PCBs.

---

**(b) Crimping Technique (7 marks):**

**Definition:** Crimping is a **cold-welding** process in which a metal terminal is plastically deformed around a stripped wire conductor using a calibrated crimping tool, forming a **permanent, gas-tight, low-resistance** electrical and mechanical joint. (1 mark)

**Step-by-Step Procedure (3 marks):**
1. **Select the correct terminal** (color-coded to wire gauge — see table below) and a **ratcheting crimping tool**.
2. **Strip the wire** to **5–7 mm** using a wire stripper — **do not nick the copper strands**.
3. **Insert the stripped wire** into the **conductor barrel** (longer metallic sleeve) of the terminal until it is fully seated. The **insulation barrel** (shorter sleeve) should grip the insulated part for strain relief.
4. **Position the terminal** in the **correct notch** of the crimping tool (matched to wire gauge).
5. **Squeeze the handles** firmly until the **ratchet releases** (for ratcheting tools) — this guarantees the correct crimp pressure.
6. **Inspect the crimp** — look for the die-mark indentation and perform a **gentle pull test** to confirm the wire is locked in.

**Insulated Terminal Color Coding (KTU Board Important — 3 marks):**

| Color | Wire Gauge (AWG) | Cross-Section (mm²) | Typical Current |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Red** | AWG 22 – 16 | 0.5 – 1.5 mm² | Up to **10 A** |
| **Blue** | AWG 16 – 14 | 1.5 – 2.5 mm² | Up to **15 A** |
| **Yellow** | AWG 12 – 10 | 4 – 6 mm² | Up to **24 A** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse the color code ordering. **Memorize it in the order Red → Blue → Yellow → larger wire.** A common mistake is writing "Yellow = small wire" — this is **wrong** and will cost you 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU GZESL208 — Final 60-Second Revision Cheat Sheet:**

- [ ] **Bread Board:** Solderless, **2.54 mm** pitch, spring-clip contacts, IC notch in center, not for high current (>1 A) or high frequency.
- [ ] **Wire Wrapping:** **AWG 30** wire, **square 0.025" post**, **7 turns** standard or **7+1 turns** modified, gas-tight, solderless.
- [ ] **Crimping:** Mechanical cold-weld; **ratcheting tool** is preferred; color code is **Red (22–16 AWG) → Blue (16–14 AWG) → Yellow (12–10 AWG)**.
- [ ] **Solder:** **60/40 or 63/37 Sn-Pb** (melts at **183°C**); **SAC305 lead-free** (melts at **217–227°C**).
- [ ] **Soldering Iron:** **25–40 W** for KTU labs; tip temperature **300–400°C**; always **tin the tip** before use.
- [ ] **Flux:** **Rosin-based (RMA)** — removes oxides, reduces surface tension, prevents re-oxidation; **not** just a dirt remover.
- [ ] **Soldering Procedure:** 9 steps — tin tip, clean, insert, heat lead+pad, apply solder, form **concave fillet**, cool without movement, inspect, trim, clean with IPA.
- [ ] **Good Joint:** **Shiny, smooth, concave fillet**; **Cold Joint:** dull, grainy, balled up — must be reworked.
- [ ] **3-Second Rule:** A solder joint should not take more than **3–5 seconds** to form to avoid lifting pads.
- [ ] **Safety:** Goggles, fume extractor, **unplug iron** after use, **never solder live circuits**, wash hands (Pb), cotton clothing, fire extinguisher nearby.
- [ ] **Soldering on Connectors:** **Pre-tinning** of pins/cups is mandatory for multi-pin connectors.
- [ ] **Crimp Inspection:** Die-mark indentation visible, **pull test** confirms strength, **no nicked strands**, insulation not cut.
- [ ] **PCB Type:** **General-purpose PCB (FR-2 or FR-4)**, **1.6 mm** thick, copper-clad single or double sided.
- [ ] **Joint Resistance Target:** < **1 mΩ** for power soldered/crimped joints; < **3 mΩ** for wire-wrapped; < **10 mΩ** for breadboard (acceptable for signals only).

> **Best of luck with your KTU GZESL208 lab exam and university viva-voce!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assembling of electronic circuit/system on general purpose PCB, test and show the functioning (Fixed voltage power supply with transformer, Rectifier diode, Capacitor filter, Zener/IC regulator, Square wave generation using IC 555 timer in IC base)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZESL208 - BASIC ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Assembling of electronic circuit/system on general purpose PCB, test and show the functioning (Fixed voltage power supply with transformer, Rectifier diode, Capacitor filter, Zener/IC regulator, Square wave generation using IC 555 timer in IC base)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Experiment Scope
This experiment consists of **two independent sub-circuits** that are fabricated on a **General Purpose PCB (GPCB)** and tested for correct functional operation:

**Sub-Experiment A — Fixed Voltage DC Power Supply**
A linear, unregulated → regulated DC power supply assembled using a step-down transformer, silicon rectifier diode, electrolytic filter capacitor, and a Zener diode / three-terminal IC regulator (e.g., 78xx series). Converts **230 V AC, 50 Hz** mains into a stable, low-ripple DC output (typically **+5 V, +9 V, or +12 V**).

**Sub-Experiment B — Square Wave Generator using IC 555**
The 8-pin **IC 555 timer** is mounted on an **IC base** (to prevent heat damage during soldering) and wired in the **Astable Multivibrator** configuration to produce a continuous square wave of selectable frequency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Mapping (GZESL208):** This experiment falls under *Module 2 – Electronic Workshop Practice* and is mapped to **CO2**: *Assemble, solder, test and demonstrate simple electronic circuits on a general purpose PCB.*

---

### 1.2 Real-World Analogies (Plain-English Intuition)

**Sub-Experiment A — "The Water Tap Analogy"**
Imagine the AC mains as a turbulent river (water level keeps rising and falling 50 times every second). A **transformer** is a "valve and bucket" that lowers the water level (voltage) to a usable height. A **rectifier diode** is a "one-way gate" that allows water to flow only forward — it blocks the backward surges. A **capacitor** is a "storage tank" that fills when water is high and slowly empties when water is low, smoothing the flow. Finally, a **Zener / IC regulator** is a "pressure-limiter" that holds the output pressure absolutely constant regardless of how much water is drawn from the tap.

**Sub-Experiment B — "The Metronome Analogy"**
A 555 astable circuit is like a **self-ticking metronome** that never stops. It charges a capacitor through a resistor, and when the capacitor's voltage reaches **2/3 V_CC**, it flicks a switch that lets the capacitor discharge through another resistor. When the voltage falls to **1/3 V_CC**, the switch flicks back, charging begins again, and the cycle repeats forever. The result is a perfect up-and-down "tick-tock" — that is the **square wave**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — General Purpose PCB (GPCB):** A copper-clad phenolic/FRP board with isolated copper pads and a grid of uniformly spaced holes (standard **2.54 mm pitch**) on which the student manually places and solders components. Unlike a custom-designed PCB, a GPCB has no pre-printed circuit tracks — the student must interconnect component leads using jumper wires and solder.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — IC Base / IC Socket:** A 8-pin DIP (Dual In-line Package) socket that is soldered onto the PCB first. The IC is then *plugged* into the socket. This protects the IC from direct soldering heat and allows easy replacement.

---

### 1.3 Key Physical & Standard Metrics

- **AC Mains (India):** **230 V RMS, 50 Hz, single phase**.
- **Rectifier Diode:** Typically **1N4007** (PIV = **1000 V**, I_F = **1 A**).
- **Filter Capacitor:** Electrolytic, typically **470 µF to 2200 µF**, rated at least **25 V** for a 12 V supply.
- **78xx IC Regulator Series:** **7805 → +5 V**, **7809 → +9 V**, **7812 → +12 V**, **7815 → +15 V** (all with **±5 %** tolerance).
- **Zener Diode:** Operates in **reverse breakdown** region; common values **5.1 V, 5.6 V, 6.2 V, 12 V**.
- **IC 555 Timer:** Operating voltage range **+4.5 V to +16 V**; output current up to **200 mA**.
- **Standard PCB Pitch:** **2.54 mm** (0.1 inch) between adjacent holes.
- **Soldering Iron Temperature:** **350 °C – 400 °C** for leaded solder (60/40 Sn-Pb).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sub-Experiment A — Block-Level Theory

The complete power supply is structured in **four cascaded stages**. Each stage performs a single, well-defined function:

#### Stage 1 — Step-Down Transformer
- **Why:** The 230 V mains must be reduced to a low AC voltage (e.g., 12 V RMS) suitable for the rectifier.
- **How:** A **center-tapped** or **single-secondary** step-down transformer uses electromagnetic induction.
- **Turns Ratio Relation:**

$$V_s = V_p \cdot \frac{N_s}{N_p}$$

- For a 230 V → 12 V transformer, the turns ratio $N_s/N_p \approx 12/230 \approx 0.052$.

#### Stage 2 — Half-Wave / Full-Wave Rectifier
- **Why:** A rectifier converts the bidirectional AC into a **unidirectional (pulsating DC)** signal.
- **How (Half-Wave):** A single diode conducts only during the positive half-cycle; the negative half-cycle is blocked. Output is **pulsating DC with a 50 % duty cycle**.
- **How (Full-Wave Bridge — most common in KTU labs):** Four diodes arranged in a **bridge** configuration conduct in pairs during both half-cycles, giving a **pulsating DC at 100 Hz** (twice the line frequency).
- **Peak Inverse Voltage (PIV)** that each diode must withstand:

$$PIV_{bridge} = V_m = V_{s(rms)} \cdot \sqrt{2}$$

- **DC Output Voltage (Full-Wave Bridge) under load:**

$$V_{DC} = \frac{2 \cdot V_m}{\pi} \approx 0.636 \cdot V_m$$

#### Stage 3 — Capacitor Filter
- **Why:** The pulsating DC still has large AC components ("ripple") that are unacceptable for digital/analog electronics.
- **How:** A large electrolytic capacitor is placed in parallel with the load. It charges to the peak voltage $V_m$ during each conduction pulse and then **slowly discharges through the load** between pulses, filling the "valleys."
- **Peak-to-Peak Ripple Voltage for Full-Wave Rectifier:**

$$V_{r(pp)} = \frac{I_L}{f_r \cdot C} = \frac{I_L}{2 f \cdot C}$$

where:
- $I_L$ = load current (Amps)
- $f$ = mains frequency (**50 Hz**)
- $f_r$ = ripple frequency = **2f = 100 Hz** (full-wave)
- $C$ = filter capacitance (Farads)

- **Required capacitance for a target ripple:**

$$C = \frac{I_L}{2 f \cdot V_{r(pp)}}$$

#### Stage 4 — Voltage Regulator (Zener or Three-Terminal IC)

**Option A — Zener Diode Shunt Regulator:**
- **Why:** Holds the output voltage constant at the Zener voltage $V_Z$ even when load or input varies.
- **How:** The Zener is connected in **reverse bias** in parallel with the load, and a series resistor $R_S$ drops the excess voltage. The Zener operates in **breakdown region**; as long as $I_Z$ stays between $I_{Z(min)}$ and $I_{Z(max)}$, $V_{out} = V_Z$ remains constant.
- **Series Resistor Calculation:**

$$R_S = \frac{V_{in(DC)} - V_Z}{I_L + I_Z}$$

**Option B — Three-Terminal IC Regulator (78xx series):**
- **Why:** Provides a much tighter output regulation (±2 % to ±5 %), built-in **current limiting** and **thermal shutdown** — none of which a Zener can offer.
- **How:** Internally contains a band-gap reference, error amplifier, and pass transistor. Just three pins — **Input (pin 1)**, **Ground (pin 2)**, **Output (pin 3)**.
- **Required bypass capacitors:** Typically a **0.33 µF** tantalum on input (pin 1) and a **0.1 µF** ceramic on output (pin 3) to prevent high-frequency oscillation.

---

### 2.2 Sub-Experiment B — IC 555 Astable Theory

- **Configuration:** **Astable Multivibrator** (no stable state — output continuously oscillates).
- **Internal Comparator Thresholds:**
  - **Upper Threshold (UT) = (2/3) V_CC** — Triggered at pin 6.
  - **Lower Threshold (LT) = (1/3) V_CC** — Triggered at pin 2.
- **Action Sequence (one full cycle):**
  1. Capacitor $C$ charges through $R_1 + R_2$ from 1/3 V_CC → 2/3 V_CC (output is HIGH).
  2. When $V_C = (2/3) V_{CC}$, internal flip-flop resets; output goes LOW; discharge transistor turns ON.
  3. Capacitor now discharges through $R_2$ only from 2/3 V_CC → 1/3 V_CC (output is LOW).
  4. When $V_C = (1/3) V_{CC}$, flip-flop sets; output goes HIGH; discharge transistor turns OFF; cycle repeats.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Standard Value |
|---|----------|---------|------------------------|
| 1 | Transformer secondary RMS voltage | $V_s = V_p \cdot (N_s/N_p)$ | Volts (RMS) |
| 2 | Peak secondary voltage | $V_m = V_{s(rms)} \cdot \sqrt{2}$ | Volts (Peak) |
| 3 | Full-wave DC output (no load) | $V_{DC(no\,load)} = V_m$ | Volts |
| 4 | Full-wave DC output (with load) | $V_{DC} = (2 V_m)/\pi$ | Volts |
| 5 | Bridge diode PIV | $PIV = V_m$ | Volts |
| 6 | Ripple frequency (full-wave) | $f_r = 2 f$ | **100 Hz** (India) |
| 7 | Peak-to-peak ripple voltage | $V_{r(pp)} = I_L / (2 f C)$ | Volts |
| 8 | Required filter capacitance | $C = I_L / (2 f V_{r(pp)})$ | Farads |
| 9 | Zener series resistor | $R_S = (V_{in} - V_Z) / (I_L + I_Z)$ | Ohms |
| 10 | 78xx regulator output | $V_{out} = V_{xx}$ (fixed) | +5, +9, +12, +15 V |
| 11 | 555 — Charging time (t_HIGH) | $t_H = 0.693 \cdot (R_1 + R_2) \cdot C$ | Seconds |
| 12 | 555 — Discharging time (t_LOW) | $t_L = 0.693 \cdot R_2 \cdot C$ | Seconds |
| 13 | 555 — Total period T | $T = 0.693 \cdot (R_1 + 2 R_2) \cdot C$ | Seconds |
| 14 | 555 — Frequency f | $f = 1.44 \, / \, \big[(R_1 + 2 R_2) \cdot C\big]$ | Hertz |
| 15 | 555 — Duty Cycle D | $D = (R_1 + R_2) / (R_1 + 2 R_2) \times 100\,\%$ | % (always > 50 %) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 0.693?** The factor $0.693$ comes from $\ln(2) \approx 0.6931$, which arises because the capacitor charges/discharges exponentially between 1/3 V_CC and 2/3 V_CC. Specifically, the ratio (2/3) ÷ (1/3) = 2, and $\ln(2) = 0.693$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite:** Q13 and Q14 are the most-tested formulas in this experiment. The 14-mark questions almost always require a numerical design calculation of $R_1, R_2, C$ for a given frequency, followed by verification of the duty cycle.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Fixed Voltage Power Supply:** Universal building block of *every* electronic device — mobile chargers, laptop adapters, microcontroller boards (Arduino, Raspberry Pi), TV sets, IoT nodes, and laboratory instruments.
- **Square Wave Generator:** Used as a **clock source** for digital logic, **pulse-width modulation (PWM)** for motor speed control, **tone generation** in alarms/buzzer circuits, and as a **timing reference** in monostable and Schmitt-trigger applications.

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Peak-to-Peak Ripple Voltage Across Filter Capacitor (Full-Wave Rectifier)

We model the situation as follows: a full-wave rectifier delivers a train of positive half-sine pulses at a ripple frequency $f_r = 2f$. Between two successive peaks, the capacitor must supply the load current $I_L$ for a duration of $\Delta t = 1/f_r$.

**Step 1 — Define the charge supplied by the capacitor during one ripple interval.**

Between two peaks, the capacitor voltage falls by an amount $V_{r(pp)}$. The charge lost by the capacitor is:

$$Q = C \cdot V_{r(pp)}$$

**Step 2 — This charge is exactly equal to the charge drawn by the load during $\Delta t$.**

$$Q = I_L \cdot \Delta t = I_L \cdot \frac{1}{f_r}$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions for Q.**

$$C \cdot V_{r(pp)} = \frac{I_L}{f_r}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the peak-to-peak ripple voltage.**

$$V_{r(pp)} = \frac{I_L}{f_r \cdot C} = \frac{I_L}{2 f \cdot C}$$

This is the **standard KTU-expected final form** (matches the formula sheet row #7). Every step is algebraically exact and uses only the definition of current ($I = Q/\Delta t$).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: IC 555 Astable Frequency and Duty Cycle

**Starting from the capacitor voltage equation** for an RC charging circuit:

$$V_C(t) = V_{\infty} - (V_{\infty} - V_{0}) \cdot e^{-t/RC}$$

**Step 1 — Charging Phase (output HIGH).**
- Final voltage $V_{\infty} = V_{CC}$ (full supply).
- Initial voltage $V_0 = (1/3) V_{CC}$.
- Final voltage at end of charging $= (2/3) V_{CC}$.

Substituting:

$$(2/3) V_{CC} = V_{CC} - \left[V_{CC} - (1/3)V_{CC}\right] \cdot e^{-t_H / [(R_1+R_2)C]}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the bracket.**

$$V_{CC} - (1/3)V_{CC} = (2/3) V_{CC}$$

So:

$$(2/3) V_{CC} = V_{CC} - (2/3) V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t_H/[(R_1+R_2)C]}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange.**

$$(2/3) V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t_H/[(R_1+R_2)C]} = V_{CC} - (2/3)V_{CC} = (1/3)V_{CC}$$

**Step 4 — Divide both sides by $(2/3) V_{CC}$.**

$$e^{-t_H/[(R_1+R_2)C]} = \frac{1/3}{2/3} = \frac{1}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Take natural logarithm on both sides.**

$$-\frac{t_H}{(R_1+R_2)C} = \ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -\ln(2)$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $t_H$.**

$$t_H = \ln(2) \cdot (R_1 + R_2) \cdot C = 0.693 \cdot (R_1 + R_2) \cdot C$$

This proves **Formula #11** of the cheat sheet.

---

**Step 7 — Discharging Phase (output LOW).**
During discharge, the capacitor discharges from $(2/3)V_{CC}$ down to $(1/3)V_{CC}$ through resistor $R_2$ only (because the internal discharge transistor shorts pin 7 to ground, bypassing $R_1$).

Applying the discharge equation:

$$V_C(t) = V_{0} \cdot e^{-t/(R_2 C)}$$

Substituting $V_0 = (2/3)V_{CC}$ and $V_C = (1/3)V_{CC}$ at $t = t_L$:

$$(1/3) V_{CC} = (2/3) V_{CC} \cdot e^{-t_L/(R_2 C)}$$

**Step 8 — Simplify and solve.**

$$e^{-t_L/(R_2 C)} = \frac{1}{2}$$

$$t_L = 0.693 \cdot R_2 \cdot C$$

This proves **Formula #12** of the cheat sheet.

---

**Step 9 — Total Period.**

$$T = t_H + t_L = 0.693 \cdot (R_1 + R_2) C + 0.693 \cdot R_2 C = 0.693 \cdot (R_1 + 2 R_2) \cdot C$$

This proves **Formula #13** of the cheat sheet.

---

**Step 10 — Frequency.** Since $f = 1/T$:

$$f = \frac{1}{0.693 \cdot (R_1 + 2R_2) \cdot C} = \frac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2) \cdot C}$$

This proves **Formula #14** of the cheat sheet.

---

**Step 11 — Duty Cycle.**

$$D = \frac{t_H}{T} = \frac{(R_1 + R_2)}{(R_1 + 2R_2)} \times 100\,\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Observation:** The duty cycle of a standard 555 astable can **never be 50 % or less**, because $R_1 + R_2$ is always strictly greater than $R_2$. To obtain a 50 % duty cycle, a **diode in parallel with $R_2$** is added so that charging and discharging both occur through $R_1$ only.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — 555 Timer Design

**Problem:** Design an astable multivibrator using IC 555 with **f = 1 kHz** and **D = 60 %**. Choose $C = 0.1\,\mu F$. Find $R_1$ and $R_2$.

**Step 1 — Express D in terms of $R_1$ and $R_2$.**

$$0.60 = \frac{R_1 + R_2}{R_1 + 2 R_2}$$

**Step 2 — Cross-multiply.**

$$0.60 \cdot (R_1 + 2 R_2) = R_1 + R_2$$

$$0.60 R_1 + 1.20 R_2 = R_1 + R_2$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to isolate $R_1$ and $R_2$.**

$$1.20 R_2 - R_2 = R_1 - 0.60 R_1$$

$$0.20 R_2 = 0.40 R_1$$

$$R_1 = 0.5 R_2$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $R_1 = 0.5 R_2$ into the frequency formula.**

$$f = \frac{1.44}{(R_1 + 2R_2) \cdot C}$$

$$1000 = \frac{1.44}{(0.5 R_2 + 2 R_2) \cdot 0.1 \times 10^{-6}}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify denominator.**

$$0.5 R_2 + 2 R_2 = 2.5 R_2$$

$$1000 = \frac{1.44}{2.5 R_2 \cdot 10^{-7}}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $R_2$.**

$$2.5 R_2 \cdot 10^{-7} = \frac{1.44}{1000} = 1.44 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$R_2 = \frac{1.44 \times 10^{-3}}{2.5 \times 10^{-7}} = 5760\,\Omega \approx 5.76\,k\Omega$$

**Step 7 — Use nearest standard E12 value: $R_2 = 5.6\,k\Omega$**

**Step 8 — Compute $R_1$.**

$$R_1 = 0.5 \times 5.6\,k\Omega = 2.8\,k\Omega \approx 2.7\,k\Omega \text{ (E12)}$$

**Step 9 — Verify the resulting frequency.**

$$f = \frac{1.44}{(2700 + 2 \times 5600) \times 0.1 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.44}{13900 \times 10^{-7}} \approx 1036\,Hz$$

This is within **±5 %** tolerance, acceptable for a KTU lab design.
