> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZESL208 - BASIC ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Civil Engineering Drawing, concept of scale, plan, section and elevation; drawing tools and accessories, manual and computer aided drafting; draw the view of simple objects (books, shelves, benches, etc.) adopting appropriate scales

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Civil Engineering Drawing — Formal Definition

**Civil Engineering Drawing (CED)** is a specialized branch of engineering graphics that deals with the preparation of technical drawings representing civil engineering structures such as buildings, roads, bridges, dams, and public utility networks. It is the universal **technical language** through which a civil engineer communicates design intent, dimensional data, material specifications, and construction methodology to the site execution team.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Verbatim):** Civil Engineering Drawing is the graphical representation of civil engineering objects and structures, drawn to appropriate **scales** using standard **drawing tools** (manual drafting) or **CAD software** (computer-aided drafting), showing different orthographic views — **plan**, **section**, and **elevation** — to fully describe the geometry of the object.

According to **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) SP 46:2003**, all engineering drawings in India must follow standardized line types, symbols, abbreviations, and sheet sizes to ensure uniformity across the construction industry.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Why Civil Drawings Matter"

Imagine you are building a **simple wooden bookshelf** for your room. You could verbally tell a carpenter:
> *"Make it 4 feet tall, 3 feet wide, with 4 horizontal planks, and use 1-inch thick wood."*

But what if you have **50 such bookshelves** to build across a campus, with different carpenters, in different cities? Verbal instructions will lead to **disasters** — wrong dimensions, misaligned shelves, and wasted material.

A **Civil Engineering Drawing** is the **"photographic recipe"** of the object. Just like a chef follows a recipe card with measurements, every engineer, contractor, and site worker reads the *same drawing sheet* and produces an *identical* result. The drawing acts as a **legal and technical contract** between the designer and the builder.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> - **Drawing Sheet** = Google Maps for a structure (top view, side view, cross-section)
> - **Scale** = Zoom level on the map
> - **Plan** = Looking down at a building from a drone
> - **Elevation** = Standing in front of a building and photographing it
> - **Section** = Slicing a cake to see the inside layers

---

## 1.3 The Three Orthographic Views — Quick Overview

In Module Experiment 2 of GZESL208, the focus is on drawing **simple objects** (books, shelves, benches). Each of these objects is best understood through three primary orthographic projections:

| View Type | Direction of Sight | What It Shows | Common Use |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------|------------|
| **Plan** | Top → Down (Bird's Eye View) | Length × Width (horizontal layout) | Floor plans of buildings, site layouts |
| **Elevation** | Front → Back (Horizontal Eye Level) | Length × Height (vertical face) | Front view of buildings, façades |
| **Section** | Cutting plane through object | Internal details, hidden features | Showing construction layers, materials |

> [!TIP]
> For a **book**: The plan shows the book's outline as seen from above (a rectangle), the elevation shows the front cover (rectangle with title), and the section shows the page thickness and binding (a detailed internal view).

---

## 1.4 Drawing Tools and Accessories — Mandatory List

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Examination Requirement:** Students appearing for the GZESL208 workshop exam must be familiar with the following tools. Examiners frequently ask: *"Identify and state the use of the given tool."*

### A. Manual Drafting Tools (Traditional)
1. **Drawing Board** — A flat wooden or metallic board on which the drawing sheet is pinned.
2. **T-Square** — A T-shaped ruler used to draw horizontal lines and as a base for set squares.
3. **Set Squares** — Two triangular scales (45°-45°-90° and 30°-60°-90°) used to draw inclined lines.
4. **Compass** — For drawing circles and arcs; consists of a needle point and a pencil/pen point.
5. **Divider** — Similar to a compass but with two needle points; used to transfer measurements and divide lines.
6. **Protractor** — A semi-circular scale (0° to 180°) used to measure and draw angles.
7. **French Curves** — Curved plastic templates used to draw irregular smooth curves.
8. **Drawing Sheet** — Standard BIS sheet (A1, A2, A3, A4) of specific dimensions.
9. **Mini Drafter** — A modern replacement for T-square + set squares; combines all functions in a single adjustable head.
10. **Drawing Pencils** — Hard grades (2H, H, HB) for construction lines, soft grades (2B, 4B) for thick lines.

### B. Computer-Aided Drafting (CAD) Tools
1. **AutoCAD** — Industry-standard 2D/3D drafting software by Autodesk.
2. **SolidWorks / CATIA** — For 3D modeling (used in advanced courses).
3. **DraftSight / FreeCAD** — Open-source alternatives.
4. **Plotter / Printer** — For producing hard copies of CAD drawings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard BIS Sheet Sizes (SP 46:2003):**
> - **A0** = 1189 mm × 841 mm
> - **A1** = 841 mm × 594 mm
> - **A2** = 594 mm × 420 mm
> - **A3** = 420 mm × 297 mm
> - **A4** = 297 mm × 210 mm

---

## 1.5 Why Scales Are Used — The Core Concept

A real building (say, a house of **30 m × 10 m**) cannot be drawn at its actual size on a small drawing sheet. We must **reduce** the size proportionally so that it fits on paper while still being dimensionally accurate.

**Scale = Ratio of Drawing Size to Actual Size**

For example, a scale of **1:100** means **1 unit on the drawing** represents **100 units in reality**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (BIS):** *"Scale is the ratio of any linear dimension of the object as represented in the drawing to the actual linear dimension of the object."*

In the context of KTU GZESL208 Module Experiment 2, students draw **simple objects** (books, shelves, benches) at scales like **1:1, 1:2, 1:5, 1:10, 1:20, 1:50**, depending on the size of the object.

---

## 1.6 Manual Drafting vs. Computer-Aided Drafting (CAD)

| Parameter | Manual Drafting | Computer-Aided Drafting (CAD) |
|-----------|-----------------|-------------------------------|
| Tools | T-square, compass, set squares | AutoCAD, SolidWorks, etc. |
| Accuracy | Depends on human skill | High precision (up to 0.01 mm) |
| Speed | Slow | Fast (commands, shortcuts) |
| Editing | Difficult; must redraw | Easy; modify, regenerate |
| Storage | Physical sheets | Digital files, cloud backup |
| Cost | Low initial cost | High software cost, but long-term savings |
| Revision | Tedious | Simple — version control |
| 3D Capability | Not possible | Full 3D modeling and rendering |

> [!TIP]
> KTU 2024 emphasises **both manual and CAD drafting**. Students are expected to draft at least one object manually on a sheet and one using AutoCAD or equivalent software during the workshop.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Concept of Scale — A Deep Mathematical Treatment

### 2.1.1 What is a Scale?

In engineering drawing, the **scale** is a numeric ratio that defines the proportional relationship between the dimensions on the drawing and the actual (real-world) dimensions of the object.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$
\text{Representative Fraction (R.F.)} = \frac{\text{Drawing Length (DL)}}{\text{Actual Length (AL)}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Representative Fraction (R.F.)** is a dimensionless ratio. It has **no units** because the units in numerator and denominator cancel out. This is the most preferred method of expressing scale in engineering practice.

### 2.1.2 Classification of Scales

Scales are classified into three categories based on the value of R.F.:

**A. Full Scale (R.F. = 1:1)**
- The drawing is the **same size** as the actual object.
- Used for very small objects like bolts, nuts, washers, small machine parts, and small workshop objects.
- Example: Drawing of a small **book** or **mobile phone** on a sheet at 1:1 scale.

**B. Reducing Scale (R.F. < 1)**
- The drawing is **smaller** than the actual object.
- Used for objects too large to be drawn at full size.
- Examples: 1:2, 1:5, 1:10, 1:20, 1:50, 1:100, 1:200, 1:500, 1:1000, 1:2000.
- **Application:** A bench of 1.5 m length drawn at scale 1:10 becomes 150 mm on paper.

**C. Enlarging Scale (R.F. > 1)**
- The drawing is **larger** than the actual object.
- Used for very small components that need detail.
- Examples: 2:1, 5:1, 10:1, 50:1, 100:1.
- **Application:** A watch gear of 5 mm diameter drawn at 10:1 becomes 50 mm on paper.

### 2.1.3 Standard Scales Recommended by BIS (SP 46:2003)

| Category | Recommended Scales |
|----------|-------------------|
| Reducing Scales | 1:1, 1:2, 1:5, 1:10, 1:20, 1:50, 1:100, 1:200, 1:500, 1:1000, 1:2000, 1:5000 |
| Enlarging Scales | 2:1, 5:1, 10:1, 20:1, 50:1 |
| Full Scale | 1:1 |

### 2.1.4 Numerical Computation Logic

Given:
- **Actual length of object (AL)** in metres or millimetres.
- **Scale (R.F.)** = some ratio.

Find:
- **Drawing length (DL)** on the paper.

The formula is simply:

$$
\text{DL} = \text{R.F.} \times \text{AL}
$$

**Example 1:** A bookshelf is **1.8 m long**. Draw it on a sheet using a scale of **1:20**.

$$
\text{DL} = \frac{1}{20} \times 1.8 \, \text{m} = \frac{1}{20} \times 1800 \, \text{mm} = 90 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Example 2:** A bench is **2.4 m long**. We want its drawing length to be **120 mm**. Find the suitable scale.

$$
\text{R.F.} = \frac{\text{DL}}{\text{AL}} = \frac{120 \, \text{mm}}{2400 \, \text{mm}} = \frac{1}{20}
$$

So scale = **1:20**.

### 2.1.5 Plain Scales vs. Diagonal Scales

For GZESL208, students should understand the basic concept of a **plain scale**:

A **plain scale** is a straight line divided into a number of equal parts, representing the unit of measurement (metres, centimetres) at a given scale.

**Construction of a Plain Scale (Example):**
- Draw a line of suitable length (e.g., 200 mm).
- Divide it into a number of equal main parts (e.g., 4 parts of 50 mm each).
- Each main part represents **1 metre** (when scale is 1:20).
- The first main part is further sub-divided into smaller sub-parts (e.g., 5 parts of 10 mm each) to represent **centimetres**.
- A scale of 1:20 can measure up to **4 metres**, accurate to **20 cm** (0.2 m).

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU GZESL208 Module 2, students are NOT expected to construct full scales on paper. They are only required to **understand the concept** and **apply scales** while drawing simple objects.

---

## 2.2 Plan, Section, and Elevation — The Three Pillars of Civil Drawing

### 2.2.1 PLAN (Top View / Horizontal Projection)

**Definition:** A plan is the **orthographic projection** of an object as seen from **above**, looking vertically downward.

- Shows the **length (X-axis)** and **width (Y-axis)** of the object.
- For a building, the plan shows room layouts, door positions, window positions, furniture arrangement.
- For a **book**: Plan = rectangle showing length × width (page layout).
- For a **bench**: Plan = rectangle showing length × width (top surface).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Rule:** The plan is always placed **below the elevation** in First Angle Projection (used in India as per BIS).

### 2.2.2 ELEVATION (Front View / Vertical Projection)

**Definition:** An elevation is the **orthographic projection** of an object as seen from the **front**, looking horizontally at eye level.

- Shows the **length (X-axis)** and **height (Z-axis)** of the object.
- For a building, the elevation shows the façade, windows, doors, roof, and architectural features.
- For a **book**: Elevation = rectangle showing length × height (cover face).
- For a **bench**: Elevation = shows the legs, top plank, and side supports.

> [!TIP]
> An object can have **multiple elevations** — Front Elevation, Side Elevation, Rear Elevation. Choose the elevation that shows the **maximum detail** and the **most characteristic features**.

### 2.2.3 SECTION (Cut View / Internal View)

**Definition:** A section is the view obtained when an object is **imagined to be cut** by a vertical plane, and the portion in front of the cutting plane is removed to reveal the **internal details**.

- Shows **internal features** that are not visible in the plan or elevation.
- The cutting plane is shown as a **section line** (long-short dashes) with arrows indicating the direction of sight.
- For a **book**: Section shows the **page thickness, binding, cover thickness**.
- For a **bench**: Section shows the **internal joints, plank thickness, leg construction**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hatching (Section Lines):** The cut surface in a section view is shown with **parallel thin lines at 45°** (called hatching). The spacing and direction of hatching may differ for different materials:
> - **Timber/Wood:** Diagonal lines
> - **Concrete:** Random short lines with circles for aggregate
> - **Brick:** Stretcher and header patterns

### 2.2.4 First Angle vs. Third Angle Projection

India follows **First Angle Projection** (BIS standard), as opposed to Third Angle Projection used in the USA.

| View | First Angle (India/BIS) | Third Angle (USA) |
|------|------------------------|-------------------|
| Plan | Below the elevation | Above the elevation |
| Left Side View | To the right of elevation | To the left of elevation |

> [!NOTE]
> In **First Angle Projection**, the object is imagined to be in the **first quadrant**. The views are projected onto planes **behind** the object, then unfolded away from the viewer.

---

## 2.3 Drawing Tools — Detailed Functional Analysis

### A. Mini Drafter (Modern Drafting Machine)

A **mini drafter** is a precision instrument that combines the functions of:
- T-square (for horizontal lines)
- Set squares (for 45° and 30°-60° lines)
- Protractor (for angle measurement)

**Components:**
- **Scales (ruler blade)** — Marked with multiple scale graduations (1:1, 1:2, 1:5, 1:10, 1:20, 1:50, 1:100, 1:200).
- **Disc (clamping head)** — Allows the blade to rotate to any angle.
- **Pivot pin** — Locks the angle.
- **Hinge** — Allows vertical movement of the blade.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Hack:** The mini drafter is the **single most important tool** for manual drafting in KTU workshops. Know its parts and how to lock/unlock the protractor disc.

### B. Compass and Divider

**Compass:**
- Used to draw **circles and arcs** of a given radius.
- Has a **needle point** (fixed) and a **pencil lead holder** (rotating).
- The distance between the two points = radius of the circle.

**Divider:**
- Has **two needle points**.
- Used to **measure and transfer distances** and **divide lines** into equal parts.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse a compass with a divider. Remember — **compass has a lead**, **divider has two needles**.

### C. Scale Ruler (Engineer's Scale)

A **flat triangular scale** with 6 different graduations on its 6 faces. Each face is marked with a specific scale (e.g., 1:100, 1:200, 1:500, 1:1000, etc.). Used to directly measure lengths on a drawing.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This table is your exam-day revision weapon. Memorise it.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---------|----------------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Representative Fraction | $\text{R.F.} = \frac{\text{DL}}{\text{AL}}$ | Dimensionless | Define the scale of drawing |
| 2 | Drawing Length | $\text{DL} = \text{R.F.} \times \text{AL}$ | mm or cm | Find paper dimension |
| 3 | Actual Length | $\text{AL} = \frac{\text{DL}}{\text{R.F.}}$ | m or mm | Find real dimension from drawing |
| 4 | Full Scale | $\text{R.F.} = 1:1$ | — | Small objects (bolt, book) |
| 5 | Reducing Scale | $\text{R.F.} < 1$ (e.g., 1:50) | — | Large objects (building, road) |
| 6 | Enlarging Scale | $\text{R.F.} > 1$ (e.g., 5:1) | — | Tiny objects (gear, watch part) |
| 7 | Unit Conversion | 1 m = 1000 mm = 100 cm | — | All scaling problems |
| 8 | Maximum length on a plain scale | $\text{ML} = (\text{number of main parts}) \times (\text{unit length})$ | m | Scale construction |
| 9 | Smallest measurable length | $\text{SL} = \frac{\text{main part length}}{\text{number of sub-parts}}$ | m or cm | Scale precision |
| 10 | A-series sheet area ratio | $A_{n+1} = \frac{1}{2} \times A_n$ | mm² | Sheet size calculations |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Scale Typically Used | Why? |
|--------|----------------------|------|
| **Site Plan** | 1:500, 1:1000, 1:2000 | Show entire plot, roads, buildings |
| **Building Plan** | 1:50, 1:100 | Show floor plan with room details |
| **Building Elevation/Section** | 1:50, 1:100 | Show façade, internal construction |
| **Furniture Design** | 1:5, 1:10, 1:20 | Show bench, shelf, table details |
| **Machine Part (small)** | 1:1, 2:1, 5:1 | Show bolt, gear, shaft in detail |
| **City Map** | 1:10000, 1:50000 | Show urban layout, roads |
| **Country Map** | 1:1000000 and above | Show entire nation, topography |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Correct Scale for a Simple Object

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU GZESL208 Module 2, you will be given a **simple object** (book, shelf, bench) and asked to draw its three views. The first step is to **select a suitable scale**. Use the following decision matrix.

### Decision Algorithm

```
Step 1: Measure actual dimensions of the object (Length × Width × Height)
Step 2: Identify the largest dimension (L_max)
Step 3: Determine available drawing sheet area (A_sheet)
Step 4: Compute maximum allowable R.F. = A_draw / A_actual
Step 5: Round down to the nearest BIS standard scale
Step 6: Verify that all three views fit with adequate spacing
```

### Worked Example: Drawing a Bench

**Given:**
- Actual bench dimensions: Length = 1.5 m, Width = 0.5 m, Height = 0.45 m
- Available sheet: A3 (420 mm × 297 mm)
- Margin to leave: 25 mm on each side
- Effective drawing area: 370 mm × 247 mm

**Step 1: Identify largest dimension.**

$$
L_{\max} = 1500 \, \text{mm} \quad (\text{Length of bench})
$$

**Step 2: Determine available drawing length for elevation.**

$$
L_{\text{avail}} = 370 \, \text{mm} \quad (\text{after leaving margins})
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the maximum R.F.**

$$
\text{R.F.}_{\max} = \frac{L_{\text{avail}}}{L_{\max}} = \frac{370}{1500} \approx 0.247
$$

This corresponds to a scale of approximately **1:4**. The nearest BIS standard scale is **1:5** (or 1:10, depending on layout).

**Step 4: Verify.**

If we use **1:5** scale:
- Drawing length = 1500 / 5 = **300 mm** ✓ (fits in 370 mm)
- Drawing width = 500 / 5 = **100 mm** ✓ (fits in 247 mm)
- Drawing height = 450 / 5 = **90 mm** ✓

So, **scale 1:5** is suitable.

**If we use 1:10 scale (preferred for more clarity):**
- Drawing length = 1500 / 10 = **150 mm** ✓
- Drawing width = 500 / 10 = **50 mm** ✓
- Drawing height = 450 / 10 = **45 mm** ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb:** Always choose the **largest possible standard scale** that fits the object with comfortable margins. This gives the **most detail** on the drawing.

---

## 3.2 Worked Out Problems — Scale Calculations

### Problem 1: Drawing a Book

**Given:** A textbook has dimensions 240 mm × 180 mm × 25 mm (L × W × H).

**Find:** Suitable scale and drawing dimensions.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify dimensions.**
- Length = 240 mm
- Width = 180 mm
- Height = 25 mm

**Step 2: Try Full Scale (1:1).**

$$
\text{DL}_{\text{length}} = 240 \, \text{mm}
$$

This is a reasonable size for a drawing sheet (A4 or A3). **Full scale 1:1** is suitable.

**Step 3: Final drawing dimensions at 1:1.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Length on paper} &= 240 \, \text{mm} \\
\text{Width on paper} &= 180 \, \text{mm} \\
\text{Height on paper} &= 25 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Layout the views.**

| View | Dimensions on Paper | Position on Sheet |
|------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Plan | 240 mm × 180 mm | Below elevation |
| Elevation | 240 mm × 25 mm | Above plan |
| Side Elevation | 25 mm × 180 mm | Right of front elevation |

---

### Problem 2: Drawing a Wooden Shelf (Wall-mounted)

**Given:** A wall-mounted wooden shelf has actual dimensions:
- Length = 1.2 m
- Width = 0.25 m
- Height (thickness) = 0.05 m

**Find:** Suitable scale.

**Step 1: Convert to mm.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= 1200 \, \text{mm} \\
W &= 250 \, \text{mm} \\
H &= 50 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Try scale 1:10.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DL}_{L} &= 120 \, \text{mm} \\
\text{DL}_{W} &= 25 \, \text{mm} \\
\text{DL}_{H} &= 5 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

This fits comfortably on an **A4 sheet (210 × 297 mm)**.

**Step 3: Try scale 1:5 (larger drawing).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DL}_{L} &= 240 \, \text{mm} \\
\text{DL}_{W} &= 50 \, \text{mm} \\
\text{DL}_{H} &= 10 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

This also fits on an **A3 sheet (297 × 420 mm)** but is too large for A4.

**Conclusion:** **1:10** is suitable for A4 sheet; **1:5** is suitable for A3 sheet.

---

### Problem 3: Drawing a Reading Bench (3-Seater)

**Given:** A 3-seater reading bench:
- Length = 1.8 m
- Width = 0.6 m
- Height = 0.9 m

**Step 1: Convert to mm.**

$$
L = 1800 \, \text{mm}, \quad W = 600 \, \text{mm}, \quad H = 900 \, \text{mm}
$$

**Step 2: Try 1:20 scale.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DL}_{L} &= 90 \, \text{mm} \\
\text{DL}_{W} &= 30 \, \text{mm} \\
\text{DL}_{H} &= 45 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

This fits on an **A4 sheet** with ample margin. Suitable.

**Step 3: Try 1:10 scale.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DL}_{L} &= 180 \, \text{mm} \\
\text{DL}_{W} &= 60 \, \text{mm} \\
\text{DL}_{H} &= 90 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

This requires an **A3 sheet** (297 × 420 mm). Also suitable if A3 is available.

**Conclusion:** **1:20** for A4 sheet, **1:10** for A3 sheet.

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Procedure to Draw the Three Views of a Book

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the standard KTU workshop procedure. Examiners award marks for following the correct sequence.**

### Phase 1: Preparation

1. **Fix the drawing sheet** on the drawing board using clips or tape.
2. **Draw the border** with a margin of **25 mm** on all sides (for A3 and below) or **20 mm** for A2 and above.
3. **Draw the title block** at the bottom-right corner with the following fields:
   - Title of the drawing
   - Name of the student
   - Register number
   - Scale used
   - Date
   - College name
4. **Mark the layout zones** for plan, elevation, and side elevation.

### Phase 2: Drawing Construction (Light Lines with 2H Pencil)

1. **Draw horizontal and vertical reference lines** through the centre of each view zone.
2. **Mark the dimensions** of each view using a scale ruler.
3. **Lightly sketch the rectangles** representing each view.
4. **Check that all views align** (projection lines from elevation to plan should be vertical, and from elevation to side view should be horizontal).

### Phase 3: Finalisation (Dark Lines with HB/2B Pencil)

1. **Darken the visible outlines** of all three views using **HB pencil** (medium line thickness).
2. **Draw the hidden lines** as **dashed lines** using a sharper **H pencil**.
3. **Add centre lines** (long-short dashes) for symmetry.
4. **Add dimensions** using extension lines, dimension lines, and arrowheads.
5. **Add section hatching** (45° parallel lines) for the section view.
6. **Erase all construction lines** carefully.
7. **Write the title, scale, and notes** neatly in the title block.

### Phase 4: Quality Check

- All three views are correctly aligned (projection lines match).
- Dimensions are correctly scaled.
- Hidden lines are correctly placed.
- Hatching is consistent.
- Title block is complete.
- Sheet is clean, without smudges.

---

## 3.4 Detailed Construction Steps — Section of a Book

For the **section view** of a book, follow these steps:

1. Imagine a **vertical cutting plane** passing through the **middle** of the book (along its length).
2. The cutting plane is shown by a **section line** (chain line — long dash, short dash, long dash) on the elevation, with **arrows** indicating the direction of sight.
3. Remove the front half of the book.
4. The cut surface reveals:
   - The **front cover** (outer rectangle)
   - The **back cover** (inner rectangle)
   - The **pages** (multiple thin lines between the covers)
   - The **spine** (binding edge)
5. Hatch the **cut solid material** (covers) with **45° parallel lines**.
6. Do **not hatch** the pages (since they are many thin sheets, they are shown as a single section with a different convention, or left unhatched with a few representative lines).

> [!TIP]
> For GZESL208, a simple convention is: **Hatch only the cover thickness, show pages as 3-4 parallel lines representing the page block.**

---

## 3.5 Comprehensive Comparison Matrix — Plan vs. Elevation vs. Section

| Parameter | Plan | Elevation | Section |
|-----------|------|-----------|---------|
| Direction of sight | Top → Down | Front → Back | Through cutting plane |
| Axes shown | X (length) × Y (width) | X (length) × Z (height) | Internal structure |
| Shape of book | Rectangle (L × W) | Rectangle (L × H) | Rectangle with cover and page details |
| Position on sheet (First Angle) | Below elevation | Top-left or top-center | Right of elevation |
| Line type for visible edges | Thick continuous | Thick continuous | Thick continuous |
| Line type for hidden edges | Dashed | Dashed | Dashed (if not cut) |
| Hatching required? | No | No | Yes (for cut surfaces) |
| Typical KTU question | "Draw the plan of a book" | "Draw the front elevation" | "Draw the section along the centre line" |

---

## 3.6 Step-by-Step CAD Procedure (AutoCAD)

For students using **AutoCAD** in the workshop, the standard procedure is:

### Step 1: Set Up the Drawing

1. Open AutoCAD → New Drawing → Select **acad.dwt** template.
2. Set **Units**: Type `UNITS` → Select **Millimeters** → Set precision to **0**.
3. Set **Drawing Limits**: Type `LIMITS` → Set to **A3 size: 420, 297**.
4. Type `ZOOM` → `All` to view the full sheet.

### Step 2: Draw the Title Block and Border

1. Use the `RECTANGLE` command to draw the border.
2. Use `LINE` command to divide the title block.

### Step 3: Draw the Three Views

1. Use `LINE` command to draw the elevation rectangle first.
2. Use `OFFSET` or `COPY` to project dimensions to the plan view below.
3. Use `MIRROR` to draw the side view if symmetric.
4. Use `BHATCH` command to add section hatching (45° angle, ANSI31 pattern).

### Step 4: Add Dimensions

1. Use `DIMLINEAR` for linear dimensions.
2. Use `DIMALIGNED` for aligned dimensions.
3. Set dimension style: `DIMSTYLE` → set text height to **2.5 mm**, arrow size to **1.5 mm**.

### Step 5: Plot / Print

1. Type `PLOT` → Select printer/plotter.
2. Set paper size to A3 or A4.
3. Set scale to **1:1** (since the drawing is already drawn at scale).
4. Preview and print.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]
**Define the term 'Representative Fraction (R.F.)' as used in engineering drawing. What does a scale of 1:50 mean?**

**Model Answer:**

**Representative Fraction (R.F.)** is the ratio of the length of a line on the drawing to the corresponding actual length of the object, both measured in the same units. It is a dimensionless quantity since the units cancel out.

$$
\text{R.F.} = \frac{\text{Drawing Length (DL)}}{\text{Actual Length (AL)}}
$$

A scale of **1:50** means that **1 unit on the drawing** (e.g., 1 mm or 1 cm) represents **50 units in reality** (50 mm or 50 cm or 50 m, depending on the unit chosen). For example, a wall of length 10 m drawn at 1:50 will have a drawing length of:

$$
\text{DL} = \frac{1}{50} \times 10 \, \text{m} = 0.2 \, \text{m} = 200 \, \text{mm}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marks Distribution (3 marks):**
> - Definition of R.F. with formula: 2 marks
> - Explanation of 1:50 with example: 1 mark

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]
**Differentiate between a 'Plan' and an 'Elevation' in civil engineering drawing with a suitable example of a book.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Plan | Elevation |
|-----------|------|-----------|
| Direction of sight | Top → Down (bird's eye view) | Front → Back (eye level) |
| Shows | Length × Width | Length × Height |
| Position on sheet (1st Angle) | Below the elevation | Above the plan |
| Application | Site layout, floor plan, roof plan | Façade, front view, side view |

**Example of a Book (dimensions 240 mm × 180 mm × 25 mm):**
- **Plan (Top View):** A rectangle of **240 mm × 180 mm**, showing the **cover page** layout.
- **Elevation (Front View):** A rectangle of **240 mm × 25 mm**, showing the **spine** of the book with the title.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marks Distribution (3 marks):**
> - Tabular differentiation: 2 marks
> - Book example: 1 mark

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**Q. (a)** What is meant by 'scale' in engineering drawing? List the standard scales recommended by BIS for reducing and enlarging scales. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
A **scale** is the ratio of the length of a given component on a drawing to its actual size in the object being represented. It is mathematically expressed as the **Representative Fraction (R.F.)**, which is dimensionless.

$$
\text{R.F.} = \frac{\text{Drawing Length}}{\text{Actual Length}}
$$

**Purpose of Scale:**
Engineering drawings are made on paper of limited size, but real engineering objects (buildings, bridges, machines) are much larger (or sometimes much smaller) than the paper. Scales allow us to represent these objects **proportionally and accurately**.

**BIS Recommended Standard Scales (SP 46:2003):**

| Reducing Scales | Enlarging Scales |
|-----------------|------------------|
| 1:1 | 2:1 |
| 1:2 | 5:1 |
| 1:5 | 10:1 |
| 1:10 | 20:1 |
| 1:20 | 50:1 |
| 1:50 | |
| 1:100 | |
| 1:200 | |
| 1:500 | |
| 1:1000 | |
| 1:2000 | |
| 1:5000 | |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Reducing Scales:** 1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100, 200, 500, 1000, 2000, 5000 — Notice the doubling-then-2.5x pattern (1→2→5→10→20→50→100...).

---

**Q. (b)** A wooden reading bench has actual dimensions 1800 mm × 600 mm × 900 mm (Length × Width × Height). If the drawing is prepared on an A3 sheet (420 mm × 297 mm) with a 25 mm margin on all sides, determine a suitable scale and calculate the dimensions of the front elevation, plan, and side elevation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1: Effective Sheet Area**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Effective Length} &= 420 - 2 \times 25 = 370 \, \text{mm} \\
\text{Effective Width} &= 297 - 2 \times 25 = 247 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Maximum Allowable R.F.**

The largest object dimension is Length = 1800 mm. The longest view (elevation or plan) should not exceed 370 mm.

$$
\text{R.F.}_{\max} = \frac{370}{1800} \approx 0.2056
$$

This corresponds to a scale of approximately **1:4.86**. The nearest BIS standard scale **smaller** than this is **1:5** (i.e., 0.2).

**Step 3: Verify Scale 1:5**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DL}_{\text{length}} &= 1800 / 5 = 360 \, \text{mm} \\
\text{DL}_{\text{width}} &= 600 / 5 = 120 \, \text{mm} \\
\text{DL}_{\text{height}} &= 900 / 5 = 180 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Total length required (Elevation + Plan, side by side) = 360 + 120 = 480 mm > 370 mm. **Does NOT fit**.

**Step 4: Try Scale 1:10**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{DL}_{\text{length}} &= 1800 / 10 = 180 \, \text{mm} \\
\text{DL}_{\text{width}} &= 600 / 10 = 60 \, \text{mm} \\
\text{DL}_{\text{height}} &= 900 / 10 = 90 \, \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

Total length (Elevation + Plan, side by side) = 180 + 60 = 240 mm < 370 mm. **FITS comfortably**.

**Step 5: Final Selection — Scale 1:10**

| View | Drawing Dimensions |
|------|--------------------|
| Front Elevation | 180 mm × 90 mm |
| Plan | 180 mm × 60 mm |
| Side Elevation | 60 mm × 90 mm |

**R.F. = 1:10** is the most suitable scale.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**Q. (a)** Explain the difference between **First Angle Projection** and **Third Angle Projection**. Which one is followed in India as per BIS? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Orthographic projection is a method of drawing 3D objects using 2D views. There are two standard systems:

**First Angle Projection:**
- The object is imagined to be in the **first quadrant**.
- The object is placed **between the observer and the plane of projection**.
- Views are projected onto planes **behind the object**.
- **Plan (Top View)** is placed **below** the front elevation.
- **Left Side View** is placed on the **right** of the elevation.

**Third Angle Projection:**
- The object is imagined to be in the **third quadrant**.
- The plane of projection is placed **between the observer and the object**.
- **Plan (Top View)** is placed **above** the front elevation.
- **Left Side View** is placed on the **left** of the elevation.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | First Angle (India, Europe) | Third Angle (USA, Canada) |
|---------|---------------------------|--------------------------|
| Quadrant | First (object before plane) | Third (plane before object) |
| Plan position | Below elevation | Above elevation |
| Left side view | Right of elevation | Left of elevation |
| Top view | Below | Above |
| Standard body | BIS (India), ISO (Europe) | ANSI (USA) |

**India follows the FIRST ANGLE PROJECTION method** as per **BIS SP 46:2003** and **ISO standards**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (7 marks):**
> - Definitions of both: 2 marks
> - Comparison table: 3 marks
> - India standard + reference: 2 marks

---

**Q. (b)** Draw the **plan, front elevation, and side elevation** of a book with dimensions 240 mm × 180 mm × 25 mm at **full scale (1:1)**. Describe the procedure briefly. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:**
- Book dimensions: 240 mm (Length) × 180 mm (Width) × 25 mm (Height/Thickness)
- Scale: 1:1 (Full scale)
- Sheet: A3 (420 mm × 297 mm)

**Step 1: Layout Planning**

Reserve zones on the A3 sheet:
- **Front Elevation (Top):** 240 mm × 25 mm
- **Plan (Below Elevation):** 240 mm × 180 mm
- **Side Elevation (Right of Front Elevation):** 25 mm × 180 mm

**Step 2: Draw the Front Elevation**

Draw a rectangle of 240 mm (horizontal) × 25 mm (vertical).

- Draw the **book title** as a horizontal line in the middle.
- Mark the **spine** as a thick line on the left edge.

**Step 3: Project Downward and Draw the Plan**

From the corners of the front elevation, project vertical lines downward by 180 mm. Close the rectangle to form the plan. The plan shows the **front cover** of the book.

- Add the **book title block** as a rectangle in the centre of the cover.
- Add **corner ornaments** if needed.

**Step 4: Project Rightward and Draw the Side Elevation**

From the corners of the front elevation, project horizontal lines to the right by 25 mm. Draw a vertical line of 180 mm on the right end. Close the rectangle to form the side elevation. The side elevation shows the **page block** and **spine binding**.

- Add **page lines** as 3-4 parallel horizontal lines representing the page edges.

**Step 5: Dimensioning**

- Add **overall dimensions** (240, 180, 25) using extension lines, dimension lines, and arrowheads.
- Add a **centre line** (long-short dashes) along the length of the book in all three views.

**Step 6: Title Block**

Write in the title block:
- Title: "Three Views of a Book"
- Scale: 1:1
- Name, Register No., Date

**ASCII Sketch (Schematic Layout):**

```
  ┌────────────────────────────────────┐
  │     FRONT ELEVATION (240×25)       │  ┌──────────┐
  │     [Title in middle]              │  │   SIDE   │
  │     [Spine on left]                │  │   VIEW   │
  └────────────────────────────────────┘  │  (25×180)│
  ┌────────────────────────────────────┐  │  [Page   │
  │                                    │  │  lines]  │
  │     PLAN / TOP VIEW (240×180)      │  │          │
  │     [Book cover layout]            │  │          │
  │     [Title block in centre]        │  │          │
  │                                    │  └──────────┘
  └────────────────────────────────────┘
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (7 marks):**
> - Procedure description: 3 marks
> - Three views correctly drawn: 3 marks
> - Title block + dimensions: 1 mark

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **TOP 5 MISTAKES STUDENTS MAKE IN GZESL208 MODULE 2 (READ THIS BEFORE EXAM!)**

**Mistake 1: Confusing Plan and Elevation placement.**
Many students draw the plan **above** the elevation. In India (BIS First Angle Projection), the plan is **ALWAYS BELOW** the front elevation. **Lose 1-2 marks** if this is wrong.

**Mistake 2: Forgetting to write the scale on the drawing.**
The scale (e.g., 1:10, 1:5) **MUST** be written in the title block AND below the view. **Lose 0.5-1 mark** if missing.

**Mistake 3: Wrong line types.**
- Object outline = **thick continuous line**
- Hidden features = **dashed line**
- Centre line = **long-short dash**
- Construction line = **thin continuous line**
Mixing these up = **lose 1-2 marks**.

**Mistake 4: Incorrect dimensioning.**
Dimensions must be placed in the **correct view** (length on elevation/plan, height on elevation, width on plan). Students often write all dimensions on a single view = **lose 1-2 marks**.

**Mistake 5: Not leaving proper margins and gaps between views.**
Views should be **spaced out** with at least 20-30 mm gap, and margins of 25 mm on all sides. Cramped drawings = **lose aesthetic marks** and may indicate poor planning.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin this checklist to your wall. Read it the night before the exam.**

### ✅ Core Concepts (Must Know)

- [x] **Civil Engineering Drawing (CED)** = Graphical language of civil engineers, governed by **BIS SP 46:2003**.
- [x] **Scale (R.F.)** = Drawing Length / Actual Length, dimensionless ratio.
- [x] **Full Scale** = 1:1 (small objects like books, bolts).
- [x] **Reducing Scale** = 1:N where N > 1 (large objects like buildings).
- [x] **Enlarging Scale** = N:1 where N > 1 (tiny objects like watch parts).

### ✅ Three Views (Must Know)

- [x] **Plan** = Top view (L × W), placed **below** the elevation in 1st angle projection.
- [x] **Elevation** = Front view (L × H), placed at the **top**.
- [x] **Section** = Cut view showing internal details, with **45° hatching** on cut surfaces.

### ✅ Projection Method (Must Know)

- [x] India uses **First Angle Projection** (BIS standard).
- [x] Plan is below elevation; Left Side View is to the right of elevation.

### ✅ Tools (Must Recognise)

- [x] **Mini Drafter** = Modern T-square + set square + protractor combination.
- [x] **Compass** = Has a pencil lead (for circles).
- [x] **Divider** = Has two needles (for transferring distances).
- [x] **Scale Ruler** = Triangular ruler with 6 faces, each with a different scale.

### ✅ Standard Sheet Sizes (BIS)

- [x] A0 = 1189 × 841 mm
- [x] A1 = 841 × 594 mm
- [x] A2 = 594 × 420 mm
- [x] A3 = 420 × 297 mm
- [x] A4 = 297 × 210 mm

### ✅ Formulae (Must Memorise)

- [x] $\text{DL} = \text{R.F.} \times \text{AL}$
- [x] $\text{AL} = \frac{\text{DL}}{\text{R.F.}}$
- [x] $\text{R.F.} = \frac{\text{DL}}{\text{AL}}$ (dimensionless)

### ✅ Drawing Procedure (Must Follow)

- [x] Step 1: Measure actual dimensions.
- [x] Step 2: Choose sheet and compute suitable R.F.
- [x] Step 3: Draw border, margins, and title block.
- [x] Step 4: Mark view zones; draw light construction lines.
- [x] Step 5: Draw front elevation first; project to plan and side view.
- [x] Step 6: Darken visible lines; add hidden lines, hatching, dimensions.
- [x] Step 7: Add section view if required; complete title block.

### ✅ Manual vs. CAD

- [x] **Manual** = T-square, compass, set squares, drawing pencils. Slow but foundational.
- [x] **CAD (AutoCAD)** = Fast, accurate, editable, 3D capable. Industry standard.

### ✅ Quick-Recall Mnemonics

- [x] **PES Order on Sheet:** **P**lan is **B**elow **E**levation (P-B-E).
- [x] **Line Types — "Continuous, Dashed, Chain, Thin":** "**C**ompany **D**eals **C**heap **T**hings" = Continuous (object), Dashed (hidden), Chain (centre), Thin (construction).
- [x] **Standard Scales — "1, 2, 5 Pattern":** Each scale is 1, 2, or 5 times the next (1, 2, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100...).

---

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip:** When asked to draw a simple object, **always state the chosen scale with justification** ("A scale of 1:10 is selected because the largest dimension of 1.8 m reduces to 180 mm, which fits comfortably within an A3 sheet"). This single sentence can fetch you **extra marks** in the KTU exam.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Draw sectional details and elevation of paneled doors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZESL208 - BASIC ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Draw sectional details and elevation of paneled doors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **paneled door** is a type of hinged or pivoted door shutter whose surface area is divided into smaller, recessed or raised rectangular, square, or geometric sections called **panels**, framed by an assembly of vertical members called **styles** and horizontal members called **rails**. According to the Bureau of Indian Standards (relevant IS 1003 and IS 2191 series) referenced in KTU engineering drawing and workshop practice, a paneled door consists of a **door frame** (head, jambs, sill) and a **door shutter** (styles, rails, panels, and muntins) joined together using traditional carpentry joints such as the **mortise and tenon joint**.

The drawing exercise of preparing the **elevation** and **sectional details** is a fundamental part of engineering drawing workshops, where students learn to represent a building component in:
- **Orthographic Elevation** — the front face view showing the panel layout, frame opening, and architectural proportions.
- **Vertical Sectional Elevation (Sectional Detail)** — a cut-through view that exposes the internal joinery, rebated portions, hinge locations, and panel embedding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the GZESL208 workshop module, the focus is on the **sectional plan**, **sectional elevation**, and **front elevation** of a single-leaf paneled door of standard residential dimensions (typically **900 mm × 2100 mm** or **1000 mm × 2100 mm**) with the shutter open at 90° in the plan view.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Picture Frame Mental Model

Think of a paneled door exactly like an **ornate picture frame that has been subdivided into multiple smaller frames**:

| Real-World Analogy | Door Component |
|---|---|
| Outer rectangular frame of a photo | **Door Frame** (head + jambs + sill) |
| Big picture behind the glass | **Panels** (the recessed wooden sheets) |
| The decorative dividers (muntins) that split the photo into segments | **Rails and Muntins** of the shutter |
| The vertical and horizontal edges of the frame | **Styles and Rails** |
| Hinges on the back of the frame | **Ironmongery** (hinges, tower bolts, handles) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** The "panels" are NOT structural — they merely fill the gaps between the load-bearing styles and rails. This is why panels are made thinner and are free to expand/contract in grooves, while styles and rails carry the door's weight.

## 1.3 Standard Nomenclature and Metrics

The following bolded metrics are the **standard residential door sizes** prescribed by IS 1003 (Timber Panelled and Glazed Shutters) and commonly adopted in KTU workshop sheets:

- **Standard door size:** 900 mm (width) × 2100 mm (height), or 1000 mm × 2100 mm.
- **Frame section (rebat):** 75 mm × 100 mm (finished) for internal doors.
- **Shutter thickness:** 30 mm, 35 mm, or 40 mm (commonly 35 mm).
- **Style width:** 100 mm to 150 mm.
- **Rail height:** 100 mm to 150 mm.
- **Panel thickness:** 20 mm to 25 mm (recessed 10–12 mm from the face).
- **Clear opening:** Frame width minus 2 × frame thickness; clear height likewise.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Component Hierarchy — Anatomy of a Paneled Door System

A complete paneled door assembly can be deconstructed into **three primary subsystems**: the **frame**, the **shutter**, and the **ironmongery (hardware)**. Each subsystem has its own sub-components, and every KTU drawing must label these explicitly to score full marks.

### 2.1.1 Door Frame Subsystem
The frame is the fixed part embedded into the wall opening. It has four members:

- **Head** — the top horizontal member spanning the full width of the opening.
- **Jambs (Left & Right)** — the two vertical side members, each anchored to the wall through hold-fasts or dowels.
- **Sill** — the bottom horizontal member, **present only in external doors**; internal doors usually have no sill and the shutter rests directly on the floor (with a 10–15 mm clearance gap).

The frame has an **internal rebate (groove)** of dimensions **30 mm × 15 mm** (depth × width) cut along its inner edge to receive the shutter when closed.

### 2.1.2 Door Shutter Subsystem
The shutter is the movable leaf. For a typical **four-panel door** (the most common KTU workshop problem), the shutter contains:

- **Styles (Vertical members):**
  - *Hinge style* — the style on which the hinges are fixed; usually 100 mm wide.
  - *Lock style* — the style on the opposite side, often slightly wider (150 mm) to house the lock mechanism.
  - *Meeting style* — present only in double-leaf doors.

- **Rails (Horizontal members):** from top to bottom:
  - *Top rail* — 100 mm high, immediately below the head.
  - *Upper frieze rail (Intermediate rail)* — divides the upper and middle panels.
  - *Lower frieze rail* — divides the middle and lower panels.
  - *Bottom rail* — 150–200 mm high, the widest rail for structural strength.

- **Panels (Infilling):**
  - *Top panel, two middle panels, and bottom panel* — thin wooden sheets (20–25 mm thick) that fit into grooves cut into the inner faces of styles and rails.
  - Panels are typically **recessed 10–12 mm** from the shutter face and held by a small **beading (glazing bead equivalent)** nailed or pinned in place.

- **Muntins (Mullions):** optional intermediate vertical dividers used in **six-panel** or **eight-panel** doors to further split the panels.

### 2.1.3 Ironmongery Subsystem
- **Hinges** (3 nos. for a 2100 mm high shutter): top, middle, and bottom — each **100 mm × 75 mm × 3 mm** (length × width × thickness) with knuckle diameter **20–25 mm**.
- **Tower bolt** (top and bottom on the lock style).
- **Mortise lock** embedded in the lock style.
- **Handle** (lever or pull handle).
- **Door stopper / buffer** fixed on the floor or wall.
- **Hasp and staple** (for double-leaf doors).

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Why These Components Exist

The paneled door design is governed by **three engineering rationales** that KTU expects you to articulate in viva:

1. **Shrinkage Compensation:** Solid timber expands and contracts with humidity. By constructing the shutter as a frame-and-panel system, the panels can **float** in grooves and shrink/expand independently without cracking the surrounding styles and rails.
2. **Load Distribution:** Styles and rails are oriented with the timber grain **parallel to their length**, providing maximum strength. Panels carry no structural load — they are merely infill.
3. **Aesthetic Modular Proportioning:** The width-to-height ratio of each panel is kept close to the **golden ratio (1 : 1.618)** for visual harmony. KTU drawing sheets often specify this as a design constraint.

> [!TIP]
> **Viva Favourite:** *"Why is the bottom rail wider than the top rail?"* → The bottom rail bears the impact of kicks and the weight of the door; hence a larger cross-section provides more bending resistance and helps resist warping at the base.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following ratios and formulas are **high-yield** and have appeared in KTU university exam Part A questions. Memorize the relations in the **bolded** column.

| # | Parameter | Formula / Ratio | Standard Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Clear opening width | $W_{clear} = W_{opening} - 2 \times T_{frame}$ | 750–850 mm | Determines shutter width |
| 2 | Clear opening height | $H_{clear} = H_{opening} - T_{frame,top} - T_{sill}$ | 2050 mm | Determines shutter height |
| 3 | Shutter width | $W_{shutter} = W_{clear} - 2 \times R_{rebat}$ | 720–820 mm | Drawing dimension |
| 4 | Shutter height | $H_{shutter} = H_{clear} - R_{rebat,top} - R_{rebat,bot}$ | 2040 mm | Drawing dimension |
| 5 | Panel width ratio | $W_{panel} : H_{panel} \approx 1 : \sqrt{2}$ | Golden rule | Aesthetic proportion |
| 6 | No. of hinges | $N_{hinges} = \dfrac{H_{shutter}}{700 \text{ mm}}$ rounded up | 3 for 2100 mm | Hardware rule of thumb |
| 7 | Style grain direction | Parallel to door height | — | Strength |
| 8 | Panel recess depth | $D_{recess} = 0.4 \times T_{panel}$ to $0.5 \times T_{panel}$ | 10–12 mm | Beading accommodation |
| 9 | Frame section (internal) | $75 \text{ mm} \times 100 \text{ mm}$ finished | — | Standard IS size |
| 10 | Shutter thickness | $T_{shutter} = T_{panel} + 2 \times \text{projection}$ | 30–40 mm | Shutter rigidity |

Where:
- $T_{frame}$ = thickness of door frame member.
- $R_{rebat}$ = rebate depth (usually 15 mm) inside the frame.
- $D_{recess}$ = how much the panel is set back from the shutter face.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The paneled door is not merely a workshop drawing exercise — it is the **gateway to understanding**:
- **Modular joinery design** used in furniture, modular kitchens, and cabinet making.
- **Timber-frame construction** principles applied in modern cross-laminated timber (CLT) buildings.
- **Detailing for estimation** — every length, area, and volume in a paneled door is computed from the elevation and section for **BOQ (Bill of Quantities)** preparation in civil engineering projects.
- **Heritage conservation** — restorers of colonial-era Kerala buildings use these exact drawings to fabricate replacement shutters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivation, Drawing Procedure & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Drawing Procedure

This is the **canonical 12-step procedure** expected in your KTU record. Each step must be performed sequentially and verified before proceeding.

### Step 1 — Read the Problem Statement
Typical KTU problem: *"Draw to scale 1:20 the elevation, vertical section, and horizontal section of a single-leaf four-panel door of size 1000 mm × 2100 mm. The frame is 75 mm × 100 mm and the shutter is 35 mm thick."*

> [!NOTE]
> Always start by writing the given data on the **top-right corner of the sheet**, just below the title block. This earns 1 mark in valuation.

### Step 2 — Adopt a Standard Sheet Layout
Lay out the **four views** in First-Angle Projection as follows (typical KTU sheet convention):

```
+--------------------------------------------------+
|  TITLE BLOCK (bottom-right)                     |
+--------------------------------+-----------------+
|                                |                 |
|     VERTICAL SECTION           |   FRONT         |
|     (Full height)              |   ELEVATION     |
|                                |   (Front face)  |
+--------------------------------+-----------------+
|                                |                 |
|     HORIZONTAL SECTION         |   PLAN          |
|     (Through shutter)          |   (Door open)   |
|                                |                 |
+--------------------------------+-----------------+
```

### Step 3 — Compute All Derived Dimensions

**Given:** $W_{opening} = 1000$ mm, $H_{opening} = 2100$ mm, $T_{frame} = 75$ mm, $R_{rebat} = 15$ mm, $T_{shutter} = 35$ mm.

Let us derive each dimension explicitly.

The clear opening width when the frame is installed:

$$
W_{clear} = W_{opening} - 2 \times T_{frame}
$$

$$
W_{clear} = 1000 - 2 \times 75 = 1000 - 150 = 850 \text{ mm}
$$

The clear opening height (assuming a sill, so subtract bottom frame thickness too):

$$
H_{clear} = H_{opening} - T_{frame,top} - T_{sill}
$$

$$
H_{clear} = 2100 - 100 - 50 = 1950 \text{ mm}
$$

(If no sill, then $H_{clear} = 2100 - 100 = 2000$ mm; KTU typically uses 1950 mm when a sill is specified.)

The shutter width, accounting for the rebate on both sides:

$$
W_{shutter} = W_{clear} - 2 \times R_{rebat}
$$

$$
W_{shutter} = 850 - 2 \times 15 = 850 - 30 = 820 \text{ mm}
$$

The shutter height, accounting for the rebate at top and bottom:

$$
H_{shutter} = H_{clear} - R_{rebat,top} - R_{rebat,bot}
$$

$$
H_{shutter} = 1950 - 15 - 15 = 1920 \text{ mm}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validation Rule:** Shutter dimensions must be slightly less than clear opening. If $W_{shutter} \ge W_{clear}$ or $H_{shutter} \ge H_{clear}$, your computation is **wrong** — recheck the rebate dimensions.

### Step 4 — Determine Rail and Style Widths (Standard Practice)

For a 820 mm × 1920 mm shutter, use the following standard member sizes:

- **Hinge style width:** 100 mm
- **Lock style width:** 150 mm
- **Top rail height:** 100 mm
- **Bottom rail height:** 200 mm (wider for strength)
- **Frieze rails (intermediate):** 100 mm each (2 nos.)

Total horizontal non-panel space at top and bottom: $100 + 200 = 300$ mm.
Total horizontal friezes: $2 \times 100 = 200$ mm.
Remaining for panels vertically: $1920 - 300 - 200 = 1420$ mm, divided into 3 panel rows ⇒ $1420 / 3 \approx 473$ mm per panel row (rounded to 470 mm).

Total vertical non-panel space: $100 + 150 = 250$ mm.
Remaining for panels horizontally per column: $820 - 250 = 570$ mm, divided into 2 panel columns ⇒ $285$ mm per panel.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU expects the panel layout to be **2 columns × 3 rows = 6 panels**, OR a single column × 3 rows = 3 panels, OR **2 columns × 2 rows = 4 panels**. The most common KTU problem is the **four-panel door** (2 × 2 grid), but the procedure here is shown for a 6-panel door for completeness.

### Step 5 — Draw the Front Elevation

1. Draw a horizontal baseline (floor level) at the bottom of the elevation area.
2. Mark the **outer frame** as a rectangle of 1000 mm × 2100 mm (1:20 scale ⇒ 50 mm × 105 mm on paper).
3. Draw the **inner opening** of 850 mm × 1950 mm.
4. Draw the **shutter** as a slightly smaller rectangle of 820 mm × 1920 mm, set inside the opening with a 15 mm rebate gap on three sides.
5. Divide the shutter vertically: mark the two styles (100 mm left, 150 mm right).
6. Divide the shutter horizontally: mark the rails (100 mm top, 200 mm bottom, 100 mm × 2 friezes).
7. Within the remaining panel area, draw the panel rectangles (recessed lines drawn as **continuous thin lines**, 5 mm inside the shutter face in the elevation).
8. Add the **hinges** (3 small rectangles on the hinge style) and the **handle** (small circle on the lock style at ~1000 mm height).

### Step 6 — Draw the Vertical Section (Sectional Elevation)

1. Take a vertical cutting plane through the **hinge side** of the door (passing through one hinge and through the lock).
2. Show the **wall** in section (brick hatch or concrete hatch as per convention).
3. Show the **frame head, jamb, and sill** in their actual cross-section (75 mm × 100 mm rectangle with 15 mm rebate cut).
4. Show the **shutter** as a 35 mm thick vertical member, recessed in the rebate.
5. Inside the shutter section, indicate the **styles** (full height), the **rails** (cross-section at 100 mm height), and the **panels** (with dotted line indicating recess).
6. Show the **hinge** in section — the two leaves with the knuckle (cylindrical pin) between them.
7. Show the **floor finish** and the **wall plaster** levels.

### Step 7 — Draw the Horizontal Section (Sectional Plan)

1. Take a horizontal cutting plane at the **lock rail level** (or 1000 mm from floor).
2. Show the wall, frame jambs, shutter, and the hinge/leaves in plan.
3. Indicate the **opening direction** of the shutter (90° open in plan is the KTU convention).

### Step 8 — Add the Conventional Symbols and Hatching

- **Brick masonry:** diagonal cross-hatch at 45°.
- **Concrete:** solid triangles or stipple pattern.
- **Timber in section:** horizontal grain lines.
- **Glass:** thin line with cross marks.
- **Hinges/iron:** solid black.

### Step 9 — Dimension the Drawing

Use the **aligned dimensioning system** (IS 11669). All dimensions in mm. Show:
- Overall frame size.
- Clear opening.
- Shutter size.
- Style widths, rail heights, panel sizes.
- Hinge positions from the top/bottom (typically 150 mm from top, 150 mm from bottom, and one in the middle).

### Step 10 — Add Title Block, Scale, and North Arrow (if applicable)

### Step 11 — Add Notes and BOM (Bill of Materials)
List the timber species, hardware schedule, and finish specification (e.g., "2 coats of melamine polish").

### Step 12 — Final Check
Verify: 1) all views aligned, 2) all dimensions consistent, 3) hidden lines as dashed, 4) visible lines as continuous thick, 5) section hatching correct.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Common Paneled Door Types

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part A questions often ask: *"Differentiate between a paneled door and a flush door."* The following matrix helps you construct a comparative answer quickly.

| # | Parameter | **Four-Panel Door** | **Six-Panel Door** | **Flush Door** | **Glazed Paneled Door** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Construction | Frame + 4 panels | Frame + 6 panels | Plywood/MDF on frame | Frame + glass panes in panels |
| 2 | Weight | Moderate | Heavier | Lightest | Moderate |
| 3 | Cost | Medium | Higher | Lowest | Higher (glass cost) |
| 4 | Privacy | Full | Full | Full | Partial (glass transparent) |
| 5 | Use | Bedrooms, main door | Living rooms | Bathrooms, modern homes | Kitchen, verandah |
| 6 | Air circulation | Poor | Poor | Poor | Good |
| 7 | Aesthetic | Traditional, classic | More decorative | Modern, plain | Vintage, colonial |
| 8 | Maintenance | Periodic polish | More joinery = more upkeep | Low | Glass cleaning needed |
| 9 | Strength against impact | High (deep rails) | Highest | Lowest (delamination risk) | Moderate |
| 10 | Common KTU problem | **Most frequent** | Occasional | Sometimes asked | Rare |

## 3.3 Derivations — Mathematical Justification of Common Proportions

### Derivation 1 — Minimum Number of Hinges

The number of hinges $N$ for a shutter of height $H_{shutter}$ is derived from the rule that each hinge can support a maximum vertical spacing of 700 mm without sagging:

$$
N_{hinges} = \left\lceil \frac{H_{shutter}}{700 \text{ mm}} \right\rceil
$$

For $H_{shutter} = 1920$ mm:

$$
N_{hinges} = \left\lceil \frac{1920}{700} \right\rceil = \left\lceil 2.74 \right\rceil = 3 \text{ hinges}
$$

Hence, the **3-hinge rule** for any standard residential door.

### Derivation 2 — Panel Recess Depth

The panel is recessed by approximately **40% to 50% of its thickness** to ensure that:
- The beading (which holds the panel in the groove) does not protrude beyond the shutter face.
- The panel edge is hidden and protected from impact.
- The aesthetic shadow line (reveal) is visually pleasing.

$$
D_{recess} = k \times T_{panel}, \quad \text{where } 0.4 \le k \le 0.5
$$

For $T_{panel} = 25$ mm:

$$
D_{recess} = 0.5 \times 25 = 12.5 \text{ mm} \approx 12 \text{ mm}
$$

### Derivation 3 — Golden Ratio Panel Proportion (Aesthetic Rule)

For a visually pleasing panel, the ratio of panel height to panel width should approximate the golden ratio $\phi = 1.618$:

$$
\frac{H_{panel}}{W_{panel}} \approx \phi \approx 1.618
$$

Given $W_{panel} = 285$ mm (computed earlier):

$$
H_{panel} = \phi \times W_{panel} = 1.618 \times 285 \approx 461 \text{ mm}
$$

This explains why designers often target panel heights between **450 mm and 480 mm** for a 285 mm wide panel in a 6-panel door.

> [!TIP]
> **University Tip:** If the KTU problem does not specify panel dimensions, use the golden ratio or the equal-division rule to propose your own panel layout. Mention the assumption in your "Notes" block.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Component Hierarchy — Mermaid Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram presents the **functional architecture flow** of a paneled door system, mapping how each subsystem interacts with the others. This is a block-level representation suitable as a KTU record supplement.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["Paneled Door Assembly"] --> nodeB["Door Frame System"]
    nodeA --> nodeC["Door Shutter System"]
    nodeA --> nodeD["Ironmongery System"]
    nodeA --> nodeE["Wall Opening"]

    nodeB --> nodeB1["Head Member"]
    nodeB --> nodeB2["Jamb Left"]
    nodeB --> nodeB3["Jamb Right"]
    nodeB --> nodeB4["Sill Bottom"]

    nodeC --> nodeC1["Styles Vertical"]
    nodeC --> nodeC2["Rails Horizontal"]
    nodeC --> nodeC3["Panels Infill"]
    nodeC --> nodeC4["Muntins Divider"]
    nodeC --> nodeC5["Beading Trim"]

    nodeC1 --> nodeC1a["Hinge Style 100mm"]
    nodeC1 --> nodeC1b["Lock Style 150mm"]

    nodeC2 --> nodeC2a["Top Rail 100mm"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2b["Bottom Rail 200mm"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2c["Upper Frieze 100mm"]
    nodeC2 --> nodeC2d["Lower Frieze 100mm"]

    nodeC3 --> nodeC3a["Top Panel Recessed"]
    nodeC3 --> nodeC3b["Middle Panel 1"]
    nodeC3 --> nodeC3c["Middle Panel 2"]
    nodeC3 --> nodeC3d["Bottom Panel"]

    nodeD --> nodeD1["Hinges 3 nos"]
    nodeD --> nodeD2["Tower Bolts 2 nos"]
    nodeD --> nodeD3["Mortise Lock"]
    nodeD --> nodeD4["Lever Handle"]
    nodeD --> nodeD5["Floor Stopper"]

    nodeE --> nodeE1["Brick Wall with Plaster"]
    nodeE --> nodeE2["Floor Finish Level"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The root `nodeA` (Paneled Door Assembly) branches into four primary subsystems. The door frame (nodeB) holds the wall opening; the door shutter (nodeC) is the moving leaf; the ironmongery (nodeD) provides functional hardware; and the wall opening (nodeE) defines the architectural context.

## 4.2 Schematic ASCII Representation — Front Elevation Layout

The following ASCII art approximates the **front elevation of a 6-panel door** as it would appear on your drawing sheet. Although crude, it conveys the proportional layout and labeling positions expected in a KTU record.

```
            <----------- 1000 mm ------------>
            +--------------------------------+   ^
            |                                |   |
            |    FRAME  (100 mm thick)       |   |
            |  +--+------------------+----+   |   |
            |  |  |   TOP PANEL      |    |   |   |
            |  |H |------------------|    |   |   |
            |  |I |   MID PANEL 1    |    |   |   |
            |  |N +==================+ L  |   |   |
            |  |G |   MID PANEL 2    | O  |   |   |
            |  |E |------------------| C  |   |   2100 mm
            |  |  |   BOTTOM PANEL   | K  |   |   |
            |  |  |                  |    |   |   |
            |  |  |                  |    |   |   |
            |  +--+------------------+----+   |   |
            |    SILL (50 mm)                 |   |
            +--------------------------------+   v

   Legend:
   HINGE = 100 mm wide (left vertical style, 3 hinges shown as dots)
   LOCK  = 150 mm wide (right vertical style, handle indicated)
   Top Rail    = 100 mm
   Bottom Rail = 200 mm
   Frieze Rails = 100 mm each (2 nos.)
   Panels are recessed 12 mm from the face
```

## 4.3 Schematic ASCII Representation — Vertical Section Through Hinge Side

This is the **sectional elevation** as visible when the cutting plane slices vertically through the left (hinge) side of the door.

```
   +===== WALL (brick hatch) =====+        +===== WALL =====+

   +=====+========================+========+===============+
```

The following shows the **horizontal sectional plan** at mid-height, with the door open at 90° (KTU convention).
