```
   Shutter shown rotated 90° from closed position
   Brick hatch on wall portions only (not on shutter)
```

> [!TIP]
> **Drawing Tip for KTU Records:** When drawing the horizontal section:
> - The **wall in section** must always be shown with the appropriate **hatch pattern** (brick, stone, or concrete as specified).
> - The **shutter and frame** are shown in their **true cross-sectional shape**, not in elevation.
> - Add a **small arrow** in the plan indicating the **opening direction** of the door (clockwise or anti-clockwise).
> - Indicate the **door opening angle** (typically **90°**) as a dimension or arc.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*List the main components of a paneled door shutter and state the function of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A paneled door shutter consists of the following main components:

1. **Styles** — The two vertical outer members of the shutter, namely the *hinge style* (on which the hinges are fixed) and the *lock style* (which carries the lock and handle). They bear the entire weight of the shutter and transfer it to the hinges. **Function:** Load-bearing vertical members.
2. **Rails** — The horizontal members connecting the two styles. These include the *top rail*, *bottom rail*, and one or two *frieze rails* (intermediate). **Function:** Hold the styles together and define the panel divisions.
3. **Panels** — The thin wooden sheets (20–25 mm) that fill the rectangular openings between styles and rails. **Function:** Provide the infill surface; they are recessed 10–12 mm from the face and held by beading.
4. **Muntins (optional)** — Intermediate vertical or horizontal members used in 6-panel or 8-panel doors to further subdivide the panels. **Function:** Additional panel division.
5. **Beading** — Small timber strips pinned along the inner edges of styles/rails. **Function:** Hold the panels securely inside their grooves while allowing free movement for expansion/contraction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Scheme:** 0.5 mark for naming each of 5 components + 0.5 mark for one-line function = 3 marks total. Diagrams are not mandatory in Part A but earn bonus marks.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]**
*What is the difference between a paneled door and a flush door? Mention any three points.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Paneled Door | Flush Door |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Composed of a frame (styles and rails) with separate **panels** fitted into grooves. | Has a **solid or semi-solid core** covered with plywood, MDF, or particle board on both faces. |
| 2 | **Jointed construction** using mortise-and-tenon joints; traditional and repairable. | **Laminated or veneered** construction; glued and pressed; not easily repairable. |
| 3 | Heavier, more expensive, requires skilled carpentry. | Lighter, cheaper, mass-produced, suitable for modern modular construction. |
| 4 | Visible panel divisions; classical aesthetic. | Smooth, plain surface suitable for paint, laminate, or veneer finish. |
| 5 | Better for traditional Kerala homes and heritage buildings. | Better for contemporary offices, apartments, and budget housing. |

(Any three points earn 1 mark each = 3 marks.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**
*(a)* Draw to a scale of **1:20** the **front elevation** and **vertical section** of a single-leaf **four-panel door** of size **1000 mm × 2100 mm** with the following specifications: frame size 75 mm × 100 mm, shutter thickness 35 mm, panel thickness 25 mm, rebate 15 mm. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* List the **ironmongery** required for the above door with brief specifications. Also explain the **function of the rebated frame**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Layout Planning (1 mark):**
Adopt a sheet layout with the **vertical section on the left** and the **front elevation on the right**, both aligned at the floor level. The cutting plane for the vertical section passes through the **hinge side** of the door, exposing the left jamb, the hinge, and the shutter cross-section.

**Step 2 — Dimension Computation (1 mark):**
As derived in Section 3.1 of these notes:

$$
W_{clear} = 1000 - 150 = 850 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
H_{clear} = 2100 - 100 - 50 = 1950 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(assuming a sill of 50 mm)}
$$

$$
W_{shutter} = 850 - 30 = 820 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
H_{shutter} = 1950 - 30 = 1920 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Front Elevation Drawing (3 marks):**
1. Draw the outer wall opening as a 1000 × 2100 mm rectangle.
2. Draw the inner frame as a 850 × 1950 mm rectangle.
3. Draw the shutter as a 820 × 1920 mm rectangle, set 15 mm inside the frame opening on three sides (top, left, right; not at bottom if sill is there).
4. Divide vertically: 100 mm hinge style (left), 150 mm lock style (right), and two panel columns of 285 mm each in between.
5. Divide horizontally: 100 mm top rail, 100 mm upper frieze, 100 mm lower frieze, 200 mm bottom rail. The three panel rows are then 473 mm high each.
6. Draw the **panels as recessed rectangles** using **continuous thin lines** offset 12 mm from the shutter face.
7. Indicate **3 hinges** on the left style at 150 mm from top, mid-height, and 150 mm from bottom.
8. Indicate the **handle** as a small circle on the lock style at 1000 mm height.
9. Add dimensions for overall size, clear opening, shutter size, style widths, rail heights, and panel sizes.

**Step 4 — Vertical Section Drawing (2 marks):**
1. Draw the **wall in section** with brick hatching on the left side.
2. Show the **frame head** at the top (75 mm × 100 mm cross-section with 15 mm rebate on the inside).
3. Show the **frame jamb** on the left (full height of 2050 mm, with rebate).
4. Show the **shutter** in cross-section as a 35 mm thick vertical member, recessed in the rebate.
5. Inside the shutter, indicate the **style in section** (100 mm wide) and the **rail cross-section** at the top and bottom (100 mm and 200 mm respectively).
6. Show the **hinge in section** — two rectangular leaves with a circular knuckle pin between them. The hinge leaves are screwed to the frame jamb and the shutter style.
7. Show the **sill** at the bottom (50 mm thick frame member resting on the floor).
8. Show the **floor finish level (FFL)** with a hatched line.
9. Indicate the **plaster line** on the wall (typically 12–15 mm thick).
10. Add the section hatching (brick for wall, grain lines for timber) and dimension all critical heights.

**Marks Distribution for Part (a):**
- Layout and dimension computation: **1 mark**
- Front elevation correctness: **3 marks**
- Vertical section correctness: **2 marks**
- Dimensioning, hatching, and title block: **1 mark**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Ironmongery List (3.5 marks):**

| S.No | Item | Specification | Quantity |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Butt Hinges | 100 mm × 75 mm × 3 mm, mild steel, brass-pin | **3 nos.** |
| 2 | Mortise Lock | 65 mm × 100 mm body, with 6 lever mechanism, brass | **1 no.** |
| 3 | Lever Handle | 100 mm long, satin chrome finish, with rose | **1 pair** (2 nos.) |
| 4 | Tower Bolt | 150 mm × 12 mm, mild steel, zinc-plated | **2 nos.** (top and bottom on lock style) |
| 5 | Door Stopper | Floor-mounted, stainless steel, 50 mm high | **1 no.** |
| 6 | Hasp and Staple | (Optional, for back door) 100 mm, mild steel | **1 no.** |
| 7 | Door Chain | (Optional) brass or SS, 200 mm | **1 no.** |

**Function of the Rebated Frame (3.5 marks):**

The **rebate** is an L-shaped groove or step cut along the inner edge of the door frame, with typical dimensions of **15 mm deep × 30 mm wide** (depth × projection). Its functions are:

1. **Shutter Seating:** The rebate provides a precise seat for the shutter when closed, ensuring that the shutter aligns flush with the frame face on the inside. This prevents the shutter from swinging through the frame opening.
2. **Draft Exclusion:** The rebate creates a partial overlap between the shutter and the frame, reducing direct air passage and light leakage. This improves insulation against dust, sound, and weather.
3. **Hinge Accommodation:** The rebate depth (15 mm) allows the hinge leaves to be recessed slightly, so the hinges do not protrude beyond the frame face and obstruct the closing action.
4. **Aesthetic Finish:** The rebate creates a clean shadow line (reveal) at the frame-shutter interface, giving the door a finished architectural look.
5. **Structural Stiffness:** The rebate effectively increases the cross-sectional depth of the frame in the closing direction, adding rigidity to the frame members.

(Any 3–4 of the above functions earn full 3.5 marks; a neat labeled sketch of the rebate cross-section earns 0.5 bonus mark.)

---

### Question B (Choice 2)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted for 2024 Scheme]**
*(a)* Draw to a scale of **1:20** the **plan** and **front elevation** of a single-leaf **six-panel door** of size **900 mm × 2100 mm**, with the shutter shown open at **90°** in the plan. Frame section: 75 mm × 100 mm, shutter thickness 40 mm, panel thickness 25 mm. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* Explain the **mortise-and-tenon joint** used between styles and rails. Why is a 6-panel door preferred over a 4-panel door in living rooms? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Dimension Computation (1 mark):**
Given $W_{opening} = 900$ mm, $H_{opening} = 2100$ mm, $T_{frame} = 75$ mm, $R_{rebat} = 15$ mm, $T_{shutter} = 40$ mm.

$$
W_{clear} = 900 - 150 = 750 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
H_{clear} = 2100 - 100 = 2000 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(no sill assumed)}
$$

$$
W_{shutter} = 750 - 30 = 720 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
H_{shutter} = 2000 - 30 = 1970 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Member Sizing (1 mark):**
For a 6-panel door (2 columns × 3 rows):
- Hinge style: 100 mm; Lock style: 150 mm.
- Top rail: 100 mm; Bottom rail: 200 mm; Frieze rails: 100 mm × 2.
- Panel column width: $(720 - 250)/2 = 235$ mm.
- Panel row height: $(1970 - 100 - 200 - 200)/3 = 490$ mm.

**Step 3 — Front Elevation (2.5 marks):**
Draw the outer frame (900 × 2100), inner opening (750 × 2000), shutter (720 × 1970), and subdivide as above. Draw 6 recessed panels. Add hinges and handle.

**Step 4 — Plan (2.5 marks):**
1. Draw the wall in plan as two parallel thick lines.
2. Draw the door frame (head + jambs) as a thin rectangle inside the wall opening.
3. Show the **shutter rotated 90°** from the closed position, projecting into the room.
4. Indicate the **hinge** as a small rectangle on the jamb, with a connecting line to the shutter.
5. Add the **opening arc** (90°) and a directional arrow.
6. Dimension the wall opening, the shutter width (40 mm thick in plan), and the swing radius.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Mortise-and-Tenon Joint (4 marks):**

The **mortise-and-tenon joint** is the traditional carpentry joint used to connect the styles and rails of a paneled door. It consists of:

- **Mortise:** A rectangular cavity (slot) cut into the edge of the *style*, with dimensions typically **25 mm deep × 100 mm wide × 35 mm thick** (matching the rail width).
- **Tenon:** A matching rectangular projection cut on the end of the *rail*, designed to fit snugly into the mortise.

**Procedure:**
1. The mortise is cut first using a mallet and chisel (or a mortising machine).
2. The tenon is shaped on the rail end using a tenon saw.
3. The tenon is inserted into the mortise, and the joint is locked either by:
   - A **wooden pin (dowelled)** driven through a hole bored through both members, OR
   - A **drawbore pin** (offset hole technique for tighter fit), OR
   - Modern **PVA adhesive** with clamping.

**Advantages:**
- High mechanical strength due to large glue surface area.
- Resists both tensile and compressive loads.
- Self-aligning during assembly.
- Allows timber movement without splitting.

**Why 6-Panel Doors are Preferred in Living Rooms (3 marks):**

1. **Aesthetic Grandeur:** A 6-panel door has more subdivisions, creating a more elegant and proportionate appearance that suits the formal, larger space of a living room.
2. **Better Proportions:** With smaller individual panels, the height-to-width ratio of each panel is closer to the golden ratio, giving a more harmonious look.
3. **Higher Strength:** The additional frieze rail and muntins add more load-bearing cross-sections, making the shutter more rigid and durable — suitable for a high-traffic, high-visibility area.
4. **Higher Resale Value:** A 6-panel door is perceived as a premium feature, increasing the perceived quality and resale value of the home.

(Any 3 reasons earn full 3 marks.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Rebate in the Section View (–2 marks):** The most common error is drawing the frame as a plain rectangle in section, omitting the **15 mm rebate step**. The rebate is mandatory in the vertical section.
> 2. **Wrong Hinge Count (–1 mark):** Drawing only **2 hinges** for a 2100 mm door. The KTU rule (and IS standard) mandates **3 hinges** at 150 mm from top, mid-height, and 150 mm from bottom.
> 3. **Not Showing the Door Open in Plan (–2 marks):** The plan must show the shutter at **90°** to the closed position, not in the closed position. Many students draw the plan with the door closed, which violates the section convention.
> 4. **Wrong Hatching in Wall (–1 mark):** Drawing the wall with horizontal lines instead of **45° brick hatch** or **concrete stipple**.
> 5. **Missing Dimensions (–1 mark per missing dimension):** The most-often missed dimensions are: (a) hinge spacing from top/bottom, (b) style and rail member sizes, (c) panel dimensions, (d) sill thickness.
> 6. **Not Writing the Scale and Title Block (–1 mark):** Always include "**Scale 1:20**" or "**Scale 1:10**" prominently, plus the title block with name, roll number, date, and page number.
> 7. **Confusing Styles and Rails:** Remember — **styles are vertical**, **rails are horizontal**. Mixing them up is a fatal error that costs 1–2 marks.
> 8. **Omitting the Mortise-and-Tenon Detail in the Section:** A small **detail callout** showing the joint at larger scale (e.g., 1:5) earns 1 bonus mark and impresses the examiner.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Pin This to Your Drawing Board:**

- ✅ A **paneled door** = frame (head, jambs, sill) + shutter (styles, rails, panels) + ironmongery (hinges, lock, handle).
- ✅ **Standard size:** 900 × 2100 mm or 1000 × 2100 mm; **frame section:** 75 × 100 mm; **shutter thickness:** 30–40 mm.
- ✅ **Styles** are vertical (hinge style = 100 mm, lock style = 150 mm); **rails** are horizontal (top = 100 mm, bottom = 200 mm, friezes = 100 mm).
- ✅ **Panels** are 20–25 mm thick, recessed 10–12 mm from the shutter face, and held by beading.
- ✅ **Three views** to draw: **Front Elevation**, **Vertical Section (through hinge side)**, **Horizontal Section/Plan (with shutter open at 90°)**.
- ✅ **Rebate** = 15 mm deep cut in the frame, where the shutter seats when closed.
- ✅ **Three hinges** for any standard residential door (rule: 1 hinge per 700 mm of height).
- ✅ **Mortise-and-tenon joint** is the standard joinery between styles and rails; sometimes secured with draw-bore pins or PVA glue.
- ✅ **Six-panel door** is preferred for living rooms (aesthetic + strength); **four-panel** for bedrooms; **flush doors** for modern/budget homes.
- ✅ Use **First-Angle Projection** for all orthographic views (KTU convention).
- ✅ Always include: **Title block**, **scale (1:20 or 1:10)**, **dimensioning in mm**, **brick/concrete hatching**, and **hidden lines as dashed**.
- ✅ The **golden ratio (1:1.618)** governs panel proportions for aesthetic appeal.
- ✅ **Section hatching:** wall = brick/cross-hatch; timber = grain lines; metal = solid black; glass = thin line with crosses.
- ✅ **Golden rule for the plan:** Shutter MUST be shown **open at 90°**, not closed.
- ✅ **Three typical KTU mistakes to avoid:** (1) missing rebate in section, (2) only 2 hinges, (3) shutter closed in plan.
- ✅ **Valuation mantra:** "Neat drawing + complete dimensions + correct hatching + accurate labels = full marks."

---

> **End of Module Experiment 2 Notes — Paneled Doors**
> *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme, GZESL208 Workshop Module 2.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Draw sectional details and elevation of wooden glazed window
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZESL208 - BASIC ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Draw sectional details and elevation of wooden glazed window

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **glazed window** is an opening in the wall of a building provided for the purpose of admitting light, air, and ventilation, which is fitted with glass panes (glazing) held within a frame of wood, metal, or other material. A **wooden glazed window** specifically uses seasoned timber (usually *teak*, *sal*, *deodar*, or *hardwood*) for its frame, shutters, and glazing bars.

In **KTU Building Drawing / Engineering Graphics** terminology, the drawing of a wooden glazed window involves:
- **Elevation**: The front view of the window as seen from outside or inside, showing the frame, shutters, panels, glazing bars, and hardware positions.
- **Sectional Details (Vertical Section / Horizontal Section)**: A cut-through view that exposes the internal construction, rebate for glass, moulding profiles, joinery details, and the relationship of the window with the surrounding wall.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** Students are expected to draw the elevation and the sectional details of a standard wooden glazed window (usually a **two-leaf / two-shutter casement window**) to a suitable scale (commonly **1:10** or **1:20**), label all members, and follow **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards)** drawing conventions — particularly **IS 962:1989** and **IS 1003 (Part 1)** for timber panelled and glazed doors/windows.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World Understanding)

Think of a wooden glazed window as a **"picture frame on a hinge"** built into a wall.

- The **outer wooden frame** is like the *wall of a house* that holds everything in place.
- The **shutter (sash)** is the *door* of that picture frame — it opens and closes.
- The **glass pane** is the *photo* in the frame — it lets light in but keeps the outside out.
- The **glazing bars (muntins / sash bars)** are like *dividers* that split the photo into smaller sections.
- The **sectional drawing** is like *cutting a cake vertically* — you can see all the layers (recessed rebate for glass, the putty/moulding holding it, the wood thickness, and the wall around it).

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Memory Hook:** "**E**levation = **E**xternal **F**ace" (what you see), "**S**ection = **S**liced view" (what's inside).

### 1.3 Standard Terminology Used in KTU Drawings

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Head** | Top horizontal member of the frame |
| **Jamb / Post / Stilts** | Vertical side members of the frame |
| **Sill** | Bottom horizontal member (sloped outward) |
| **Shutter / Sash / Leaf** | The movable/hinged part holding the glass |
| **Mullion / Transom** | Intermediate vertical/horizontal frame members (for large windows) |
| **Glazing Bar / Muntin** | Thin wooden strips dividing the glass panes |
| **Rebate** | A stepped recess cut into the shutter to seat the glass |
| **Bead / Moulding** | Small wooden strip fixed over glass to hold it in the rebate |
| **Horn** | Projection of the frame member beyond the shutter (for anchoring in masonry) |
| **Horns of Head / Sill** | Projecting ends of head/sill embedded into wall for stability |

### 1.4 Standard Sizes (As per IS 1003 and KTU Practice)

- **Wall opening size**: Generally from **600 mm × 900 mm** to **1800 mm × 1500 mm**.
- **Frame thickness (depth)**: Usually **75 mm** or **90 mm** (depending on wall thickness).
- **Frame width (breadth)**: Typically **75 mm × 75 mm** or **65 mm × 75 mm**.
- **Shutter thickness**: Usually **30 mm, 35 mm, or 40 mm** finished.
- **Glazing bar thickness**: **20 mm × 25 mm** typical.
- **Glass thickness**: **3 mm to 5.5 mm** for ordinary glazing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024 Scheme drawing questions, assume a **standard two-shutter casement window of size 1200 mm (width) × 1500 mm (height)** with a **75 mm × 75 mm** wooden frame and **30 mm thick** glazed shutters, drawn at **1:10 scale**, unless otherwise specified.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Wooden Glazed Windows (Classification)

Understanding the **type** of window is critical because the elevation and section both change with it.

1. **Casement Window (Side-Hung):** Shutters hung on side hinges; most common in Indian practice. Can be **single-shutter** or **double-shutter (two-leaf)**.
2. **Sash Window (Vertically Sliding):** Shutters slide vertically in the frame; common in older European-style buildings.
3. **Pivoted Window:** Shutter rotates about a central vertical or horizontal axis.
4. **Sliding Window (Horizontal):** Shutters slide horizontally past each other.
5. **Fixed / Dead Window:** No shutter; glass fixed directly into the frame.
6. **Fan Light:** A small glazed window above a door or larger window.
7. **Louvered Window:** Has horizontal angled slats (Jali-type) for ventilation.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Focus:** The most frequently asked drawing is the **double-shutter side-hung casement window** because it allows both leaves to open and is a standard problem in engineering graphics labs.

### 2.2 Component Hierarchy of a Glazed Window (Why Each Part Exists)

The window is not a random assembly — every member has a structural and functional role. Below is the **"Why and How"** logic.

#### A. Outer Frame (Chowkhat) — The "Skeleton"
- **Members:** Head (top), Sill (bottom), two Jambs/Stilts (sides).
- **Why it exists:** It anchors the entire window into the wall opening and transfers wind/water loads to the masonry.
- **How it works:** Made of thicker section (75 × 75 mm) to bear the weight of shutters; joined using **mortise & tenon joints**; embedded into masonry using **holdfasts (MS flat iron 25 mm × 6 mm × 150 mm long)**.
- **Special features:** The sill is **sloped outward at 1:20 to 1:30** to drain rainwater; the sill projects beyond the wall face by **25 mm to 50 mm** as a **drip groove** to prevent water staining the wall.

#### B. Shutters / Sashes — The "Hinged Doors of Glass"
- **Members:** Top rail, Bottom rail, two Stiles (vertical), and intermediate **Glazing bars (muntins)** dividing the glass into panels.
- **Why it exists:** Holds the glass panes; allows controlled ventilation.
- **How it works:** Hinged to the jamb on the outer side; closed via **tower bolts** and **handles**; closed against the frame's **recessed rebate** for an air-tight fit.
- **Standard cross-section:** Top & bottom rails = 75 mm to 100 mm wide × 30 mm thick; Stiles = 75 mm to 100 mm wide × 30 mm thick; Muntins = 20 mm to 25 mm × 30 mm.

#### C. Glazing (Glass + Bead + Putty) — The "Transparent Skin"
- **Sequence (from outside in):** Glass → Putty bed → Rebate of shutter → Bead (moulding) → Pin.
- **Why each layer:** 
  - *Putty* (linseed oil + chalk) seals the glass against water and air.
  - *Bead / Moulding* mechanically clamps the glass.
  - *Glazing pins* (small nails) hold the bead.
- **Modern alternative:** Silicone sealant + wooden bead.

#### D. Hardware (The "Joints and Locks")
- **Hinges:** 75 mm or 100 mm MS butt hinges, **3 nos. per shutter** (top, middle, bottom).
- **Tower bolt:** 150 mm or 200 mm long, fixed on the inner stile of one shutter.
- **Handle:** 75–100 mm brass or aluminium D-handle.
- **Stay hook:** Holds the shutter open at desired angle.
- **Hasp and staple** (optional): For external locking.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Drawing Conventions Cheat Sheet

Since the topic involves **drafting**, here is the essential conventions table.

| Element | Line Type / Convention |
|---|---|
| Visible outlines of frame, shutter, members | **Thick continuous line (0.7 mm)** |
| Glass panes | **Thin continuous line (0.25 mm)** — shown as single lines, NOT hatched |
| Putty / Bead / Moulding | **Medium continuous line (0.4 mm)** |
| Hidden parts (behind frame) | **Dashed line (0.25 mm)** |
| Centre lines, axis of hinges | **Chain/centre line (thin long-short-long)** |
| Hatching for cut timber in section | **Thin continuous lines at 45° to horizontal**, evenly spaced (~2 mm apart) |
| Sectional hatching for masonry/wall | **Thin continuous lines at 45°**, alternate thick and thin for brickwork |
| Dimensioning style | As per **IS 962:1989** — arrows, dimension lines, and values in **mm** without unit symbol |
| Title block | Bottom-right corner: Name of drawing, Scale, Student name, Roll no., Date |
| Scale for this topic | **1:10** (most common), or **1:5** for small details, **1:20** for very large windows |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Convention:** Glass is **NOT** hatched. The convention is to show glass as a **rectangle bounded by a thin continuous line**, sometimes with a **single diagonal "X" line** inside to indicate transparency (or simply left blank). Hatching is reserved for **wood (45° regular)** and **brickwork (45° staggered)**.

### 2.4 Sectioning Logic — Where to Cut?

For a wooden glazed window, KTU typically asks for the **vertical section through the window**, which is a cut taken:
- **Plane:** Vertical, passing through the window opening.
- **Cuts through:** One jamb (or centre of a shutter), the sill, the head, and a portion of the wall above and below.
- **Reveals:** Frame depth, shutter thickness, glass rebate, position of putty/bead, hinge placement, sill slope, holdfasts, and plaster finish on the wall.

> [!NOTE]
> If the question asks for a **horizontal section**, the cut is taken through the **transom level** or through the **glazing bars**, revealing the mullion, transom, and shutter joining in plan.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Architectural Drawing:** Every building plan must specify window elevations on elevation sheets; sectional details go into working/construction drawings.
- **Quantity Surveying:** The number, size, and type of windows determine **BOQ (Bill of Quantities)** for woodwork, glazing, and hardware.
- **Joinery Workshops:** The sectional drawing acts as a **manufacturing drawing** for the carpenter, showing every rebate, joint, and moulding.
- **Cost Estimation:** Proportions directly affect cost — a window with too many glazing bars requires more labour and joints.
- **Energy Efficiency:** Section reveals air-tightness quality; modern windows add **weather strips** in the rebate.

## 3. Step-by-Step Drawing Procedure & Analytical Matrix

This section provides the **exhaustive, step-by-step procedure** to draw the sectional details and elevation of a standard wooden glazed window — the kind that fetches full marks in KTU 2024 lab exams.

### 3.1 Given Data (Standard KTU Problem)

Assume a standard question:

> *"Draw to a scale of 1:10, the elevation and a vertical section through a wooden glazed window of size 1200 mm × 1500 mm (clear opening) with two side-hung shutters. The wall is 300 mm thick. Label all the parts."*

| Parameter | Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Clear wall opening (W × H) | 1200 mm × 1500 mm | Net opening in masonry |
| Wall thickness | 300 mm | Brickwork (one brick thick) |
| Frame section (depth × width) | 75 mm × 75 mm | All sides |
| Shutter thickness | 30 mm | Finished thickness |
| Top rail / Bottom rail width | 100 mm | Visible width in elevation |
| Stiles (vertical) width | 100 mm | Visible width in elevation |
| Glazing bar section | 20 mm × 30 mm | Muntin |
| Glass thickness | 3 mm | Single glazing |
| Sill projection beyond wall | 50 mm | With drip groove |
| Sill slope | 1 in 20 | Towards outside |
| Hinges | 100 mm butt, 3 per shutter | Top, middle, bottom |

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Drawing Procedure

#### **STEP 1 — Draw the Wall Opening (Vertical Section Drawing First)**

- Draw the wall as a rectangle of width = **300 mm** and add a clear opening of **1200 mm (W) × 1500 mm (H)**.
- Hatch the masonry using 45° lines; **leave the opening area unhatched** in the elevation, but in section, hatch the cut portions of brickwork.

#### **STEP 2 — Draw the Outer Frame Inside the Opening**

- The frame sits in the opening. 
- Draw the **head** at the top — 75 mm deep × 75 mm wide.
- Draw the **jambs** (two sides) — 75 mm × 75 mm.
- Draw the **sill** at the bottom — 75 mm deep × 75 mm wide, **sloped 1 in 20** towards the outside, projecting **50 mm** beyond the wall.

#### **STEP 3 — Show the Shutter in Section**

- The shutter is shown open at **90°** (or partly open) for clarity, OR shown **closed** against the rebate. For KTU, **closed position in section** is preferred because it shows the rebated contact.
- The shutter is 30 mm thick. It is **recessed (rebated)** by 10–12 mm into the frame on the inside face of the stile to make it air-tight.
- The shutter carries the glass in a **rebate of ~10 mm depth × 8 mm width** on the outer face, with glass resting on a **putty bed** and held by a **wooden bead (moulding)** nailed on top.

#### **STEP 4 — Show the Glass, Putty, and Bead in Section**

- Glass appears as a **single thick line** (~3 mm, drawn slightly thicker) inside the rebate.
- Below the glass: a thin line for **putty bed**.
- Outside the glass: a small triangular or rectangular section of the **wooden bead** holding it.

#### **STEP 5 — Show Hardware in Section**

- Hinges: drawn as a small rectangle on the inner face of the jamb, with knuckles visible (use 3 hinges: 150 mm from top, 150 mm from bottom, one in middle).
- Tower bolt: drawn as a small projection on the inner stile.
- Holdfast: an MS flat iron strip (25 × 6 mm) extending from the back of the frame into the masonry joint — shown as a small horizontal rectangle in the masonry.

#### **STEP 6 — Add Wall Finish Details**

- **Plaster** on the inner wall face: shown as a thin layer of 12–15 mm, hatched differently from brickwork (sometimes shown as a wavy/hatched fine layer).
- **Architrave / Architrave moulding**: A decorative timber strip (50 mm wide × 15 mm thick) on the inner wall face around the window — to cover the joint between frame and plaster.
- **Sill nosing**: The front edge of the sill is often moulded (round or chamfered).

#### **STEP 7 — Draw the Elevation (Front View)**

The elevation is drawn **above or beside** the section. Procedure:

- Draw a rectangle of size = (Opening + Frame widths) = **(1200 + 75 + 75) mm × (1500 + 75 + 75) mm = 1350 mm × 1650 mm**.
- Inside this, draw the **shutters** as two equal leaves, each of width = (1200 – rebate gap of 3 mm) / 2 ≈ **598.5 mm** ≈ **600 mm** in drawing.
- In each shutter, divide vertically into **panels of glass** using glazing bars. Common pattern for a 1500 mm tall shutter:
  - Top rail: 100 mm
  - Two glass panels (upper and lower) each ~600 mm
  - Horizontal glazing bar between them: 20–25 mm
  - Bottom rail: 100 mm
  - Stiles (left and right): 100 mm each
- Show **hinges** as small rectangles on one side (the side where it opens).
- Show **tower bolt** and **handle** in the centre meeting stiles.

#### **STEP 8 — Dimensioning and Labelling**

- Mark the overall size of the window.
- Mark the opening size.
- Mark the sill projection and slope.
- Use **leader lines** to label: 1. Head, 2. Sill, 3. Jamb, 4. Top rail, 5. Bottom rail, 6. Stiles, 7. Glazing bar / Muntin, 8. Glass pane, 9. Putty, 10. Moulding / Bead, 11. Hinge, 12. Tower bolt, 13. Handle, 14. Holdfast, 15. Plaster, 16. Wall, 17. Architrave.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Quick Reference Table for Drawing

| Step No. | What to Draw | Line Type | Scale Reference (1:10) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Wall outline + opening | Thick continuous | 30 mm = 300 mm wall |
| 2 | Frame (head, jambs, sill) | Thick continuous | 7.5 mm = 75 mm |
| 3 | Shutter in section | Thick continuous | 3 mm = 30 mm shutter |
| 4 | Glass in section | Thin continuous, slightly thicker | <1 mm |
| 5 | Putty & Bead | Medium continuous | <1 mm |
| 6 | Hinges, bolts, holdfasts | Thick + Centre line | — |
| 7 | Brickwork hatching | 45° thin lines (staggered) | — |
| 8 | Plaster | Wavy fine lines or thin 45° | — |
| 9 | Timber section hatching | Regular 45° thin lines | — |
| 10 | Dimensioning + Labeling | Thin + arrows + leader lines | — |

### 3.4 Sample Labelling List (Examiner-Friendly Wording)

Below is the **canonical labelling set** in the order KTU examiners expect:

1. **Wall** (300 mm thick brickwork)
2. **Plaster** (12–15 mm thick)
3. **Architrave** (optional)
4. **Head** (top frame member)
5. **Jamb / Stilts** (side frame members)
6. **Sill** (sloped, projected)
7. **Horns** (of head, sill — projections into wall)
8. **Holdfast** (MS flat iron)
9. **Hinge** (MS butt hinge)
10. **Shutter / Sash**
11. **Top rail** of shutter
12. **Bottom rail** of shutter
13. **Stile** of shutter
14. **Glazing bar / Muntin**
15. **Glass pane**
16. **Rebate** (for glass)
17. **Putty** (bed of putty)
18. **Bead / Moulding**
19. **Tower bolt**
20. **Handle**

> [!NOTE]
> **Proportional Rule (Golden Ratio for Glazing):** For aesthetic elevation, the height of glass panels should be roughly **1.5 to 2 times** the width. Thus, for a 600 mm wide shutter, glass panel height of **900–1200 mm** is ideal.

### 3.5 Numerical Verification of a Typical Layout (For Practice)

For a **1350 mm wide × 1650 mm tall** outer frame (clear opening 1200 × 1500), the layout arithmetic:

$$
\text{Stile width (left)} = 100 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
\text{Stile width (right)} = 100 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
\text{Centre gap between two shutters} = 3 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
\text{Width of each shutter} = \frac{1200 - 3}{2} = 598.5 \text{ mm} \approx 600 \text{ mm}
$$

For the shutter height breakdown (1500 mm clear shutter height):

$$
\text{Top rail} = 100 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
\text{Bottom rail} = 100 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
\text{Glazing bar} = 25 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
\text{Glass panel} = 1500 - 100 - 100 - 25 = 1275 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
\text{Split into two panels} = \frac{1275}{2} = 637.5 \text{ mm} \approx 640 \text{ mm each}
$$

> This arithmetic is **typical KTU 2024 problem-solving** — students may be asked to compute the **glass panel size** given frame and shutter dimensions, which is a 3-mark direct question.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

Because the physical drawing of a window cannot be replicated in a Mermaid diagram, I provide a **functional block-architecture flow** of the window assembly. This serves as the **labelling reference** and **drawing construction sequence** that students can reproduce on paper.

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Wooden Glazed Window

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    W[WALL OPENING - 1200 MM X 1500 MM] --> F[OUTER FRAME - CHOWKHAT]
    F --> F1[HEAD - 75 X 75 MM]
    F --> F2[JAMB LEFT - 75 X 75 MM]
    F --> F3[JAMB RIGHT - 75 X 75 MM]
    F --> F4[SILL - SLOPED 1 IN 20]

    F1 --> HF1[HORN PROJECTION INTO WALL]
    F4 --> HF2[HORN PROJECTION INTO WALL]

    F2 --> HD1[HOLDFAST MS FLAT 25X6X150]
    F3 --> HD2[HOLDFAST MS FLAT 25X6X150]

    S[SHUTTER ASSEMBLY - 2 LEAVES] --> ST[STILE - 100 MM]
    S --> TR[TOP RAIL - 100 MM]
    S --> BR[BOTTOM RAIL - 100 MM]
    S --> GB[GLASS BAR / MUNTIN - 20 X 30 MM]

    S --> G1[UPPER GLASS PANE - 3 MM]
    S --> G2[LOWER GLASS PANE - 3 MM]

    G1 --> PT[PUTTY BED]
    G1 --> BD[BEAD / MOULDING - 15 X 10 MM]
    G2 --> PT
    G2 --> BD

    HW[HARDWARE] --> H1[BUTT HINGE - 100 MM - 3 NOS]
    HW --> H2[TOWER BOLT - 200 MM]
    HW --> H3[HANDLE - D TYPE 100 MM]
    HW --> H4[STAY HOOK - OPTIONAL]

    F4 --> DG[DRIP GROOVE - 6 MM]
    W --> PL[PLASTER - 12 MM]
    F --> AR[ARCHITRAVE - 50 X 15 MM]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[STEP 1 - DRAW WALL WITH OPENING] --> B[STEP 2 - DRAW FRAME IN SECTION]
    B --> C[STEP 3 - DRAW SHUTTER IN SECTION]

```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**Define a glazed window. List any four main parts of a wooden glazed window.**

**Model Answer:**
A glazed window is an opening in a wall of a building fitted with glass panes held in a wooden or metal frame, used to admit light, air, and provide outside view.

**Four main parts:**
1. **Frame (Chowkhat)** — Consisting of head, jambs, and sill.
2. **Shutter (Sash)** — The hinged movable part holding the glass.
3. **Glazing bars (Muntins)** — Wooden strips dividing the glass into panels.
4. **Glazing (Glass + Putty + Bead)** — The transparent pane sealed with putty and held by a bead moulding.
5. **Hardware** — Hinges, tower bolt, handle, holdfasts.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
**What is the purpose of (a) a drip groove and (b) a sloped sill in a wooden window?**

**Model Answer:**
(a) **Drip Groove:** It is a small recess (about 6 mm × 6 mm) cut on the underside of the projecting sill, which causes rainwater to **drop off** before it can travel back along the underside of the sill to the wall. This **prevents staining of the wall** and protects plaster from dampness.

(b) **Sloped Sill:** The sill of a window is sloped outward at a slope of **1 in 20 to 1 in 30**. This ensures **rainwater drains away from the wall** rather than pooling and seeping into the frame–wall joint, improving the **water-tightness and durability** of the window.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** Draw to a scale of **1:10** the **elevation** of a wooden glazed window of clear opening **1200 mm × 1500 mm** having **two side-hung shutters**. The frame is **75 mm × 75 mm** and the shutter is **30 mm** thick. Show the position of hinges, tower bolt, and handle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Draw to a scale of **1:10** the **vertical section** through the same window, passing through a jamb and a shutter. The wall is **300 mm** thick. Show the sill slope (1 in 20), drip groove, holdfast, and putty/bead detail. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Elevation (7 marks):**

1. **Overall outer frame size** = 1200 + 75 + 75 = **1350 mm (W) × 1500 + 75 + 75 = 1650 mm (H)**. Draw a rectangle of **135 × 165 mm** at 1:10 scale.
2. Divide width into: Left stile 100 + Left shutter (1200 − 3)/2 ≈ 600 + Centre meeting stiles 100 + 100 + Right shutter 600 + Right stile 100 = **1600 mm**, but adjusted to fit inside the **1350 mm** frame.
3. Practical KTU division: 
   - Inside the outer frame, the **two shutters** of width ≈ 600 mm each are drawn.
   - Each shutter has stiles (100 mm), top rail (100 mm), bottom rail (100 mm), and a horizontal glazing bar (25 mm) splitting the glass into two equal panels of ~640 mm height each.
4. **Hardware placement:**
   - **Hinges** on the left edge of the left shutter (3 nos. — top, middle, bottom).
   - **Tower bolt** on the inner stile of the right shutter (vertical, 200 mm).
   - **Handle** on the inner stile of the right shutter (centrally placed).
5. **Dimensioning and labelling** as per IS 962:1989.

**(b) Vertical Section (7 marks):**

1. Draw the wall as a rectangle of width = **30 mm (at 1:10 scale) = 300 mm** with an opening of **120 × 150 mm**.
2. Inside the opening, draw the **outer frame** of section **7.5 × 7.5 mm**.
3. The **sill** is shown sloping outward at 1:20, projecting **5 mm** beyond the wall face.
4. The **drip groove** is a small notch (1 × 1 mm) on the underside of the sill.
5. The **shutter** is shown closed against the rebate of the frame, **3 mm thick** in drawing, with the glass rebate (~1 mm) on the outer face.
6. The **glass** is shown as a single slightly-thick line; the **putty** as a thin line beneath it; the **bead/moulding** as a small triangular/rectangular section.
7. **Hinges** are shown on the inner face of the jamb, **3 in number** with knuckles drawn.
8. **Holdfasts** are shown as small MS strips (2.5 mm wide in drawing) extending from the back of the frame into the masonry joint.
9. **Plaster** is shown as a 1.2–1.5 mm thin layer on the inner wall face, hatched finely.
10. **Brickwork** in section is hatched at 45° with **staggered** thick-thin pattern; **timber** (frame, shutter, bead) is hatched at 45° with **regular parallel** lines.
11. **Dimensions and labels** marked as per the 20-point list given in Section 3.4.

**Mark Distribution (KTU 2024):**
- Elevation drawing accuracy: 3 marks
- Section drawing accuracy: 3 marks
- Dimensioning and scale: 1 mark
- Labelling (minimum 12 parts): 2 marks
- Line conventions and hatching: 1 mark
- Title block and neatness: 1 mark

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the **difference between a panel door and a glazed window shutter**, giving the sectional details of a **glazing bar holding a glass pane with putty and bead**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wooden glazed window has a clear opening of **1500 mm × 1200 mm** (W × H). The frame is **75 mm × 75 mm**, shutter thickness is **30 mm**, top and bottom rails are **100 mm** wide, stiles are **100 mm** wide, and the central horizontal glazing bar is **25 mm** wide. Calculate the **size of each glass pane** in the two-shutter arrangement. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Difference between Panel Door and Glazed Window Shutter (7 marks):**

| Feature | Panel Door Shutter | Glazed Window Shutter |
|---|---|---|
| Filling material | Timber panels (recessed or raised) | Glass panes |
| Weight | Heavier | Lighter |
| Light transmission | No light passes | Light passes through glass |
| Glazing bars | Not present | Present (vertical & horizontal) |
| Rebate | For panel seating | For glass seating |
| Common thickness | 30–40 mm | 25–35 mm |
| Hardware | Hinges, lock, handle, tower bolt | Hinges, tower bolt, handle, stay |

**Sketch of glazing bar detail:**
- Draw the glazing bar (20 × 30 mm) in section.
- On one side, show the glass rebate of 10 mm depth.
- Place the 3 mm glass inside, with putty below.
- Fix a bead (15 × 10 mm) on the outside of the glass to clamp it.
- Show a glazing pin in the bead.

**(b) Calculation (7 marks):**

Given:
- Clear opening: W = 1500 mm, H = 1200 mm.
- Frame: 75 × 75 mm.
- Shutter thickness: 30 mm.
- Top rail / Bottom rail width: 100 mm.
- Stiles width: 100 mm.
- Glazing bar (horizontal, central): 25 mm.

**Step 1 — Overall outer frame size:**
$$
W_{frame} = 1500 + 75 + 75 = 1650 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
H_{frame} = 1200 + 75 + 75 = 1350 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Width of each shutter:**
$$
\text{Inside frame width} = 1500 \text{ mm (clear opening)}
$$
$$
\text{After deducting meeting stile overlap (3 mm)} = 1500 - 3 = 1497 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
\text{Width of each shutter} = \frac{1497}{2} = 748.5 \approx 749 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Inside-shutter available width for glass:**
$$
\text{Available width inside stiles} = 749 - (100 + 100) = 549 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
\text{After deducting rebate and bead allowance (3 mm + 3 mm)} = 549 - 6 = 543 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
\boxed{\text{Glass width per pane} \approx 543 \text{ mm} \approx 540 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 4 — Height calculation:**
$$
\text{Clear shutter height} = 1200 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
\text{Top rail} = 100 \text{ mm}, \quad \text{Bottom rail} = 100 \text{ mm}, \quad \text{Glazing bar} = 25 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
\text{Total solid wood in height} = 100 + 100 + 25 = 225 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
\text{Total glass height} = 1200 - 225 = 975 \text{ mm}
$$
$$
\text{Split into two equal panels (upper and lower)} = \frac{975}{2} = 487.5 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5 — Final glass pane size:**
$$
\boxed{\text{Each glass pane size} = 543 \text{ mm (W)} \times 487.5 \text{ mm (H)} \approx 540 \times 490 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Conclusion:** Two glass panes per shutter, each of approximately **540 mm × 490 mm**, with the upper and lower panes separated by a horizontal glazing bar of 25 mm width.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT hatch the glass.** Glass must be shown as a single line (or left blank with a thin border). Hatching glass = −1 mark.
> 2. **Do NOT forget the sill slope and drip groove.** Many students draw a flat sill; −1 to −2 marks deducted.
> 3. **Do NOT confuse rebated and non-rebated shutters.** The rebate is the recessed step where the glass sits — missing it loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Do NOT omit the holdfast.** It is a critical part for stability.
> 5. **Do NOT draw glass as a thick double line.** Use a single thin line slightly thicker than construction lines.
> 6. **Do NOT use a single hatch pattern for both brick and wood.** Use **regular 45° lines for timber** and **staggered thick-thin 45° lines for brickwork**.
> 7. **Do NOT miss dimensioning** the overall size, opening size, and at least 5 individual members.
> 8. **Do NOT forget the title block** in the bottom-right corner.
> 9. **Do NOT use freehand drawing.** Use **instruments (T-square, set-square, scale, compass)** — this is a workshop/graphics exam.
> 10. **Do NOT mix up the side of the hinge.** Hinges go on the **outer stile** (the one fixed to the jamb), tower bolt on the **meeting stile** (the one that meets the other shutter).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** A wooden glazed window = timber frame + hinged shutter + glass pane held by putty and bead.
- **KTU 2024 Drawing Standard:** Scale **1:10**, two-leaf casement, opening **1200 × 1500 mm**, frame **75 × 75 mm**, shutter **30 mm**, glass **3 mm**.
- **Outer Frame = Chowkhat:** Head + 2 Jambs + Sill (sloped 1:20, projected 50 mm, drip groove 6 × 6 mm).
- **Shutters (2 leaves):** Stiles (100 mm) + Top rail (100 mm) + Bottom rail (100 mm) + Glazing bars (20–25 mm).
- **Glazing sequence (outside → inside):** Glass → Putty → Rebate (10 mm) → Bead/Moulding (15 × 10 mm) → Pin.
- **Hardware:** 3 butt hinges per shutter (100 mm, MS), 1 tower bolt (200 mm), 1 handle (D-type, 100 mm), holdfasts (25 × 6 × 150 mm MS flat).
- **Glass pane formula:** Glass width = Shutter width − 2 × (stile width) − bead allowance; Glass height = (Shutter height − top rail − bottom rail − glazing bar) / 2.
- **Hatching rule:** 
   - *Timber* = 45° regular parallel thin lines.
   - *Brickwork* = 45° staggered thick-thin lines.
   - *Plaster* = wavy fine line or fine 45°.
   - *Glass* = NO hatching; single thin line.
- **IS Codes to remember:** **IS 962:1989** (drawing conventions), **IS 1003 (Part 1): 2003** (timber panelled and glazed shutters), **IS 4021:1995** (timber door/window shutters — quality).
- **Drawing sequence:** Section first (for clarity) → Elevation above → Dimensions and labels → Hatching → Title block.
- **Common mistakes:** Forgetting sill slope, missing drip groove, hatching the glass, omitting holdfast, drawing hinges on wrong side.
- **Examiner's hot keywords:** "Vertical section," "scale 1:10," "double-shutter," "rebated shutter," "putty & bead," "drip groove."

> **Final Note:** The wooden glazed window is one of the **highest-scoring topics** in KTU 2024 building drawing questions because it tests both **drafting skill** and **knowledge of joinery**. Practice drawing it at least 3 times before the exam, focusing on **proportions, hatching, and labelling**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Draw elevation, section and detailing of connection between members for steel roof truss
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZESL208 - BASIC ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Draw elevation, section and detailing of connection between members for steel roof truss

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Steel Roof Truss:** A steel roof truss is a triangulated framework of structural steel members (angles, channels, T-sections, or hollow sections) arranged in a single plane, designed to carry the roof covering (sheeting/purlins) over a span by transferring loads as axial forces (tension or compression) in its members, ultimately to the supporting walls or columns.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme Workshop Module Experiment 2, the student is expected to **draw three orthographic projections** of a typical steel roof truss:

1. **Elevation View** — the front projection of the truss showing the outline, member arrangement, geometric proportions, span ($L$), rise ($R$), and pitch.
2. **Sectional View** — a vertical section through the truss revealing the construction depth, purlin positions, and rafter slope.
3. **Connection Detail** — an enlarged detail drawing of the **heel joint**, **apex (ridge) joint**, or any intermediate gusseted node, showing the **gusset plate, rivets/bolts, member sections, and clearances**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Relevant IS Codes for Reference:**
> - **IS 800:2007** — General Construction in Steel (Limit State Design)
> - **IS 875 (Part 1 to 3)** — Code of Practice for Design Loads (Dead, Live, Wind)
> - **IS 808:1989** — Dimensions for Hot Rolled Steel Sections
> - **SP 38 (S&T)** — Hand Book of Typified Designs for Structures

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a simple **wooden ladder laid flat on the ground** — it is just a long beam that bends when you stand on it. Now, prop the same ladder against a wall to form a **triangle** (with the ground as the base). The ladder (acting as a rafter) no longer bends much because the triangle's geometry converts the vertical load into **pure compression along the rafter** and **pure tension along the tie** (the imaginary base).

A **steel roof truss is exactly this triangulated system scaled up**:
- The **rafter (top chord)** is in **compression** (like a leaning ladder).
- The **tie (bottom chord)** is in **tension** (like the bottom string holding the ladder base).
- The **internal diagonals and verticals** distribute shear and reduce the buckling length of the compression member.

> [!TIP]
> **Why triangles?** A triangle is the only polygon that is **rigid by shape alone** — it cannot deform without changing the length of its sides. This is why every roof truss is a network of triangles.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| **Span ($L$)** | **6 m – 30 m** | Typical workshop range |
| **Rise ($R$)** | **$L/4$ to $L/6$** | Slope of rafter |
| **Pitch** | **1/4 to 1/6** | Rise/Span ratio |
| **Spacing of trusses** | **3 m – 4.5 m** | Centre-to-centre |
| **Purlin spacing** | **1.2 m – 1.8 m** | Along the slope |
| **Standard gusset thickness** | **8 mm, 10 mm, 12 mm, 16 mm** | Mild steel plate |
| **Standard rivet diameter** | **16 mm, 18 mm, 20 mm, 22 mm** | IS 800 |
| **Standard bolt diameter** | **M16, M18, M20, M24** | Property Class 4.6 / 8.8 |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Steel Roof Truss — Member-by-Member Breakdown

> [!NOTE]
> **A truss has 5 functional zones. Master this and you can draw ANY truss.**

1. **Top Chord (Rafter / Upper Chord)** — The inclined top member running from the heel to the apex. It is in **pure compression** and is usually a **double-angle** section (back-to-back) connected to a gusset plate, or a single channel/tubular section.

2. **Bottom Chord (Main Tie / Lower Chord)** — The horizontal member at the bottom. It is in **pure tension** and resists the outward thrust generated by the rafter. Sections: **double angle, T-section, or channel**.

3. **Web Members** — Internal members forming the triangulation:
   - **Verticals (King Post, Queen Posts):** Carry panel-point loads from purlins to bottom chord.
   - **Diagonals (Struts, Ties):** Transfer shear between top and bottom chord.

4. **Joints / Nodes** — Points where members meet:
   - **Heel Joint** (support, where rafter meets tie) — usually the **largest** gusset.
   - **Apex / Ridge Joint** (top centre) — connects the two rafters.
   - **Intermediate Panel Joints** — where diagonals/verticals connect to chords.

5. **Gusset Plate** — A flat steel plate (usually **8–16 mm** thick) bolted or riveted to all members meeting at a joint. It is the **splice element** that physically connects members and transfers forces.

### 2.2 Classification of Trusses (By Geometry)

| Truss Type | Span Range | Special Feature | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **King Post** | 5 – 8 m | One central vertical | Small houses, sheds |
| **Queen Post** | 8 – 12 m | Two verticals | Medium residences |
| **Howe Truss** | 6 – 30 m | Diagonals slope **towards centre** | Heavy load, railway sheds |
| **Pratt Truss** | 6 – 30 m | Diagonals slope **away from centre** | Industrial buildings |
| **Fink Truss** | 6 – 12 m | "W"-pattern in the web | Light roofs, most common |
| **Warren Truss** | 8 – 30 m | Equilateral triangles, no verticals | Modern industrial |
| **North Light Truss** | 10 – 30 m | One steep, one flat rafter (asymmetric) | Textile mills, saw-tooth roofs |
| **Fan Truss** | 10 – 15 m | Radiating struts from apex | Where heavy load near supports |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Span | $L$ | m | Given in problem |
| 2 | Rise | $R = L/k$ | m | $k = 4 \text{ to } 6$ |
| 3 | Pitch | $p = R/L$ | ratio | $1/4$ to $1/6$ |
| 4 | Rafter length | $L_r = \sqrt{(L/2)^2 + R^2}$ | m | Half-span as base |
| 5 | Rafter slope angle | $\theta = \tan^{-1}(2R/L)$ | degrees | With horizontal |
| 6 | Panel length (horiz.) | $p_{panel} = L / (2N)$ | m | $N$ = no. of panels per side |
| 7 | Dead load on truss (DL) | $DL = \text{Area} \times \text{unit weight}$ | kN/m² | Sheeting + purlin + truss |
| 8 | Live load (LL) | $0.75 \text{ kN/m}^2$ (no access) | kN/m² | IS 875 Pt.2 |
| 9 | Wind load (WL) | $WL = C_p \times q_z \times A$ | kN/m² | IS 875 Pt.3 |
| 10 | Load per panel point | $P = (\text{spacing}) \times (\text{spacing}) \times (\text{load})$ | kN | At each top-chord node |
| 11 | Edge distance for rivet | $e = 1.5 d + 2$ (min) | mm | IS 800 |
| 12 | Pitch of rivets | $p_r = 2.5 d$ to $3 d$ | mm | Along force direction |
| 13 | Gauge of rivets | $g = 2.5 d$ | mm | Across member axis |
| 14 | Gusset plate thickness | $t_g = 1.1 t_{max}$ to $1.2 t_{max}$ | mm | $t_{max}$ = thickest member |
| 15 | Rivet shear strength | $V_{dsb} = (\pi/4) d^2 \cdot \tau_{vf}$ | N | IS 800 Table |
| 16 | Rivet bearing strength | $V_{dpb} = d \cdot t \cdot \sigma_{pf}$ | N | On weaker plate |
| 17 | Rivet value (single) | $R_v = \min(V_{dsb}, V_{dpb})$ | N | Capacity of one rivet |
| 18 | No. of rivets required | $n = \text{Force} / R_v$ | integer | Round up |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always use `\vert` for absolute value or "such that" in any table cell** — never use the unescaped `|` pipe inside a markdown table or it will break formatting.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Shed Roofing** — Trusses carry AC sheets, GI sheets, or insulated panels over large column-free areas.
- **Auditoriums & Warehouses** — Allow 20–30 m clear spans without intermediate columns.
- **Footbridges** — Same triangulated principle scaled and inverted.
- **Crane Gantries & Transmission Towers** — Same axial-force logic.
- **Modern Adaptation** — Today, welded tubular sections (CHS/RHS) are replacing riveted angle sections because welding eliminates gusset plates and reduces self-weight by 15–25 %.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Geometric Construction of a Fink Truss (Step-by-Step Derivation)

> **Problem Statement (Typical KTU Workshop Problem):**
> *"Draw to scale: (i) Elevation, (ii) Sectional elevation, and (iii) Connection detail at the heel joint of a **Fink steel roof truss** for a workshop building with the following data:*
> *Span $L = 12$ m, Rise $R = L/4 = 3$ m, Spacing of trusses = 4 m, Purlin spacing along slope = 1.5 m."*

#### Step 1: Determine the Rafter Slope Angle

The rafter rises from heel (support) to apex (ridge). Half-span is the horizontal projection.

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{R}{L/2}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{3}{6}\right)$$

$$\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.5) = 26.565^\circ \approx 26^\text{o}34'$$

**Logic:** The rafter angle is the **arctangent of rise over half-span** because the rafter forms the hypotenuse of a right triangle whose base is the half-span and height is the rise.

#### Step 2: Compute the Rafter (Slope) Length

$$L_r = \sqrt{\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^2 + R^2} = \sqrt{6^2 + 3^2} = \sqrt{36 + 9} = \sqrt{45}$$

$$L_r = 6.708 \text{ m}$$

**Logic:** This is a direct application of the **Pythagorean theorem** to the half-span and rise. The rafter is the hypotenuse.

#### Step 3: Determine the Number of Panels per Rafter

For a **Fink truss**, the standard pattern uses **4 panels per rafter side** (so 8 triangles total, symmetric about the ridge).

$$\text{No. of panels per side} = 2N = 4 \implies N = 2$$

**Panel length (horizontal projection):**

$$p_{panel} = \frac{L/2}{2N} = \frac{6}{4} = 1.5 \text{ m}$$

**Logic:** Fink geometry subdivides the rafter so that the inner web members form a "W" pattern. The standard is **2N = 4 panels per rafter** for span up to 12 m.

#### Step 4: Compute the Purlin Positions

Number of purlin spaces along rafter $\approx$ (Rafter length / Purlin spacing):

$$n_{purlins} = \frac{L_r}{s_p} = \frac{6.708}{1.5} = 4.47 \implies 5 \text{ purlins per rafter}$$

**Adopt 5 purlins per rafter side**, located at equal spacing along the slope from heel to ridge.

**Logic:** Purlins transmit roof sheeting loads to the truss as **concentrated (panel-point) loads** at the top-chord nodes.

#### Step 5: Compute the Height of the King Post (or Equivalent Web Node)

In a Fink truss, the central vertical of the "W" is the **king post height**.

$$h_{king} = R - \frac{R}{3} = 3 - 1 = 2 \text{ m (approx.)}$$

A more precise geometric construction: the Fink web has its central vertical drop one-third of the rise from the apex. This is a **typified proportion**, not a load-driven value.

**Logic:** The Fink geometry is a typified pattern from **SP 38**. The internal nodes are located so that:
- The first interior node on the bottom chord is at $L/4 = 3$ m from the heel.
- The first interior node on the top chord is at the midpoint of the rafter.
- The "W" is formed by two inclined struts meeting at the king-post head.

#### Step 6: Connection Detail at the Heel Joint — Design Procedure

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The heel joint is the most heavily loaded gusset connection** because it transfers the full rafter compression, the full tie tension, and the support reaction.

**Step 6a: Compute the Support Reaction**

For a symmetrically loaded Fink truss with purlin loads $P$ at each top-chord node:

$$R_A = R_B = \frac{n_{purlins/total} \times P}{2}$$

Assuming 5 purlins per rafter and each purlin carries a tributary area of $4 \text{ m} \times 1.5 \text{ m} = 6 \text{ m}^2$:

Assume total factored load (DL + LL) = $1.5 \text{ kN/m}^2$ (typified):

$$P = 6 \times 1.5 = 9 \text{ kN per purlin (working)}$$

Total load on truss = $5 \times 2 \times 9 = 90$ kN (excluding self-weight).

$$R_A = R_B = 45 \text{ kN (vertical)}$$

**Step 6b: Resolve Rafter Force**

The rafter force at the heel, by method of joints at $A$:

$$\sum F_y = 0 \implies R_A + F_{rafter,y} = 0 \implies F_{rafter}\sin\theta = R_A$$

$$F_{rafter} = \frac{R_A}{\sin\theta} = \frac{45}{\sin 26.565^\circ} = \frac{45}{0.4472}$$

$$F_{rafter} = 100.62 \text{ kN (compression)}$$

**Step 6c: Resolve Tie Force**

$$\sum F_x = 0 \implies F_{tie} + F_{rafter}\cos\theta = 0$$

$$F_{tie} = F_{rafter}\cos\theta = 100.62 \times 0.8944$$

$$F_{tie} = 90.0 \text{ kN (tension)}$$

**Logic:** This is the **Method of Joints** applied at the heel node. Vertical equilibrium gives the rafter force; horizontal equilibrium gives the tie force.

**Step 6d: Select Member Section**

Adopt a **double-angle back-to-back section** for the rafter:
- Top chord rafter: **2 × ISA 75 × 75 × 8 mm** (long legs back-to-back, gusset in between).
- Bottom chord tie: **2 × ISA 80 × 50 × 8 mm** (unequal angles, 80 mm leg vertical).

**Step 6e: Design the Gusset Plate**

- **Thickness:** $t_g = 1.2 \times t_{max} = 1.2 \times 8 = 9.6$ mm → adopt **$t_g = 10$ mm**.
- **Shape:** Rectangular/trapezoidal to enclose all rivet lines with a clear edge margin of **$e = 1.5 d + 2$ mm**.

**Step 6f: Design the Rivets (20 mm diameter, IS 800)**

For a **20 mm diameter rivet** in **mild steel plate**:
- Single shear strength (factory rivet): $V_{dsb} = 400 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 20^2 = 125\,664 \text{ N} \approx 125.7 \text{ kN}$
- Bearing strength on 8 mm plate: $V_{dpb} = 300 \times 20 \times 8 = 48\,000 \text{ N} = 48 \text{ kN}$

> Bearing governs. Therefore the **single rivet value** is:

$$R_v = \min(125.7, 48) = 48 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 6g: Number of Rivets on the Tie (Bottom Chord)**

$$n_{tie} = \frac{F_{tie}}{R_v} = \frac{90}{48} = 1.875 \implies \text{adopt } 2 \text{ rivets}$$

Provide **2 rivets on the tie** (in a single line along the member axis) with pitch $p_r = 2.5 d = 50$ mm.

**Step 6h: Number of Rivets on the Rafter (Top Chord)**

$$n_{rafter} = \frac{F_{rafter}}{R_v} = \frac{100.62}{48} = 2.10 \implies \text{adopt } 3 \text{ rivets}$$

Provide **3 rivets on the rafter** (in a single line) with pitch $p_r = 50$ mm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention:** Rivet values are looked up from **IS 800:2007 Table IX** for the relevant rivet/plate grade. For a workshop drawing, typified values from a design table are acceptable.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Drawing Conventions for a Workshop Submission

| Drawing Element | Convention / Rule | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Title block | Bottom-right, 180 mm × 80 mm | KTU/IS standard |
| Scale of elevation | 1:50 or 1:100 | For 12 m span |
| Scale of section | Same as elevation | Consistency |
| Scale of connection detail | 1:5 or 1:10 | To show rivets clearly |
| Line thickness — outline | **0.7 mm** | IS 9609 |
| Line thickness — hidden | **0.35 mm** (dashed) | Distinguish from solid |
| Line thickness — centre line | **0.35 mm** (long-dash-dot) | Axes of symmetry |
| Line thickness — section hatch | **0.35 mm** at 45° | Cutting plane indicator |
| Dimensioning | mm, with arrows, on extension lines | SP 46 |
| Section hatching | 45° lines, 2 mm apart, on cut steel | IS 9609 |
| Rivet/bolt in elevation | Solid circle (visible) / dashed circle (hidden) | Standard symbol |
| Rivet/bolt in section | Two parallel lines (head + shank) | Standard symbol |
| North arrow & scale bar | Bottom-left of sheet | Drawing sheet norms |

### 3.3 Verification Logic — Sanity Check

After computing forces, always verify:

$$\frac{F_{rafter}}{F_{tie}} = \frac{1}{\cos\theta}$$

For our example: $\dfrac{100.62}{90.0} = 1.118$ and $\dfrac{1}{\cos 26.565^\circ} = \dfrac{1}{0.8944} = 1.118$ ✓

**Logic:** This is a **geometric identity** for a simply supported triangular truss — it must hold true irrespective of loading, because the angle $\theta$ alone dictates the force ratio.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid is used here to render the **block-level functional architecture** of the truss and the **detailing logic flow** for the connection drawing — since the workshop drawing itself is a hand/AutoCAD deliverable.

### 4.1 Fink Truss — Member Layout Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[HEEL LEFT A] --> B[BOTTOM CHORD TIE L 12 m]
    A --> C[RAFTER LEFT]
    C --> D[APEX RIDGE TOP]
    D --> E[RAFTER RIGHT]
    E --> F[HEEL RIGHT B]
    B --> F

    C --> G[NODE TOP CHORD LEFT MID 1]
    G --> H[NODE BOTTOM CHORD LEFT MID 1]
    D --> I[KING POST HEAD]
    I --> J[KING POST VERTICAL]
    J --> B
    G --> I
    H --> J
    I --> K[NODE TOP CHORD RIGHT MID 1]
    K --> L[NODE BOTTOM CHORD RIGHT MID 1]
    K --> E
    L --> B
    K --> J

    style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#FFB347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style J fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.2 Connection Detail — Heel Joint Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[SUPPORT PLATE 200x150x10 mm] --> B[GUSSET PLATE 300x250x10 mm]
    B --> C[RAFTER DOUBLE ANGLE 2xISA 75x75x8]
    B --> D[BOTTOM TIE DOUBLE ANGLE 2xISA 80x50x8]
    B --> E[WEBS DIAGONAL ISA 65x65x6]
    C --> F[RIVETS ON RAFTER 3 nos d 20]
    D --> G[RIVETS ON TIE 2 nos d 20]
    E --> H[RIVETS ON WEB 2 nos d 16]
    A --> I[ANCHOR BOLTS 2 nos M20]
    A --> J[BEARING ON MASONRY 150 mm]

    style A fill:#F4A460,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#D2B48C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style G fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style H fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.3 Drawing-Sheet Layout Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[OUTER FRAME A1 SHEET 841x594 mm] --> Q1[TOP LEFT ELEVATION 1 to 50]
    S1 --> Q2[TOP RIGHT SECTION AA 1 to 50]
    S1 --> Q3[BOTTOM LEFT HEEL JOINT DETAIL 1 to 5]
    S1 --> Q4[BOTTOM RIGHT APEX JOINT DETAIL 1 to 5]
    S1 --> Q5[TITLE BLOCK BOTTOM RIGHT]
    S1 --> Q6[SCALE BAR AND NORTH ARROW BOTTOM LEFT]
    S1 --> Q7[NOTES PANEL TOP RIGHT]
    Q7 --> N1[Span 12 m]
    Q7 --> N2[Rise 3 m]
    Q7 --> N3[Material Fe 410]
    Q7 --> N4[Rivets 20 mm dia]
    Q7 --> N5[Gusset 10 mm thick]

    style S1 fill:#FAEBD7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Q1 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style Q2 fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style Q3 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style Q4 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style Q5 fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.4 Method-of-Joints Force Flow at the Heel (Free-Body Diagram Block)

```mermaid
graph TD
    H[HEEL NODE A] --> R[REACTION RA 45 kN UP]
    H --> F1[RAFTER FORCE 100.62 kN COMPRESSION INWARDS UP ALONG SLOPE]
    H --> F2[TIE FORCE 90 kN TENSION HORIZONTAL TOWARDS RIGHT]
    H --> F3[SUPPORT PLATE BEARING REACTION]

    style H fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:3px
    style R fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.5 Verbal Schematic of the Three Required Drawings

#### (a) Elevation View
- Draw the **outline** as a triangle with internal triangulation.
- **Dimensions to mark:** Span $L = 12\,000$ mm (overall), Rise $R = 3\,000$ mm, Panel lengths (3 numbers: $3\,000$ mm + $1\,500$ mm + $1\,500$ mm or equivalent).
- **Members to label:** Top chord, Bottom chord, Verticals, Diagonals, with member section designations.
- **Symmetry line** to be drawn as centre line (long-dash-dot).

#### (b) Sectional View (Section A–A)
- **Cutting plane** taken through the **mid-span**, vertical, cutting the rafter, king post, and tie.
- **Show** the section hatching of cut members (top chord, bottom chord, king post).
- **Show** the **purlins** as small rectangles in section, sitting on the rafter.
- **Show** the roof sheeting (AC/GI) as a thin line above the purlins.
- **Mark** the section plane and viewing arrows.

#### (c) Connection Detail — Heel Joint (1:5 or 1:10)
- Show **gusset plate** with all 4 members meeting it.
- Show **rivet/bolt positions** as small circles in plan and as two-line symbols in section.
- Mark **pitch** ($p_r = 50$ mm) and **edge distance** ($e = 32$ mm for 20 mm rivet).
- Mark **gauge lines** (e.g., $g_1 = 40$ mm, $g_2 = 80$ mm).
- Show the **support plate** and **anchor bolt** at the base.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistakes that cost marks in this experiment:**
> 1. **Wrong scale chosen** — Drawing a 12 m span on an A1 sheet at 1:10 is a guaranteed zero. Use 1:50 or 1:100 for elevation; **1:5 or 1:10 ONLY for connection details**.
> 2. **Missing section plane line** — A section view without the cutting-plane line and viewing arrows gets 0 marks for that view.
> 3. **Forgetting dimensioning** — Workshop examiners **deduct 1–2 marks per drawing** for missing dimensions. Always show span, rise, panel lengths, member sizes, and rivet pitch/gauge.
> 4. **Confusing compression and tension in member labels** — A rafter should be marked **"TC – 2 ISA 75×75×8"** and a tie **"BC – 2 ISA 80×50×8"**. Wrong member size = full marks lost on that member.
> 5. **Wrong rivet symbol** — Rivets in **elevation** are **solid circles** (visible) or **dashed circles** (hidden). Rivets in **section** are **two parallel lines** (head and shank). Mixing these is a frequent error.
> 6. **No title block or scale** — A drawing without a title block and scale is considered **incomplete** in KTU valuation.
> 7. **Forgetting section hatching at 45°** — The cut portion of steel members in a sectional view **must be hatched** at 45° spacing 2–3 mm. Missing hatch = 0.5 mark deduction.
> 8. **Showing purlins in elevation** — Purlins are **NOT** shown in elevation. They are shown only in **sectional view** as small rectangles.
> 9. **Wrong truss type** — If the question asks for a **Fink truss** and you draw a **Howe truss**, the geometry is entirely wrong.
> 10. **Ignoring the gusset plate clearance** — The gusset must extend **at least 1.5 d beyond the outermost rivet line** in every direction.

### 5.2 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1]`

**List the different types of steel roof trusses commonly used for industrial buildings. State the typical span range for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Sl. No. | Type of Truss | Typical Span Range | Distinguishing Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | King Post | 5 – 8 m | Single central vertical |
| 2 | Queen Post | 8 – 12 m | Two verticals, with straining beam |
| 3 | Howe | 6 – 30 m | Diagonals slope **towards centre** (in compression) |
| 4 | Pratt | 6 – 30 m | Diagonals slope **away from centre** (in tension) |
| 5 | Fink | 6 – 12 m | "W"-shaped internal web |
| 6 | Warren | 8 – 30 m | Equilateral triangles, no verticals |
| 7 | North Light | 10 – 30 m | Asymmetric — one steep, one flat rafter |
| 8 | Fan | 10 – 15 m | Radiating struts from apex |

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q2]`

**What is a gusset plate? State its function and the standard thickness range.**

**Model Answer:**

A **gusset plate** is a flat steel plate (usually **mild steel, grade Fe 410** as per IS 226) used at the joints of a steel roof truss to **physically connect two or more members** and to **transfer the forces** between them by means of rivets, bolts, or welding.

**Functions:**
1. Acts as the **splice element** that joins rafter, tie, and web members at a node.
2. Distributes the **concentrated joint load** over a larger rivet/bolt group.
3. Provides a **rigid connection** that maintains the geometric stability of the truss.

**Standard thickness range:** **8 mm, 10 mm, 12 mm, 16 mm** — typically taken as **1.1 to 1.2 times the maximum thickness** of the connected members.

---

### 5.3 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / 14 Marks]`

**A workshop building has to be roofed using a steel Fink roof truss.**
**Given:** Span $L = 12$ m, Rise $R = L/4$, Spacing of trusses = 4 m, Purlin spacing = 1.5 m, Height of eaves = 6 m.

**Draw to a suitable scale:**
**(a)** The **elevation** of the truss showing all members, panel dimensions, and member sizes. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** A **sectional elevation** through the mid-span showing purlins, roof sheeting, and rafter. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Geometric data:**

- Span: $L = 12\,000$ mm
- Rise: $R = L/4 = 3\,000$ mm
- Half-span: $L/2 = 6\,000$ mm
- Rafter length: $L_r = \sqrt{6^2 + 3^2} = 6\,708$ mm
- Rafter slope: $\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.5) = 26^\circ\,34'$
- Panels per rafter: 4 (Fink typified pattern)
- Panel length (horizontal): $p = 1\,500$ mm

**Step 2 — Member sizes (typified design from SP 38 for 12 m span):**

| Member | Designation | Section | Force Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| Top Chord (Rafter) | TC | 2 ISA 75×75×8 mm | Compression |
| Bottom Chord (Tie) | BC | 2 ISA 80×50×8 mm | Tension |
| King Post | KP | 1 ISA 65×65×6 mm | Tension |
| Diagonals (Struts) | D1, D2 | 1 ISA 65×65×6 mm | Compression |
| Verticals | V1, V2 | 1 ISA 50×50×6 mm | Tension |

**Step 3 — Drawing the Elevation (7 marks allocation):**

| Drawing Element | Marks |
|---|---|
| Truss outline (triangle with internal Fink "W") | 2 |
| Member sizes labelled on each member | 2 |
| Dimensions: span, rise, panel lengths | 1.5 |
| Title block, scale, symmetry line | 1 |
| Centring and neatness | 0.5 |

**Step 4 — Drawing the Sectional Elevation (7 marks allocation):**

| Drawing Element | Marks |
|---|---|
| Cutting plane line AA with viewing arrows | 1 |
| Hatching (45°) on cut rafter, tie, king post | 1.5 |
| Purlins shown as small rectangles in section | 1.5 |
| Roof sheeting shown above purlins | 1 |
| Heights: eaves (6 m), ridge (6 + 3 = 9 m), dimensioned | 1 |
| Title block, scale, section label | 1 |

**Step 5 — Key dimensions to mark:**
- Span (overall) = 12 000 mm
- Rise = 3 000 mm
- Eaves height = 6 000 mm
- Ridge height = 9 000 mm
- Panel horizontal = 1 500 mm (× 4 panels per rafter side)
- Rafter length = 6 708 mm

---

#### **QUESTION B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 / 14 Marks]`

**For the Fink truss in Question A, draw the connection detail at the heel joint to a scale of 1:5. Show the gusset plate, rivets, member sections, and edge clearances. Design the rivets connecting the rafter and the tie to the gusset.**

---

##### **Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Forces at the heel (from Q-A analysis):**

- Support reaction: $R_A = 45$ kN
- Rafter force: $F_{TC} = 100.62$ kN (compression)
- Tie force: $F_{BC} = 90$ kN (tension)

**Step 2 — Rivet selection:**

- Adopt **20 mm diameter** power-driven rivets in **M.S. plates (Fe 410)**.
- **Single shear strength** (IS 800 Table IX): $V_{dsb} = 400 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times 20^2 \approx 125.7$ kN
- **Bearing strength** on 8 mm thick plate: $V_{dpb} = 300 \times 20 \times 8 = 48$ kN
- **Rivet value** (governing): $R_v = 48$ kN

**Step 3 — Number of rivets:**

- On rafter: $n_{TC} = \dfrac{100.62}{48} = 2.10 \implies$ **provide 3 rivets**
- On tie: $n_{BC} = \dfrac{90}{48} = 1.875 \implies$ **provide 2 rivets**
- On each web: $n_{web} = 1$ minimum, provide 2

**Step 4 — Gusset plate dimensions:**

- Thickness: $t_g = 1.2 \times 8 = 9.6$ mm → adopt **10 mm**
- Plan size: must enclose all rivet lines with edge clearance $e = 1.5d + 2 = 32$ mm minimum.
- Adopt gusset **300 mm (horizontal) × 250 mm (vertical) × 10 mm**.

**Step 5 — Rivet layout:**

- **Pitch** (along force): $p_r = 2.5 d = 50$ mm
- **Gauge** (across member): $g = 2.5 d = 50$ mm (single line) or 40–40 mm for double line
- **Edge distance**: $e = 32$ mm minimum

**Layout on rafter (3 rivets in a single line):**
- Line 1: 3 rivets at 50 mm pitch, edge distance 32 mm
- Total length occupied = $32 + 2 \times 50 + 32 = 164$ mm

**Layout on tie (2 rivets in a single line):**
- Line 1: 2 rivets at 50 mm pitch, edge distance 32 mm
- Total length occupied = $32 + 50 + 32 = 114$ mm

**Step 6 — Drawing elements (14 marks allocation):**

| Drawing Element | Marks |
|---|---|
| Gusset plate shown correctly with thickness 10 mm | 2 |
| Rafter double-angle section in plan, with 3 rivets | 2 |
| Tie double-angle section in plan, with 2 rivets | 2 |
| Web member connections (2 rivets each) | 1.5 |
| Edge distance (32 mm) and pitch (50 mm) dimensioned | 2 |
| Support plate, anchor bolts, and bearing on masonry | 1.5 |
| Title block, scale (1:5), hatching, member labels | 1.5 |
| Neatness, centring, line weights | 1.5 |

**Step 7 — Validation check:**

- Maximum force per rivet on rafter = $100.62 / 3 = 33.54$ kN $< 48$ kN ✓ (Safe)
- Maximum force per rivet on tie = $90 / 2 = 45$ kN $< 48$ kN ✓ (Safe)
- Pitch check: $50$ mm $> 2.5 d = 50$ mm ✓ (Minimum OK)
- Edge check: $32$ mm $> 1.5 \times 20 + 2 = 32$ mm ✓ (Minimum OK)

---

### 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-revision checklist for the KTU Viva and Workshop Record:**

- [x] A **steel roof truss** is a 2D triangulated framework carrying axial forces only.
- [x] **Top chord = rafter = compression; Bottom chord = tie = tension.**
- [x] **Web members** transfer shear between chords; diagonals + verticals together.
- [x] **Five mandatory drawings**: (1) Elevation, (2) Section, (3) Connection details.
- [x] **Span $L$**, **Rise $R$**, **Pitch $p = R/L$** — always mark these.
- [x] **Fink truss** = "W" web, span 6–12 m, most common for light roofs.
- [x] **Howe truss** = diagonals slope towards centre; **Pratt** = diagonals slope away.
- [x] **Gusset plate** thickness = **1.1 to 1.2 × max member thickness**; standard 8/10/12/16 mm.
- [x] **Rivet diameter** standard: 16, 18, 20, 22 mm; **bolt**: M16, M18, M20, M24.
- [x] **Pitch of rivets** $p_r = 2.5 d$ to $3 d$; **Edge distance** $e = 1.5 d + 2$ mm.
- [x] **Bearing strength usually governs** rivet design in 8–10 mm plates.
- [x] **Rivet value** $R_v = \min(V_{dsb}, V_{dpb})$; **no. of rivets** = Force / $R_v$.
- [x] **Drawing scale**: Elevation & Section = 1:50; Connection detail = 1:5 or 1:10.
- [x] **Line weights** (IS 9609): Outline 0.7 mm, hidden/centre 0.35 mm.
- [x] **Section hatching**: 45° lines, 2–3 mm apart, on cut steel members only.
- [x] **Purlins** in section view = small rectangles ON the rafter; **NOT** in elevation.
- [x] **Method of joints at heel** gives rafter force and tie force using $\theta$.
- [x] **Heel joint** = heaviest loaded gusset; **apex joint** = simplest.
- [x] **Anchor bolts** at heel = 2 nos, M20, embedded 450 mm in concrete pedestal.
- [x] **IS Codes**: IS 800:2007 (design), IS 875 (loads), IS 808 (sections), IS 9609 (drawing).
- [x] **Title block**, **scale bar**, **north arrow**, **section plane** are **mandatory** — missing any = marks deducted.
- [x] For **method of joints** at heel: $\sum F_y = 0 \Rightarrow F_{rafter} = R_A / \sin\theta$; $\sum F_x = 0 \Rightarrow F_{tie} = F_{rafter} \cos\theta$.
- [x] **Verification identity**: $F_{rafter}/F_{tie} = 1/\cos\theta$ — must always hold.
