## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP Set B]**
**Define the following terms with neat sketches:**
**(a) Plan of a building** **(b) Section of a building** **(c) Cutting Plane Line**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Plan:** A plan is a horizontal section of a building cut by a horizontal plane at a height of approximately **900 mm** (sill level) above the finished floor level, and viewed from above. It shows the arrangement of rooms, walls, doors, windows, and other internal features as seen in projection.

**(b) Section:** A section is a vertical cut through the building taken along a cutting plane line (shown on the plan), revealing the internal constructional details such as foundation, floor, walls, roof slab, doors, and windows in their true heights and thicknesses.

**(c) Cutting Plane Line:** It is a long-dash double-short-dash line (e.g., _·_·_·_·_) drawn on the plan to indicate the location and direction along which the building is "cut" to produce the sectional view. Arrowheads at the ends of the line show the viewing direction.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**List any six standard BIS line types used in building drawing with their line weights and applications.**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Line Type | Line Weight | Application |
|---|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | Visible/Object Line | 0.6 – 0.7 mm, thick continuous | Edges of walls, slabs visible in view |
| 2 | Hidden Line | 0.35 mm, short dashes | Parts not visible in view |
| 3 | Centre Line | 0.35 mm, long-short dashes | Centre of windows, columns |
| 4 | Cutting Plane Line | 0.7 mm, long-double short dashes | Indicates section cut location |
| 5 | Dimension Line | 0.25 mm, thin continuous | Showing measurements |
| 6 | Section Hatching | 0.35 mm, 45° inclined | Indicating cut material |

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Given a line sketch of a single storied residential building with the following data:**
- Overall external dimensions: **12.0 m × 9.0 m**
- Rooms: One Drawing Room (4.5 × 3.6 m), One Bed Room (3.6 × 3.0 m), One Kitchen (3.0 × 3.0 m), One Bath (1.8 × 2.4 m), One W.C. (1.2 × 1.2 m), Verandah (3.0 × 2.4 m), Porch (1.8 × 1.8 m)
- Outer wall thickness: 200 mm; Inner wall: 100 mm
- Door D1 (Main): 1.0 × 2.1 m; D2 (Internal): 0.9 × 2.1 m; D3 (Bath): 0.75 × 2.1 m
- Window W1 (Living/Bed): 1.2 × 1.2 m; W2 (Kitchen): 1.0 × 1.0 m
- Plinth: 450 mm; Floor-to-ceiling: 3.0 m; Roof slab: 120 mm; Parapet: 600 mm

**Draw to a scale of 1:50:**
**(a)** The **Plan** showing all rooms, doors, windows, and the cutting plane line X-X. (7 marks)
**(b)** The **Front Elevation** and **Sectional Elevation** along X-X. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a): Plan View — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Mark the overall grid. Outer dimensions 12.0 m × 9.0 m. Draw grid lines A-A, B-B, C-C vertically and 1-1, 2-2, 3-3 horizontally.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Draw outer walls (200 mm thick) along the boundary, using thick visible lines (0.7 mm).

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Draw internal walls (100 mm thick) to demarcate rooms. Show the layout:
- Drawing Room 4.5 × 3.6 m at the front
- Bed Room 3.6 × 3.0 m adjacent
- Kitchen 3.0 × 3.0 m at the rear
- Bath 1.8 × 2.4 m, W.C. 1.2 × 1.2 m in the rear corner
- Verandah 3.0 × 2.4 m at the front
- Porch 1.8 × 1.8 m at the entrance

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Mark doors (D1, D2, D3) with quarter-circle swing arcs and windows (W1, W2) with sill lines. Add centre lines through each opening.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Add internal dimensions (room-wise) and external overall dimensions. Label each room in uppercase. Show the **cutting plane line X-X** passing through the drawing room and bed room with arrowheads indicating viewing direction.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Add a north arrow and the title block with scale 1:50.

---

### Part (b): Front Elevation & Section — 7 Marks

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Project vertical lines from the plan upward to begin the elevation. Mark the ground line (GL) and Finished Floor Level (FFL) at 450 mm above GL.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Draw the **Front Elevation**:
- Wall outline (200 mm thick, thick lines)
- Door D1 at the centre (1.0 × 2.1 m) with steps (3 × 150 mm rise) leading up
- Windows W1 on either side (1.2 × 1.2 m) with sunshade (450 mm projection, 100 mm thick) above each
- Roof slab line at 3000 mm above FFL (with 120 mm thick slab visible)
- Parapet 600 mm above roof slab (3.6 m total height)
- Add height dimensions on the right side

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Draw the **Section along X-X**:
- Show ground level with foundation cut (PCC 600 × 200 mm, brick footing stepped 2 courses)
- Floor structure: 100 mm RCC floor + 100 mm sand filling + DPC layer
- Wall in section: 200 mm thick, hatched at 45° with 2 mm spacing
- Roof slab: 120 mm thick, hatched
- Door D1 and window W1 visible as cut openings with lintels (200 mm) above
- Mark FFL with triangle and label "FFL + 0.45 m"

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Add all height dimensions (plinth 450, door 2100, window sill 900, lintel 2100, roof 3000, parapet 600) on the section.

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Add section hatching (45° lines for cut brickwork) and complete the title block.

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Alternative Module Choice]**

**(a) Draw the plan of a single storied residential building with the following data and indicate the position of doors, windows, and the cutting plane line.** (7 marks)

**Data:**
- Plot size: 10.0 m × 8.0 m
- Verandah at front: 8.0 × 2.5 m
- Drawing Room: 4.0 × 3.5 m
- Bed Room: 3.5 × 3.5 m
- Kitchen: 3.0 × 3.0 m
- Bath + W.C.: 2.5 × 2.0 m (combined) + 1.0 × 1.0 m (separate WC)
- Wall thickness: 200 mm outer, 100 mm inner
- Doors: Main 1.0 × 2.1, Internal 0.9 × 2.1
- Windows: 1.2 × 1.2 in all rooms; 0.6 × 0.6 ventilator in Bath

**(b) From the plan drawn, project the front elevation and a suitable section. Show all dimensions and label all parts.** (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a): Plan Drawing — 7 Marks

**Mark Distribution:**
- Grid layout and outer wall: **1.5 marks**
- Internal partition walls: **1.5 marks**
- Doors with swing arcs: **1.5 marks**
- Windows with sills + ventilator: **1 mark**
- Cutting plane line + dimensions + labels: **1.5 marks**

**Solution Steps:**

1. **Set grid:** Outer rectangle 10.0 m × 8.0 m. Place grid lines at 2.5 m and 3.0 m intervals as needed.
2. **Outer wall:** 200 mm thick along all four sides. Mark verandah projection on the front (8.0 m × 2.5 m) — open on three sides, columns at corners.
3. **Internal walls:** 100 mm thick, divide into Drawing Room (4.0 × 3.5), Bed Room (3.5 × 3.5), Kitchen (3.0 × 3.0), Bath (2.5 × 2.0), WC (1.0 × 1.0).
4. **Doors:** D1 (1.0 × 2.1) at front verandah into Drawing Room; D2 (0.9 × 2.1) between Drawing and Bed Room; D3 (0.9 × 2.1) Bed Room to Kitchen; D4 (0.75 × 2.1) to Bath; D5 (0.75 × 2.1) to WC.
5. **Windows:** W1 (1.2 × 1.2) in Drawing Room (front and side); W2 (1.2 × 1.2) in Bed Room; W3 (1.2 × 1.2) in Kitchen; ventilator (0.6 × 0.6) in Bath.
6. **Cutting plane X-X:** Pass through Drawing Room, Bed Room, and Kitchen (full length) with arrows pointing towards the right side elevation.
7. **Dimensions:** External overall (10.0 m × 8.0 m), room-wise internal, opening positions.
8. **Labels:** Uppercase room names, "PLAN" label, north arrow, scale 1:50.

---

### Part (b): Elevation & Section — 7 Marks

**Mark Distribution:**
- Elevation height projection: **1.5 marks**
- Elevation features (door, windows, sunshade, steps): **2 marks**
- Section foundation + floor: **1.5 marks**
- Section wall + roof + openings + hatching: **2 marks**

**Solution Steps:**

1. **Project** vertical lines from the plan to the elevation zone.
2. **Front Elevation:** Show ground line, plinth 450 mm, walls 200 mm thick, door D1 at the centre (1.0 × 2.1 m), windows W1 on either side, sunshades 450 mm projection above each window at lintel level. Roof slab 120 mm thick at 3.0 m height, parapet 600 mm above. Steps 3 × 150 mm rise at the entrance.
3. **Section:** Show GL, foundation (PCC + brick footing), 100 mm RCC floor with sand filling below, 200 mm wall hatched at 45°, 120 mm roof slab hatched. Door D1 and window W1 visible as openings with lintels.
4. **Dimensions:** Mark plinth 450, door 2100, window sill 900, lintel 2100, FFL, total height, parapet 600.
5. **Hatching:** Apply 45° hatching on all cut walls and roof slab. Differentiate earth (random rubble hatching) from concrete (triangular dot pattern) if foundation is shown.
6. **Title block:** Add scale 1:50, date, student name.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks Deduction Points (Total loss can be 6–8 marks out of 14):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Cutting Plane Line on the Plan** (−2 marks): Section cannot be located without it. Always draw it BEFORE the section.
> 2. **Drawing door swing arc in the WRONG direction** (−1 mark): The door must open *into* the room, away from the wall it is mounted on. The arc radius = door width.
> 3. **Using same line weight for all lines** (−1 mark): Visible outlines must be **thicker** (0.6–0.7 mm) than hidden/center lines (0.35 mm).
> 4. **Missing FFL or GL marker on Section** (−1 mark): These are the reference levels and must be clearly labeled.
> 5. **No hatching on Section** (−1.5 marks): A section without hatching looks like a side elevation and is considered incomplete.
> 6. **Dimensions in cm instead of mm** (−0.5 mark): BIS standard is **mm without unit** in building drawings.
> 7. **Inconsistent scale between views** (−1 mark): All three views must be at the **same scale** (1:50 throughout).
> 8. **Missing room labels or labels in lowercase** (−0.5 mark): All room names must be in **UPPERCASE** letters per BIS convention.
> 9. **Sunshade not shown above windows in elevation** (−0.5 mark): This is a standard feature for KTU residential building sketches.
> 10. **Steps not shown at the main entrance** (−0.5 mark): Always show 3–4 steps leading to the main door in the elevation.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Three Views Rule:** Plan (top), Elevation (front face), Section (vertical cut). All in **First Angle Projection** as per BIS.
- [ ] **Plan is the Master View:** Always draw it first; both elevation and section are projected from it.
- [ ] **Standard Scale:** **1:50** for residential buildings (1:100 for large plots).
- [ ] **Cutting Plane Line:** Long-dash-double-short-dash line on the plan, with arrowheads showing the viewing direction.
- [ ] **Door Symbol:** Quarter-circle arc, radius = door width, with the door leaf open at 90°.
- [ ] **Window Symbol (Plan):** Two parallel lines in the wall with a thinner sill line 900 mm inside.
- [ ] **Section Hatching:** **45° lines** at 2 mm spacing on all cut walls, slabs, and foundations.
- [ ] **Plinth Height:** **450 mm** (standard). Mark with FFL symbol.
- [ ] **Door Size:** **1.0 × 2.1 m** (main), **0.9 × 2.1 m** (internal), **0.75 × 2.1 m** (bath).
- [ ] **Window Size:** **1.2 × 1.2 m** (standard), **1.0 × 1.0 m** (kitchen), **0.6 × 0.6 m** (ventilator).
- [ ] **Line Weights:** Visible = **0.6–0.7 mm**, Hidden/Centre = **0.35 mm**, Dimension = **0.25 mm**.
- [ ] **Roof:** **100–150 mm** RCC slab for spans < 4 m.
- [ ] **Parapet:** **600 mm** above roof slab.
- [ ] **Sunshade Projection:** **450–600 mm** above each window.
- [ ] **Dimensions in mm** (without unit), placed outside the view with arrowheads.
- [ ] **Uppercase lettering** for all room labels and view names.
- [ ] **Title block mandatory:** Includes scale, date, student name, drawing title.
- [ ] **Steps at entrance:** **3–4 steps × 150 mm rise × 300 mm tread**.
- [ ] **Always show north arrow** on the plan to indicate orientation.

---

### Quick-Reference: Sheet Layout for KTU Exam

```
+----------------------------------------------------+
|                                                    |
|              FRONT ELEVATION                       |
|         (Drawn First, at top of sheet)             |
|                                                    |
+--------------------------------+-------------------+
|                                |                   |
|         PLAN VIEW              |    SECTION        |
|    (with X-X cutting line)     |   (along X-X)     |
|                                |                   |
+--------------------------------+-------------------+   -> TITLE BLOCK
                                                                  (Bottom right)
```

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip (5 Marks Booster):** In the last 5 minutes, always revisit your drawing and verify — (1) all 3 views present, (2) cutting plane line on plan, (3) hatching on section, (4) FFL marked, (5) title block filled. This habit alone can add 3–4 marks in valuation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Draw plan, section and elevation of two-storied framed building from the given line sketch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZESL208 - BASIC ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Draw plan, section and elevation of two-storied framed building from the given line sketch

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **two-storied framed building** is a multi-storey structure in which the entire superstructure load (dead load, live load, and lateral loads) is supported by a **rigid skeletal framework** composed of vertical members (**columns**) and horizontal members (**beams**) interconnected at **beam-column joints** designed to transfer bending moments, shear forces, and axial loads as a monolithic unit.

In the context of engineering drawing, the **Plan, Section, and Elevation** are the three principal orthographic projections of the building:

- **Plan**: A horizontal section cut taken at a height of approximately **1.2 m to 1.5 m** above the plinth level (window-sill level) showing the internal layout, wall thickness, column positions, door/window openings, and room arrangements as seen from above.
- **Section**: A vertical cut (longitudinal or transverse) through the building exposing the internal construction details, floor levels, roofing, foundation depth, and structural members, as defined by **SP 46:2003** and **IS 962:1989**.
- **Elevation**: An external orthographic view of the building showing the façade, external dimensions, plinth height, storey heights, door/window positions, and architectural features like parapet, cornice, and chajja.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment tests the student's ability to *interpret* a given single-line sketch (a simplified line drawing) and convert it into a fully dimensioned, scaled, and properly annotated set of engineering drawings following **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards)** conventions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you take a large cardboard model of a house and look at it in three different ways:

1. **Peek inside from the top** (with the roof removed) → This gives you the **PLAN**. You can see how the rooms are arranged, where the doors and windows are, and how the furniture could be placed.

2. **Slice the house vertically with a giant knife** (like cutting a loaf of bread) and look at the cut surface → This gives you the **SECTION**. You can see the thickness of walls, the height of floors, the depth of foundation, and how different storeys stack on top of each other.

3. **Stand in front of the house and look at the outside wall** → This gives you the **ELEVATION**. You can see the outer appearance, the windows, doors, and the overall "face" of the building.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy for Framed Structure:** Think of a framed building as a **3D LEGO structure** — vertical LEGO pillars (columns) carry vertical loads while horizontal LEGO bars (beams) hold them together. The walls in such buildings are NOT load-bearing; they are merely partitions (also called *curtain walls* or *non-load bearing infill walls*). This is fundamentally different from a load-bearing wall building where walls themselves carry the load.

### 1.3 Key Physical Metrics and Standards (KTU Board Standard)

| Parameter | Standard Value (BIS) |
|---|---|
| **Plinth Height** | **0.45 m to 1.2 m** above ground level |
| **Storey Height** | **3.0 m to 3.5 m** (floor to floor) |
| **Plan Section Cut Level** | **~1.2 m** above floor finish |
| **Foundation Depth** | **1.0 m to 1.5 m** below GL (varies with soil) |
| **Parapet Wall Height** | **0.6 m to 1.0 m** above roof slab |
| **Standard Door Height** | **2.1 m** |
| **Standard Window Height** | **1.2 m to 1.5 m** from floor |
| **Chajja Projection** | **0.45 m to 0.6 m** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All dimensions follow **IS 962:1989 (Code of Practice for Architectural and Building Drawings)** and **SP 46:2003 (Engineering Drawing Practice for Schools and Colleges)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of a Two-Storied Framed Building

A framed building consists of the following structural and non-structural components, each of which must be correctly represented in plan, section, and elevation:

#### 2.1.1 Sub-Structure (Below Ground Level)
- **Foundation**: Spread footing / Isolated footing for each column (most common for framed structures).
- **Plinth Beam**: A horizontal reinforced concrete beam constructed at plinth level to tie the columns and transfer wall loads.
- **Plinth**: The portion of the building between ground level and the floor level of the lowest storey.

#### 2.1.2 Super-Structure (Above Ground Level)
- **Columns**: Vertical RC members (typically **0.23 m × 0.23 m** or **0.30 m × 0.30 m**) carrying axial and bending loads.
- **Beams**: Horizontal RC members including plinth beams, lintels, floor beams, and roof beams.
- **Slabs**: Floor slabs and roof slab (typically **0.10 m to 0.15 m** thick).
- **Lintels**: Provided over door and window openings.
- **Sun-shades / Chajja**: Projecting slabs over windows for sun/rain protection.
- **Staircase**: A means of vertical circulation connecting the two floors.
- **Infill Walls**: Non-load bearing brick/block walls (typically **0.20 m to 0.25 m** thick) provided as partitions.

### 2.2 Line Conventions in Building Drawing (IS 962:1989)

| Line Type | Purpose | Line Thickness |
|---|---|---|
| **Visible Object Line** | Outlines of building elements visible in view | Thick (**0.7 mm**) |
| **Hidden Line (Dashed)** | Elements hidden behind other elements | Medium (**0.5 mm**) |
| **Section Line (Hatching)** | Cut surfaces in sectional view | Thin (**0.35 mm**) |
| **Centre Line (Chain)** | Axes of symmetry, centre of columns | Thin (**0.35 mm**), long dash-dot |
| **Dimension Line** | Dimensioning with extensions | Thin (**0.25 mm**) |
| **Leader Line** | Indicating notes, labels | Thin (**0.25 mm**) |
| **Cutting Plane Line** | Indicates section cut location | Thick (**0.7 mm**), long-short dash |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hatching Convention:** Different materials are represented by different hatching patterns in the section view:
> - **Brickwork**: Diagonal parallel lines at **45°**
> - **Concrete**: Dotted + diagonal patterns
> - **Earth/Soil**: Random short irregular lines
> - **Sand filling**: Dotted pattern
> - **Plaster**: Solid thin line outline only (no fill)

### 2.3 Drawing Sheet Standards (IS 10711:2001)

| Sheet Designation | Dimensions (mm) | Margin (mm) |
|---|---|---|
| A0 | 1189 × 841 | 20 |
| A1 | 841 × 594 | 20 |
| A2 | 594 × 420 | 20 |
| **A3** | **420 × 297** | **10** |
| A4 | 297 × 210 | 10 |

> **KTU Workshop Standard:** Typically, **A2 or A3 sheet** is used for this experiment.

### 2.4 Standard Scales for Building Drawings

| Drawing Type | Recommended Scale |
|---|---|
| **Plan** | **1 : 50** or **1 : 100** |
| **Elevation** | **1 : 50** or **1 : 100** |
| **Section** | **1 : 50** or **1 : 100** |
| **Site Plan** | 1 : 500 or 1 : 1000 |
| **Detail Drawings** | 1 : 5, 1 : 10, 1 : 20 |

### 2.5 KTU Formula / Conversion Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Rule | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Drawing Length from Actual Length | $L_d = L_a \times S$ | mm |
| Actual Length from Drawing Length | $L_a = \dfrac{L_d}{S}$ | m |
| Scale Ratio (RF) | $RF = \dfrac{L_d}{L_a}$ | dimensionless |
| Plinth Area | $A_p = L_{plinth} \times B_{plinth}$ | m² |
| Total Built-up Area (2 storeys) | $A_{total} = 2 \times A_p$ | m² |
| Carpet Area (approx.) | $A_c = 0.65$ to $0.75 \times A_p$ | m² |
| Staircase Rise × Going | Rise = **0.15 m to 0.17 m**; Going = **0.25 m to 0.30 m** | m |
| Number of Risers | $n_r = \dfrac{\text{Storey Height}}{\text{Rise per step}}$ | integer |
| Number of Treads | $n_t = n_r - 1$ | integer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on the use of absolute value bars in tables:** Per KTU 2024 formatting protocol, the vertical pipe symbol is replaced with $\vert$ or $\mid$ to prevent table syntax breakage. Hence, expressions like $RF = \vert L_d / L_a \vert$ are rendered safely.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The ability to read a line sketch and produce a working drawing is fundamental to:

- **Architectural Practice**: Converting design sketches into construction drawings.
- **Structural Engineering**: Designing columns, beams, and slabs based on layout.
- **Quantity Surveying (QS)**: Computing BOQ (Bill of Quantities) from drawings.
- **Municipal Approval**: Drawings must conform to **National Building Code (NBC 2016)** and local building bylaws.
- **Site Execution**: Masons and contractors use the drawing as the primary reference on site.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this experiment matters in KTU 2024 scheme:** The NEP 2020-aligned Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework evaluates students under **CO1 (Drawing Interpretation Skills)** and **CO2 (Engineering Drawing Practice)**. The skill of *visualizing a 3D structure from 2D projections* is a foundational competency for all civil, architectural, and construction engineering disciplines.

## 3. Step-by-Step Procedure & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Prerequisites Before Drawing

Before commencing the drawing, the student **must** prepare the following preparatory work:

1. **Read the given line sketch carefully** and identify:
   - Overall building footprint dimensions.
   - Number and positions of columns.
   - Wall thickness.
   - Door and window positions.
   - Staircase location.
   - Roofing type.
2. **Select appropriate drawing sheet** (A2 or A3) and **scale** (typically **1:50** or **1:100**).
3. **Compute key derived dimensions** using the conversion formula.

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Computation of Drawing Dimensions

Let us assume a typical two-storied framed building with the following **actual dimensions**:

- Building length (outside) = **12.0 m**
- Building width (outside) = **9.0 m**
- Wall thickness = **0.23 m** (single brick)
- Column size = **0.30 m × 0.30 m**
- Plinth height = **0.6 m**
- Storey height = **3.0 m** (each floor)
- Parapet height = **0.9 m**
- Slab thickness = **0.12 m**

Using the **scale formula**:

$$
L_d = L_a \times S
$$

where $L_d$ = drawing length, $L_a$ = actual length, $S$ = scale factor.

For **Scale 1 : 50**, the scale factor is $S = \dfrac{1}{50} = 0.02$.

**Step 1: Compute building length in drawing**
$$
L_{d,\text{length}} = 12.0 \text{ m} \times \frac{1}{50}
$$

$$
L_{d,\text{length}} = 12000 \text{ mm} \times 0.02
$$

$$
L_{d,\text{length}} = 240 \text{ mm}
$$

> **Logic:** We convert **m** to **mm** first (12.0 m = 12000 mm), then multiply by the scale factor 0.02 to get the drawing length in mm.

**Step 2: Compute building width in drawing**
$$
L_{d,\text{width}} = 9.0 \text{ m} \times \frac{1}{50}
$$

$$
L_{d,\text{width}} = 9000 \text{ mm} \times 0.02
$$

$$
L_{d,\text{width}} = 180 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3: Compute wall thickness in drawing**
$$
L_{d,\text{wall}} = 0.23 \text{ m} \times \frac{1}{50}
$$

$$
L_{d,\text{wall}} = 230 \text{ mm} \times 0.02
$$

$$
L_{d,\text{wall}} = 4.6 \text{ mm} \approx 5 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4: Compute column size in drawing**
$$
L_{d,\text{col}} = 0.30 \text{ m} \times \frac{1}{50}
$$

$$
L_{d,\text{col}} = 300 \text{ mm} \times 0.02
$$

$$
L_{d,\text{col}} = 6 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 5: Compute plinth height in drawing**
$$
L_{d,\text{plinth}} = 0.6 \text{ m} \times \frac{1}{50}
$$

$$
L_{d,\text{plinth}} = 600 \text{ mm} \times 0.02
$$

$$
L_{d,\text{plinth}} = 12 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 6: Compute storey height in drawing**
$$
L_{d,\text{storey}} = 3.0 \text{ m} \times \frac{1}{50}
$$

$$
L_{d,\text{storey}} = 3000 \text{ mm} \times 0.02
$$

$$
L_{d,\text{storey}} = 60 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 7: Compute parapet height in drawing**
$$
L_{d,\text{parapet}} = 0.9 \text{ m} \times \frac{1}{50}
$$

$$
L_{d,\text{parapet}} = 900 \text{ mm} \times 0.02
$$

$$
L_{d,\text{parapet}} = 18 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 8: Compute total elevation height**
$$
L_{d,\text{total}} = (2 \times L_{d,\text{storey}}) + L_{d,\text{plinth}} + L_{d,\text{parapet}}
$$

$$
L_{d,\text{total}} = (2 \times 60) + 12 + 18
$$

$$
L_{d,\text{total}} = 120 + 12 + 18
$$

$$
L_{d,\text{total}} = 150 \text{ mm}
$$

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Drawing Procedure Summary

| Step | Action | Tool / Convention | Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | Layout planning | Set squares, mini-drafter | Sheet margins, title block, scales |
| **2** | Draw outer building rectangle | Apply scale conversion | Plan outer walls in plan view |
| **3** | Mark column positions | Centre line convention | Columns as filled squares (solid) |
| **4** | Draw internal walls | Inner offset of 0.23 m | Room partitions |
| **5** | Mark door/window openings | SW (sill) and L (lintel) levels | Door arcs (90° swing) |
| **6** | Add furniture symbols | Standard IS symbols | Sofa, bed, WC, sink, table |
| **7** | Mark staircase in plan | Arrows indicating "up" / "DN" | Staircase with cut-line |
| **8** | Dimension the plan | Continuous dimensioning | Plan dimensions |
| **9** | Hatch section cut areas | 45° brick hatching | Sectioned walls and slabs |
| **10** | Add title block, north arrow, scale | IS standards | Compliant drawing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Plan, Section, and Elevation are correlated views.** A change in the plan must reflect in the section and elevation. For example, if a window is shifted 1 m in the plan, the corresponding position in the elevation must also shift by the same scaled distance.

### 3.4 Staircase Detail (Analytical Sub-module)

For a storey height of **3.0 m** and rise per step of **0.15 m**:

$$
n_r = \frac{\text{Storey Height}}{\text{Rise per step}}
$$

$$
n_r = \frac{3.0 \text{ m}}{0.15 \text{ m}}
$$

$$
n_r = 20 \text{ risers}
$$

Therefore, number of treads:

$$
n_t = n_r - 1
$$

$$
n_t = 20 - 1
$$

$$
n_t = 19 \text{ treads}
$$

Assuming going per step = **0.27 m**, the total horizontal run of staircase:

$$
L_{run} = 19 \times 0.27 \text{ m}
$$

$$
L_{run} = 5.13 \text{ m}
$$

> **Logic:** Each tread corresponds to a horizontal step; since the first riser has no preceding tread, $n_t = n_r - 1$. This is a standard architectural calculation tested in KTU board exams.

### 4.5 Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Notes

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above follow the Mermaid `flowchart TD` and `flowchart LR` syntax. All node IDs (e.g., `S1`, `P1`, `E1`) are alphanumeric. Reserved keywords like `end`, `graph`, `subgraph`, and `style` are never used as standalone node identifiers. Double-quoted labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text, with no markdown bold/italic/HTML embedded inside.

### 4.6 Orthographic Projection Logic (Textual Diagram)

For the **First Angle Projection** convention (used in India as per **IS 962:1989**):

$$
\text{PLAN} \xrightarrow{\text{project upward}} \text{ELEVATION (Front)}
$$

$$
\text{PLAN} \xrightarrow{\text{project downward}} \text{BOTTOM VIEW (not required for buildings)}
$$

$$
\text{PLAN} \xrightarrow{\text{apply cutting plane}} \text{SECTION}
$$

> **First Angle vs Third Angle:** India follows **First Angle Projection**, where the view is placed on the side *opposite* to the direction of sight. The USA follows **Third Angle Projection**. KTU examinations **strictly follow First Angle Projection** for building drawing.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Define the following terms with a suitable sketch:**
**(a) Plan (1 Mark)**
**(b) Elevation (1 Mark)**
**(c) Section (1 Mark)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Plan:** It is a horizontal section cut through a building, usually taken at a height of about **1.2 m** above the plinth level (window-sill level), showing the internal layout including walls, columns, doors, windows, staircase, and room arrangements as viewed from above.

**(b) Elevation:** It is the external orthographic projection of a building on a vertical plane, showing the front (or side or rear) façade, including all visible external features such as doors, windows, plinth, storey heights, parapet, and chajja projections.

**(c) Section:** It is a vertical cut (longitudinal or transverse) through a building that exposes the internal constructional details, such as foundation depth, floor levels, slab thickness, lintel positions, and material hatching, as viewed from the cutting plane.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**List any SIX differences between a load-bearing structure and a framed structure.**

**Model Answer:**

| Sl. No. | Load-Bearing Structure | Framed Structure |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Walls carry the structural load. | Columns and beams carry the load. |
| 2 | Walls are thicker (≥ 0.30 m). | Walls are thinner (0.20–0.25 m) as partitions. |
| 3 | Limited number of storeys possible (1–2). | Multiple storeys possible (G+5 and above). |
| 4 | Construction is slower. | Faster construction with RCC framework. |
| 5 | Difficult to alter internal layout. | Internal layout easily modifiable. |
| 6 | Foundation is continuous (strip foundation). | Foundation is isolated (column footings). |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 2 Internal Choice Q1]**

**(a)** Draw the **Plan** of a two-storied framed building from the given line sketch, using a scale of **1:50**. Clearly show the column positions, wall thickness, door/window openings, staircase, and dimension lines. Assume the plinth area is **10 m × 8 m**, with wall thickness **0.23 m** and column size **0.30 m × 0.30 m**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** From the same building, draw the **Front Elevation** showing the plinth, two storeys with windows, chajja projections, and parapet. Indicate all dimensions. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Plan Drawing (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert actual dimensions to drawing scale (1:50):**

$$
L_{d,\text{length}} = 10.0 \text{ m} \times \frac{1}{50} = 200 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
L_{d,\text{width}} = 8.0 \text{ m} \times \frac{1}{50} = 160 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
L_{d,\text{wall}} = 0.23 \text{ m} \times \frac{1}{50} = 4.6 \text{ mm} \approx 5 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
L_{d,\text{col}} = 0.30 \text{ m} \times \frac{1}{50} = 6 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Construction sequence:**

1. Draw the outer rectangle of **200 mm × 160 mm** using **thick visible object lines (0.7 mm)**.
2. Mark the column positions at corners and at **2 m to 3 m intervals** along the perimeter. Draw columns as **6 mm × 6 mm filled squares** with **centre lines (chain dotted)**.
3. Draw internal partition walls as **double parallel lines** of **5 mm spacing**.
4. Mark door openings (typically **0.9 m to 1.0 m wide**) with a **90° swing arc** in dotted line.
5. Mark window openings (typically **1.2 m wide × 1.2 m high**) on outer walls.
6. Show staircase with **flight arrows** and a **cut-line indicator**.
7. Add furniture symbols (sofa, bed, WC, sink) as per IS conventions.
8. Provide **continuous dimensioning** outside the plan: overall dimensions first, then internal room dimensions.

> **Mark Distribution:** Outer wall drawing (1.5), columns (1.0), internal walls (1.0), openings (1.0), staircase (1.0), dimensions and symbols (1.5).

**(b) Front Elevation Drawing (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute elevation drawing height:**

$$
L_{d,\text{plinth}} = 0.6 \text{ m} \times \frac{1}{50} = 12 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
L_{d,\text{storey}} = 3.0 \text{ m} \times \frac{1}{50} = 60 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
L_{d,\text{parapet}} = 0.9 \text{ m} \times \frac{1}{50} = 18 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
L_{d,\text{total}} = 12 + 2(60) + 18 = 150 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Construction sequence:**

1. Draw a **horizontal ground line (GL)** as thick line.
2. From GL, mark plinth height of **12 mm**.
3. Draw **plinth top** as a thick horizontal line and indicate the **plinth offset (0.45 m × 0.45 m)**.
4. Project the column lines from the plan (using **vertical projection lines** in thin construction lines).
5. Draw the ground floor walls (200 mm wide) with door and window openings matching the plan.
6. Above each window, draw a **chajja projection** (typically **0.45 m × 0.10 m thick** as 9 mm × 2 mm in drawing) at the lintel level.
7. Mark the **first-floor slab line** (12 mm thick → 2.4 mm in drawing, but shown as 6–8 mm for visibility) at height **12 + 60 = 72 mm** from GL.
8. Repeat the wall, door, and window layout for the first floor.
9. Mark the **roof slab** at height **12 + 60 + 60 = 132 mm** from GL.
10. Draw the **parapet wall** of 18 mm height with coping (rounded top).
11. Add **vertical dimensions** on the right side: GL to plinth, plinth to first floor, first floor to second floor, and parapet.

> **Mark Distribution:** Vertical projections (1.5), door/window positions (1.5), chajja and slab lines (1.0), parapet and cornice (1.0), vertical dimensions (1.0), line conventions and neatness (1.0).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2 Internal Choice Q2 (Alternative)]**

**(a)** Draw the **Longitudinal Section** of a two-storied framed building passing through the staircase. The building has a plinth height of **0.6 m**, storey height of **3.0 m**, foundation depth of **1.0 m** below GL, and roof slab thickness of **0.12 m**. Use scale **1:50**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **rise, tread, and number of steps** required for the staircase, given storey height = 3.0 m, riser = 0.15 m, and tread = 0.27 m. Verify the design with NBC 2016 standards. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Longitudinal Section (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert dimensions using $L_d = L_a \times S$:**

$$
L_{d,\text{foundation}} = 1.0 \text{ m} \times \frac{1}{50} = 20 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
L_{d,\text{plinth}} = 0.6 \text{ m} \times \frac{1}{50} = 12 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
L_{d,\text{storey}} = 3.0 \text{ m} \times \frac{1}{50} = 60 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
L_{d,\text{slab}} = 0.12 \text{ m} \times \frac{1}{50} = 2.4 \text{ mm} \approx 4 \text{ mm (visible)}
$$

**Step 2 — Construction sequence:**

1. Draw the **ground line (GL)** as a thick horizontal line.
2. Below GL, mark **foundation depth (20 mm)** showing **isolated column footings** with **footing offset of 0.45 m** (9 mm in drawing). The footings rest on a **PCC bed (0.10 m thick)** shown hatched with concrete pattern.
3. Above the footing, draw the **column (6 mm × 6 mm)** as a solid filled rectangle continuing upward.
4. At **12 mm above GL**, mark the **plinth beam** (continuous horizontal RC member, 0.23 m × 0.30 m → 4.6 mm × 6 mm in drawing).
5. Continue column upward to first floor slab level (12 + 60 = 72 mm above GL).
6. Draw the **first floor slab** of **2.4 mm** thickness at this level.
7. Draw the staircase flight from the ground floor to the first floor with steps shown as a series of lines (each riser 3 mm, each tread 5.4 mm in drawing).
8. Mark the **landing slab** at the mid-landing position.
9. Continue column up to **second floor slab** at **132 mm** above GL.
10. Draw the **roof slab** and **parapet wall** (18 mm) at the top.
11. **Hatch all cut surfaces** of brick walls with **45° diagonal lines** and concrete members with **dotted + diagonal pattern**.

> **Mark Distribution:** Foundation detail (1.5), columns and beams (1.0), slabs and floor levels (1.0), staircase in section (1.5), hatching and line work (1.0), dimensions (1.0).

**(b) Staircase Calculation (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- Storey height = **3.0 m**
- Riser = **0.15 m**
- Tread = **0.27 m**

**Step 1 — Number of Risers:**

$$
n_r = \frac{\text{Storey Height}}{\text{Rise per step}} = \frac{3.0}{0.15}
$$

$$
n_r = 20 \text{ risers}
$$

**Step 2 — Number of Treads:**

$$
n_t = n_r - 1 = 20 - 1 = 19 \text{ treads}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Horizontal Run:**

$$
L_{run} = n_t \times \text{Tread} = 19 \times 0.27
$$

$$
L_{run} = 5.13 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification with NBC 2016:**

Per **NBC 2016, Part 4 — Fire and Life Safety, Cl. 4.4.3.1**:
- Riser should be between **0.13 m to 0.19 m** → 0.15 m ✓
- Tread should be between **0.25 m to 0.30 m** → 0.27 m ✓
- Minimum stair width for residential = **1.0 m** (assumed provided)
- Head room clearance ≥ **2.2 m** (assumed provided)

**Step 5 — Two-Flight Staircase (assumed standard):**

For a standard two-flight staircase with landing:
- Treads per flight = $\dfrac{19}{2} = 9.5$ → use **10 treads per flight** with one extra
- Risers per flight = **10**
- Landing length = 1.0 m (minimum)
- Total run = 10 × 0.27 + 1.0 = 3.7 m per flight

> **Mark Distribution:** Riser & tread formula (2.0), $n_r$ and $n_t$ computation (2.0), $L_{run}$ calculation (1.5), NBC verification (1.5).

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Frequently Observed in KTU Valuation):**
>
> 1. **Wrong projection direction:** Students often draw the elevation on the WRONG SIDE of the plan (e.g., elevation drawn below the plan instead of above it). In **First Angle Projection**, the elevation MUST be drawn **above** the plan, NOT below. **Penalty: Up to 2 marks.**
>
> 2. **Missing Cutting Plane Line in Section:** The **XY cutting plane line** must be drawn on the plan and labeled. If absent, the section is unidentifiable. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
>
> 3. **No hatching or wrong hatching:** Cut surfaces in the section must be hatched. Walls get **45° brick hatching**, concrete gets **dotted + diagonal pattern**. **Penalty: 1–2 marks.**
>
> 4. **Ignoring scale conversion:** Drawing at 1:1 or arbitrary scale instead of specified 1:50. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
>
> 5. **No dimension lines or extension lines:** Many students write dimensions but do not use proper **dimension lines with arrowheads and extension lines**. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
>
> 6. **Confusion between plinth, lintel, and sill levels:** Plinth = 0.45–1.2 m above GL; Lintel = 2.1 m above floor; Sill = 0.9 m above floor. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
>
> 7. **Omitting parapet, chajja, or sun-shade:** These are *defining features* of a building elevation and must be shown. **Penalty: 1 mark.**
>
> 8. **Drawing load-bearing building symbols in a framed building:** Show **isolated column footings** (NOT strip foundation). **Penalty: 1 mark.**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- ☐ **Three primary views** of a building: **Plan** (horizontal cut at 1.2 m), **Elevation** (external vertical view), **Section** (vertical cut through the building).
- ☐ **First Angle Projection** is the KTU-mandated convention. **Elevation above plan, Section beside elevation.**
- ☐ **Cutting plane line** is labeled **X–X** (or **A–A**) on the plan to indicate the section location.
- ☐ **Standard scales**: Plan/Elevation/Section at **1:50** or **1:100**; Details at **1:5, 1:10, 1:20**.
- ☐ **Line weights**: Visible object lines (thick 0.7 mm), Hidden lines (medium 0.5 mm), Centre/dimension lines (thin 0.35 mm).
- ☐ **Hatching patterns**: Brick (45° diagonal), Concrete (dotted + diagonal), Earth (irregular).
- ☐ **Plinth height**: 0.45–1.2 m; **Storey height**: 3.0–3.5 m; **Parapet**: 0.6–1.0 m; **Foundation depth**: 1.0–1.5 m.
- ☐ **Wall thickness**: Outer wall 0.23 m (single brick), Inner wall 0.115 m (half brick) for partition.
- ☐ **Column size** (typical): 0.23 m × 0.23 m or 0.30 m × 0.30 m; **drawn as filled squares** in plan.
- ☐ **Stair formula**: $n_r = \text{Storey height} / \text{Riser}$; $n_t = n_r - 1$; $L_{run} = n_t \times \text{Tread}$.
- ☐ **Frame vs Load-bearing**: In framed structures, walls are *non-load bearing*; columns carry all loads.
- ☐ **Always include**: Title block, North arrow (for site plan), Scale notation, and a clear legend for symbols.
- ☐ **NBC 2016 compliant dimensions**: Riser 0.13–0.19 m; Tread 0.25–0.30 m; Stair width ≥ 1.0 m (residential).
- ☐ **Scale conversion formula**: $L_d = L_a \times S$ (use mm for both, with $S$ as the scale ratio numerator/denominator).
- ☐ **Sheet sizes** as per IS 10711: A0 (1189×841), A1 (841×594), A2 (594×420), A3 (420×297), A4 (297×210).
- ☐ **Projection from plan → elevation**: Vertical lines from plan features extend UPWARD to create the elevation.
- ☐ **Dimensional consistency**: The width in plan = width in elevation (when both are along the same axis).
- ☐ **BIS codes** to cite: **IS 962:1989** (Building Drawing), **IS 10711:2001** (Sheet Sizes), **SP 46:2003** (Drawing Practice), **NBC 2016** (Building Standards).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Draw plan, section and elevation of an industrial building
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZESL208 - BASIC ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Draw plan, section and elevation of an industrial building

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Industrial Building?

An **industrial building** is a large-span structure designed to house manufacturing processes, heavy machinery, storage, assembly lines, or production facilities. Unlike residential or commercial buildings, industrial buildings require:
- **Wide column-free spans** (often 15 m to 30 m or more)
- **High floor-to-roof heights** (typically **6 m to 12 m** at eaves)
- **Roof trusses or portal frames** to support large spans
- **Ventilation systems** (ridge ventilators, louvers, north-light glazing)
- **Cranes / gantries** for material handling
- **Heavy floor load capacity** (≥ **10 kN/m²**)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A building whose primary purpose is to support industrial operations, characterised by large floor areas, high clearances, structural framing systems (steel trusses or RCC frames), and provision for natural light and ventilation.

## 1.2 The Three Orthographic Views — Core Definitions

When we represent a three-dimensional building on a two-dimensional drawing sheet, we use three principal orthographic projections:

| View | What it Shows | Cut Direction | KTU Standard Term |
|------|--------------|---------------|-------------------|
| **Plan** | Horizontal slice, viewed from above | Horizontal cut at sill/window lintel level | "Horizontal Projection" |
| **Section** | Vertical slice through the building, viewed from the side | Vertical cut (cutting plane line) | "Vertical Sectional Elevation" |
| **Elevation** | External vertical view of the building, no cut | No cut — outer face projection | "Front/Side/End Elevation" |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU 2024 scheme, students are required to draw all three views (Plan + Section + Elevation) of a *single* industrial building. The drawing must be to a specified scale (commonly **1:50, 1:100, or 1:200**), on a standard sheet size (A2 or A1).

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Watermelon Concept"

Imagine slicing a **watermelon** in three different ways:

1. **Horizontal slice** (cut across the middle, look down) → This is the **PLAN**. You see the seeds, the flesh pattern, the rind outline — exactly as if you cut the building horizontally and looked straight down.

2. **Vertical slice straight through the middle** (cut from top to bottom, look at the cut face) → This is the **SECTION**. You see the rind thickness, the flesh, the seeds along that cut — this reveals what is *inside* the building (walls, floors, roof slab, truss depth, etc.).

3. **External view without any cut** (just look at the outside skin) → This is the **ELEVATION**. You see only the outer face — doors, windows, cladding, roof profile, parapet.

> [!NOTE]
> A common student mistake: confusing the **Section** with the **Elevation**. The **Section** shows the *interior* (revealing slab thickness, truss profile, footing depth). The **Elevation** shows only the *outer face* (no internal details exposed).

## 1.4 Purpose of Each View in Practice

- **Plan** → Used by contractors to lay out foundations, columns, walls, floor finishes, drainage, and electrical conduits.
- **Elevation** → Used by architects/clients to visualise the external appearance, choose cladding materials, plan fenestration.
- **Section** → Used by structural engineers to understand vertical elements (footing → plinth → column → truss → roof), to verify clear heights, and to detail construction.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Standard:** A complete set of "Good for Construction" (GFC) drawings includes all three views. The Plan-Section-Elevation trio is the foundational language in which every building is described on paper.

---

## 1.5 Typical Components of an Industrial Building (KTU Vocabulary)

The student must be able to *identify and label* the following in the section/elevation:

- **Plinth beam** (at +300 mm to +600 mm above GL)
- **Columns** (RCC or steel I-section, spaced at 6 m to 9 m centres)
- **Wall panels** (brick masonry, height up to lintel)
- **Roof truss** (Pratt, Warren, Howe, or lattice girder)
- **Purlins, sheeting, ridge piece**
- **Ridge ventilator** (for hot air exhaust)
- **North-light roof / saw-tooth roof** (for diffused natural light)
- **Gantry girder** (for EOT crane operation)
- **Flooring** (granite, IPS, or heavy-duty industrial floor)
- **Doors, rolling shutters, windows, louvers**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard dimensions a KTU student must memorise:
> - **Plinth height:** **600 mm** (minimum, above Ground Level)
> - **Standard door size (industrial):** 2.0 m × 2.4 m
> - **Rolling shutter size:** 3.0 m × 3.6 m
> - **Standard window size:** 1.5 m × 1.5 m
> - **Truss span:** 15 m, 18 m, 20 m, 25 m (most common)
> - **Truss rise:** Span / 6 to Span / 4
> - **Column spacing along span (longitudinally):** 5 m to 6 m

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Drawing Conventions Used in KTU Workshop

### 2.1.1 Line Types (as per BIS / SP 46)

| Line Type | Line Weight | Purpose |
|-----------|-------------|---------|
| Thick continuous | **0.7 mm** | Visible outlines, edges, main features |
| Medium continuous | **0.5 mm** | Hidden outlines, dimension lines |
| Thin continuous | **0.25 mm** | Hatching, centre lines, leader lines |
| Dashed (short) | **0.25 mm** | Hidden edges |
| Chain (long-short) | **0.25 mm** | Centre lines, cutting plane lines |
| Chain (long-short-long) | **0.25 mm** | Section cutting plane indicator |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU evaluation heavily penalises students who use **a single line weight for all lines**. The examiner expects clear distinction between **thick, medium, and thin** lines.

### 2.1.2 Standard Scales for Building Drawings

| Drawing Type | Recommended Scale |
|--------------|-------------------|
| Site plan | **1:500** or 1:1000 |
| Floor plan | **1:50** or 1:100 |
| Elevation | **1:50** or 1:100 |
| Section | **1:50** or 1:100 |
| Detail drawings | **1:10**, 1:20, or 1:5 |

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic: How the Three Views Relate to Each Other

The three views are **not independent** — they are projections of the same 3D object and must be **dimensionally consistent**.

**Core principle:** *First-angle projection* (as per BIS standard used in India)

1. **Plan** is placed at the **top-centre** of the sheet.
2. **Section** is placed **below** the plan (because section is a vertical cut, and the cut surface "drops down" below the plan in first-angle projection).
3. **Elevation** is placed **above** the section (the front view sits on top of the section in first-angle layout, or aligned with the section horizontally).
4. The **width** (X-direction) of all three views is **identical** and must align vertically.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU expects a **dimensionally consistent set** of views. If the plan shows 20 m width, the elevation and section must also show 20 m width. **A common mistake is to redraw with different proportions — instant mark loss.**

## 2.3 Structural Logic of an Industrial Building Section

Reading the section **bottom-to-top**:

1. **Foundation** (footings, depth typically 1.5 m to 2.0 m below GL)
2. **Plinth beam** (at +600 mm GL)
3. **Columns** (rise from plinth to truss bottom)
4. **Walls** (up to lintel level, typically 2.1 m to 2.4 m)
5. **Windows / Louvers** (in wall panels)
6. **Roof truss bottom chord** (clear height typically 6 m to 8 m)
7. **Truss members** (top chord, bottom chord, verticals, diagonals)
8. **Ridge line** (apex of truss)
9. **Ridge ventilator** (above ridge, raised by 600 mm to 900 mm)
10. **Roof sheeting / purlins** (on top of truss)

> [!TIP]
> **KTU frequently asked question:** *"List the parts of an industrial building as seen in the section."* Memorise the above list in order.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula / Standard | Unit |
|---|-----------|--------------------|------|
| 1 | Truss rise (R) | $R = \dfrac{L}{6} \text{ to } \dfrac{L}{4}$ | m |
| 2 | Truss span (L) | Given in problem | m |
| 3 | Truss spacing (longitudinally) | $S_t = 5$ to $6$ | m |
| 4 | Column spacing (along span) | $S_c = 6$ to $9$ | m |
| 5 | Plinth height (h_p) | $h_p = 0.6$ (minimum) | m |
| 6 | Clear height (H_c) | $H_c = 6$ to $10$ | m |
| 7 | Ridge ventilator rise | $h_v = 0.6$ to $0.9$ | m |
| 8 | Ventilator throat width | $W_v = 1.2$ to $1.8$ | m |
| 9 | Number of panels in truss | $N = 6, 8, 10$ | — |
| 10 | Panel length | $p = L/N$ | m |
| 11 | Floor area load (industrial) | $10$ to $30$ | kN/m² |
| 12 | Roof live load | $0.75$ to $1.5$ | kN/m² |
| 13 | Drawing scale (Plan/Section/Elevation) | $1:50$ or $1:100$ | ratio |
| 14 | Wind load (basic) | $V_b = 33$ to $47$ (zone-wise) | m/s |
| 15 | Brick wall thickness | $0.23$ (single), $0.345$ (1.5) | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on table syntax:** Vertical bars `|` are used **only** as column separators. For absolute value / magnitude in formulas, use `\vert` (e.g., $\vert V_b \vert$).

## 2.5 Why Trusses? — The Engineering "Why"

A roof truss converts vertical and gravity loads into **axial forces** in its members:
- **Top chord** → Compression
- **Bottom chord** → Tension
- **Diagonals** → Alternating tension and compression
- **Verticals** → Tension (in most Pratt configurations)

> The truss spans large distances **without intermediate columns**, giving the **clear floor area** essential for industrial operations.

**Real-world utility:**
- **Warehouses** → truss allows stacking cranes and pallet racks
- **Manufacturing shops** → clear span accommodates assembly lines
- **Aircraft hangars** → very large spans (40 m+) require deep trusses
- **Power plants** → gantry crane rail supported on columns + truss roof

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Procedure, Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem Statement (Typical KTU Exam)

> *"Draw to a scale of **1:100** the plan, section, and elevation of an industrial building having a clear span of **20 m**, length **30 m**, clear height **8 m** at eaves, with a **Pratt roof truss** of rise **3 m**, ridge ventilator of **1 m** rise, brick walls of **230 mm** thickness, columns at **6 m** centres along the length, rolling shutters of **3 m × 3.6 m**, and standard windows."*

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Procedure for Drawing the Plan

### Step 1 — Choose the Sheet and Layout

- Sheet size: **A2** (594 × 420 mm) or **A1** (841 × 594 mm)
- Divide the sheet into three zones:
  - Top: Title block + Plan
  - Middle: Section
  - Bottom (or right): Elevation

### Step 2 — Mark the Grid Lines on the Plan

Given: Length = 30 m, Column spacing = 6 m along length.

$$
N_{\text{columns along length}} = \dfrac{L_{\text{length}}}{S_c} + 1 = \dfrac{30}{6} + 1 = 6
$$

So there are **6 columns** along the longitudinal direction, with **5 bays** of 6 m each.

### Step 3 — Mark the Span Direction

Given: Span = 20 m, with columns at the two ends (no intermediate column since 20 m is large).

$$
N_{\text{trusses}} = N_{\text{columns along length}} = 6
$$

So **6 roof trusses**, spaced at 6 m c/c.

### Step 4 — Draw Column Positions (Plan)

Draw small squares (300 × 300 mm) at the grid intersections. Mark them with dimension lines:
- Longitudinal direction: 6 m × 5 = 30 m
- Transverse direction: 20 m (one span)

### Step 5 — Draw Walls

- Longitudinal walls: thickness **0.23 m** (single brick)
- Gable walls (end walls): thickness **0.23 m**

### Step 6 — Mark Openings (Doors and Windows)

- **Rolling shutters** on longitudinal walls (e.g., 2 per bay, 3 m × 3.6 m)
- **Windows** in gable walls (for natural light, 1.5 m × 1.5 m)
- Show door/window swings with quarter-circle arcs at 90° opening

### Step 7 — Dimension and Label

Add dimensions on **all four sides** of the plan. Mark column numbers (C1, C2, ..., C6) and bay labels (B1, B2, ..., B5).

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Procedure for Drawing the Section

### Step 1 — Project Vertically Below the Plan

The section is drawn **directly below** the plan (first-angle projection), aligned on the centreline.

### Step 2 — Mark the Ground Level (GL) Line

GL is the **reference datum**. All heights are measured from GL upward.

### Step 3 — Draw the Foundation (Below GL)

- Depth of footing below GL: assume **1.5 m**
- Footing width: 1.5 m × 1.5 m (for illustration)
- Plinth beam at +0.6 m above GL

### Step 4 — Draw the Columns

- Column height from plinth to bottom of truss: **8 m** (clear height)
- Column cross-section: 300 mm × 300 mm (RCC) or steel I-section
- Draw the column as a vertical rectangle in section

### Step 5 — Draw the Walls

- Wall from plinth to lintel: 0.23 m thick, 2.4 m high
- Above lintel: open gable area (if any) or wall continues to truss bottom

### Step 6 — Draw the Roof Truss

- Span: 20 m (horizontally)
- Rise: 3 m
- Truss apex at: +8 m + 3 m = **+11 m** above GL
- Use **Pratt configuration**:
  - Bottom chord: horizontal
  - Top chord: sloped from eaves to apex
  - Verticals: at panel points
  - Diagonals: sloped toward centre (in tension)

> [!NOTE]
> **Panel calculation:** With 8 panels and span 20 m:
> $$p = \dfrac{L}{N} = \dfrac{20}{8} = 2.5 \text{ m}$$

### Step 7 — Draw the Ridge Ventilator

- Above the truss apex, raise a small structure: 1 m rise, 1.2 m wide throat
- Side louvers on ventilator for hot air exhaust

### Step 8 — Add Sheeting, Purlins, and Roof Slope

- Purlins at ~1.4 m c/c along top chord
- Roof sheeting on top of purlins (corrugated GI / asbestos / metal sheet)

### Step 9 — Mark All Heights and Levels

Annotate the section with:
- **+0.00 m** → GL
- **+0.60 m** → Plinth level
- **+2.40 m** → Lintel level
- **+8.00 m** → Eaves / Truss bottom
- **+11.00 m** → Ridge apex
- **+12.00 m** → Top of ventilator

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Procedure for Drawing the Elevation

### Step 1 — Project from the Section or Plan

The **front elevation** is drawn by projecting horizontally from the section's outer face.

### Step 2 — Draw the Outline

- Overall width = 20 m (or 30 m, depending on which face is chosen)
- Overall height = +12 m at ridge
- Trapezoidal roof profile (sloped top chord)

### Step 3 — Show Wall Openings

- Doors / rolling shutters on the elevation (rectangular, with shutter lines)
- Windows: small squares, with sill at +0.9 m and lintel at +2.1 m
- Louvers: horizontal slat patterns

### Step 4 — Add Cladding / Finishing Details

- Plaster lines (if any)
- Gable end shape (triangular, following the truss slope)
- Parapet wall (if any) above the roof line
- Rain water down-take pipes (RWP) at column locations
- Name board / sign board area (industrial buildings often have these)

### Step 5 — Dimension and Label

- Overall dimensions: width × total height
- Eaves height, ridge height
- Opening sizes (e.g., "RS 3.0 × 3.6")
- North direction indicator (on plan)

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Component Visibility Across Views

| Building Component | Visible in Plan? | Visible in Section? | Visible in Elevation? |
|--------------------|:---------------:|:-------------------:|:---------------------:|
| Footings | No (below cut) | **Yes** (below GL) | No |
| Plinth beam | **Yes** (line) | **Yes** (line) | No |
| Columns | **Yes** (square) | **Yes** (rectangle) | Sometimes (edge) |
| Floor slab | **Yes** (outline) | **Yes** (line) | No |
| Walls | **Yes** (thick line) | **Yes** (hatched) | **Yes** (outer face) |
| Doors / Shutters | **Yes** (arc + line) | **Yes** (in cut) | **Yes** (outer face) |
| Windows | **Yes** (arc + line) | **Yes** (in cut) | **Yes** (outer face) |
| Roof truss | No | **Yes** (full profile) | No (hidden) |
| Ridge ventilator | No | **Yes** | **Yes** (silhouette) |
| Purlins | No | **Yes** (thin line) | No |
| Gantry girder | **Yes** (line at +5 m) | **Yes** | No |
| North direction | **Yes** | No | No |
| Title block | No | No | No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convention:** Hidden features in a view are drawn using **dashed lines** (short, evenly spaced dashes). In elevation, the truss is hidden behind the gable wall, so it appears as a dashed line.

---

## 3.6 Comprehensive Worked Example — Numerical Derivations

**Given:**
- Span $L = 20$ m
- Length $L_{\text{len}} = 30$ m
- Clear height at eaves $H_c = 8$ m
- Truss rise $R = 3$ m
- Ventilator rise $h_v = 1$ m
- Column spacing along length $S_c = 6$ m
- Brick wall thickness $t_w = 0.23$ m
- Scale: 1:100 (1 cm on paper = 1 m actual)

**Step A — Number of columns along the length:**

$$
N_c = \dfrac{L_{\text{len}}}{S_c} + 1 = \dfrac{30}{6} + 1 = 5 + 1 = 6 \text{ columns}
$$

**Step B — Number of bays:**

$$
N_{\text{bays}} = N_c - 1 = 6 - 1 = 5 \text{ bays of 6 m each}
$$

**Step C — Number of trusses:**

$$
N_{\text{trusses}} = N_c = 6 \text{ trusses}
$$

**Step D — Panel length in the truss (assuming 8-panel Pratt):**

$$
p = \dfrac{L}{N_{\text{panels}}} = \dfrac{20}{8} = 2.5 \text{ m per panel}
$$

**Step E — Total height at ridge:**

$$
H_{\text{ridge}} = H_c + R = 8 + 3 = 11 \text{ m above GL}
$$

**Step F — Total height at ventilator top:**

$$
H_{\text{total}} = H_{\text{ridge}} + h_v = 11 + 1 = 12 \text{ m above GL}
$$

**Step G — Drawing dimensions on the sheet (scale 1:100, 1 cm = 1 m):**

| Actual Dimension | Paper Dimension (cm) |
|------------------|----------------------|
| 30 m length | 30 cm |
| 20 m span | 20 cm |
| 12 m total height | 12 cm |
| 8 m clear height | 8 cm |
| 0.23 m wall thickness | 0.23 cm ≈ 2.3 mm |
| 3 m × 3.6 m rolling shutter | 3 cm × 3.6 cm |

**Step H — Roof slope (for verification):**

$$
\theta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{R}{L/2}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{3}{10}\right) \approx 16.7^{\circ}
$$

This is a typical industrial roof slope (between 10° and 22°).

**Step I — Truss member count (Pratt truss, 8 panels):**

- Top chord: 2 × 4 = 8 segments
- Bottom chord: 8 segments
- Verticals: 7 (in an 8-panel Pratt)
- Diagonals: 6 (sloping toward centre)

$$
N_{\text{members}} = 8 + 8 + 7 + 6 = 29 \text{ members}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This member count is useful when the question asks *"Sketch a typical Pratt truss and label its members"* — a common 7-mark sub-part.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Relationship Between the Three Views

The following Mermaid block diagram explains how the three views are generated from the same 3D building, and how they relate to each other in the drawing sheet.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[3D Industrial Building] --> B[Cutting Plane Selected]
    B --> C[Horizontal Cut at Sill Level]
    B --> D[Vertical Cut Through Mid Span]
    C --> E[PLAN View]
    D --> F[SECTION View]
    A --> G[No Cut - Outer Face Only]
    G --> H[ELEVATION View]
    E --> I[Drawing Sheet Layout]
    F --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Title Block and Scale]
    I --> K[Dimensioning and Labels]
    I --> L[North Direction Indicator]
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Components of an Industrial Building (Section View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Ridge Ventilator Top 12.0m] --> B[Ridge Ventilator Throat 11.0m]
    B --> C[Truss Apex 11.0m]
    C --> D[Top Chord Sloped Member]
    D --> E[Bottom Chord 8.0m Eaves]
    E --> F[Vertical Column 8.0m]
    F --> G[Wall Panel 0.6m to 2.4m]
    G --> H[Plinth Beam at 0.6m]
    H --> I[Foundation Footing 1.5m below GL]
    I --> J[Ground Level 0.0m]
    style A fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000
    style C fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000
    style E fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style F fill:#4682B4,stroke:#000
    style H fill:#8B4513,stroke:#000
    style I fill:#696969,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style J fill:#228B22,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Pratt Roof Truss Member Configuration (8-Panel)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[Left Support] --> N2[Bottom Panel Point 1]
    N2 --> N3[Bottom Panel Point 2]
    N3 --> N4[Bottom Panel Point 3]
    N4 --> N5[Bottom Panel Point 4 Centre]
    N5 --> N6[Bottom Panel Point 5]
    N6 --> N7[Bottom Panel Point 6]
    N7 --> N8[Bottom Panel Point 7]
    N8 --> N9[Right Support]
    N1 --> T1[Top Panel Point 1]
    T1 --> T2[Top Panel Point 2]
    T2 --> T3[Top Panel Point 3]
    T3 --> T4[Top Panel Point 4 Centre Apex]
    T4 --> T5[Top Panel Point 5]
    T5 --> T6[Top Panel Point 6]
    T6 --> T7[Top Panel Point 7]
    T7 --> N9
    T1 --- N2
    T2 --- N3
    T3 --- N4
    T4 --- N5
    T5 --- N6
    T6 --- N7
    T7 --- N8
    N1 --- T2
    N2 --- T3
    N3 --- T4
    N4 --- T3
    N5 --- T2
    N5 --- T6
    N6 --- T5
    N7 --- T4
    N8 --- T3
    N9 --- T6
    style N5 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style T4 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above is a **schematic representation** of a Pratt truss. In an actual drawing exam, the student draws the truss as a triangle with internal triangulated members, not as a flowchart. The diagram above is a logical representation only.

## 4.4 Sheet Layout Block Diagram (First-Angle Projection)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A[TOP ZONE OF SHEET]
        P[PLAN VIEW - Scale 1 to 100]
    end
    subgraph B[MIDDLE ZONE OF SHEET]
        S[SECTION VIEW - Scale 1 to 100]
    end
    subgraph C[RIGHT SIDE OF SHEET]
        E[ELEVATION VIEW - Scale 1 to 100]
    end
    subgraph D[BOTTOM RIGHT CORNER]
        T[TITLE BLOCK with Name, Date, Scale, Drawing No]
    end
    P --> S
    P --> E
    style P fill:#87CEEB
    style S fill:#90EE90
    style E fill:#FFA07A
    style T fill:#DDA0DD
```

## 4.5 Functional Flow — Steps to Produce the Final Drawing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Read Problem Statement] --> S2[Step 2: Identify Span Length and Clear Height]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Calculate Number of Columns and Bays]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Calculate Truss Rise and Ridge Height]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Convert All Dimensions to Drawing Scale]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Draw Plan at Top of Sheet]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Project Section Vertically Below Plan]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Project Elevation from Section or Plan]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Add Dimensions, Labels, Hatching]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Add Title Block and North Mark]
    S10 --> S11[Final Sheet with Three Views Ready for Evaluation]
    style S11 fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between Plan, Section, and Elevation of a building. List the components visible in each view of an industrial building.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Plan | Section | Elevation |
|--------|------|---------|-----------|
| **Cut direction** | Horizontal | Vertical | No cut |
| **Viewing direction** | From top | From side (cut face) | From side (outer face) |
| **Shows** | Layout, columns, walls, openings | Internal structure (truss, footings, slab) | Outer appearance |
| **Components visible** | Columns, walls, doors, windows, rolling shutters | Truss, purlins, sheeting, footing, plinth beam, ventilator | Walls, doors, windows, ventilator silhouette, parapet |

**(3 marks)** — Awarded for clear distinction + component list.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: What is a Pratt roof truss? Sketch and label its members.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Pratt roof truss** is a triangulated steel/wooden framework used in industrial building roofs, in which:
- **Vertical members** are in **tension**
- **Diagonal members** slope **toward the centre** (also in tension under gravity load)
- **Top chord** is in **compression**
- **Bottom chord** is in **tension**

**Sketch (text description for 3-mark answer):**
- Draw a triangle with **horizontal bottom chord** of length 20 m
- Mark apex at centre, rise 3 m
- Divide bottom chord into 8 panels (2.5 m each)
- Draw **verticals** at each panel point
- Draw **diagonals** sloping from eaves toward the centre
- Label: Top Chord, Bottom Chord, Verticals, Diagonals, Ridge, Eaves, Panel Point

**(3 marks)** — 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for labelled sketch.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module — Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Q: An industrial building has a clear span of 18 m, length 30 m, clear height at eaves 7 m, and a Pratt roof truss of rise 2.5 m with a ridge ventilator of 0.9 m rise. Columns are spaced at 6 m centres along the length. Brick walls are 230 mm thick. Draw to a scale of 1:100 the:**
**(a) Plan of the building (7 marks)**
**(b) Section and Front Elevation of the building (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Plan (7 marks):**

1. **Column calculation:**
   $$
   N_c = \dfrac{30}{6} + 1 = 6 \text{ columns}
   $$
2. **Truss count:** 6 trusses (one per column row)
3. **Draw the outline** of 30 m × 18 m at scale 1:100 (i.e., 30 cm × 18 cm on paper)
4. **Mark column positions** as 300 × 300 mm squares at the 6 grid lines
5. **Draw walls** (230 mm = 2.3 mm thick lines on paper)
6. **Mark rolling shutters** on longitudinal walls (2 per bay, 3 m × 3.6 m)
7. **Mark windows** in gable walls
8. **Add dimensions** on all four sides, label column lines (A, B, C, D, E, F) and bay numbers
9. **Mark North direction** arrow

**Mark allocation:**
- Correct column count: 1 mark
- Accurate scale and proportions: 2 marks
- Wall and opening placement: 2 marks
- Dimensioning and labelling: 1 mark
- North direction and neatness: 1 mark

**Part (b) — Section and Front Elevation (7 marks):**

1. **Section drawing (4 marks):**
   - Draw GL at the bottom
   - Foundation: 1.5 m below GL
   - Plinth beam: at +0.6 m
   - Columns: from plinth to +7 m (eaves)
   - Walls: from +0.6 m to +2.4 m (lintel level)
   - Truss: span 18 m, rise 2.5 m → ridge at +9.5 m
   - Ridge ventilator: +9.5 m to +10.4 m
   - Hatch all cut sections (walls, columns, footings)
   - Dimension all key levels

2. **Elevation drawing (3 marks):**
   - Width = 18 m (front face view)
   - Total height = 10.4 m
   - Gable end shape (triangular, with truss slope)
   - Show rolling shutters, windows
   - Add parapet, RWP, name board area

**Mark allocation:**
- Correct truss geometry: 2 marks
- Ventilator and roof details: 1 mark
- Foundation and plinth: 1 mark
- Elevation: 2 marks
- Dimensioning and hatching: 1 mark

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023] — Alternative Choice

**Q: Draw to a scale of 1:50 the Plan, Section, and Elevation of a single-storey industrial workshop with the following data:**
- **Clear span:** 15 m
- **Length:** 24 m
- **Eaves height:** 6 m
- **Truss type:** Howe truss
- **Truss rise:** 2.5 m
- **Ventilator rise:** 0.75 m
- **Column spacing along length:** 6 m
- **Doors:** 2 rolling shutters of 3 m × 3 m on each longitudinal side
- **Windows:** 1.2 m × 1.2 m in gable walls
- **Walls:** 230 mm brick masonry

**Explain briefly why Howe trusses are preferred for industrial buildings in Kerala.**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Plan (7 marks):**

1. Columns:
   $$
   N_c = \dfrac{24}{6} + 1 = 5 \text{ columns per row}
   $$
2. Trusses: 5 roof trusses
3. Draw at scale 1:50 → sheet size 48 cm × 30 cm (1:50 means 1 cm = 0.5 m; so 15 m = 30 cm and 24 m = 48 cm)
4. Mark column positions
5. Mark rolling shutters: 2 × 3 m on each longitudinal side
6. Mark windows in gable walls
7. Add column line labels (1, 2, 3, 4, 5)

**Part (b) — Section and Elevation (7 marks):**

1. **Section:**
   - Ridge height: 6 + 2.5 = **8.5 m**
   - Ventilator top: 8.5 + 0.75 = **9.25 m**
   - Panel length: $p = 15 / 8 = 1.875$ m (8 panels)
   - **Howe truss specifics:** Diagonals slope **outward from the centre** (toward the eaves) — opposite of Pratt. Verticals are in compression, diagonals in tension under gravity.
2. **Elevation:** Front face of 15 m wide × 9.25 m high

**Why Howe trusses in Kerala:**
- Heavy monsoon rainfall → rainwater flows outward easily on Howe (matching Kerala's typical roof slope)
- Easy fabrication with timber (traditional in Kerala)
- Diagonals in tension can use steel rods; verticals in compression use timber
- Suitable for shorter spans (15 m is within Howe's efficient range)

**Mark allocation:**
- Plan drawing: 3.5 marks
- Section drawing: 2 marks
- Elevation drawing: 1.5 marks
- Explanation of Howe truss suitability: 2 marks
- (Total 9 marks — but capped at 7 with rest going to neatness and dimensions)

> Adjust the marking distribution to fit the 7+7 scheme as the examiner sees fit.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-loss areas in the Plan-Section-Elevation drawing question:**
>
> 1. **Inconsistent scales** between views (Plan at 1:50, Section at 1:100) → **−2 to −3 marks**
> 2. **Single line weight** for all lines (no thick/medium/thin distinction) → **−1 to −2 marks**
> 3. **No hatching** on cut portions of walls, columns, footings → **−2 marks**
> 4. **Missing North direction** indicator on plan → **−0.5 mark**
> 5. **No title block** (or incomplete) → **−1 mark**
> 6. **Rolling shutter drawn as a single rectangle** (without the horizontal slat lines indicating it's a shutter) → **−1 mark**
> 7. **Confusing Section with Elevation** (no internal truss profile shown in "section") → **−3 marks**
> 8. **Forgetting the ridge ventilator** in the section → **−1 mark**
> 9. **No foundation shown in section** (footing missing) → **−1 mark**
> 10. **Dimensions placed on the inside of the drawing** (should be outside, with witness lines) → **−1 mark**
> 11. **Handwriting / line work is messy** — KTU values neatness heavily; expect **−1 to −2 marks** for poor presentation
> 12. **Truss drawn as a simple triangle** without internal members (in section, the truss profile must show the actual triangulated web) → **−2 marks**

> [!TIP]
> **Topper's strategy:** Always include the following in your answer sheet:
> - A clear **title block** (Name, Roll No, Date, Scale, Drawing Title)
> - A **legend** explaining line types
> - **Hatching at 45°** on all cut masonry / concrete
> - **North direction** symbol on the plan
> - A **small list of abbreviations** (GL = Ground Level, FF = Finished Floor, RS = Rolling Shutter, W = Window, D = Door)

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Exam**

### A. Definitions (must memorise verbatim)
- ✅ **Plan** — Horizontal projection of a building, obtained by a horizontal cut at sill/window level.
- ✅ **Section** — Vertical projection of a building, obtained by a vertical cut, showing internal details.
- ✅ **Elevation** — External vertical projection of a building, showing only the outer face.
- ✅ **Industrial building** — A structure designed for manufacturing/storage, with large spans, high clearances, and crane provisions.
- ✅ **Pratt truss** — Diagonals slope toward the centre; verticals in tension.
- ✅ **Howe truss** — Diagonals slope toward the eaves; verticals in compression.
- ✅ **Ridge ventilator** — Raised structure on the ridge for hot air exhaust.
- ✅ **Plinth** — The portion of the wall between GL and floor level (min 600 mm above GL).

### B. Standard Numerical Values
- ✅ Plinth height = **0.6 m**
- ✅ Brick wall thickness = **0.23 m** (single), **0.345 m** (1.5 brick)
- ✅ Truss rise = L/6 to L/4
- ✅ Truss panel length = **2 to 3 m** (typical)
- ✅ Column spacing along length = **5 to 6 m**
- ✅ Eaves height for industrial = **6 to 10 m**
- ✅ Standard rolling shutter = **3 m × 3.6 m**
- ✅ Standard window = **1.2 m to 1.5 m square**
- ✅ Ventilator rise = **0.6 to 1.0 m**

### C. Drawing Rules
- ✅ Use **thick line (0.7 mm)** for visible outlines
- ✅ Use **medium line (0.5 mm)** for hidden lines
- ✅ Use **thin line (0.25 mm)** for centre lines, hatching, dimensions
- ✅ Hatch cut portions at **45°**, spaced at **3 to 5 mm**
- ✅ Always include **title block, legend, north direction**
- ✅ Views must be **dimensionally consistent** (same width aligned vertically)
- ✅ Standard sheet: **A2 or A1**
- ✅ Standard scale: **1:50, 1:100, or 1:200**

### D. Component-to-View Mapping
- ✅ Foundation → Section only
- ✅ Columns → All three views
- ✅ Walls → All three views
- ✅ Doors / Windows → All three views
- ✅ Truss → Section only (hidden line in elevation)
- ✅ Purlins → Section only
- ✅ Ventilator → Section + Elevation
- ✅ Gantry → Plan + Section (hidden in elevation)

### E. Quick Formulas
- ✅ Number of columns: $N_c = \dfrac{L_{\text{len}}}{S_c} + 1$
- ✅ Number of bays: $N_{\text{bays}} = N_c - 1$
- ✅ Number of trusses: $N_{\text{truss}} = N_c$
- ✅ Panel length: $p = \dfrac{L_{\text{span}}}{N_{\text{panels}}}$
- ✅ Ridge height: $H_r = H_c + R$
- ✅ Total height: $H_{\text{total}} = H_r + h_v$

### F. Examiner's Pet Questions
- ⭐ "Differentiate Plan, Section, and Elevation"
- ⭐ "List the components of an industrial building as seen in section"
- ⭐ "Sketch and label a Pratt truss"
- ⭐ "Calculate the number of columns for a given building"
- ⭐ "Why is a ridge ventilator provided?"
- ⭐ "What is the minimum plinth height as per IS code?"
- ⭐ "Explain first-angle projection"

---

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Tip:** Practice drawing the **plan, section, and elevation of the same building** in a single sitting. Time yourself — you should complete the full drawing in **90 minutes** for full marks. Always start with the plan (it's the most information-dense view), then project the section vertically below, and finally the elevation aligned to the section. This sequence prevents dimensional inconsistencies, which is the #1 reason students lose marks in this experiment.

---

**End of Notes — Module Experiment 2: Plan, Section and Elevation of an Industrial Building**

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
