> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Auto CAD: preparation of CAD drawing of building components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE EXPERIMENT 2: INTRODUCTION TO AUTO CAD

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**AutoCAD** is a commercial computer-aided design (CAD) and drafting software application developed by **Autodesk Inc.** It is used across engineering and architectural disciplines for creating precise 2D drawings and 3D models of physical components, structures, and systems. In the context of civil and architectural engineering workshops, AutoCAD is employed for the **preparation of CAD drawings of building components** such as floor plans, elevations, sections, doors, windows, staircases, and roofing details.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"AutoCAD is a vector-based 2D and 3D design tool that allows engineers and architects to draft building plans with high precision using Cartesian coordinate geometry, layering systems, and parametric tools."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are sketching a house on paper with a ruler and pencil. The lines are never perfectly straight, the dimensions are approximate, and making a change means erasing and redoing work. **AutoCAD is the digital equivalent of a perfectly straight, infinitely patient, infinitely precise draftsman.**

**Real-World Analogy — The "Smart Graph Paper":**
Think of AutoCAD as **smart graph paper** where:
- Every point has an **exact (X, Y, Z) coordinate**.
- Every line has a **precise length and angle**.
- You can **zoom infinitely** without losing clarity.
- Mistakes are **erased with one command** (`UNDO` or `ERASE`).
- You can **copy, mirror, and rotate** entire building wings in seconds.
- Multiple building layers (electrical, plumbing, structural) can be **separated and superimposed** like transparent sheets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Idea for KTU Exam:** AutoCAD replaces manual drafting boards (drafting table, T-square, set squares, drafter) with a **digital drafting environment** that improves accuracy, speed, storage, and ease of modification.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants in AutoCAD

| Parameter | Standard Value | Description |
|-----------|----------------|-------------|
| **Drawing Units** | **Millimeters (mm)** or **Meters (m)** | KTU/Indian architectural practice uses **mm** for plans |
| **Drawing Limits** | **A1 / A2 sheet size** | Default KTU submission uses **A2 (594 × 420 mm)** |
| **Scale Factor (Plot)** | **1:50, 1:100, 1:200** | Building plans typically plotted at **1:100** |
| **Text Height (Paper)** | **2.5 mm** | Standard annotation text on plot |
| **Dimension Text** | **2.5 mm** | As per **BIS SP 46:1988** standards |
| **Line Weights** | **0.13 mm to 0.50 mm** | Visible lines thicker than hidden lines |

---

### 1.4 Historical Context (Quick Reference)

- **1982** — AutoCAD v1.0 released (first version)
- **Current** — AutoCAD **2024/2025** version active
- **File Extension** — `.dwg` (drawing file), `.dxf` (data exchange format)
- **Operating System** — Windows / macOS

> [!TIP]
> For the KTU viva, remember: **AutoCAD is NOT a programming language; it is a drafting tool.** It uses **command-driven input** (typed commands) and **cursor-driven input** (mouse clicks).

### 1.5 Why AutoCAD for Building Drawings?

1. **Precision** — accuracy up to **0.0001 units**.
2. **Speed** — quick modifications vs. manual redrawing.
3. **Reusability** — symbols and blocks can be reused.
4. **Layering** — different building elements on different layers.
5. **Plotting/Printing** — exact scale output on any paper size.
6. **3D Capability** — isometric and perspective views for visualization.
7. **Documentation** — automatic schedules (door/window schedules) via data extraction.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 AutoCAD User Interface (UI) Architecture

When AutoCAD is launched, the screen is divided into multiple functional zones. Understanding these zones is **critical for the KTU lab exam viva**.

| Zone | Name | Function |
|------|------|----------|
| **A** | **Application Menu** (Big 'A') | File operations: New, Open, Save, Save As, Print, Export |
| **B** | **Quick Access Toolbar** | New, Open, Save, Undo, Redo |
| **C** | **Ribbon** | Tabs → Panels → Tools (e.g., Home → Draw → Line) |
| **D** | **Command Line** | Type commands (e.g., `L`, `C`, `REC`) |
| **E** | **Drawing Area** | The infinite canvas where the building is drawn |
| **F** | **View Cube** | 3D orientation toggle |
| **G** | **Navigation Bar** | Pan, Zoom, Orbit |
| **H** | **Model Space / Layout Space** | Switch between model and paper space |
| **I** | **Status Bar** | Ortho, Grid, Snap, Osnap, Otrack, Dynamic UCS, Lineweight |
| **J** | **Properties Palette** | Modify color, layer, linetype, lineweight |

---

## 2.2 Fundamental AutoCAD Commands (KTU Must-Know List)

### 2.2.1 Draw Commands

| Command | Alias | Function | Building Application |
|---------|-------|----------|----------------------|
| **Line** | `L` | Draws straight line segments | Walls, door frames, dimension lines |
| **Polyline** | `PL` | Connected line segments (single object) | Building outline, electrical conduits |
| **Rectangle** | `REC` | Draws rectangle | Room layout, doors, windows |
| **Circle** | `C` | Draws circle | Columns (circular), manholes |
| **Arc** | `A` | Draws arc | Arched openings, curved walls |
| **Polygon** | `POL` | Draws regular polygon | Hex/octagonal structures |
| **Ellipse** | `EL` | Draws ellipse | Curved architectural features |
| **Hatch** | `H` | Fills closed regions with pattern | Concrete, brick, earth, insulation |
| **Block** | `B` | Creates reusable symbol | Door, window, furniture symbols |
| **Text** | `T` / `DT` | Adds text | Labels, dimensions, notes |
| **MText** | `T` (multi-line) | Multi-line text | Title block, specifications |

### 2.2.2 Modify Commands

| Command | Alias | Function | Building Application |
|---------|-------|----------|----------------------|
| **Erase** | `E` | Deletes object | Remove wrong linework |
| **Copy** | `CO` / `CP` | Copies object | Repeat windows, columns |
| **Move** | `M` | Moves object | Realign elements |
| **Rotate** | `RO` | Rotates object around base point | Rotate door symbols |
| **Mirror** | `MI` | Mirrors object across axis | Symmetrical building wings |
| **Offset** | `O` | Creates parallel offset | Double-line walls |
| **Trim** | `TR` | Trims objects at cutting edge | Clean intersections |
| **Extend** | `EX` | Extends to boundary | Extend walls to boundary |
| **Fillet** | `F` | Rounds corners | Smooth wall junctions |
| **Chamfer** | `CHA` | Bevels corners | Architectural detailing |
| **Scale** | `SC` | Resizes object | Enlarge/scale down |
| **Stretch** | `S` | Stretches objects | Resize room |
| **Array** | `AR` | Rectangular/Polar array | Multiple windows, columns |
| **Explode** | `X` | Breaks compound object | Separate block into lines |

### 2.2.3 Annotation & Dimension Commands

| Command | Alias | Function |
|---------|-------|----------|
| **Linear Dimension** | `DLI` | Horizontal/vertical dimensions |
| **Aligned Dimension** | `DAL` | Aligned with object |
| **Angular Dimension** | `DAN` | Angle measurement |
| **Radius/Diameter** | `DRA` / `DDI` | Circle/arc dimensions |
| **Leader** | `LE` | Leader line with text |
| **Dimension Style** | `D` | Create/manage dimension styles |

### 2.2.4 Layer Management

| Command | Alias | Function |
|---------|-------|----------|
| **Layer Properties** | `LA` | Open Layer Properties Manager |
| **Layer On/Off** | Click bulb icon | Show/hide layer |
| **Layer Freeze** | Click snowflake | Freeze (improves performance) |
| **Layer Lock** | Click lock | Prevent editing |
| **Set Current** | Click green check | Make layer active |

---

## 2.3 Coordinate Systems in AutoCAD

AutoCAD uses two principal coordinate systems. **This is THE most tested concept in KTU lab exams.**

### 2.3.1 Absolute Coordinates (Cartesian)

- Origin: **(0, 0)** — intersection of X and Y axes.
- Format: `(X, Y)` — e.g., `100,200` means 100 units in X, 200 in Y.
- Distance between two points $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2)$:

$$
d = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}
$$

### 2.3.2 Relative Coordinates

- **@** symbol denotes **relative to last point**.
- Format: `@dx,dy` — e.g., `@100,50` means move 100 in X, 50 in Y from previous point.
- **Polar format:** `@distance<angle` — e.g., `@100<45` means 100 units at 45°.

### 2.3.3 Snapping & Object Snap (OSNAP) — Critical for Building Drawings

| OSNAP Mode | Symbol | Use in Building Drawings |
|------------|--------|--------------------------|
| **Endpoint** | ▢ | End of wall lines |
| **Midpoint** | ▽ | Center of wall segment |
| **Center** | ⊙ | Center of circular column |
| **Intersection** | ✕ | Where walls meet |
| **Perpendicular** | ⊥ | 90° from a line |
| **Tangent** | ○ | Touch point of arc/circle |
| **Quadrant** | ◇ | N/S/E/W of circle |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Exam:** Always **enable ORTHO mode (F8)** for horizontal/vertical building lines, and use **OSNAP (F3)** for precise wall junctions.

---

## 2.4 Building Components Drawn in AutoCAD

The KTU syllabus specifically focuses on **building components**. Below is the technical breakdown:

### 2.4.1 Walls (Single-Line & Double-Line Representation)

**Single-Line Wall (Plan view):**
- Walls are drawn as a single thick line in plan.
- Outer wall thickness: **200 mm** to **300 mm**.
- Inner partition wall: **100 mm** to **150 mm**.

**Double-Line Wall (Plan view):**
- Two parallel lines, offset by wall thickness.
- Use the **`OFFSET` command** to create the second line.

$$
\text{Wall offset distance} = \text{Wall thickness in mm}
$$

### 2.4.2 Doors

| Door Type | Symbol in Plan | Swing Arc | Use |
|-----------|---------------|-----------|-----|
| Single-Leaf | Rectangle (door leaf) + arc (swing) | **90° arc** | Main door |
| Double-Leaf | Two leaves + double arc | **90°** each | Living room |
| Sliding | Rectangle with sliding arrows | No arc | Cupboards, patios |
| Sliding & Folding | Parallelogram | No arc | Modern interiors |

**Standard Door Sizes (BIS):**

$$
\text{Door width} \in \{700, 800, 900, 1000, 1200\} \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{Door height} = 2100 \text{ mm (standard)} \quad \text{or} \quad 2400 \text{ mm (tall)}
$$

### 2.4.3 Windows

- Shown as **three parallel lines** in plan (sill, opening, and shutter).
- Or shown as a **rectangle with diagonal hatching**.

**Standard Window Sizes:**

$$
W \in \{600, 900, 1000, 1200, 1500, 1800\} \text{ mm}
$$

$$
H \in \{1200, 1500\} \text{ mm} \text{ (standard window height)}
$$

### 2.4.4 Staircase

- Drawn as a series of **parallel rectangles** (treads and risers).
- **Standard Rise:** $R = 150$ mm.
- **Standard Tread (Going):** $T = 250$ to $300$ mm.

**Empirical Rule (Blondel's Formula):**

$$
2R + T = 580 \text{ to } 620 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(comfortable range)}
$$

**Number of Risers Calculation:**

$$
N_{risers} = \frac{\text{Floor-to-Floor Height}}{R} = \frac{3000 \text{ mm}}{150 \text{ mm}} = 20 \text{ risers}
$$

$$
N_{treads} = N_{risers} - 1 = 19 \text{ treads}
$$

### 2.4.5 Columns

- **Square column:** Solid square (e.g., 300 × 300 mm) or filled hatch.
- **Circular column:** Circle (e.g., 400 mm diameter) with solid fill.
- **Rectangular:** Rectangle with cross-hatch.

**Column Placement Rule (IS 456:2000):**

$$
\text{Minimum column size} = 230 \text{ mm} \times 230 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\text{Pedestal column} \geq 300 \text{ mm} \times 300 \text{ mm}
$$

---

## 2.5 Layers Used in Building Drawings (KTU Standard)

| Layer Name | Color | Line Type | Lineweight | Purpose |
|------------|-------|-----------|------------|---------|
| **WALL-EXT** | Red | Continuous | 0.50 mm | External walls |
| **WALL-INT** | Magenta | Continuous | 0.35 mm | Internal walls |
| **DOOR** | Yellow | Continuous | 0.25 mm | Doors + swing |
| **WINDOW** | Cyan | Continuous | 0.25 mm | Windows |
| **STAIR** | Green | Continuous | 0.35 mm | Staircase |
| **COLUMN** | Blue | Continuous | 0.50 mm | Columns |
| **FURNITURE** | 250 (grey) | Continuous | 0.18 mm | Furniture blocks |
| **DIM** | Red | Continuous | 0.18 mm | Dimensions |
| **TEXT** | Green | Continuous | 0.18 mm | Annotations |
| **HATCH** | 8 (grey) | Continuous | 0.13 mm | Hatching patterns |
| **TITLE** | White | Continuous | 0.50 mm | Title block |
| **GRID** | Grey | Dashed | 0.13 mm | Grid lines A, B, C, 1, 2, 3 |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Viva Question:** *"Why do we use different layers?"*
> **Answer:** Layers allow (1) selective visibility, (2) easier editing, (3) logical grouping, (4) selective plotting, and (5) interference-free coordination between disciplines (architectural, structural, electrical, plumbing).

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Quick Revision)

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Application |
|---|-------------------|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | Distance between two points | $d = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2}$ | Verify diagonal of room |
| 2 | Polar coordinate | $r = d$, $\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}\right)$ | Draw diagonal brace |
| 3 | Offset (wall thickness) | $D = t_{wall}$ | Create double-line wall |
| 4 | Area of room (rectangular) | $A = L \times B$ | Carpet area calculation |
| 5 | Area by Polyline in AutoCAD | `AREA` command | Verify room area |
| 6 | Blondel's rule for stairs | $2R + T = 600 \pm 20$ mm | Staircase design |
| 7 | Number of risers | $N = \frac{H_{floor}}{R}$ | Staircase design |
| 8 | Scale factor (plot) | $S = \frac{\text{Model units}}{\text{Paper units}}$ | 1:100 means 1 mm paper = 100 mm model |
| 9 | Plot scale (annotation) | $\text{Annotative scale}$ | Text/dim height auto-adjusts |
| 10 | Conversion (mm → m) | $1 \text{ m} = 1000 \text{ mm}$ | Carpet area in m² |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

1. **Architectural Firms** — Floor plans, elevations, sections, 3D BIM models.
2. **Structural Engineering** — Column layout, beam layout, reinforcement detailing.
3. **MEP (Mechanical, Electrical, Plumbing)** — HVAC ducting, electrical wiring, plumbing layouts.
4. **Interior Design** — Furniture layout, false ceiling plans.
5. **Urban Planning** — Site plans, zoning maps.
6. **Construction Management** — Quantity take-off, BOQ (Bill of Quantities) extraction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality:** Modern Indian construction industry has shifted from **manual drafting** to **BIM (Building Information Modeling)**. AutoCAD is the **gateway software** — every civil/architectural engineer must master it. KTU's inclusion of this module ensures industry-readiness.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Procedure: Drawing a 2BHK Building Plan in AutoCAD

Below is the **exhaustive, step-by-step procedure** that a KTU student would follow in the lab. **Every command, coordinate, and step is explicitly written out.**

### 3.1.1 Setting Up the Drawing Environment

**Step 1: Set Drawing Units**

Type at command line: `UNITS` → `Enter`

| Unit Type | Selection |
|-----------|-----------|
| Type | Decimal |
| Insertion scale | **Millimeters** |
| Precision | 0 (no decimal) |

> Rationale: Indian architectural drawings use **mm** for all dimensions.

**Step 2: Set Drawing Limits (Optional, but recommended for KTU lab)**

Type: `LIMITS` → `Enter`

$$
\text{Lower-left corner} = (0,0)
$$

$$
\text{Upper-right corner} = (59400, 42000) \quad \text{(A1 size in mm)}
$$

Then type `Z` (Zoom) → `A` (All) to view the limits.

**Step 3: Set Grid and Snap (For accuracy)**

- Type `GRID` → set spacing to **100 mm** (for 1:100 scale).
- Type `SNAP` → set spacing to **50 mm** (or use **OSNAP** for object-based snapping).
- **Press F7** to toggle Grid on, **F9** to toggle Snap on.
- **Press F8** to toggle ORTHO on (this is **critical** for building walls which are always horizontal/vertical).

**Step 4: Set Up Layers**

Type: `LA` (Layer Properties Manager) → `Enter`

Create the layers as per the KTU standard layer table (refer Section 2.5).

**Step 5: Set Dimension Style**

Type: `D` (Dimension Style Manager) → `Enter` → `New` → Name: "KTU_ARCH"

| Property | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Text height | **2.5 mm** |
| Arrow size | **2.0 mm** |
| Extension line offset | **1.0 mm** |
| Dimension line spacing | **6.0 mm** |
| Linear scale factor | **1.0** (model) |

Set as **Current** and close.

---

### 3.1.2 Drawing the Outer Wall Outline

**Step 6: Activate WALL-EXT Layer**

Click on the layer dropdown → select **WALL-EXT** → set as current.

**Step 7: Draw Outer Wall with Polyline**

Type: `PL` (Polyline) → `Enter`

| Prompt | User Input | Action |
|--------|-----------|--------|
| Start point | `0,0` | Press Enter for absolute |
| Next point | `@10000,0` | Wall 10 m along +X (East) |
| Next point | `@0,8000` | Wall 8 m along +Y (North) |
| Next point | `@-10000,0` | Wall back along -X (West) |
| Next point | `C` (Close) | Close back to (0,0) |

This creates a **rectangle of 10 m × 8 m** as the outer building footprint.

**Step 8: Offset for Wall Thickness**

Type: `O` (Offset) → `Enter`

| Prompt | Input |
|--------|-------|
| Offset distance | **230** (mm) |
| Select object | Click outer rectangle |
| Side to offset | **Inside** (click inside) |
| Erase source? | **No** |

This creates a **second polyline offset 230 mm inward**, defining the **inner face of the wall**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Wall thickness = 230 mm** is standard for **external load-bearing walls** in India (per IS 4326 and IS 1905).

---

### 3.1.3 Drawing Internal Walls

**Step 9: Activate WALL-INT Layer**

Set **WALL-INT** as the current layer.

**Step 10: Internal Wall 1 (Vertical partition)**

Type: `L` (Line) → `Enter`

| Prompt | Input |
|--------|-------|
| First point | `4000,0` (4 m from left wall, on south wall) |
| Next point | `4000,5000` (5 m north, stopping before north wall for door opening) |

Then use **TRIM** (`TR`) to clean ends.

**Step 11: Internal Wall 2 (Horizontal partition)**

Type: `L` → `Enter`

| Prompt | Input |
|--------|-------|
| First point | `0,5000` (5 m from south wall, on west wall) |
| Next point | `4000,5000` (4 m east, joins with Wall 1) |

**Step 12: Internal Wall 3 (Bedroom separation)**

Type: `L` → `Enter`

| Prompt | Input |
|--------|-------|
| First point | `4000,5000` (joins Wall 1 & Wall 2 junction) |
| Next point | `10000,5000` (goes east to outer wall) |

This creates an internal layout:
- **Room A (Living):** 4 m × 5 m (south-west)
- **Room B (Kitchen):** 6 m × 5 m (south-east)
- **Room C (Bedroom 1):** 4 m × 3 m (north-west)
- **Room D (Bedroom 2):** 6 m × 3 m (north-east)

---

### 3.1.4 Creating Door Openings

**Step 13: Activate DOOR Layer**

Set **DOOR** as the current layer.

**Step 14: Draw Main Door (Living Room — South Wall)**

Type: `REC` (Rectangle) → `Enter`

| Prompt | Input |
|--------|-------|
| First corner | `1500,0` (1.5 m from left of south wall) |
| Other corner | `@1000,0` (door width 1000 mm along +X) |

Wait — this draws on the wall, not as an opening. The correct procedure is:

**Correct Method: Break the wall, then insert door**

a. Use **BREAK** command (`BR`) to create a **1000 mm opening** in the south wall.
b. Draw door leaf as a rectangle inside the room at 90° to wall.
c. Draw the **swing arc** using `A` (Arc) command with **Start, Center, End** method.

**Door Leaf Construction:**

- Door panel: Rectangle of size **1000 mm × 50 mm** (door thickness).
- Hinge point at one end of opening.
- **Swing arc:** Radius = 1000 mm (door width), from 0° to 90°.

Type: `A` → `Enter`

| Prompt | Input |
|--------|-------|
| Start point | Hinge point (one corner of door opening) |
| Center | Other corner of door opening |
| End | End of door leaf (90° from start) |

> [!TIP]
> The **swing arc** always starts from the **hinge point** and curves to the **door leaf's far end**.

**Step 15: Repeat for Internal Doors**

Doors between:
- **Living ↔ Bedroom 1** (1000 × 2100 mm)
- **Kitchen ↔ Bedroom 2** (800 × 2100 mm)
- **Bedroom 1 ↔ Bedroom 2** (900 × 2100 mm)

---

### 3.1.5 Creating Windows

**Step 16: Activate WINDOW Layer**

Set **WINDOW** as current.

**Step 17: Living Room Window (South Wall)**

- Use `BREAK` to create a **1500 mm opening** in the south wall (between the wall edge and the main door).
- Draw **three parallel lines** inside the opening at distances **50 mm apart** (sill, opening, and shutter).
- Or simply use a **RECTANGLE** of size **1500 × 200 mm** as a single-line window symbol.

**Step 18: Bedroom Windows (North Wall)**

Repeat for two bedrooms, each with a **1200 × 1500 mm** window on the north wall.

**Step 19: Kitchen Window (East Wall)**

One **900 × 1200 mm** window on the east wall of the kitchen.

---

### 3.1.6 Drawing the Staircase

**Step 20: Staircase Location**

Place the staircase in the **north-east corner** of the living room (common 2BHK layout).

- Staircase room size: **2500 mm × 5000 mm** (width × length).
- Floor-to-floor height: **3000 mm**.
- Rise: $R = 150$ mm.
- Tread: $T = 250$ mm (verify Blondel's: $2(150) + 250 = 550$ mm ✓ within 580-620).

**Number of risers:**

$$
N = \frac{H_{floor}}{R} = \frac{3000}{150} = 20 \text{ risers}
$$

**Number of treads:**

$$
N_{treads} = N_{risers} - 1 = 19 \text{ treads}
$$

**Step 21: Draw Treads**

Type: `REC` → `Enter`

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Tread width | **250 mm** |
| Tread depth | **1000 mm** (going) |
| First tread | At start of staircase room |
| Repeat | Use `CO` (Copy) or `AR` (Array) — **Rectangular, 19 items, 250 mm apart** |

**Step 22: Draw Direction Arrow**

- Add an **UP arrow** at the top of the staircase with text "UP" using `T` (MTEXT).
- Add a **CUT LINE** (zigzag break line) at the bottom indicating truncation.

---

### 3.1.7 Adding Columns (If Load-Bearing Structure)

**Step 23: Place Columns at Corners and Junctions**

- Size: **300 mm × 300 mm** (typical).
- Locations: At **all four outer corners** + **at T-junctions of internal walls**.

Type: `REC` → `Enter` → `0,0` → `@300,300` (column at SW corner).

**Copy** to other corners using `CO` command.

**For column fill:** Use `H` (Hatch) → select **SOLID pattern** → click inside rectangle.

---

### 3.1.8 Dimensioning the Plan

**Step 24: Set DIM Layer Active and Annotative Scale**

Set **DIM** as current layer.

**Step 25: Apply Linear Dimensions**

Type: `DLI` (Dimension Linear) → `Enter`

| Prompt | Action |
|--------|--------|
| First extension line origin | Click first wall corner |
| Second extension line origin | Click second wall corner |
| Dimension line location | Drag to position outside plan |

**Required dimensions for a plan:**

1. **Overall dimensions** — full width and depth (outermost).
2. **Wall-to-wall dimensions** — internal room dimensions.
3. **Door/window opening dimensions**.
4. **Column locations** (grid-to-grid).

**Step 26: Add Room Labels**

Type: `T` (MTEXT) → click inside room → type "LIVING - 4.00 × 5.00 m" → `Enter`.

Repeat for each room with name and dimension.

---

### 3.1.9 Applying Hatch (if required)

**Step 27: Hatch the Walls**

- Type `H` → `Enter`.
- Pattern: `ANSI31` (diagonal lines, 45°).
- Scale: **50** (for 1:100 drawing).
- Click inside the wall thickness area (between outer and inner wall polylines).
- Press `Enter`.

---

### 3.1.10 Creating the Title Block

**Step 28: Title Block in Layout Space**

Switch to **Layout1** tab (bottom of screen).

- Draw a **rectangle 180 mm × 60 mm** at the bottom-right corner of the sheet.
- Add cells for: Project name, Client, Scale, Date, Drawn by, Checked by, Drawing No., Revision.

---

### 3.1.11 Plotting / Printing

**Step 29: Set Page Setup**

- Type `PAGESETUP` → choose printer (PDF or physical).
- Paper size: **A2 (594 × 420 mm)** or **A1 (841 × 594 mm)**.
- Plot scale: **1:100** (1 mm on paper = 100 mm in model).
- Plot area: **Layout** (entire sheet).

**Step 30: Plot Preview and Print**

- Type `PLOT` → `Enter`.
- Click **Preview** → verify → right-click → **Plot**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Typical KTU 2BHK Plan Dimensions

| Room | Length (m) | Width (m) | Area (m²) | Door (mm) | Window (mm) | Floor Finish |
|------|-----------|-----------|-----------|-----------|-------------|--------------|
| Living | 5.00 | 4.00 | 20.00 | 1000 × 2100 | 1500 × 1500 | Vitrified tiles |
| Kitchen | 5.00 | 3.00 | 15.00 | 800 × 2100 | 900 × 1200 | Vitrified tiles |
| Master Bedroom | 4.50 | 3.50 | 15.75 | 900 × 2100 | 1200 × 1500 | Wooden flooring |
| Bedroom 2 | 4.00 | 3.50 | 14.00 | 900 × 2100 | 1200 × 1500 | Wooden flooring |
| Bathroom 1 | 2.50 | 1.50 | 3.75 | 700 × 2100 | 600 × 600 (vent) | Anti-skid tiles |
| Bathroom 2 | 2.00 | 1.50 | 3.00 | 700 × 2100 | 600 × 600 (vent) | Anti-skid tiles |
| Passage | 5.00 | 1.20 | 6.00 | — | — | Vitrified tiles |
| **Total Carpet Area** | — | — | **77.50 m²** | — | — | — |

**Built-up Area (with 230 mm walls):**

$$
A_{built-up} = (10 + 0.23) \times (8 + 0.23) = 10.23 \times 8.23 = 84.19 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Plinth Area (with 0.6 m projection on all sides):**

$$
A_{plinth} = (10.23 + 1.2) \times (8.23 + 1.2) = 11.43 \times 9.43 = 107.78 \text{ m}^2
$$

---

## 3.3 Common Errors in KTU Lab & Their Fixes

| # | Error | Cause | Fix |
|---|-------|-------|-----|
| 1 | Walls not orthogonal | ORTHO off | Press F8 |
| 2 | Doors not snapping to walls | OSNAP off | Press F3, enable Endpoint |
| 3 | Dimensions too small on plot | Wrong annotative scale | Set annotative scale to 1:100 |
| 4 | Hatch shows as solid black | Wrong pattern scale | Reduce scale to 25-50 |
| 5 | Wall lines have gaps | OFFSET closed polyline issue | Use `PEDIT` to join, then OFFSET |
| 6 | Cannot edit layer | Layer locked | Unlock in Layer Manager |
| 7 | Staircase treads overlap | Wrong array spacing | Recompute using Blondel's rule |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define AutoCAD. List any four advantages of using AutoCAD for building drawing over manual drafting.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
AutoCAD is a computer-aided design (CAD) software developed by Autodesk Inc., used for creating precise 2D and 3D drawings of building components, mechanical parts, and electrical circuits using vector-based graphics and Cartesian coordinate geometry.

**Four Advantages (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **High Precision:** Accuracy up to 0.0001 units, eliminating human drafting errors.
2. **Easy Editing:** Modifications are made instantly with commands like ERASE, TRIM, MOVE, COPY, avoiding manual redrawing.
3. **Reusability:** Blocks and templates allow repeated use of standard symbols (doors, windows, furniture).
4. **Layer Organization:** Multiple disciplines (architectural, structural, electrical) can be separated into layers and superimposed as needed.
5. **Easy Storage and Sharing:** `.dwg` files are compact, easily emailed, and version-controlled.
6. **Plotting at Any Scale:** Drawings can be plotted at any scale (1:50, 1:100, 1:200) on any paper size.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Explain the function of the following AutoCAD commands: (i) OFFSET, (ii) TRIM, (iii) HATCH.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 each):**

**(i) OFFSET (1 mark):**
The OFFSET command creates a parallel copy of a selected object at a specified distance. **Application in building drawing:** Used to create **double-line walls** by offsetting the outer wall polyline inward by the wall thickness (e.g., 230 mm for external walls, 115 mm for internal partitions).
- Command alias: `O`
- Example: `OFFSET → 230 → select wall → click inside`

**(ii) TRIM (1 mark):**
The TRIM command removes portions of objects that extend beyond a selected cutting edge. **Application:** Used to clean up wall intersections, remove excess portions of door swings, and tidy line ends where walls meet.
- Command alias: `TR`
- Example: `TRIM → select cutting edges → click portions to remove`

**(iii) HATCH (1 mark):**
The HATCH command fills a closed region with a predefined pattern (e.g., solid, diagonal, brick, concrete). **Application:** Used to indicate **wall material (brick/concrete)**, **earth in section drawings**, and **concrete in columns/slabs**.
- Command alias: `H`
- Common patterns: `ANSI31` (diagonal), `SOLID` (filled), `AR-CONC` (concrete), `AR-BRICK` (brickwork).

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a) Explain the AutoCAD user interface in detail. Describe the functions of the Ribbon, Command Line, Layer Properties Manager, and Status Bar. (7 marks)**

**(b) Draw the plan of a 2BHK residential building (10 m × 8 m) using AutoCAD. List the steps, commands used, and standard dimensions for walls, doors, windows, and staircase. (7 marks)**

#### Model Answer:

**(a) AutoCAD User Interface (7 marks):**

The AutoCAD user interface consists of the following key components:

**1. Ribbon (2 marks):**
The Ribbon is the main toolbar at the top of the screen, organized into **tabs**, **panels**, and **tools**. Key tabs include:
- **Home tab:** Draw panel (Line, Circle, Rectangle, Polyline, Hatch), Modify panel (Erase, Copy, Move, Trim, Offset, Mirror, Rotate, Array), Annotation panel (Dimensions, Text, Leaders).
- **Insert tab:** Block, External Reference, Image, Layout.
- **Annotate tab:** Dimensions, Tables, Hatches.
- **View tab:** Zoom, Pan, Viewports, Named Views.
- **Output tab:** Plot, Publish, Sheet Set.
- **Manage tab:** Layers, Blocks, CAD Standards.

**2. Command Line (1.5 marks):**
The command line is the text input box at the bottom of the screen. Users can type commands using their full name or alias. For example, `L` for Line, `C` for Circle, `REC` for Rectangle, `CO` for Copy, `M` for Move. The command line provides prompts and feedback, e.g., "Specify first point:" or "Select objects:".

**3. Layer Properties Manager (2 marks):**
Accessed via the `LA` command, this dialog box allows users to create, rename, delete, and modify layers. Each layer can be assigned:
- **Name** (e.g., WALL-EXT, DOOR, WINDOW).
- **Color** (e.g., red, yellow, cyan).
- **Linetype** (e.g., continuous, dashed, dotted).
- **Lineweight** (e.g., 0.25 mm, 0.50 mm).
- **On/Off, Freeze, Lock** states.

Layers are critical for organizing complex building drawings by element type.

**4. Status Bar (1.5 marks):**
The status bar at the bottom provides toggle buttons for:
- **ORTHO (F8):** Restricts cursor to horizontal/vertical — **essential for building walls.**
- **GRID (F7):** Displays dot grid in drawing area.
- **SNAP (F9):** Restricts cursor to grid spacing.
- **OSNAP (F3):** Object snap modes (Endpoint, Midpoint, Center, Intersection).
- **POLAR (F10):** Angle tracking.
- **OTRACK (F11):** Object snap tracking.
- **DYN (F12):** Dynamic input for in-cursor dimension entry.
- **Lineweight:** Visual display of line weights on screen.

---

**(b) 2BHK Plan Drawing Steps (7 marks):**

| Step | Action | Command | Specification |
|------|--------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Set units to mm | `UNITS` | Decimal, mm |
| 2 | Create layers | `LA` | WALL-EXT, WALL-INT, DOOR, WINDOW, STAIR, DIM, TEXT |
| 3 | Draw outer wall | `PL` | 10000 × 8000 mm rectangle |
| 4 | Offset inner wall | `O` | 230 mm inward |
| 5 | Draw internal walls | `L` | 100–150 mm thick (single line) |
| 6 | Insert doors | `REC` + `A` | Main: 1000 × 2100 mm; Internal: 800–900 × 2100 mm |
| 7 | Insert windows | `REC` | 1200–1500 × 1200–1500 mm |
| 8 | Draw staircase | `REC` + `AR` | 1000 mm wide, 19 treads × 250 mm |
| 9 | Place columns | `REC` + `H` | 300 × 300 mm at corners and junctions |
| 10 | Dimension plan | `DLI` | Overall + internal dimensions |
| 11 | Add labels | `T` (MTEXT) | Room names + sizes |
| 12 | Title block | `REC` + `T` | A2 sheet, bottom-right |
| 13 | Save | `CTRL+S` | File: `2BHK_Plan.dwg` |
| 14 | Plot | `PLOT` | A2 sheet, scale 1:100, PDF |

**Standard dimensions used:**

- **External wall:** 230 mm
- **Internal wall:** 115–150 mm
- **Doors:** Width ∈ {700, 800, 900, 1000} mm; Height = 2100 mm
- **Windows:** Width ∈ {900, 1200, 1500, 1800} mm; Height = 1200–1500 mm
- **Staircase:** Riser = 150 mm; Tread = 250 mm; Width = 1000 mm
- **Columns:** 230 × 230 to 300 × 300 mm

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a) Explain the concept of Layers in AutoCAD. Describe the standard layer convention used for building drawings with appropriate colors, linetypes, and lineweights. (7 marks)**

**(b) With neat sketches, explain the conventions used in AutoCAD for drawing (i) doors, (ii) windows, and (iii) staircase in plan view. (7 marks)**

#### Model Answer:

**(a) Layers in AutoCAD (7 marks):**

**Concept of Layers (3 marks):**
A **layer** in AutoCAD is a logical grouping of objects that share common visual properties (color, linetype, lineweight). Think of layers as **transparent sheets** stacked on top of each other. Each sheet contains drawing elements of a specific type (e.g., walls, doors, dimensions). Layers enable:
1. **Selective visibility** — turn off unnecessary layers for clarity.
2. **Easier editing** — lock a layer to prevent accidental modification.
3. **Logical organization** — group elements by discipline (architectural, structural, MEP).
4. **Selective plotting** — plot only the required layers.
5. **Performance** — freezing unused layers speeds up regeneration.

**Standard Layer Convention for Building Drawings (4 marks — 0.5 per layer):**

| Layer Name | Color | Linetype | Lineweight | Purpose |
|------------|-------|----------|------------|---------|
| WALL-EXT | Red (1) | Continuous | 0.50 mm | External walls |
| WALL-INT | Magenta (6) | Continuous | 0.35 mm | Internal walls |
| DOOR | Yellow (2) | Continuous | 0.25 mm | Doors + swing arcs |
| WINDOW | Cyan (4) | Continuous | 0.25 mm | Window openings |
| STAIR | Green (3) | Continuous | 0.35 mm | Staircase treads |
| COLUMN | Blue (5) | Continuous | 0.50 mm | Columns (load-bearing) |
| FURNITURE | Grey (250) | Continuous | 0.18 mm | Interior furniture |
| DIM | Red (1) | Continuous | 0.18 mm | Dimensions |
| TEXT | Green (3) | Continuous | 0.18 mm | Annotations |
| HATCH | Grey (8) | Continuous | 0.13 mm | Material hatching |
| GRID | Grey (9) | Dashed (DASHED) | 0.13 mm | Grid lines |
| TITLE | White (7) | Continuous | 0.50 mm | Title block border |

---

**(b) Plan View Conventions (7 marks):**

**(i) Door Conventions (2.5 marks):**

| Door Type | Plan Symbol | Description |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| Single-leaf hinged door | Rectangle (door leaf) + 90° arc (swing direction) | Most common for main entrances |
| Double-leaf door | Two rectangles + two arcs | Used for living rooms, drawing rooms |
| Sliding door | Rectangle + arrows showing direction | Used for wardrobes, partitions |
| Folding door | Multiple parallel rectangles | Used for store rooms |
| Flush door | Plain rectangle (no arc) | Indicated by single rectangle only |

- **Door leaf width** = clear opening width.
- **Swing arc radius** = door width.
- **Hinge point** is the fixed end of the door.
- **Door symbol is drawn on the side where the door opens** (inside the room it opens into).

**Standard door sizes (as per BIS 1003):**

$$
W \in \{700, 800, 900, 1000, 1200\} \text{ mm}, \quad H = 2100 \text{ mm (residential)}
$$

---

**(ii) Window Conventions (2.5 marks):**

| Window Type | Plan Symbol | Description |
|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| Casement window (single shutter) | Three parallel lines in opening | Most common residential type |
| Casement window (double shutter) | Three lines with center mullion | Wider windows |
| Sliding window | Rectangle with two overlapping frames | Used in modern apartments |
| Fixed window | Rectangle with single line (no shutter) | Used for ventilators |
| Louvered window | Multiple angled lines | Bathroom/toilet windows |

- **Window is shown as the OPENING in the wall**, with two or three lines indicating sill, frame, and shutter.
- **Sill height from floor** = 900 mm (typical).
- **Lintel height** = 2100 mm from floor.

**Standard window sizes:**

$$
W \in \{600, 900, 1000, 1200, 1500, 1800\} \text{ mm}
$$

$$
H \in \{1200, 1500\} \text{ mm}
$$

---

**(iii) Staircase Conventions (2 marks):**

**Plan View of Staircase:**
- Drawn as a series of **parallel rectangles** (each rectangle = one tread).
- Treads are **equally spaced** by the tread width (going).
- The **first tread** is at the bottom (ground floor), and treads continue upward.
- A **zigzag break line** at the end indicates truncation (the staircase continues above).
- An **arrow with "UP" or "DN"** indicates direction of ascent.
- The **width** of the staircase (perpendicular to treads) = clear width for movement.
- A **landing** is shown as a wider rectangle at floor levels or mid-height.

**Empirical Rule (Blondel's Formula):**

$$
2R + T = 600 \pm 20 \text{ mm}
$$

For a 3000 mm floor height with $R = 150$ mm:

$$
T = 600 - 2(150) = 300 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
N_{risers} = \frac{3000}{150} = 20
$$

$$
N_{treads} = 20 - 1 = 19
$$

**Standard staircase width (residential):**

$$
W = 1000 \text{ mm (minimum)} \quad \text{to} \quad 1200 \text{ mm (preferred)}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Model Space vs Layout Space:** Students often plot directly from Model Space and lose scale. **Always plot from Layout Space** with proper viewport scale (1:100).
> 2. **ORTHO mode disabled:** Drawing walls at slight angles (e.g., 89.5°) is a **fatal drafting error** for buildings. Always press **F8** before drawing walls.
> 3. **Wrong units:** Using meters instead of millimeters leads to 1000× dimensional errors. **Verify units** at the start.
> 4. **Missing wall thickness:** Drawing a wall as a single line is **acceptable in plan view only if thickness is annotated**. For double-line representation, use OFFSET.
> 5. **No dimensioning:** A plan without dimensions is **incomplete** for KTU submission. KTU requires **overall + internal + door/window opening** dimensions.
> 6. **Door swing arc missing:** The arc must show **opening direction** clearly.
> 7. **Staircase treads overlapping or mis-spaced:** Use `AR` (Array) with exact **tread width** as spacing.
> 8. **Hatch on wrong layer:** Always create a separate **HATCH** layer with appropriate scale.
> 9. **Forgetting to save:** Save frequently with **Ctrl+S** — examiners may deduct marks for unsaved work.
> 10. **Not converting relative to absolute coordinates when asked:** In viva, be ready to explain **both coordinate systems**.

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

✅ **Definition:** AutoCAD = vector-based 2D/3D CAD software by Autodesk for precise drafting.

✅ **File Formats:** `.dwg` (native), `.dxf` (exchange), `.dwf` (web view).

✅ **Units:** Indian practice uses **millimeters (mm)** for architectural drawings.

✅ **Coordinate Systems:** Absolute `(X,Y)`, Relative `@(dx,dy)`, Polar `@d<θ`.

✅ **Critical Toggle Keys:**
- `F3` — OSNAP (object snap)
- `F7` — GRID display
- `F8` — ORTHO mode (**must be ON for walls**)
- `F9` — SNAP (grid snap)
- `F10` — POLAR tracking
- `F12` — Dynamic input

✅ **Most-Used Draw Commands:** `L` (Line), `PL` (Polyline), `REC` (Rectangle), `C` (Circle), `A` (Arc), `H` (Hatch), `T` (Text).

✅ **Most-Used Modify Commands:** `E` (Erase), `CO` (Copy), `M` (Move), `O` (Offset), `TR` (Trim), `EX` (Extend), `MI` (Mirror), `RO` (Rotate), `AR` (Array), `SC` (Scale).

✅ **Wall Thickness (BIS Standard):**
- External load-bearing wall: **230 mm**
- Internal partition: **115–150 mm**
- Framed structure partition: **100–150 mm**

✅ **Standard Door:** 1000 × 2100 mm (main), 800–900 × 2100 mm (internal).

✅ **Standard Window:** 1200 × 1500 mm or 1500 × 1500 mm, sill at 900 mm.

✅ **Staircase Formula (Blondel):** $2R + T = 600 \pm 20$ mm.
- Standard: R = 150 mm, T = 250–300 mm.

✅ **Layer Convention:** Each building element type on a separate layer with unique color/linetype/lineweight.

✅ **Plot Scale:** 1:100 is most common for residential building plans.

✅ **Title Block:** Always include — Project name, Client, Scale, Date, Drawn by, Checked by, Drawing No., Revision.

✅ **Plotting Process:** Model → Layout → Viewport → Set Scale 1:100 → PageSetup → Plot.

✅ **Industry Relevance:** AutoCAD is a **prerequisite skill** for civil, architectural, interior, structural, and MEP engineers; gateway to **BIM tools** like Revit.

✅ **KTU Viva Mantra:** *"Always start with units, layers, and limits. Always save. Always dimension. Always use ORTHO for walls. Always plot from Layout space with proper scale."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preparation of CAD drawing of plan, section and elevation of single storied residential building
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** GZESL208 - BASIC ELECTRICAL AND ELECTRONICS ENGINEERING WORKSHOP
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Preparation of CAD drawing of plan, section and elevation of single storied residential building

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a CAD Drawing?

**Computer-Aided Design (CAD)** is the use of computer software to create, modify, analyse, and optimise two-dimensional (2D) and three-dimensional (3D) engineering drawings. For the context of this experiment, we use 2D CAD software such as **AutoCAD** to prepare architectural drawings of a single-storied residential building.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):** A CAD drawing is a digital representation of a building's geometry produced using vector-based drafting software, conforming to the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) – IS 962:1989** for architectural drawings and **National Building Code of India (NBC 2016)** conventions.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are building a house with LEGO blocks. Before you start stacking, you need an instruction manual that shows you the **TOP view** (which blocks go where on the floor), the **FRONT view** (what the house looks like from the street), and a **CROSS-SECTION** (a sliced view showing internal layers like walls, floor, and roof). A CAD drawing does exactly this — it is the digital instruction manual for constructing a real building. The **Plan** is the top view, the **Elevation** is the front/side view, and the **Section** is the sliced view that reveals what is hidden inside.

## 1.2 The Three Fundamental Views of a Building

Any residential building, when drawn for construction purposes, is represented by three primary orthographic projections:

### (a) Plan View (Horizontal Projection)
- A **plan** is a **horizontal cross-section** of a building taken at a height of approximately **900 mm (3 feet)** above the finished floor level.
- It shows the layout of rooms, walls, doors, windows, stairs, and furniture arrangement.
- It is essentially a **top-down view** as if the roof were removed and you are looking straight down.

### (b) Section View (Vertical Cut)
- A **section** is a **vertical cut** through the building, typically along a plane passing through important features like doors, windows, and staircases.
- It reveals the internal construction details: foundation depth, plinth height, wall thickness, floor levels, lintel/beam positions, and roof slab.
- The cut plane is shown as a **Section Line A-A** on the plan.

### (c) Elevation View (External Vertical Projection)
- An **elevation** is the **external vertical face** of the building as seen from one side (front, rear, left, or right).
- It shows the external appearance: doors, windows, roof line, parapet, plinth, steps, and architectural finishes.
- It does **not** show what is inside (unlike a section).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Conventions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Units Used in Indian Architectural Practice (BIS):**
> - Drawing units: **Millimetres (mm) for large buildings, centimetres (cm) for small plans**
> - Standard wall thickness: **230 mm (9 inches) for external, 115 mm (4.5 inches) for internal partitions**
> - Standard door size: **900 mm × 2100 mm**
> - Standard window size: **1200 mm × 1200 mm**
> - Standard ceiling height: **3000 mm (10 feet) from floor to roof**
> - Plinth height: **450 mm to 600 mm above ground level**
> - Drawing sheet size: **A1 (594 × 841 mm) for full architectural drawings, A2 for individual views**

## 1.4 Software Tools Used

| Software | Type | Used In Industry |
|----------|------|------------------|
| **AutoCAD** | Commercial (Autodesk) | Industry standard |
| **DraftSight** | Commercial (Dassault Systèmes) | Mid-size firms |
| **FreeCAD** | Open-source | Educational use |
| **LibreCAD** | Open-source | Beginners |
| **SketchUp** | Commercial (Trimble) | 3D conceptualisation |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU GZESL208 workshop examination, the standard software prescribed is **AutoCAD (any version from 2018 onwards)**. Students may also use open-source alternatives if AutoCAD is unavailable in the laboratory.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Standard Components of a Residential Building

A single-storied residential building consists of the following structural and architectural components, each of which must be correctly represented in the CAD drawing:

### A. Sub-Structure (Below Ground)
1. **Foundation** – Transfers load from superstructure to soil. Typically made of **reinforced concrete (RCC)** or **masonry**. For residential buildings, depth is usually **900 mm to 1200 mm** below ground level.
2. **Plinth** – The portion of the wall between the ground level and the finished floor level. Height: **450 mm to 600 mm**.

### B. Super-Structure (Above Ground)
3. **Walls** – Load-bearing (230 mm / 9") and partition walls (115 mm / 4.5").
4. **Lintel** – A horizontal RCC/masonry beam above door/window openings. Depth: **150 mm to 200 mm**.
5. **Sill** – The bottom horizontal member of a window frame. Height from floor: **900 mm**.
6. **Doors** – Standard size **900 × 2100 mm**. Types: panel, flush, glazed.
7. **Windows** – Standard size **1200 × 1200 mm**. Types: casement, sliding, louvered.
8. **Roof Slab** – RCC slab of thickness **120 mm to 150 mm** for residential buildings.
9. **Parapet Wall** – Low wall above the roof slab, height **900 mm to 1000 mm**.

## 2.2 Drawing Conventions (BIS / NBC Standards)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Conventions — Must Remember for Examination:**

| Element | Line Type | Line Weight |
|---------|-----------|-------------|
| Wall outline (cut) | Continuous thick | **0.7 mm** |
| Wall outline (visible) | Continuous medium | **0.5 mm** |
| Centre line | Centre line (long-short dash) | **0.25 mm** |
| Hidden line | Dashed | **0.35 mm** |
| Section line (cutting plane) | Chain line (thick) with arrows | **0.7 mm** |
| Dimension line | Continuous thin | **0.25 mm** |
| Hatching (section cut) | Continuous thin at 45° | **0.25 mm** |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Reference Table

> [!NOTE]
> While CAD drawing is largely a drafting exercise, certain **quantitative formulas** are essential for calculating areas, scales, and dimensioning. These are high-yield for KTU theory questions.

### Table: Essential Formulas for Residential Building Drafting

| Concept | Formula | Variables and Units | Application |
|---------|---------|---------------------|-------------|
| Plinth Area | $A_p = L_o \times B_o$ | $L_o$ = outer length, $B_o$ = outer breadth (m) | Total built-up footprint |
| Carpet Area | $A_c = L_i \times B_i$ | $L_i$ = inner length, $B_i$ = inner breadth (m) | Usable floor area |
| Built-up Area | $A_{bu} = A_c \times 1.20$ | Empirical multiplier of **1.20** | Includes walls (20% allowance) |
| Plot Coverage | $\% = \dfrac{A_p}{A_{plot}} \times 100$ | Ratio as percentage | NBC 2016 maximum: **60% for plots < 100 m²** |
| Scale Conversion (Drawing → Real) | $R = D \times S$ | $D$ = drawn length, $S$ = scale factor | E.g., 1:100 means 1 mm = 100 mm |
| Scale Conversion (Real → Drawing) | $D = \dfrac{R}{S}$ | $R$ = real length, $S$ = scale factor | E.g., 5 m at 1:100 = 50 mm on paper |
| Plinth Area to Floor Area Ratio | FAR = $\dfrac{A_{p,total}}{A_{plot}}$ | Both in m² | NBC 2016 FAR for residential: typically **1.5 to 2.0** |
| Stair Rise Calculation | $R_{total} = N_r \times h_r$ | $N_r$ = number of risers, $h_r$ = rise per step (mm) | Standard $h_r$ = **150 mm** |
| Stair Going Calculation | $G_{total} = N_t \times g_t$ | $N_t$ = number of treads, $g_t$ = going per step (mm) | Standard $g_t$ = **250 mm to 300 mm** |

## 2.4 Standard Drawing Scales (BIS IS 962)

| Drawing Type | Recommended Scale |
|--------------|-------------------|
| **Site Plan** | 1:500 or 1:1000 |
| **Floor Plan** | 1:50 or 1:100 |
| **Elevation** | 1:50 or 1:100 |
| **Section** | 1:50 or 1:100 |
| **Detailed Component Drawings** | 1:5, 1:10, or 1:20 |
| **Location Plan** | 1:2500 or 1:5000 |

## 2.5 Layers Used in AutoCAD

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Layer Management is critical in CAD drafting. KTU expects students to use proper layers.**

| Layer Name | Colour | Line Type | Purpose |
|------------|--------|-----------|---------|
| **A-WALL** | White/Black | Continuous | Walls (thick line) |
| **A-DOOR** | Cyan | Continuous | Doors and swings |
| **A-WIND** | Magenta | Continuous | Windows |
| **A-DIM** | Green | Continuous | Dimensions |
| **A-TEXT** | Yellow | Continuous | Text and labels |
| **A-HATCH** | Grey | Continuous | Section hatching |
| **A-CENTRE** | Red | Centre | Centre lines |
| **A-HIDDEN** | Blue | Dashed | Hidden lines |
| **A-TITLE** | Magenta | Continuous | Title block |
| **A-FURN** | Light blue | Continuous | Furniture layout |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

The ability to prepare CAD drawings of residential buildings is foundational for careers in:
- **Architectural firms** (designing homes, apartments, villas)
- **Structural engineering consultancies** (analysing load paths)
- **Construction companies** (interpreting drawings for site execution)
- **Government town planning departments** (approving building plans)
- **Real estate and property development** (marketing floor plans)
- **Interior design studios** (space planning and renovation)

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Insight:** As per the **Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs (MoHUA)**, all building plan approvals in India under the **Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana (PMAY)** require digitally signed CAD drawings. Mastery of CAD drafting is therefore a direct employability skill for KTU graduates.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Procedure and Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Pre-Drawing Stage: Gathering Building Data

Before opening AutoCAD, the following design data must be fixed. The example below uses a **typical KTU workshop problem** of a small single-storied residential building.

### Example Building Data (Given Problem)

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Plot size | **9.0 m × 12.0 m** (30' × 40') |
| Number of floors | 1 (Single storey) |
| Plinth height | **600 mm** above ground |
| Floor to ceiling height | **3000 mm** |
| Wall thickness (external) | **230 mm** (9") |
| Wall thickness (internal) | **115 mm** (4.5") |
| Door size | **900 mm × 2100 mm** |
| Window size | **1200 mm × 1200 mm** |
| Sill height | **900 mm** |
| Lintel depth | **150 mm** |
| Roof slab thickness | **120 mm** |
| Parapet height | **900 mm** |
| Scale for drawings | **1:50** (Plan, Section, Elevation) |
| Software | AutoCAD |
| Sheet size | A2 (420 × 594 mm) |

### Room Layout (Designed in the Plan)

| Room | Internal Dimensions | Area (m²) |
|------|---------------------|-----------|
| Living Room | 4.0 m × 4.5 m | 18.0 |
| Bed Room 1 | 3.5 m × 4.0 m | 14.0 |
| Bed Room 2 | 3.0 m × 3.5 m | 10.5 |
| Kitchen | 3.0 m × 3.5 m | 10.5 |
| Bathroom | 2.0 m × 2.5 m | 5.0 |
| Toilet | 1.2 m × 2.0 m | 2.4 |
| Verandah | 2.0 m × 4.5 m | 9.0 |
| Passage | 1.2 m × 4.0 m | 4.8 |
| **Total Carpet Area** | — | **74.2 m²** |

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Procedure in AutoCAD

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following is the **standard KTU workshop procedure** to be followed step-by-step. Examiners will check for the logical sequence of commands and final output.

### STEP 1: Initial Setup

**Action 1.1:** Open AutoCAD → Click **New Drawing** → Select **acadiso.dwt** template.

**Action 1.2:** Set drawing units.
- Type `UNITS` in the command line → Select **Decimal** → Insertion scale: **Millimetres**.

**Action 1.3:** Set drawing limits (for A2 sheet at 1:1).
- Type `LIMITS` → Lower-left corner: `0,0` → Upper-right corner: `594,420` (A2 landscape) or `420,594` (A2 portrait).

**Action 1.4:** Set grid and snap.
- Type `GRID` → Set spacing: **10 mm**.
- Type `SNAP` → Set spacing: **5 mm**.

### STEP 2: Create Layers

**Action 2.1:** Type `LAYER` (or `LA`) → Layer Properties Manager opens.

**Action 2.2:** Create the following layers with the specified properties:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Layer 1: } & \text{Name} = \text{A-WALL}, \quad \text{Colour} = 7 \text{ (White/Black)}, \quad \text{LineWeight} = 0.70 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Layer 2: } & \text{Name} = \text{A-DOOR}, \quad \text{Colour} = 4 \text{ (Cyan)}, \quad \text{LineWeight} = 0.50 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Layer 3: } & \text{Name} = \text{A-WIND}, \quad \text{Colour} = 6 \text{ (Magenta)}, \quad \text{LineWeight} = 0.50 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Layer 4: } & \text{Name} = \text{A-DIM}, \quad \text{Colour} = 3 \text{ (Green)}, \quad \text{LineWeight} = 0.25 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Layer 5: } & \text{Name} = \text{A-TEXT}, \quad \text{Colour} = 2 \text{ (Yellow)}, \quad \text{LineWeight} = 0.25 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Layer 6: } & \text{Name} = \text{A-HATCH}, \quad \text{Colour} = 8 \text{ (Grey)}, \quad \text{LineWeight} = 0.25 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Layer 7: } & \text{Name} = \text{A-CENTRE}, \quad \text{Colour} = 1 \text{ (Red)}, \quad \text{LineType} = \text{Centre} \\
\text{Layer 8: } & \text{Name} = \text{A-TITLE}, \quad \text{Colour} = 7 \text{ (White)}, \quad \text{LineWeight} = 0.50 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

### STEP 3: Draw Grid Lines

**Action 3.1:** Make **A-CENTRE** layer current.

**Action 3.2:** Use the `XLINE` (Construction Line) command to draw horizontal and vertical grid lines at intervals corresponding to the structural grid.

**Action 3.3:** Identify each grid intersection with a circle (radius 5 mm) and label (1, 2, 3, … and A, B, C, …) using `TEXT` or `MTEXT`.

### STEP 4: Draw the Plan View

**Action 4.1:** Make **A-WALL** layer current.

**Action 4.2:** Use `RECTANGLE` or `PLINE` (Polyline) command to draw the outer wall outline. Centre coordinates calculation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Outer length} & = 12000 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(plot length, walls built on boundary)} \\
\text{Outer breadth} & = 9000 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Plinth area} & = L_o \times B_o = 12.0 \times 9.0 = 108.0 \text{ m}^2 \\
\text{Plot coverage} & = \dfrac{108.0}{108.0} \times 100 = 100\% \text{ (full coverage)}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a more realistic case where the building occupies a portion of the plot:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Outer length of house} & = 11.5 \text{ m} \\
\text{Outer breadth of house} & = 8.5 \text{ m} \\
\text{Plinth area} & = 11.5 \times 8.5 = 97.75 \text{ m}^2 \\
\text{Setback on each side} & = \dfrac{9.0 - 8.5}{2} = 0.25 \text{ m} \quad \text{(and similarly for length)} \\
\text{Plot coverage} & = \dfrac{97.75}{108.0} \times 100 = 90.5\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Action 4.3:** Use `OFFSET` command to draw inner wall lines.

For a **230 mm external wall**, the inner wall line is offset by **230 mm** inward.
For a **115 mm internal partition**, the offset is **115 mm** from centre line.

**Action 4.4:** Use `TRIM` and `EXTEND` commands to clean up wall intersections.

**Action 4.5:** Make **A-DOOR** layer current. Use `RECTANGLE` and `ARC` commands to draw doors (900 × 2100 mm) and their quarter-circle swing arcs.

**Action 4.6:** Make **A-WIND** layer current. Use `RECTANGLE` command to draw windows (1200 × 1200 mm). Show the window with two parallel lines representing the frame and sill.

**Action 4.7:** Use `HATCH` command (set on **A-HATCH** layer) to add section hatching for walls. Pattern: **ANSI31**, Scale: **1**, Angle: **0°** or **45°**.

### STEP 5: Dimension the Plan

**Action 5.1:** Make **A-DIM** layer current.

**Action 5.2:** Type `DIMSTYLE` → Create a new dimension style named **"ARCH-50"** based on the scale **1:50**:
- Text height: **2.5 mm**
- Arrow size: **1.5 mm**
- Extension line offset: **1.0 mm**

**Action 5.3:** Use `LINEAR` dimension for horizontal/vertical dimensions, `ALIGNED` for slanted walls, `CONTINUE` for chained dimensions.

**Action 5.4:** Add the following dimension chains:
- **Overall dimensions** (outermost): total length and breadth
- **Structural dimensions**: grid to grid
- **Detailed dimensions**: room sizes, door/window widths

**Action 5.5:** Use `MLEADER` (Multi-leader) command to add room name labels (e.g., "BED ROOM 1", "KITCHEN").

### STEP 6: Add Plan Annotations

**Action 6.1:** Add the **Section Line A-A** using `PLINE` (Chain thick line) on the plan, with arrows at both ends and labels "A" at the start and "A" at the end.

**Action 6.2:** Add the **North Direction Symbol** using `BLOCK` and insert it. North arrow should point to the top of the drawing.

**Action 6.3:** Add a **Scale Notation**: "SCALE 1:50" using `MTEXT`.

### STEP 7: Draw the Section View

**Action 7.1:** Establish a new base point for the section drawing. Use `COPY` with a base point at the end of section line A-A.

**Action 7.2:** The section view is drawn with the following vertical levels:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Ground Level (G.L.)} & = 0 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Foundation depth} & = -900 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(below G.L.)} \\
\text{Foundation top} & = -300 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(footing base level)} \\
\text{Plinth level} & = +600 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(finished floor level, F.F.L.)} \\
\text{Sill level} & = +1500 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(F.F.L. + 900 mm)} \\
\text{Lintel level} & = +2100 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(top of door/window opening)} \\
\text{Ceiling level} & = +3000 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(bottom of roof slab)} \\
\text{Roof slab top} & = +3120 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(ceiling + 120 mm slab thickness)} \\
\text{Parapet top} & = +4020 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(roof slab + 900 mm parapet)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Action 7.3:** Use `LINE` command to draw:
- The ground line (continuous thick).
- The foundation outline (showing PCC bed, footing, plinth beam).
- The wall outline (230 mm thick, continuous thick).
- The lintel band (150 mm thick, hatched).
- The roof slab (120 mm thick, hatched).
- The parapet wall (115 mm thick, 900 mm high).

**Action 7.4:** Show the door and window openings in the section:
- Door opening: **900 mm wide × 2100 mm high**, sill at floor level.
- Window opening: **1200 mm wide × 1200 mm high**, sill at +900 mm, lintel at +2100 mm.

**Action 7.5:** Add **HATCH** (ANSI31 pattern) to all cut elements: foundation, walls, lintel, roof slab, parapet.

**Action 7.6:** Add ground texture (sloping diagonal lines at 45°) below the ground level using `HATCH` with **EARTH** pattern or custom line pattern.

**Action 7.7:** Add dimensions on the right side for all vertical levels: GL, PL, SL, LL, CL, RL, PT.

### STEP 8: Draw the Elevation View

**Action 8.1:** The elevation is projected from the section. It is a flat 2D view of the building's external face.

**Action 8.2:** Use the same horizontal dimensions as the section but show the following:
- **Plinth**: Up to 600 mm above GL. Show plinth protection (a small projection at the bottom).
- **Walls**: From plinth top to lintel bottom (continuous thick line outline).
- **Door and Windows**: Show the front view of openings with frame details.
  - Door height visible: **2100 mm** (from FFL to lintel).
  - Window height visible: **1200 mm** (from sill to lintel).
- **Lintels**: A horizontal band above openings.
- **Sunshade/Chajja**: A projected element above windows, typically **600 mm** wide and **75 mm** thick, sloped.
- **Roof Slab Edge**: Visible as a 120 mm projection.
- **Parapet**: 900 mm above roof slab, often with a coping (cover slab).
- **Steps**: A series of 3–4 steps at the entrance, each **150 mm rise** and **300 mm going**.

**Action 8.3:** Add dimensions on the right side and bottom of the elevation.

**Action 8.4:** Add floor level indicator: "FFL +0.60" (600 mm above GL).

### STEP 9: Add Title Block

**Action 9.1:** Draw a rectangular title block at the bottom-right corner of the sheet. Typical size: **180 mm × 90 mm**.

**Action 9.2:** Include the following information:

| Field | Example Value |
|-------|---------------|
| Project Title | "SINGLE STORIED RESIDENTIAL BUILDING" |
| Drawing Title | "PLAN, SECTION & ELEVATION" |
| Scale | 1:50 |
| Drawn By | [Student Name] |
| Roll No. | [Student Roll Number] |
| Date | [Submission Date] |
| Sheet No. | 1 of 1 |
| Institution | "APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY" |
| Department | "Workshop – GZESL208" |

### STEP 10: Final Review and Plotting

**Action 10.1:** Type `ZOOM` → `EXTENTS` to view the entire drawing.

**Action 10.2:** Type `PURGE` to remove unused layers/blocks.

**Action 10.3:** Type `AUDIT` to check for drawing errors.

**Action 10.4:** Use `PLOT` (Ctrl+P) command to print. Set:
- Printer/Plotter: DWG To PDF (or physical printer)
- Paper size: A2
- Plot scale: 1:1 (drawing is already drawn at 1:1 mm; the 1:50 scale is noted in the title block)
- Plot area: Layout

## 3.3 Calculation of Staircase (Optional Component)

If the single-storied building has a staircase to the terrace:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Floor-to-floor height} & = 3000 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Rise per step} (h_r) & = 150 \text{ mm (standard)} \\
\text{Number of risers} (N_r) & = \dfrac{3000}{150} = 20 \text{ risers} \\
\text{Number of treads} (N_t) & = N_r - 1 = 19 \text{ treads} \\
\text{Going per step} (g_t) & = 250 \text{ mm (standard)} \\
\text{Total horizontal length} & = N_t \times g_t = 19 \times 250 = 4750 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Width of stair} & = 1000 \text{ mm (1.0 m, standard for residential)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This staircase occupies a footprint of **4750 mm × 1000 mm** in the plan view.

## 3.4 Verification of Drawing Accuracy

After completing the drawing, verify the following:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Check 1: Wall thickness consistency} & = 230 \text{ mm (external)} = 230 \text{ mm in all drawings} \quad \checkmark \\
\text{Check 2: Door size consistency} & = 900 \times 2100 \text{ mm in plan and elevation} \quad \checkmark \\
\text{Check 3: Section line in plan} & = \text{Must pass through the building} \quad \checkmark \\
\text{Check 4: Plinth height in section} & = 600 \text{ mm above GL} \quad \checkmark \\
\text{Check 5: Parapet height} & = 900 \text{ mm above roof slab top} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

## 4.2 Relationship Between Plan, Section, and Elevation (Projection Diagram)

```mermaid
graph LR
    planA[PLAN VIEW<br/>Top View at 900 mm above FFL<br/>Shows Rooms Walls Doors Windows]

    elevSide -. Shared horizontal dimensions .-> planA

    secAA -. Shared vertical levels .-> elevSide
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Define the terms (i) Plan, (ii) Section, and (iii) Elevation as applied to a residential building drawing. State the section plane height used for drawing the plan.**

**Model Answer:**

(i) **Plan:** A plan is a horizontal section of a building cut by a horizontal plane at a height of approximately **900 mm (3 feet)** above the finished floor level. It shows the layout of rooms, walls, doors, windows, and other features as seen from above.

(ii) **Section:** A section is a vertical cut through a building along a defined cutting plane (e.g., Section A-A). It reveals the internal constructional details such as the foundation, plinth, walls, lintels, floor, and roof slab.

(iii) **Elevation:** An elevation is the external vertical view of a building from one side (front, rear, left, or right). It shows the external appearance, including walls, doors, windows, roof line, parapet, and steps, but does not show the internal construction.

The **section plane height** for the plan is conventionally taken as **900 mm above the finished floor level (FFL)** so that the cut passes through all window openings but remains below the lintel.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**List the standard sizes of (a) a residential door, (b) a residential window, and (c) the height of a parapet wall as per BIS conventions.**

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Standard residential door size: 900 mm × 2100 mm** (3' × 7') — This is the most commonly used size for main doors and internal doors in Indian residential buildings.

(b) **Standard residential window size: 1200 mm × 1200 mm** (4' × 4') — This size is used for most rooms. Bathroom/toilet windows may be smaller, typically **600 mm × 600 mm** or **900 mm × 900 mm**.

(c) **Standard parapet wall height: 900 mm** above the roof slab level. The parapet wall is provided on the periphery of the terrace for safety and is typically **115 mm thick** with a coping (cover slab) on top.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern for the workshop course, Part B carries 14 marks with internal choice. Students must answer ONE full question. Each sub-part is for 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model 2024]

**Q.A (a)** [7 Marks]: List and explain the different types of lines used in architectural CAD drawings as per BIS standards. State their application with line weights.

**Q.A (b)** [7 Marks]: For a single-storied residential building with the following data, calculate the plinth area, built-up area, and carpet area:
- Plot size: **10 m × 15 m**
- Building outer dimensions on plot: **9 m × 13 m** (with setbacks)
- External wall thickness: **230 mm**
- Internal wall thickness: **115 mm**
- Verandah area: **12 m²** (counted at 50% as per local byelaws)

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Line Types in Architectural CAD (BIS IS 962):**

| Line Type | Appearance | Line Weight | Application |
|-----------|------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Outline (visible)** | Continuous thick | **0.7 mm** | Visible edges of walls, doors, windows |
| **Outline (hidden)** | Continuous medium | **0.5 mm** | Edges seen but not on the cut plane |
| **Hidden line** | Dashed (short dashes) | **0.35 mm** | Edges hidden behind solid surfaces |
| **Centre line** | Long-short dash | **0.25 mm** | Centres of circles, symmetric features, grid lines |
| **Dimension line** | Continuous thin | **0.25 mm** | All dimensioning |
| **Leader line** | Continuous thin | **0.25 mm** | Annotations and notes |
| **Section line (cutting plane)** | Chain line thick with arrows | **0.7 mm** | Indicates where the section is cut |
| **Hatching/break line** | Continuous thin | **0.25 mm** | Section cut indication, long material breaks |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important BIS Rule:** All visible outlines on a building drawing must be **0.7 mm thick continuous lines**, while all hidden lines, dimensions, and annotations must be **0.25 mm thin lines**. This contrast is essential for readability.

**Part (b) — Area Calculations:**

**Step 1: Calculate the Plinth Area**
The plinth area is the built-up area at the plinth level (footprint of the building).

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_p & = L_o \times B_o \\
A_p & = 9.0 \text{ m} \times 13.0 \text{ m} \\
A_p & = 117.0 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Built-up Area**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{bu} & = A_p = 117.0 \text{ m}^2 \quad \text{(plinth area equals built-up area for single storey)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the Carpet Area (Inner usable area)**

The internal dimensions are obtained by subtracting wall thicknesses from outer dimensions. For simplicity, subtracting an average wall zone of **230 mm** on all four sides:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Internal length} & = 13.0 - 2 \times 0.230 = 12.54 \text{ m} \\
\text{Internal breadth} & = 9.0 - 2 \times 0.230 = 8.54 \text{ m} \\
A_{c,envelope} & = 12.54 \times 8.54 = 107.09 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Subtracting internal partition walls (assume 12% of the envelope area for partitions):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{c} & = 107.09 - (0.12 \times 107.09) \\
A_{c} & = 107.09 - 12.85 \\
A_{c} & = 94.24 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Account for Verandah (counted at 50%)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{c,verandah,eff} & = 12.0 \times 0.50 = 6.0 \text{ m}^2 \\
A_{c,total} & = 94.24 + 6.0 = 100.24 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Summary:**

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Plinth Area | **117.0 m²** |
| Built-up Area | **117.0 m²** |
| Carpet Area | **100.24 m²** |
| Plot Coverage | $\dfrac{117.0}{150.0} \times 100 = $ **78.0%** (within NBC 2016 limit of 60–75% for 100–150 m² plots) |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model 2024]

**Q.B (a)** [7 Marks]: Describe with a neat sketch the procedure to draw a section view (Section A-A) of a single-storied residential building in AutoCAD. Mention the various vertical levels and their standard values.

**Q.B (b)** [7 Marks]: For a staircase in the same residential building with floor-to-floor height of 3000 mm, calculate the (i) number of risers, (ii) number of treads, (iii) total horizontal length of the staircase, and (iv) verify the design satisfies the "2R + G = 600 mm" rule (where R = rise, G = going).

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Procedure to Draw Section A-A:**

The procedure to draw the section in AutoCAD is as follows:

1. **Identify the Section Line** in the plan view. Section A-A is typically a thick chain line passing through the building, marked with arrows at both ends and labels "A" at start and "A" at end.

2. **Set up a new drawing area** below the plan view (or in a separate layout) to draw the section.

3. **Draw the Ground Line (G.L.)** as a continuous thick line at the base.

4. **Establish vertical levels** (using offset from GL):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{GL} & = 0 \text{ mm (Reference)} \\
\text{Foundation depth} & = -900 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Foundation top (footing top)} & = -300 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Plinth Level (FFL)} & = +600 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Sill Level} & = +1500 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Lintel Level} & = +2100 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Ceiling Level} & = +3000 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Roof Slab Top} & = +3120 \text{ mm} \\
\text{Parapet Top} & = +4020 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

5. **Draw the foundation** (continuous thick outline) showing PCC (Plain Cement Concrete) bed, brick masonry footing, and plinth beam.

6. **Draw the wall** (continuous thick) of **230 mm thickness** from plinth to lintel level, and from lintel to roof slab.

7. **Draw door and window openings** at the correct locations on the wall.

8. **Draw the lintel band** (150 mm thick, hatched) at +2100 mm level.

9. **Draw the roof slab** (120 mm thick, hatched) at +3000 mm to +3120 mm.

10. **Draw the parapet** (115 mm thick) from +3120 mm to +4020 mm.

11. **Apply HATCH** (ANSI31 pattern) to all cut portions of walls, lintel, roof, and parapet.

12. **Add ground texture** below the GL using diagonal hatching.

13. **Add dimension lines** on the right side indicating all vertical levels.

14. **Add labels** for the section: "SECTION A-A" and "SCALE 1:50".

**Part (b) — Staircase Calculations:**

Given: Floor-to-floor height = **3000 mm**, Standard rise per step = **150 mm**, Standard going per step = **250 mm**.

**Step 1: Number of Risers**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_r & = \dfrac{\text{Floor-to-floor height}}{\text{Rise per step}} \\
N_r & = \dfrac{3000}{150} \\
N_r & = 20 \text{ risers}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Number of Treads**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_t & = N_r - 1 \\
N_t & = 20 - 1 \\
N_t & = 19 \text{ treads}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Total Horizontal Length (Run of Stair)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{stair} & = N_t \times g_t \\
L_{stair} & = 19 \times 250 \\
L_{stair} & = 4750 \text{ mm} = 4.75 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Verification Using 2R + G Rule**

The empirical rule for comfortable stair design states:

$$
2R + G = 600 \text{ mm} \quad \text{(ideal range: 600 to 640 mm)}
$$

Substituting R = 150 mm and G = 250 mm:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2R + G & = 2(150) + 250 \\
2R + G & = 300 + 250 \\
2R + G & = 550 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $2R + G = 550$ mm, which is slightly **less than 600 mm**. This indicates the stair is **slightly steep**. To satisfy the rule, either:
- Increase the going to 300 mm: $2(150) + 300 = 600$ mm ✓
- This gives $L_{stair} = 19 \times 300 = 5700$ mm = 5.70 m

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Mistakes to Avoid:**
> 1. **Confusing Plan and Section:** Many students draw the plan with vertical walls shown or draw the elevation with internal furniture. Remember: **Plan = top view, Section = cut view, Elevation = external face only**.
> 2. **Wrong Section Plane Height:** The plan is cut at **900 mm above FFL**, not at the floor level. Drawing the plan at FFL will hide all window openings.
> 3. **Missing Section Line A-A:** Always draw the section cutting plane line on the plan with arrows and labels. Without it, the section cannot be interpreted.
> 4. **Inconsistent Dimensions:** The same door/window must be the **same size** in plan, section, and elevation. Examiners check for this consistency strictly.
> 5. **Forgetting Hatching in Section:** All cut elements (walls, lintel, roof, foundation) **must be hatched**. Unhatched sections lose 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Not Showing North Arrow and Title Block:** These are mandatory components of an architectural drawing. Missing them costs 1 mark.
> 7. **Wrong Layer Usage:** Drawing all elements on a single layer (Layer 0) is unprofessional. Use proper layer management.
> 8. **Forgetting Dimensions:** Drawings without dimensions cannot be used for construction. Always add overall, structural, and detailed dimensions.
> 9. **Confusing Plinth with Foundation:** Plinth is the wall portion **above ground up to floor level** (600 mm). Foundation is **below ground** (depth 900 mm).
> 10. **Ignoring Scale Notation:** Always write "SCALE 1:50" in the title block and near each view. Drawings without scale lose marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorise for KTU Exam:**

### Drawing Conventions
- [ ] Plan is cut at **900 mm above FFL**
- [ ] Section is cut along a **chain line with arrows**, labelled A-A
- [ ] Elevation is the **external vertical face**, no internal details
- [ ] Wall thickness: **230 mm (external)**, **115 mm (internal)**
- [ ] Standard door: **900 × 2100 mm**
- [ ] Standard window: **1200 × 1200 mm**
- [ ] Sill height: **900 mm**, Lintel depth: **150 mm**
- [ ] Plinth height: **450–600 mm**, Foundation depth: **900–1200 mm**
- [ ] Ceiling height: **3000 mm**, Roof slab: **120 mm thick**
- [ ] Parapet: **900 mm high**, **115 mm thick**

### AutoCAD Commands (in Order of Use)
- [ ] `UNITS` → Set to millimetres (decimal)
- [ ] `LIMITS` → Set sheet size (A2: 594 × 420)
- [ ] `LAYER` → Create 8 standard layers
- [ ] `XLINE` → Construction lines for grid
- [ ] `PLINE` / `RECTANGLE` → Wall outline
- [ ] `OFFSET` → Inner wall lines
- [ ] `TRIM`, `EXTEND` → Clean wall corners
- [ ] `HATCH` → Section hatching (ANSI31)
- [ ] `DIMSTYLE` → Create ARCH-50 style
- [ ] `LINEAR`, `ALIGNED`, `CONTINUE` → Dimensions
- [ ] `MLEADER` → Room labels
- [ ] `PLOT` → Print/PDF output

### Area Formulas
- [ ] Plinth Area = $L_o \times B_o$
- [ ] Carpet Area ≈ 0.80 × Plinth Area
- [ ] Built-up Area = Plinth Area (single storey)
- [ ] Plot Coverage = (Plinth Area ÷ Plot Area) × 100
- [ ] NBC 2016 max plot coverage: **60–75%** depending on plot size

### Staircase Rule
- [ ] Rise per step (R): **150 mm** (residential)
- [ ] Going per step (G): **250 mm** (residential)
- [ ] Comfort rule: $2R + G = 600$ mm
- [ ] Number of treads = Number of risers − 1

### BIS Standards to Remember
- [ ] **IS 962:1989** – Code of practice for architectural drawings
- [ ] **IS 883:1966** – Code of practice for timber doors
- [ ] **IS 1038:1983** – Code of practice for steel windows
- [ ] **NBC 2016** – National Building Code of India
- [ ] **IS 1077:1992** – Common burnt clay building bricks

### Key Takeaways
1. **Plan, Section, and Elevation are the three orthographic views** of any building — they are mutually consistent.
2. **CAD drafting requires strict adherence to BIS standards** for lines, dimensions, symbols, and scales.
3. **Layer management is a professional practice** — use at least 8 layers for clarity.
4. **Dimensions must be consistent** across all three views.
5. **The section is the most informative drawing** — it shows what cannot be seen in plan or elevation.
6. **Title block, north arrow, and scale notation are mandatory** for any architectural drawing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to "prepare" a CAD drawing, examiners will check for: (1) correct software usage, (2) adherence to BIS conventions, (3) dimensioning accuracy, (4) layer management, (5) title block completeness, and (6) consistency between plan, section, and elevation. Practise at least **3 full drawing exercises** before the exam to gain speed and accuracy.
